# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Task-Driven Selection of Degrees of Freedom

The number of independent position variables a robot must control is **mathematically derived** from the task's dimensionality. This is a foundational KTU-level derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Task Dimensionality

A task in 3D space requires positioning the end-effector at a target point. Each point has **3 translational coordinates** $(x, y, z)$. In addition, to orient the end-effector (e.g., align a welding torch normal to a surface), we need **3 rotational coordinates** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$.

$$
n_{task} = 3_{translation} + 3_{orientation} = 6
$$

### Step 2 — Match to Manipulator DOF

For the robot to fully control the task, its degrees of freedom must satisfy:

$$
DOF_{manipulator} \geq n_{task}
$$

Therefore:

$$
DOF_{manipulator} \geq 6
$$

### Step 3 — Application-Driven Reduction

Many industrial tasks are **lower-dimensional** (planar or cylindrical), so the required DOF is reduced:

**Case A — Pick-and-Place on a conveyor (top-down):**
The end-effector descends vertically and picks. The orientation is fixed (gripper pointing down).

$$
n_{task} = (x, y) + 0_{orientation} + z_{prismatic} = 3 \text{ DOF}
$$

A **Cartesian (3P)** or **SCARA (3R + 1P)** manipulator suffices.

**Case B — Arc Welding on a planar seam:**
The torch must follow a planar path *and* maintain a constant orientation (normal to the seam).

$$
n_{task} = (x, y)_{path} + \theta_{constant} + z_{standoff} = 3 \text{ DOF}
$$

A **3-DOF Cartesian gantry** is technically sufficient, but a **6-DOF articulated arm** is preferred for seam accessibility in 3D.

**Case C — Full 3D welding (e.g., car body):**
The torch must reach arbitrary $(x, y, z)$ and orient itself along the seam normal (which changes along the path).

$$
n_{task} = (x, y, z) + (\phi, \theta, \psi) = 6 \text{ DOF}
$$

A **6-DOF articulated manipulator** is mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Workspace Volume as a Function of Application

For a **spherical-coordinate articulated robot** (common in welding/painting), the reachable workspace is bounded by:

- **Minimum reach:** $r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$ (links folded).
- **Maximum reach:** $r_{max} = l_1 + l_2$ (links extended).

The total **reachable workspace volume** for a 2-link planar arm in a single plane is:

$$
V_{planar} = \pi \left( r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2 \right)
$$

Substituting the limits:

$$
V_{planar} = \pi \left[ (l_1 + l_2)^2 - (l_1 - l_2)^2 \right]
$$

Expanding the bracket:

$$
(l_1 + l_2)^2 - (l_1 - l_2)^2 = \left(l_1^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 + l_2^2\right) - \left(l_1^2 - 2 l_1 l_2 + l_2^2\right)
$$

$$
= 4 l_1 l_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{planar} = 4 \pi l_1 l_2}
$$

For a full **3D spherical workspace** swept by the 2-link arm, integrate over the joint range:

$$
V_{spherical} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left( r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3 \right)
$$

### Application Hook:

For a **palletizing robot** stacking boxes of size $s$ on a pallet of area $A_{pallet}$, the required planar reach is:

$$
r_{required} = \sqrt{\frac{A_{pallet}}{\pi}} + s_{safety}
$$

Setting $r_{required} \leq r_{max}$ defines the **link-length design constraint** for the application.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application → Design Decision Mapping

This matrix is the **gold-standard KTU reference** for cross-checking any design question.

| Application | Kinematic Choice | Drive Choice | Sensor Suite | Safety Standard | Typical Cell Configuration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCB Pick-and-Place | Delta / SCARA | Direct-drive servo | Vision (downward) | ISO 10218-1 | Inline conveyor cell |
| Arc Welding | 6-DOF Articulated | AC servo + harmonic | Seam tracker, vision | ISO 10218-1, ISO 15066 | Rotary table cell |
| Spot Welding | Heavy 6-DOF | Servo (or hydraulic) | None (pre-programmed) | ISO 10218-1 | Inline transfer line |
| Painting | 6-DOF + Hollow Wrist | Explosion-proof servo | None (pre-programmed) | ATEX, ISO 10218-1 | Floor-mounted, enclosed booth |
| Assembly | SCARA / Cobot | Direct-drive servo | Force/torque, vision | ISO/TS 15066 | Shared workspace |
| Palletizing | 4-Axis Articulated | High-payload servo | None | ISO 10218-1 | End-of-line standalone |
| Machining | 6-DOF + stiff gearbox | Servo (no backlash) | Force, vibration | ISO 10218-1 | Heavy casting cell |
| Surgery | 6–7 DOF | Back-drivable servo | Force, vision, haptics | IEC 80601-2-77 | Teleoperation master–slave |
| Space | 6–7 DOF long-reach | Space-qualified | Vision, LIDAR | NASA / ESA standards | Remote teleoperation |
| Underwater | 6-DOF pressure-rated | Oil-filled submersible | Sonar, cameras | DNV / ABS | ROV-mounted skid |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A pharmaceutical company wants to automate vial pick-and-place from a moving conveyor to a blister tray. The conveyor is 1.2 m wide, the tray is 0.8 m × 0.6 m, and vials weigh 50 g each. Determine the minimum DOF, suitable kinematic configuration, and approximate link lengths.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Determine DOF:**
The task is top-down pick-and-place with fixed gripper orientation.

$$
DOF_{required} = (x, y)_{conveyor-to-tray} + z_{vertical} = 3 \text{ DOF}
$$

**Step 2 — Select Configuration:**
A **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** is ideal — fast in the horizontal plane, compliant vertically, and cost-effective for small payloads.

**Step 3 — Compute Reach:**
The diagonal span from one end of the conveyor to the opposite end of the tray:

$$
L_{reach} = \sqrt{(1.2 + 0.8)^2 + (0.6)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{(2.0)^2 + (0.6)^2} = \sqrt{4.0 + 0.36} = \sqrt{4.36}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{reach} \approx 2.09 \text{ m}}
$$

To avoid singularity at full extension, set the **maximum reach** at 110% of the required reach:

$$
r_{max} = 1.10 \times 2.09 \approx 2.30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Payload:**
Vial weight = $50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$. Add gripper mass (~$2 \text{ kg}$) and safety factor (2×):

$$
P_{design} = 2 \times (0.05 + 2.0) \approx 4.1 \text{ kg}
$$

A standard SCARA with **5 kg payload rating** is acceptable.

> **Answer:** A **3-DOF SCARA** with **$r_{max} \approx 2.3$ m** reach and **$\geq 5$ kg payload** rating is the appropriate choice.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Classify the major application areas of industrial robotic manipulators. Mention at least four with their typical kinematic configurations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major industrial application areas of robotic manipulators are:

1. **Material Handling and Pick-and-Place** — uses **Cartesian (PPP)** or **SCARA (RRP)** configurations for high-speed, top-down transfers.

2. **Welding (Spot and Arc)** — employs **6-DOF articulated (RRRRRR)** manipulators with hollow wrists to route cables and maintain torch orientation.

3. **Assembly Operations** — uses **SCARA** for planar insertions and **6-DOF cobots** for 3D force-controlled assembly, often equipped with **Remote Center Compliance (RCC)** devices.

4. **Painting and Coating** — requires **6-DOF hollow-wrist articulated** robots with **ATEX-certified explosion-proof** drives to operate safely in solvent-rich environments.

5. **Machining and Material Removal** — uses **6-DOF high-stiffness articulated** arms with vibration-damped gearboxes for milling and deburring.

*(Any four with correct kinematic linkage — 3 marks: 0.5 mark for each application + 0.5 mark for the configuration.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Differentiate between an industrial robot and a service robot with two examples each. State the ISO standard that governs collaborative robot safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Industrial Robot | Service Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Environment** | Structured factory floor, fenced | Semi/unstructured human environment |
| **Task Type** | Repetitive, high-precision manufacturing | Variable, human-interactive |
| **Safety** | Physical barriers, ISO 10218-1 | Intrinsic safety, ISO/TS 15066 |
| **Example 1** | 6-DOF Arc Welding Robot (e.g., Fanuc ARC Mate) | Surgical Robot (e.g., Da Vinci) |
| **Example 2** | SCARA PCB Assembly Robot (e.g., Epson LS-Series) | Domestic Vacuum Robot (e.g., iRobot Roomba) |

**ISO Standard for Collaborative Robot Safety:** **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — specifies force and pressure limits (≤ **150 N** quasi-static contact, ≤ **65 N/m²** maximum pressure) for human–robot coexistence.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

#### **(a)** [7 Marks]
A manufacturing company intends to deploy a robotic manipulator for **arc welding of motorcycle frames**. Identify the suitable **kinematic configuration**, **degrees of freedom**, **payload**, and **repeatability** required. Justify each selection with reference to the application's geometric and process constraints.

#### **(b)** [7 Marks]
For the same application, sketch the **work-cell layout** including the manipulator, positioner, safety enclosure, and control cabinet. Discuss **two** key safety standards applicable.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Manipulator Specification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Kinematic Configuration:**
A **6-DOF articulated (anthropomorphic) robot** with a **hollow wrist** is selected.
- **Justification:** A motorcycle frame consists of 3D tubular joints requiring the welding torch to reach into concave corners and orient the electrode normal to the seam at varying angles. The **6 DOF** provides full $(x, y, z)$ positioning plus $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ orientation. A **hollow wrist** allows the welding cable and gas hose to be routed *internally* through the arm, eliminating external cable snag and ensuring consistent wire feed.

**Step 2 — Degrees of Freedom:**
$$
DOF = 6
$$
- 3 DOF for arm positioning (shoulder, elbow, vertical lift).
- 3 DOF for wrist orientation (roll, pitch, yaw — `RRR` wrist).
- **Justification:** The arc welding task requires the torch tip to maintain a constant **stand-off distance** (2–4 mm) and a constant **travel angle** (0°–15°) along a 3D seam — achievable only with full 6-DOF control.

**Step 3 — Payload:**
$$
P = m_{torch} + m_{cable} + m_{fixture} + m_{safety} \approx 5 + 3 + 2 + 5 = 15 \text{ kg}
$$
A **20 kg payload** rated robot is selected (factor of safety ≈ 1.33).
- **Justification:** The end-effector assembly (torch + wire feeder + bracket) weighs ~8–10 kg. Process stability requires the robot to handle this mass at full reach without violating acceleration limits.

**Step 4 — Repeatability:**
$$
R = \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$
- **Justification:** Arc weld bead quality (penetration, width) is highly sensitive to torch position. Industry standards (AWS D16.1) demand ±0.1 mm or better for thin-wall tube welding. A ±0.05 mm robot ensures consistent bead geometry.

**Step 5 — Reach:**
Motorcycle frames are typically ≤ 1.5 m long. With a 2-axis positioner, the manipulator needs a reach of:
$$
L_{reach} \approx 1.5 \text{ m}
$$
A **1.6–1.8 m reach** robot is selected to provide access from multiple approach angles.

---

##### Part (b) — Work-Cell Layout and Safety Standards (7 marks)

**Work-Cell Layout Description:**

The cell comprises the following components arranged in an **L-shaped floor layout**:
- **Robot pedestal** at the center, mounted on a 200 mm riser.
- **2-axis servo positioner** (headstock + tailstock) holding the motorcycle frame in front of the robot.
- **Welding power source** and **wire feeder unit** placed behind the robot (cable length minimization).
- **Control cabinet** to the side, with HMI pendant accessible from outside the fence.
- **Light curtain** on the operator loading side; **hard fencing** on the remaining three sides.
- **Fume extraction duct** mounted above the welding zone.

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  FUME EXTRACTION DUCT (overhead)       │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
   ┌─────────┐         ┌───────────────┐
   │CONTROL  │         │  WELDING      │
   │CABINET  │         │  POWER SOURCE │
   └─────────┘         └───────────────┘
        ↑                      ↑
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
   │   LIGHT CURTAIN (operator side)      │
   │   ┌─────────────────────┐            │
   │   │  6-DOF ROBOT ARM    │            │
   │   │  (pedestal mounted) │            │
   │   └─────────────────────┘            │
   │         ↓ Torch                      │
   │   ┌─────────────────────┐            │
   │   │  2-AXIS POSITIONER  │            │
   │   │  (motorcycle frame) │            │
   │   └─────────────────────┘            │
   │  HARD FENCE (3 sides)                │
   └──────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Safety Standards:**

1. **ISO 10218-1:2011 / ISO 10218-2:2011** — Governs industrial robot safety design and integration. Specifies **separated safeguarded space**, **emergency stop categories** (Stop 0 and Stop 1), and **reduced speed** (< 250 mm/s) during teaching.

2. **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** (US equivalent, often co-cited) — Defines **risk assessment methodology** and **safeguarding devices** (light curtains, safety mats, interlocks).

3. **ISO 14744-1** — Specific to **welding safety** (fume extraction, arc flash protection, electrical isolation of the welding circuit from the robot controller).

*(Any two standards with correct technical content — full marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

#### **(a)** [7 Marks]
Explain the role of robotic manipulators in **medical surgery** and **space exploration**. Compare the two applications in terms of DOF, accuracy, environmental challenges, and control architecture.

#### **(b)** [7 Marks]
For an **underwater ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) manipulator** designed for pipeline inspection at 1500 m depth, list **three** critical design challenges and propose engineering solutions for each.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Medical vs Space Manipulators (7 marks)

**Medical Surgery Robots (e.g., Da Vinci Surgical System):**
- **DOF:** Typically **6–7 DOF** at the patient-side manipulator (PSM) plus **4 DOF at the end-effector instrument** (roll, pitch, yaw, grip) → effectively 10 DOF per arm.
- **Accuracy:** Sub-millimeter (**±0.01 to ±0.05 mm**).
- **Control Architecture:** **Master–slave teleoperation** with **scaled motion** (typical 3:1 to 10:1 scaling ratio) and **haptic force feedback** to the surgeon.
- **Environment:** Sterile, temperature-controlled, human body cavity (constrained workspace).

**Space Manipulators (e.g., Canadarm2 on ISS, Perseverance Robotic Arm):**
- **DOF:** **6–7 DOF** with long-reach links (Canadarm2: 17.6 m fully extended).
- **Accuracy:** **±1 cm** at full reach (relative positioning, often GPS-aided).
- **Control Architecture:** **Ground teleoperation with predictive displays** + **autonomous capture** using force-torque compliance and vision.
- **Environment:** Vacuum, extreme thermal cycling (–150 °C to +150 °C), radiation, microgravity (zero-g dynamics dominate).

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Medical Surgery | Space Exploration |
|---|---|---|
| **DOF** | 6–10 (master + slave) | 6–7 |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.01 mm | ±10 mm (long reach) |
| **Dominant Dynamics** | Friction, hysteresis | Zero-g momentum conservation |
| **Sensors** | Force, vision, haptics | Vision, LIDAR, IMU, FT |
| **Control** | Teleop (scaled, haptic) | Teleop + autonomous |
| **Design Priority** | Sterility, patient safety | Radiation hardening, thermal stability |

---

##### Part (b) — Underwater ROV Manipulator Design (7 marks)

**Challenge 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Collapse:**
At 1500 m depth, hydrostatic pressure is:
$$
P = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1500 \approx 14.7 \text{ MPa} \approx 147 \text{ atm}
$$
- **Engineering Solution:** Use a **pressure-balanced oil-filled joint** design — the internal cavity is filled with incompressible silicone oil, and a **compensator bladder** on the surface adjusts volume with depth. All seals use **radial O-rings on metal-to-metal faces** (per API 17D standard).

**Challenge 2 — Tether Management and Signal Latency:**
The ROV is connected via an **umbilical cable** carrying power, data, and fiber optics. Drag forces on the tether limit manipulator payload.
- **Engineering Solution:** Use a **fiber-optic tether** for high-bandwidth, low-loss communication, paired with **on-board pressure-tolerant electronics** (rated to 6000 m) to minimize the number of conductors. Implement **model-based feedforward control** to compensate for tether-induced drag and pendulum oscillation.

**Challenge 3 — Vision and Sensing in Murky Water:**
Suspended particles cause **backscatter**, degrading optical vision.
- **Engineering Solution:** Integrate a **multi-sensor suite** including:
  - **Structured-light stereo vision** for close-range (≤ 2 m) inspection.
  - **Sonar (acoustic) imaging** for long-range (> 5 m) obstacle detection.
  - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) + Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** for dead-reckoning navigation.
  - **LED ring illuminator** with polarization filters to suppress backscatter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Application Area Questions (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DOF with Axes:** A 6-axis robot has 6 *revolute* joints, giving 6 DOF. Students often write "6 axes = 6 degrees of freedom = can reach any point" — WRONG. 6 DOF is the *minimum* for full spatial positioning, but the *workspace* is still bounded by link lengths.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the Application Context:** Writing generic DH-parameter solutions without stating the application loses **1–2 marks** in KTU valuation. Always preface: *"Considering a 6-DOF articulated robot used in arc welding..."*
>
> 3. **Payload vs. Reach Trade-off:** Students often assume the rated payload applies at full reach. In reality, payload is **rated at full reach and full speed**. For welding, add a **derating factor of 1.3–1.5×** to be safe.
>
> 4. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy:** **Repeatability** = ability to return to a taught position (bidirectional). **Accuracy** = ability to reach an absolute commanded position (unidirectional). KTU expects this distinction.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Safety Standards:** Application area questions without citing **ISO 10218-1** or **ISO/TS 15066** are considered incomplete. Always include at least one standard.
>
> 6. **Mixing Up Standards:** Collaborative robots → **ISO/TS 15066**. Industrial robots → **ISO 10218-1/-2**. Medical robots → **IEC 80601-2-77**. Don't interchange them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Recap — Application Area**

- **Application Area** = task-environment pair that defines robot design, not the robot itself.
- **Industrial robotics** is the largest segment (~70% of annual installations globally per IFR 2023).
- **6 application areas** dominate industry: Material Handling, Welding, Assembly, Painting, Machining, Inspection.
- **Kinematic choice** follows the **3D vs. planar** rule: planar → SCARA / Cartesian; 3D → 6-DOF Articulated.
- **Payload determines drive type**: small (< 10 kg) → direct-drive servo; large (> 100 kg) → geared servo or hydraulic.
- **Repeatability tiers:** precision (< 0.05 mm), standard (0.05–0.5 mm), rough (> 0.5 mm).
- **Workspace volume** of a 2-link planar arm: $V_{planar} = 4 \pi l_1 l_2$.
- **Application → DOF rule:** planar task → 3 DOF; spatial task → 6 DOF minimum.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots)** are governed by **ISO/TS 15066** with a force limit of **150 N**.
- **Industrial robots** are governed by **ISO 10218-1/-2**; **medical** by **IEC 80601-2-77**; **space** by **NASA-STD-5001** / **ECSS-E-ST-33**; **underwater** by **API 17D** / **DNV-OS-E101**.
- **Service robotics** is the fastest-growing segment (~30% CAGR 2020–2024).
- **Field robotics** faces the *3D challenge*: Dirty, Dangerous, Dull.
- **Surgical robots** use *scaled teleoperation* (3:1 to 10:1 motion scaling) with *haptic feedback*.
- **Space robots** must satisfy *zero-g momentum conservation* — no gravity to "reset" the dynamics.
- **Underwater robots** use *pressure-balanced oil-filled joints* and *acoustic + optical sensor fusion*.
- **Always link** the application area → kinematic choice → payload → repeatability → safety standard in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Method of Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCRUT402 – Robotic Manipulators and Cell Design**, the *Method of Control* refers to the systematic set of techniques, algorithms, and feedback architectures employed to govern the dynamic behaviour, position, velocity, and force/torque output of a robotic manipulator's actuators such that the **End-Effector** follows a desired trajectory or maintains a desired interaction with the environment.

According to standard robotics literature, control methods are broadly classified along two orthogonal axes:

1. **Loop Architecture**: *Open-Loop* (non-servo) vs *Closed-Loop* (servo) control.
2. **Task Space**: *Joint Space Control* vs *Cartesian (Task) Space Control*.

A formal classification also distinguishes control by the **actuation medium** — **Hydraulic, Pneumatic,** and **Electric** — and by the **control law** used (PID, Computed Torque, Adaptive, Impedance, Hybrid Force/Position, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The examiner expects students to clearly differentiate between *servo* and *non-servo* control, justify the use of feedback in trajectory tracking, and derive the governing equation of a **PID-based joint servo controller** along with the **Computed Torque Method (CTM)** for rigid-link manipulators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Driving a Car"

Imagine two ways of steering a car along a curved highway:

| Real-World Analogy | Robotic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You set the steering wheel angle to a fixed value and close your eyes. | **Open-Loop (Non-Servo) Control** — the command is sent once; the actuator blindly moves without checking the result. |
| You continuously watch the road, compare your car's position to the lane line, and adjust the steering wheel proportionally to the error. | **Closed-Loop (Servo) Control** — a **sensor** measures actual joint state, an **error** is computed against the desired state, and the **controller** commands corrective action every cycle. |

Just as a driver uses *visual feedback* to stay in the lane, a robot uses *encoders, resolvers, tachometers, and force/torque sensors* to stay on its commanded path.

## 1.3 Core Control Vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> **Set-Point ($q_d$):** The desired joint position commanded by the trajectory planner.
>
> **Measured State ($q$):** The actual joint position reported by the encoder.
>
> **Error ($e$):** The difference between desired and actual, $e = q_d - q$.
>
> **Control Signal ($u$):** The voltage/current/hydraulic pressure sent to the actuator.
>
> **Process / Plant:** The robotic manipulator dynamics described by $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.
>
> **Bandwidth:** The maximum frequency (in **Hz** or **rad/s**) at which the closed-loop system can faithfully track a command.

## 1.4 Why "Method of Control" Matters in Industry

- **Pick-and-Place** operations often use simple **open-loop pneumatic** control because the end-points are fixed and the payload is repeatable.
- **Welding, painting, and assembly** require high-fidelity trajectory tracking, demanding **closed-loop servo control with PID or Computed Torque**.
- **Deburring, polishing, and contour following** demand *force feedback*, leading to **Hybrid Position/Force** or **Impedance Control**.
- **Surgical and rehabilitation robots** demand *adaptive* or *model-predictive* control to handle patient variability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Robot Control Methods

The methods of control for robotic manipulators are best understood as a hierarchical classification:

### A. By Loop Architecture

1. **Open-Loop (Non-Servo) Control**
   - No feedback sensor.
   - Actuator is driven for a pre-calculated *duration* or *count* of steps.
   - Examples: **Pneumatic pick-and-place arms**, stepper-motor-driven Cartesian stages, and *bang-bang* timed mechanisms.
   - **Advantages:** Cheap, simple, no sensor noise.
   - **Disadvantages:** No error correction; inaccurate when load or friction varies.

2. **Closed-Loop (Servo) Control**
   - Continuous feedback from **encoders, resolvers, tachometers, or force sensors**.
   - Error $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ is computed and fed to a *controller* that drives the actuator.
   - **Advantages:** High accuracy, disturbance rejection, repeatable trajectory tracking.
   - **Disadvantages:** Costlier; subject to stability issues if gains are mistuned.

### B. By Actuation Medium

| Actuation | Typical Control Valve / Drive | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | Servo-valve (e.g., Moog 4-way valve) | Heavy-payload manipulators (e.g., excavator arms, large forging robots). High force density. |
| **Pneumatic** | Proportional pressure regulator / 5/3 solenoid valve | Light, fast pick-and-place, soft robotics. Compressible fluid → hard to control precisely. |
| **Electric** | PWM amplifier / servo-drive (e.g., Kollmorgen, Yaskawa) | Most modern industrial robots (KUKA, ABB, FANUC). Excellent bandwidth. |

### C. By Task-Space

1. **Joint-Space Control** — Control laws are formulated in joint coordinates $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The simplest and most common scheme.
2. **Cartesian (Task) Space Control** — Control laws are formulated in $X, Y, Z, \phi, \theta, \psi$ coordinates of the end-effector. Requires the **Inverse Jacobian** $J^{-1}(q)$ at every instant.
3. **Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)** — A specific Cartesian scheme where joint velocities are computed as $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q) \cdot \dot{X}_d$.

### D. By Control Law

- **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative)** — Workhorse of industrial robot servos.
- **Computed Torque Method (CTM)** — Model-based feedback linearization.
- **Adaptive Control** — Online parameter estimation.
- **Impedance Control** — Regulates the *relationship* between force and position (Hogan, 1985).
- **Hybrid Position/Force Control (Raibert–Craig, 1981)** — Splits task space into *position-controlled* and *force-controlled* subspaces using a *selection matrix* $S$.

## 2.2 Block Diagram of a Single-Axis Servo Loop

The canonical closed-loop architecture is:

**Set-Point $\rightarrow$ Comparator $\rightarrow$ Controller $\rightarrow$ Amplifier $\rightarrow$ Motor/Actuator $\rightarrow$ Plant (Robot Joint) $\rightarrow$ Sensor $\rightarrow$ Feedback to Comparator.**

Each block is represented by a **transfer function** in the Laplace domain:

$$G_c(s) \cdot G_a(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot H(s)$$

where $G_c$ is the controller, $G_a$ the power amplifier, $G_p$ the plant (motor + link), and $H$ the sensor gain.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for the KTU Board Exam.** Memorise these.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ | rad or m |
| 2 | PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | N·m or V |
| 3 | PID in Laplace domain | $U(s) = \left(K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$ | — |
| 4 | Robot dynamics (Euler–Lagrange) | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | $M$ is $n \times n$ inertia matrix |
| 5 | Computed Torque Method | $\tau = M(q)\left(\ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e\right) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | $e = q_d - q$ |
| 6 | Linearized error dynamics under CTM | $\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ | Choose $K_p, K_d > 0$ for stability |
| 7 | Cartesian velocity mapping | $\dot{X} = J(q) \dot{q}$ | $J$ is Jacobian |
| 8 | Resolved Motion Rate Control | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q) \dot{X}_d$ | Requires $\det(J) \neq 0$ |
| 9 | Impedance control | $M_d(\ddot{X} - \ddot{X}_d) + B_d(\dot{X} - \dot{X}_d) + K_d(X - X_d) = -F_{ext}$ | $F_{ext}$ is contact force |
| 10 | Hybrid force/position selection | $\tau = J^T \left[ S \cdot F_{pos} + (I - S) \cdot F_{force} \right]$ | $S = \mathrm{diag}(s_1,\dots,s_m)$ with $s_i \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 11 | Natural frequency (2nd order) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p \cdot K_t / (J_m R)}$ | rad/s; $J_m$ motor inertia |
| 12 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p \cdot K_t \cdot J_m / R}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the cheat sheet, the **inverse Jacobian** appears in Cartesian control and is the *most common source of numerical issues* near **kinematic singularities** where $\det(J) = 0$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **Computed Torque Method** is the theoretical foundation of *model-based control* in modern manipulators. The KUKA LBR iiwa and the Franka Emika Panda use a variant of CTM with online parameter adaptation.
- The **Hybrid Force/Position** scheme is the workhorse for *robotic assembly* where a peg must slide into a hole: the **insertion axis** is force-controlled while the *lateral* axes remain position-controlled.
- **Impedance control** dominates *physical human-robot interaction (pHRI)*, since it regulates stiffness rather than rigid position.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PID Control Law for a Robot Joint

### Step 1 — Define the Tracking Error
Let the desired joint trajectory be $q_d(t)$ and the actual joint position be $q(t)$. The instantaneous tracking error is:

$$e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$$

**Reasoning:** This is the simplest scalar measure of "how far off" the joint is from where it should be. It is always the *input* to any servo controller.

### Step 2 — Define the Three Classical Control Actions

- **Proportional action:** Apply a corrective effort *proportional* to the current error.
- **Integral action:** Apply an effort proportional to the *accumulated* past error — eliminates steady-state offset.
- **Derivative action:** Apply an effort proportional to the *rate of change* of error — damps oscillations and anticipates future error.

### Step 3 — Combine the Three Actions
The total control signal $u(t)$ is the sum of all three:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the **Universal Approximator** for linear systems. With proper tuning it can stabilise any *minimum-phase* plant.

### Step 4 — Convert to the Laplace Domain
Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + K_i \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d s E(s)$$

Factor out $E(s)$:

$$U(s) = \left[ K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right] E(s) = G_c(s)\, E(s)$$

**Reasoning:** The bracketed term is the **PID transfer function** $G_c(s)$. It is *proper* (numerator and denominator have the same order) and contains an *integral pole* at the origin — which guarantees zero steady-state error to a step input.

### Step 5 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function
For a plant $G_p(s)$ and unity feedback $H(s) = 1$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{Q(s)}{Q_d(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G_p(s)}$$

**Reasoning:** This ratio tells us how faithfully $Q$ follows $Q_d$. Substituting $G_c$ gives the classical textbook expression. The denominator $1 + G_c G_p$ is the **characteristic equation** whose roots determine stability (Routh–Hurwitz criterion).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Computed Torque Method (CTM)

### Step 1 — Start with the Rigid-Body Dynamics
The general equation of motion of an $n$-DOF manipulator is:

$$M(q) \ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q}) \dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Reasoning:** $M(q)$ is the symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix; $C$ encodes Coriolis and centrifugal effects; $G$ is the gravity vector; $\tau$ is the joint torque vector produced by the actuators.

### Step 2 — Define the Tracking Error and Its Derivatives
$$e = q_d - q,\quad \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q},\quad \ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$$

### Step 3 — Specify a Desired Error Dynamics (Servo Law)
Choose a stable, critically-damped second-order decay for the error:

$$\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

Solving for $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Reasoning:** This is the *outer loop* PD servo law. With $K_p, K_d$ symmetric positive-definite matrices, the error decays to zero exponentially.

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Dynamics Equation
Replacing $\ddot{q}$ in the dynamics:

$$\tau = M(q)\left( \ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e \right) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

**Reasoning:** This is the **Computed Torque** $\tau$. Notice it *exactly cancels* the nonlinear terms $C$ and $G$ using the model, leaving a *linear decoupled* error dynamics for each joint.

### Step 5 — Resulting Linear Error Dynamics
Subtracting the actual dynamics from the commanded torque yields:

$$M(q)(\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e) = 0$$

Since $M(q)$ is invertible (positive-definite), we get the **decoupled linear error dynamics**:

$$\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

**Reasoning:** This is the *key result* of CTM — the highly coupled, nonlinear robot has been transformed into $n$ independent second-order linear systems that can be tuned by standard methods (pole placement, Ziegler–Nichols, LQR).

### Step 6 — Gain Selection (Critical Damping)
For each joint, choosing $K_d = 2\sqrt{K_p}$ gives a damping ratio $\zeta = 1$ (critically damped, no overshoot).

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p}}$$

**Reasoning:** Critical damping gives the fastest non-oscillatory response. Industrial robots often use $\zeta \in [0.7, 1.0]$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Method | When to Use | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Open-loop (bang-bang) | Low-cost pick-and-place, repeatable loads | No disturbance rejection |
| PID joint servo | Mid-accuracy trajectory tracking, known payload | Performance degrades at high speed (neglected nonlinearities) |
| Computed Torque | High-speed, high-accuracy, varying payloads | Needs accurate model of $M, C, G$ |
| Adaptive Control | Unknown or slowly time-varying payloads | Complex; convergence proofs nontrivial |
| Impedance Control | Contact tasks, safe human-robot interaction | Indirect force tracking |
| Hybrid Position/Force | Constrained tasks (assembly, deburring) | Requires accurate *constraint surface* model |
| Resolved Motion Rate | Cartesian trajectory with redundant manipulators | Singularity issues at $\det J = 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Servo Control of a Single Robot Joint

The following flowchart captures the canonical *negative feedback* architecture as specified in the KTU Module 1 syllabus. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword restrictions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSP["Desired Set Point q_d t"]
    nodeSUM["Comparator Summing Junction"]
    nodeERR["Error Signal e t"]
    nodeCTRL["Controller Gc s PID or CTM"]
    nodeAMP["Power Amplifier Ga s"]
    nodeMTR["DC Servo Motor and Gearbox"]
    nodePLT["Plant Robotic Joint Gp s"]
    nodeSEN["Encoder Resolver H s"]
    nodeFB["Feedback Path"]

    nodeSP --> nodeSUM
    nodeSUM --> nodeERR
    nodeERR --> nodeCTRL
    nodeCTRL --> nodeAMP
    nodeAMP --> nodeMTR
    nodeMTR --> nodePLT
    nodePLT --> nodeSEN
    nodeSEN --> nodeFB
    nodeFB --> nodeSUM

    nodeDIST["Disturbance Torque td"] -.-> nodePLT
    nodeOUT["Actual Joint Position q t"] --> nodeSEN
```

**Reading guide:**

- The forward path is `qd -> Comparator -> Controller -> Amplifier -> Motor -> Plant -> Output`.
- The feedback path is `Encoder -> Comparator` (negative sign by convention).
- The dashed line `Disturbance` enters at the *Plant* node — the controller must reject this.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Computed-Torque Control Architecture

This diagram shows the *inner nonlinear cancellation loop* and the *outer linear servo loop* of CTM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Desired Trajectory qd qdotd qddotd"]
    B["Outer PD Servo Law e qdot e"]
    C["Inverse Dynamics Model M q C q qdot G q"]
    D["Computed Torque Vector tau"]
    E["Robot Manipulator Plant M C G"]
    F["Actual Joint State q qdot"]
    G["Encoder Feedback"]
    H["Error Computation e = qd minus q"]

    A --> H
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
```

**Reading guide:** The block labelled `Inverse Dynamics Model` *in real time* computes the torque required to follow the desired trajectory. This is the cornerstone of modern model-based robot control.

## 4.3 Mermaid Classification Tree: Method of Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MOC["Method of Control for Robotic Manipulators"]

    MOC --> L1["By Loop Architecture"]
    MOC --> L2["By Actuation Medium"]
    MOC --> L3["By Task Space"]
    MOC --> L4["By Control Law"]

    L1 --> L1A["Open Loop Non Servo"]
    L1 --> L1B["Closed Loop Servo"]

    L2 --> L2A["Hydraulic Servo Valve"]
    L2 --> L2B["Pneumatic Proportional Valve"]
    L2 --> L2C["Electric PWM Drive"]

    L3 --> L3A["Joint Space Control"]
    L3 --> L3B["Cartesian Space Control"]
    L3 --> L3C["Resolved Motion Rate Control"]

    L4 --> L4A["PID"]
    L4 --> L4B["Computed Torque"]
    L4 --> L4C["Adaptive Control"]
    L4 --> L4D["Impedance Control"]
    L4 --> L4E["Hybrid Position Force"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Classify the methods of control of robotic manipulators."* Memorise this four-axis classification — it scores full marks if drawn clearly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
Differentiate between **open-loop (non-servo)** and **closed-loop (servo)** control of robotic manipulators. Give one industrial example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Loop (Non-Servo) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback sensor | Absent | Present (encoder, resolver, force sensor) |
| Accuracy | Poor; dependent on timing | High; corrected every sampling instant |
| Cost | Low | Higher (sensor + controller) |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Yes |
| Stability concerns | None (open loop) | Must satisfy Nyquist/Routh criterion |
| Industrial example | Pneumatic pick-and-place arm, stepper-driven X-Y table | KUKA KR 16 welding robot, ABB IRB 1200 servo arm |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for table/differences, 1 for example.)**

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
What is the **Computed Torque Method (CTM)**? Write its governing torque equation.

**Model Answer:**
The Computed Torque Method is a *model-based feedback linearization* technique in which the nonlinear dynamics of the manipulator are *exactly cancelled* in real time by computing the required joint torque from the known inertia, Coriolis, and gravity terms. The error dynamics then reduce to a set of decoupled linear second-order systems.

The governing equation is:

$$\tau = M(q)\left( \ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e \right) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

where $e = q_d - q$ is the tracking error and $K_p, K_d$ are positive-definite gain matrices.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for explanation of $K_p$, $K_d$.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]`**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **closed-loop servo control system** for a single robot joint driven by a DC servo motor. Identify each block and its function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **PID control law** in both the time domain and the Laplace domain, and state the role of each of the three actions ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) in trajectory tracking. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Architecture (7 marks):**

The closed-loop architecture for a single joint of a manipulator consists of the following cascaded blocks (refer Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Desired Set-Point $q_d(t)$** — generated by the trajectory planner in joint coordinates.
2. **Comparator (Summing Junction)** — computes the error $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$. The negative feedback is denoted by the minus sign.
3. **Controller $G_c(s)$** — implements the PID law. Output is a low-power control voltage $u(t)$.
4. **Power Amplifier $G_a(s)$** — boosts $u(t)$ to a current $i_a(t)$ capable of driving the motor (PWM amplifier or servo drive).
5. **DC Servo Motor and Gearbox** — converts electrical current to torque $\tau_m$ and rotational motion.
6. **Plant (Robot Joint) $G_p(s)$** — the mechanical load with inertia $J_m$, damping $B$, and stiffness $K$.
7. **Encoder / Resolver $H(s)$** — produces a measurable signal proportional to the actual joint angle $q(t)$.

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{Q(s)}{Q_d(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\, G_a(s)\, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G_a(s)\, G_p(s)\, H(s)}$$

The characteristic equation $1 + G_{open}(s) = 0$ governs stability; gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are tuned (Ziegler–Nichols or root locus) for desired transient response.

**(Marks: 1 diagram, 1 block identification, 2 equation, 2 stability/tuning discussion, 1 conclusion.)**

**Part (b) — PID Derivation (7 marks):**

**Time domain** — the three classical actions are combined:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Laplace domain** — taking the transform with zero initial conditions:

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s\, E(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$$

The bracketed term is the **PID transfer function** $G_c(s)$.

**Role of each term:**

- **$K_p$ (Proportional):** Produces an immediate corrective action proportional to the current error. Increasing $K_p$ reduces rise time but increases overshoot and may cause oscillation.
- **$K_i$ (Integral):** Accumulates past error; guarantees **zero steady-state error** for step inputs. Side effect: introduces a pole at the origin, which can cause *integrator wind-up* if not anti-wind-up protected.
- **$K_d$ (Derivative):** Acts on the *rate* of change of error; introduces a *zero* in the transfer function that damps oscillations and improves phase margin. Pure derivative is noise-sensitive; in practice a *filtered* derivative $\dfrac{K_d s}{1 + \tau_f s}$ is used.

**Conclusion:** With proper tuning, PID provides fast, accurate, and stable joint tracking. For highly nonlinear manipulators, the *Computed Torque Method* is preferred over pure PID.

**(Marks: 1 time-domain law, 1 Laplace law, 1 factored form, 3 roles of gains, 1 conclusion.)**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]`**
**(a)** With reference to the **Euler–Lagrange dynamics** of a rigid manipulator, derive the **Computed Torque control law** and show that the resulting error dynamics is **decoupled and linear**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Hybrid Position/Force control** using a *selection matrix* $S$. How is the constraint surface modelled, and what is the role of the matrix $S$? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Computed Torque Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the rigid-body dynamics:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau \quad (1)$$

Define tracking error and its derivatives:

$$e = q_d - q,\quad \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q},\quad \ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$$

Choose a stable linear error dynamics:

$$\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0 \quad (2)$$

Solving (2) for $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e \quad (3)$$

Substitute (3) into (1):

$$\tau = M(q)\left(\ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e\right) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) \quad (4)$$

Equation (4) is the **Computed Torque law**. It uses the *inverse dynamics* model $(M, C, G)$ in real time to cancel the nonlinear coupling.

Plugging (4) back into the plant equation (1) and subtracting gives:

$$M(q)\left(\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e\right) = 0$$

Since $M(q)$ is positive-definite, hence invertible:

$$\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

This is **decoupled** (each joint has its own second-order ODE with its own gains) and **linear** (no products of $q$ or $\dot{q}$). Stability is guaranteed by choosing $K_p$ and $K_d$ as symmetric positive-definite matrices.

**(Marks: 1 dynamics equation, 1 error definition, 1 servo law, 1 CTM equation, 2 linearisation result, 1 stability statement.)**

**Part (b) — Hybrid Position/Force Control (7 marks):**

In many contact tasks (peg-in-hole, polishing, deburring), the end-effector is *constrained* by the environment along certain Cartesian directions and *free* along others. **Raibert and Craig (1981)** proposed decomposing the task space into two orthogonal subspaces.

**Constraint surface:** Modelled as an $m$-DOF hypersurface $\Phi(X) = 0$ in the $m$-dimensional task space. The natural constraint says:

- Along the *constrained* directions, the velocity $\dot{X}_n = 0$ and the force $F_n$ is controlled.
- Along the *free* directions, the force $F_t = 0$ and the velocity $\dot{X}_t$ is controlled.

**Selection matrix** $S = \mathrm{diag}(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m)$ with $s_i \in \{0, 1\}$:
- $s_i = 1$ → $i$-th task direction is **position-controlled**.
- $s_i = 0$ → $i$-th task direction is **force-controlled**.

The task-space command is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{X}_{cmd} \\ F_{cmd} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S & 0 \\ 0 & (I - S) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{X}_d \\ F_d \end{bmatrix}$$

The joint torque is then:

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ S \cdot \left( K_{px} \tilde{X} + K_{dx} \dot{\tilde{X}} \right) + (I - S) \cdot F_d \right]$$

where $\tilde{X} = X_d - X$ is the Cartesian position error and $F_d$ is the desired contact force.

**Role of $S$:** It enforces *orthogonality* — the same direction cannot be simultaneously position-controlled and force-controlled, preserving task compatibility.

**Application example:** A robot inserting a peg into a hole — the **insertion axis** is force-controlled (constant push force) while the **lateral axes** are position-controlled (centering the peg).

**(Marks: 1 constraint surface, 1 natural/Artificial constraint, 2 selection matrix definition, 2 hybrid torque law, 1 example.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the error** — Writing $e = q - q_d$ instead of $e = q_d - q$ will flip the sign of the entire control law and cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PID with PD** — Many students write $u = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ and forget the integral term; KTU explicitly tests the *three* actions.
> 3. **Skipping the step where $M(q)$ is inverted in CTM** — A common mistake is to claim "decoupled linear dynamics" without stating that $M(q)$ is positive-definite and therefore invertible. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring units in the formula sheet** — Always mention SI units (rad, rad/s, N·m) in derivations; this signals professional maturity.
> 5. **Drawing the block diagram without a feedback arrow** — Marks are reserved specifically for the **closed loop**; an open-loop diagram is *not* acceptable.
> 6. **In Hybrid control, failing to define $s_i \in \{0, 1\}$** — The selection matrix is meaningless without explicit binary assignment.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Method of Control"**

- **Open-loop vs Closed-loop:** Open-loop has *no* sensor; closed-loop uses *negative feedback* of actual state.
- **Servo vs Non-Servo:** Servo = closed-loop with continuous error correction; Non-servo = open-loop with end-stop or timed actuation.
- **Actuation media:** *Hydraulic* (high force), *Pneumatic* (light, fast, compressible), *Electric* (most common, high bandwidth).
- **PID law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$. $K_p$ → speed, $K_i$ → steady-state accuracy, $K_d$ → damping.
- **CTM:** $\tau = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e) + C\dot{q} + G$. Cancels nonlinearity → decoupled linear error dynamics.
- **Cartesian control:** Uses the Jacobian $J(q)$. Velocity mapping $\dot{X} = J \dot{q}$. Inverse is **RMRC**.
- **Singularities:** $\det J(q) = 0$ → loss of DOF; Jacobian becomes non-invertible. Always check.
- **Hybrid Position/Force:** Uses selection matrix $S = \mathrm{diag}(s_1, \dots, s_m)$, $s_i \in \{0,1\}$.
- **Impedance Control:** Regulates the dynamic *relationship* $M_d \ddot{\tilde{X}} + B_d \dot{\tilde{X}} + K_d \tilde{X} = -F_{ext}$.
- **Stability check:** For CTM, choose $K_p, K_d$ symmetric **positive-definite** → $\zeta > 0$ and $\omega_n > 0$.
- **Industrial examples:**
  - *Pneumatic non-servo* → SCARA pick-and-place, packaging lines.
  - *PID servo* → KUKA KR AGILUS, FANUC LR-Mate.
  - *CTM/Model-based* → KUKA LBR iiwa, Franka Panda.
  - *Hybrid force/position* → Robotic polishing of turbine blades.
  - *Impedance* → da Vinci surgical robot, collaborative cobots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Manipulator Geometry** is the foundational branch of robotics that deals with the spatial and structural arrangement of a robot's mechanical components — namely, the **links**, **joints**, and **end-effector** — together with the mathematical description of their positions and orientations in a 3D workspace.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the geometry of a manipulator is described by its **kinematic chain architecture**, the **number of Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, the **type of joints** connecting consecutive links, and the **reachable workspace** of the end-effector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> A *robotic manipulator* is an open (or closed) kinematic chain of rigid bodies (**links**) interconnected by **joints**, designed to perform tasks by positioning and orienting an **end-effector** within a defined geometric workspace.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a robotic manipulator exactly like a **human arm**:

- The **shoulder, elbow, and wrist** are analogous to **joints** (the moving connection points).
- The **upper arm, forearm, and palm** are analogous to **links** (the rigid segments between joints).
- The **fingers grasping a tool** represent the **end-effector**.
- The **entire reach** of your hand (without moving your torso) is the **workspace**.

Just as you can describe where your palm is by knowing the angles of your shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints, robot geometry uses **joint variables** (angles for revolute joints, displacements for prismatic joints) to mathematically define the position and orientation of every part of the manipulator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU:**
> The number of independent parameters required to completely specify the configuration of a manipulator from a fixed base is termed its **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**. For a generic 3D task, **6 DOF** is the practical maximum (3 for position + 3 for orientation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Manipulator Geometry

The geometry of a robotic manipulator is analyzed across four hierarchical layers:

1. **Mechanical Layer (Link-Joint Chain)**
   - The manipulator is a sequence of rigid bodies (**links**) connected by movable couplings (**joints**).
   - Each link has its own **length, shape, and material** properties.
   - Each joint defines a **single axis of relative motion** between two adjacent links.

2. **Joint-Type Layer (Mobility Classification)**
   - **Revolute (R) Joint:** Rotary motion about a fixed axis (1 DOF per joint).
   - **Prismatic (P) Joint:** Linear sliding motion along an axis (1 DOF per joint).
   - **Other joints** (cylindrical, spherical, planar, screw) are combinations, not used in standard KTU problems.

3. **Configuration Layer (Architecture)**
   - **Open Chain:** Serial manipulator — links form an unbranched sequence (e.g., articulated robot arm).
   - **Closed Chain:** Parallel manipulator — links form loops (e.g., Delta robot, Stewart platform).

4. **Workspace Layer (Reachability)**
   - **Dexterous Workspace:** Points reachable with **all orientations** possible.
   - **Reachable Workspace:** Points reachable with **at least one orientation**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Condition / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom (Planar) | $DOF$ | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| Degrees of Freedom (Spatial, Gruebler) | $DOF$ | $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $j_i$ = joints with $i$ DOF |
| Number of Joint Variables | $n$ | $n$ = number of actuated joints | Equals DOF in non-redundant manipulators |
| Link Length (Common Notation) | $a_i$ | Distance between joint axes $i$ and $i+1$ | in **mm** or **m** |
| Joint Offset (Common Notation) | $d_i$ | Distance along joint axis $i$ to the next link | in **mm** or **m** |
| Twist Angle (Common Notation) | $\alpha_i$ | Angle between two successive joint axes | in **radians** or **degrees** |
| Joint Angle (Common Notation) | $\theta_i$ | Rotation about joint axis $i$ | in **radians** or **degrees** |
| Position Vector (2-link planar) | $P_x, P_y$ | $P_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | $L_i$ = link length |
| Position Vector (2-link planar) | $P_y$ | $P_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | derived from geometry |
| Workspace Radius (Planar 2R) | $R_{max}$ | $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ | fully extended |
| Workspace Inner Radius (Planar 2R) | $R_{min}$ | $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | fully folded |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The geometric analysis of robotic manipulators is the **gateway to all advanced robotics** — kinematics, dynamics, trajectory planning, and control all depend on a correct geometric model. In industry:

- **Automotive assembly lines** use 6-DOF articulated arms for welding and painting.
- **Surgical robots** (e.g., Da Vinci system) require precise geometric calibration for sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Pick-and-place SCARA robots** in electronics manufacturing use 4-DOF geometry optimized for vertical insertion tasks.
- **Space manipulators** (Canadarm on the ISS) use long-reach geometry for satellite servicing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Gruebler's Equation for Degrees of Freedom

The Degrees of Freedom of any planar or spatial mechanism is derived from the trade-off between the total mobility of the unconnected links and the constraints imposed by the joints.

**Starting Point — Number of Links and Free Movement:**
A mechanism has $n$ links. In a spatial (3D) environment, each link free in space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- Total free DOF of $n$ links in 3D = $6n$
- The ground (or base) link is **fixed**, so we subtract 6: total = $6(n-1)$

**Joint Constraints:**
- A **1-DOF joint** (revolute or prismatic) removes **5 DOF** → contributes $-5j_1$
- A **2-DOF joint** (universal) removes **4 DOF** → contributes $-4j_2$
- A **3-DOF joint** (e.g., spherical) removes **3 DOF** → contributes $-3j_3$

**Combining Mobility and Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Kutzbach–Gruebler equation** for spatial mechanisms, the standard KTU reference for DOF calculation.

**For Planar (2D) Mechanisms:**
Each link in 2D has only **3 DOF** (2 translations + 1 rotation), and joints remove fewer degrees of freedom:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF_{planar} &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Forward Position of a 2-Link Planar Manipulator

This is the most frequently tested geometry problem in KTU examinations. Consider a planar manipulator with two revolute joints and link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.

**Step 1:** Place the base at the origin. The first joint axis is at $(0, 0)$ and rotates by angle $\theta_1$.

**Step 2:** The end of link 1 (which is the second joint) is located at:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\
y_1 &= L_1 \sin\theta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The total angle from the horizontal to link 2 is $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$, where $\theta_2$ is the relative joint angle.

**Step 4:** The end-effector position $(P_x, P_y)$ is the vector sum:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
P_y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The end-effector orientation (the angle of link 2 with respect to the horizontal) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \theta_1 + \theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (Boundary Cases):**
- When $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended along x-axis):
  $P_x = L_1 + L_2$, $P_y = 0$ ✓
- When $\theta_1 = \pi/2$, $\theta_2 = -\pi/2$ (arm folded upward):
  $P_x = 0$, $P_y = L_1 - L_2$ ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Workspace Radius of a 2R Planar Manipulator

The **outer boundary** of the workspace is reached when the arm is fully extended:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{P_x^2 + P_y^2} \\
        &= \sqrt{(L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2 + (L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding using $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ and $\cos(A)\cos(B) + \sin(A)\sin(B) = \cos(A-B)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^2 &= L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum value occurs at $\theta_2 = 0$ (fully extended): $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$.
Minimum value occurs at $\theta_2 = \pi$ (fully folded back): $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Problem Mapping

| **Problem Type** | **Primary Equations** | **Key Concept Tested** |
|---|---|---|
| DOF of 6R serial arm | $DOF = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 6$ | Spatial Gruebler formula |
| DOF of 4-bar planar linkage | $DOF = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 1$ | Planar Gruebler formula |
| 2R end-effector position | $P_x, P_y$ as derived above | Forward geometry |
| Workspace area of 2R | Area $= \pi[(L_1+L_2)^2 - (L_1-L_2)^2]$ | Reachability geometry |
| Redundant manipulator | $DOF > 6$ (or task space DOF) | Dexterity and redundancy |
| SCARA architecture | 2R + 1P + 1R (4 DOF) | Configuration classification |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Serial Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseA[Base Link 0] --> jointA[Joint 1 Revolute]
    jointA --> linkA[Link 1 Length L1]
    linkA --> jointB[Joint 2 Revolute]
    jointB --> linkB[Link 2 Length L2]
    linkB --> jointC[Joint 3 Revolute]
    jointC --> linkC[Link 3 Length L3]
    linkC --> endNode[End Effector Position Px Py]
    jointA -->|theta1| ctrlA[Joint Variable Vector]
    jointB -->|theta2| ctrlA
    jointC -->|theta3| ctrlA
    ctrlA --> geomMod[Geometry Forward Model]
    geomMod --> endNode
    endNode --> ws[Workspace Boundary Rmax Rmin]
    ws --> outA[Reachable Region]
```

### 4.2 Joint Type Classification Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    jt[Robot Joint Types]
    jt --> rev[Revolute Joint R]
    jt --> pris[Prismatic Joint P]
    jt --> cyl[Cylindrical Joint]
    jt --> sph[Spherical Joint]
    jt --> uni[Universal Joint]
    rev --> rdesc[Rotary motion about fixed axis - 1 DOF]
    pris --> pdesc[Linear sliding along axis - 1 DOF]
    cyl --> cdesc[R and P combined along same axis - 2 DOF]
    sph --> sdesc[3 rotational DOF about a point]
    uni --> udesc[2 rotational DOF about perpendicular axes]
```

### 4.3 DOF Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Identify Mechanism] --> q1{Planar or Spatial?}
    q1 -->|Planar| plan[Use DOF equals 3 n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    q1 -->|Spatial| spat[Use DOF equals 6 n minus 1 minus 5 j1 minus 4 j2 minus 3 j3]
    plan --> check1{DOF greater than 0?}
    spat --> check2{DOF greater than 0?}
    check1 -->|Yes| ok1[Valid Movable Mechanism]
    check1 -->|No| bad1[Structure or Locked Mechanism]
    check2 -->|Yes| ok2[Valid Movable Mechanism]
    check2 -->|No| bad2[Structure or Locked Mechanism]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. Define the Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator. State Gruebler's equation for a spatial mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**
Degrees of Freedom is the minimum number of independent parameters required to completely specify the configuration of a mechanism. For a spatial mechanism with $n$ links and joints of various mobility:

$$
DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

For a serial 6R manipulator with $n = 7$ and $j_1 = 6$:

$$
DOF = 6(6) - 5(6) = 36 - 30 = 6
$$

This represents the standard 6 DOF required for full position-and-orientation control in 3D space. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P) joints with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Revolute (R) Joint** | **Prismatic (P) Joint** |
|---|---|---|
| Motion Type | Rotary (rotational) | Linear (translational) |
| Joint Variable | Angle $\theta_i$ (radians) | Displacement $d_i$ (meters) |
| DOF | 1 | 1 |
| Industrial Example | Base rotation of a 6-axis welding arm | Vertical lift of a Cartesian gantry crane |
| Constraint Removed | 5 DOF from relative motion | 5 DOF from relative motion |

A SCARA robot uses both: revolute joints for horizontal motion and a prismatic joint for vertical insertion in PCB assembly. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m and $L_2 = 0.3$ m. The joint angles are $\theta_1 = 60°$ and $\theta_2 = 30°$. Determine the end-effector position $(P_x, P_y)$ and orientation $\phi$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the maximum and minimum reach (workspace radii) of the manipulator. Also find the area of the reachable workspace (annulus). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Using the derived forward geometry equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
    &= 0.5 \cos(60°) + 0.3 \cos(60° + 30°) \\
    &= 0.5 \times 0.5 + 0.3 \times \cos(90°) \\
    &= 0.25 + 0.3 \times 0 \\
    &= 0.25 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
    &= 0.5 \sin(60°) + 0.3 \sin(60° + 30°) \\
    &= 0.5 \times 0.866 + 0.3 \times \sin(90°) \\
    &= 0.433 + 0.3 \times 1 \\
    &= 0.733 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Orientation of the end-effector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 60° + 30° = 90°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** End-effector is at **(0.25 m, 0.733 m)** with orientation **90°** (pointing straight up). **(7 Marks — 3 for setup, 3 for evaluation, 1 for final answer with units)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

Maximum reach (arm fully extended, $\theta_2 = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= L_1 + L_2 = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum reach (arm fully folded, $\theta_2 = 180°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.3 \vert = 0.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Area of the annular workspace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \\
  &= \pi (0.8^2 - 0.2^2) \\
  &= \pi (0.64 - 0.04) \\
  &= 0.6\pi \\
  &\approx 1.885 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $R_{max} = 0.8$ m, $R_{min} = 0.2$ m, $A \approx 1.885$ m². **(7 Marks — 2 for $R_{max}$, 2 for $R_{min}$, 3 for area calculation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the components of a robotic manipulator with the help of a labeled block diagram. List the types of joints used in industrial robots. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-bar planar mechanism has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Compute its Degrees of Freedom. If a 5th link is added connecting the coupler to the ground, recompute the DOF. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Components of a Robotic Manipulator (Block Diagram):**

1. **Base (Link 0):** The fixed reference link mounted to the floor or platform.
2. **Links (Link 1 to n):** Rigid segments that provide the geometric reach of the arm.
3. **Joints (1 to n):** Movable connections providing controlled motion between links.
4. **End-effector:** The tool or gripper mounted at the tip of the last link.
5. **Actuators:** Motors (servo, stepper, hydraulic) at each joint providing the input motion.
6. **Sensors:** Encoders, resolvers, force/torque sensors for feedback.

**Joint Types in Industrial Robots:**
- **Revolute (R)** — Rotational, most common (e.g., articulated arms).
- **Prismatic (P)** — Linear, used in Cartesian and SCARA robots.
- **Cylindrical** — Combines R and P along the same axis.
- **Spherical (Ball-and-Socket)** — 3 rotational DOF, used in wrist mechanisms.
- **Universal** — 2 rotational DOF about perpendicular axes. **(7 Marks — 3 for block diagram/description, 4 for joint types with examples)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Case 1: Original 4-bar mechanism**
- Number of links $n = 4$
- Number of 1-DOF joints $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute)
- Number of 2-DOF joints $j_2 = 0$

Using the planar Gruebler equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
    &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
    &= 3 \times 3 - 8 \\
    &= 9 - 8 \\
    &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The 4-bar mechanism has **1 DOF** — a classic result consistent with Grashof's law analysis. **(3 Marks)**

**Case 2: After adding a 5th link closing a loop**
- New number of links $n = 5$
- New number of 1-DOF joints $j_1 = 5$ (one additional revolute joint to ground)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
    &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) - 0 \\
    &= 3 \times 4 - 10 \\
    &= 12 - 10 \\
    &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The closed-chain mechanism has **2 DOF**. **(4 Marks — 2 for setup, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for final answer with interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians in trigonometric functions costs 1–2 marks. Always state angles in degrees in the solution and convert before computing.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** Joint angles are **measured CCW from the positive x-axis**. Mixing up signs gives wrong end-effector positions.
> 3. **Gruebler's Equation Trap:** Students often forget to subtract 1 from the link count ($n-1$) for the base. This is a **2-mark deduction** in the KTU scheme.
> 4. **Workspace Confusion:** $R_{max}$ and $R_{min}$ are **radii**, not areas. Writing "$R_{max} = $ area" loses a mark.
> 5. **Joint Variable vs DOF:** A manipulator with 6 actuators may still have less than 6 DOF if joints are coupled — always apply Gruebler, not just count actuators.
> 6. **Folding Direction:** $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ — absolute value is **mandatory**, even if $L_1 > L_2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robotic Manipulator Geometry** describes the spatial arrangement of links, joints, and end-effector.
- A **link** is a rigid body; a **joint** is a movable connection between two links.
- **Revolute (R) Joint** → rotation (1 DOF); **Prismatic (P) Joint** → translation (1 DOF).
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** = number of independent parameters to fully describe configuration.
- **Gruebler's Equation (Spatial):** $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- **Gruebler's Equation (Planar):** $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- A standard industrial 6R serial manipulator has **6 DOF** — sufficient for any 3D position-and-orientation task.
- **2R Planar Forward Position:**
  $P_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$
  $P_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$
- **Workspace of 2R Planar Manipulator** is an annulus with $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ and $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$.
- **Dexterous Workspace** = all orientations reachable; **Reachable Workspace** = at least one orientation reachable.
- **Redundant Manipulator:** has $DOF > 6$ (extra mobility for obstacle avoidance).
- **SCARA Robot:** Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — 4 DOF, optimized for vertical assembly tasks.
- **Configuration types:** Open-chain (serial) vs. closed-chain (parallel) — affects DOF calculation.
- **Always convert angles** to radians before computing sine/cosine in numerical problems.
- **Industrial Examples to Remember:** PUMA 560 (6R articulated), SCARA (RRPR), Cartesian gantry (PPP), Delta robot (parallel).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Kinematic Arrangements (Structure and Workspace) - Articulated Configuration (RRR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Common Kinematic Arrangements (Structure and Workspace) - Articulated Configuration (RRR) # Common Kinematic Arrangements (Structure and Workspace) – Articulated Configuration (RRR) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > The **Articulated Configuration (RRR)** is a robotic manipulator kinematic arrangement consisting of **three revolute joints** arranged in series — with the first joint rotating about a vertical axis (waist) and the next two rotating about parallel horizontal axes (shoulder and elbow). It is the most common industrial robot architecture, often extended with a spherical wrist (RRR) to provide six degrees of freedom. ### 1.1 Formal Definition (As per KTU Board Standards) An **Articulated Robot** (also called an **anthropomorphic** or **jointed-arm robot**) is an open-kinematic-chain manipulator whose primary positioning structure is composed of **three rotary joints**: - **Joint 1 (Waist/Trunktion):** Revolute joint whose axis is **vertical** → provides swing/rotation about the base. - **Joint 2 (Shoulder):** Revolute joint whose axis is **horizontal** → provides elevation of the upper arm. - **Joint 3 (Elbow):** Revolute joint whose axis is **horizontal** (parallel to shoulder) → provides articulation of the forearm. When augmented with a **three-revolute spherical wrist** (Roll-Pitch-Yaw), the configuration becomes the **6-DOF RRR-RRR manipulator** — the standard industrial workhorse. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Human Arm) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy – The Human Arm** > Think of your **own arm**. Your *torso rotation* corresponds to **Joint 1 (waist)**. Your *shoulder elevation* (lifting your upper arm forward/backward) corresponds to **Joint 2 (shoulder)**. Your *elbow bending* corresponds to **Joint 3 (elbow)**. The **upper arm** is Link 2, the **forearm** is Link 3, and the **wrist + palm** is the optional spherical wrist. This is precisely why it is called *anthropomorphic* (human-shaped). ### 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics | Metric | Typical Industrial Value | |---|---| | **Degrees of Freedom (Primary Arm)** | **3** (all revolute) | | **Full Manipulator DOF** | **6** (with spherical wrist) | | **Payload Capacity** | **5 kg – 1000 kg** | | **Reach Radius** | **0.5 m – 3.0 m** | | **Repeatability** | **±0.05 mm – ±0.1 mm** | | **Joint Notation** | **R – R – R** (Revolute-Revolute-Revolute) | ### 1.4 Standard Industrial Examples > [!TIP] > **Industry Reference:** The most cited articulated robot in academic literature is the **PUMA 560** (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly), originally built by Unimation. Modern examples include the **ABB IRB 1200**, **KUKA KR 16 R2010**, **Fanuc M-10iA**, and **Yaskawa MH12**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Architecture (Joint-by-Joint Breakdown) The articulated configuration decomposes into three rotational kinematic pairs. Each joint contributes exactly **one degree of freedom** to the manipulator: 1. **Joint 1 – Waist (Trunnion Joint):** - Axis: **Vertical (z₀)** - Range: Typically **±170° to ±360°** (full rotation allowed by most designs). - Function: Orients the entire arm within the horizontal plane — *moves the hand left/right around a vertical pole*. 2. **Joint 2 – Shoulder:** - Axis: **Horizontal, perpendicular to z₀** (after Joint 1 rotation). - Range: Typically **−150° to +150°** (limited due to cabling). - Function: Lifts the upper arm **up/down** in the vertical plane. 3. **Joint 3 – Elbow:** - Axis: **Horizontal, parallel to Joint 2** (offset by link length a₂). - Range: Typically **±150°** (often limited by mechanical stops). - Function: Bends the forearm relative to the upper arm. ### 2.2 Link Parameter Nomenclature | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Value (PUMA) | |---|---|---| | **d₁ (or a₁)** | Base height / shoulder offset above base | **0 mm – 600 mm** | | **a₂** | Upper arm (shoulder-to-elbow) length | **432 mm – 650 mm** | | **a₃** | Forearm (elbow-to-wrist) length | **432 mm – 700 mm** | | **d₄** | Wrist offset (if spherical wrist added) | **~50 mm – 150 mm** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The total reach (from shoulder axis to end-effector tip, fully extended) is given by: > $$R_{max} = a_2 + a_3 + d_4$$ > This defines the **outer radius of the workspace envelope**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table contains every formula you will need for KTU University Examination questions on the RRR configuration. | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | DOF (Kutzbach/Gruebler) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_i$ = joints of class $i$ | | 2 | Total Reach | $R_{max} = a_2 + a_3 + d_4$ | Lengths in **mm** | | 3 | Minimum Reach (above base) | $R_{min} = \vert a_2 - a_3 \vert + d_4$ | mm | | 4 | Vertical Reach (above shoulder) | $h_{up} = a_2 \sin\theta_2^{max} + a_3$ | mm | | 5 | DH Transform Matrix | $^{i-1}_iT = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i & 0 & a_{i-1} \\ s\theta_i\,c\alpha_{i-1} & c\theta_i\,c\alpha_{i-1} & -s\alpha_{i-1} & -s\alpha_{i-1}\,d_i \\ s\theta_i\,s\alpha_{i-1} & c\theta_i\,s\alpha_{i-1} & c\alpha_{i-1} & c\alpha_{i-1}\,d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Standard DH frame | | 6 | Workspace Volume (approx.) | $V \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3) \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = shape factor $\approx 0.5$ | | 7 | Inverse Kin – Cosine of θ₃ | $\cos\theta_3 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}$ | See derivation | | 8 | Singularity (elbow straight) | $\sin\theta_3 = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_3 = 0$ | Lost DOF | > **Critical Exam Note:** In the formulas above, $\theta_3 = 0$ corresponds to the **arm fully extended** (boundary singularity); $\theta_3 = \pm 180°$ corresponds to the **arm folded back on itself**. Always declare your **branch choice** in KTU answers. ### 2.4 Why Articulated? (Engineering Justification) The RRR configuration dominates industry because of: - **Largest dexterous workspace** among 3-DOF designs (volume-to-footprint ratio is excellent). - **Inherent obstacle avoidance** — the arm can "reach around" fixtures. - **Mimics human ergonomics** → intuitive teach-pendant programming. - **6-DOF extension is straightforward** via spherical wrist. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation – Degrees of Freedom of an RRR Arm Using **Gruebler's Equation** for a spatial mechanism: $$ \text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 $$ **Step 1 — Count links ($n$):** For the 3-DOF primary arm: base (link 0), upper arm (link 1), forearm (link 2), end-effector plate (link 3). Thus $n = 4$ links. **Step 2 — Count joints:** - **3 revolute joints** ($j_1 = 3$) → each removes 5 constraints, leaves 1 DOF. - No prismatic or planar joints ($j_2 = 0$). **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DOF} &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 0 \\ &= 3(3) - 6 \\ &= 9 - 6 \\ &= 3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{DOF of RRR primary arm} = 3} $$ This confirms each revolute joint contributes exactly one independent rotational DOF. --- ### 3.2 Derivation – DH Parameter Table for Standard Articulated Robot We follow the **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) convention** — the foundation of KTU board questions. **Step 1 — Establish the base frame (Frame 0):** - $\hat{z}_0$ aligned with Joint 1 axis (vertical, upward). - Origin O₀ at the base mounting surface. **Step 2 — Establish shoulder frame (Frame 1):** - $\hat{z}_1$ along Joint 2 axis (horizontal). - $\hat{x}_1$ perpendicular to both $\hat{z}_0$ and $\hat{z}_1$. - Twist: $\alpha_0 = -90°$ (rotation needed to align $\hat{z}_0$ with $\hat{z}_1$). - Offset: $a_0 = 0$ (joints 1 and 2 intersect at the shoulder). - Link length along $\hat{x}_1$ from O₀ to O₁: $a_0 = 0$. **Step 3 — Establish elbow frame (Frame 2):** - $\hat{z}_2$ along Joint 3 axis (parallel to $\hat{z}_1$). - $\hat{x}_2$ along the common normal between Joint 2 and Joint 3 axes. - Twist: $\alpha_1 = 0°$ (axes are parallel). - Link length: $a_1 = a_2$ (upper arm length, measured along $\hat{x}_2$). - Offset: $d_2 = 0$. **Step 4 — Establish wrist frame (Frame 3):** - $\hat{z}_3$ along wrist Joint 4 (if present) or final link. - Twist: $\alpha_2 = 0°$ (collinear/parallel). - Link length: $a_2 = a_3$ (forearm length). **Resulting DH Parameter Table (for 3-DOF RRR arm):** | Link $i$ | $\alpha_{i-1}$ | $a_{i-1}$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | **0°** | **0** | **0** | $\theta_1^*$ | | 2 | **−90°** | **0** | **0** | $\theta_2^*$ | | 3 | **0°** | $a_2$ | **0** | $\theta_3^*$ | (Stars $*$ denote joint variables.) > **Alternative formulation** (for articulated robots with offset shoulder, e.g., FANUC): > | 1 | −90° | 0 | $d_1$ | $\theta_1^*$ | > | 2 | 0° | $a_1$ | 0 | $\theta_2^*$ | > | 3 | −90° | $a_2$ | 0 | $\theta_3^*$ | --- ### 3.3 Derivation – Forward Kinematics Transformation Matrix $^{0}_3T$ We multiply the three DH transforms.
