## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a parallel manipulator. List any four advantages of parallel manipulators over serial manipulators.**

**Model Answer:**

A **parallel manipulator** is a closed-loop kinematic mechanism in which the end-effector (moving platform) is connected to a fixed base through two or more independent serial kinematic chains (legs) operating in parallel. All actuators are typically mounted on the base, and motion is transmitted concurrently to the platform.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **High Stiffness:** The load is shared among multiple legs, resulting in much higher structural rigidity compared to serial arms.

2. **High Payload-to-Weight Ratio:** Because actuators are on the base, the moving mass is minimal. Delta robots achieve ratios exceeding 10:1.

3. **High Speed and Acceleration:** Low moving inertia allows extremely fast dynamic motion (Delta: >150 picks/min).

4. **High Position Accuracy:** Errors in individual legs average out across the closed-loop structure, yielding sub-millimeter accuracy.

5. *(Optional fifth)* **Better Force/Torque Sensing:** Multiple force sensors in legs enable precise wrench measurement.

**(3 Marks: Definition 1.5M + 4 advantages with brief explanations 1.5M)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the structure of a Stewart Platform and state its degrees of freedom.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Stewart Platform** consists of a moving hexagonal platform connected to a fixed base by **six independently actuated legs**. Each leg is typically a **Universal-Prismatic-Spherical (UPS)** kinematic chain:

- The **Universal (U) joint** at the base end allows 2 rotational DOF.
- The **Prismatic (P) joint** in the middle is the actuated linear slider (1 DOF).
- The **Spherical (S) joint** at the platform end allows 3 rotational DOF.

**Degrees of Freedom Calculation (Kutzbach Criterion):**

$$M = 6(n - j - 1) + \sum f_i - F$$

With $n = 14$, $j = 18$, $\sum f_i = 36$, $F = 0$:

$$M = 6(14 - 18 - 1) + 36 = 6(-5) + 36 = -30 + 36 = 6$$

**DOF = 6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

**Applications:** Flight simulators, motion platforms, tire testing rigs, radio telescopes, and surgical robots.

**(3 Marks: Sketch + brief description 1.5M + DOF calculation with Kutzbach 1.5M)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Kutzbach criterion** for computing the degrees of freedom of a parallel manipulator. Apply it to compute the DOF of a **3-RPR planar parallel manipulator** with all prismatic joints actuated. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Delta robot** in detail. With a neat block diagram, explain its kinematic structure and derive its inverse kinematics. Discuss its industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Kutzbach Criterion Definition:**

The Kutzbach criterion (modified Grübler equation) is used to determine the mobility (DOF) of a planar or spatial mechanism with multiple closed loops:

$$M = \lambda \cdot (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i - F$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mobility (DOF)
- $\lambda = 3$ (planar) or $6$ (spatial)
- $n$ = Total links
- $j$ = Total joints
- $f_i$ = DOF of each joint
- $F$ = Redundant (passive) DOF

**Application to 3-RPR Planar Parallel Manipulator:**

In a 3-RPR manipulator:
- Three identical R-P-R (Revolute-Prismatic-Revolute) legs.
- Each leg connects the fixed base to the moving platform.
- The two end revolute joints are passive; the prismatic is actuated.

**Counting Components:**
- Links: 1 base + 3 leg-segment-1 (before P joint) + 3 leg-segment-2 (after P joint) + 1 platform = $n = 8$
- Joints: 3 R (base) + 3 P (middle) + 3 R (platform) = $j = 9$
- Joint DOFs: All R and P joints have $f_i = 1$, so $\sum f_i = 9$
- Planar: $\lambda = 3$
- Redundant: $F = 0$

**Applying Kutzbach:**

$$M = 3 \cdot (8 - 9 - 1) + 9 = 3 \times (-2) + 9 = -6 + 9 = 3$$

**DOF = 3** — the platform can achieve 2 translations (X, Y) and 1 rotation (about Z).

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Delta Robot Description:**

The Delta robot is a **3-DOF translational parallel manipulator** invented by Reymond Clavel (1985) at EPFL, Switzerland. It is the industry standard for high-speed pick-and-place operations.

**Kinematic Structure:**
- A fixed top base with three **actuated revolute joints** (motors M1, M2, M3).
- Three **upper arms** of length $a$ rotating about the actuated joints.
- Each upper arm connects to a **parallelogram forearm** (two parallel links forming a 4-bar).
- The forearms connect to the **moving platform** at three passive revolute joints.
- The parallelogram structure ensures the platform **remains parallel** to the base at all times → **pure translation, no rotation**.

**Inverse Kinematics Derivation:**

Given desired platform position $P = (x, y, z)$:

For each leg $i = 1, 2, 3$, the elbow point $B_i$ is computed as follows:

The base joint of leg $i$ is at $A_i$ on the base, and the platform attachment point is at $C_i$ on the moving platform. The upper arm has length $a$ and the forearm has length $b$.

**Step 1:** Define the vertical projection of the elbow:

$$x_{B_i} = x + (b/a) \cdot (x - x_{A_i}) \cdot \text{(correction factor)}$$

In a simplified standard form (assuming base joints at $(R_b \cos\phi_i, R_b \sin\phi_i, 0)$ and platform joints at $(R_p \cos\phi_i, R_p \sin\phi_i, 0)$):

**Step 2:** The actuated angle $\theta_i$ satisfies:

$$\cos\theta_i = \frac{x_{B_i}^2 + y_{B_i}^2 + z_{B_i}^2 - a^2 - b^2}{2ab}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\theta_i$:

$$\theta_i = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_i}, \cos\theta_i)$$

**Industrial Applications:**
1. **Food packaging** (biscuits, chocolates, candies) — speeds up to 300 picks/min.
2. **Pharmaceutical packaging** — blister packs, vial handling.
3. **Electronics assembly** — PCB component placement.
4. **Medical device manufacturing** — syringe handling, test tube sorting.
5. **Solar panel and semiconductor wafer handling.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Compare **serial and parallel manipulators** based on at least eight parameters. Explain why parallel manipulators exhibit higher stiffness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable kinematic diagram, explain the **Stewart Platform** mechanism. Apply the Kutzbach-Grübler criterion to verify its 6-DOF mobility. Discuss two major industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Solution: Comparison Table**

| **Parameter** | **Serial Manipulator** | **Parallel Manipulator** |
|---|---|---|
| Kinematic Structure | Open chain | Closed chain with multiple legs |
| Workspace | Large, dexterous, irregular | Limited, compact, well-defined |
| Stiffness | Low (cumulative compliance) | High (load shared by legs) |
| Payload Capacity | Low (motors carry all downstream links) | High (motors fixed to base) |
| Position Accuracy | Low to moderate (errors accumulate) | High (errors average across legs) |
| Forward Kinematics | Trivial (closed-form D-H) | Complex (nonlinear) |
| Inverse Kinematics | Complex (multiple solutions) | Trivial (closed-form leg length) |
| Speed | Limited by moving mass | Very high (lightweight platform) |
| Actuator Location | Distributed along the arm | Fixed to base |
| Cost | Moderate | High (precision joints required) |

**Why Parallel Manipulators Have Higher Stiffness:**

In a serial manipulator, the **end-effector deflection** is the sum of deflections of all links and joints. The joint compliance dominates, and the long cantilever structure amplifies deflection. Each link is loaded in bending/torsion, and the structure is compliant in the direction of the load.

In a parallel manipulator, the **load is shared among $k$ legs** in parallel. Each leg experiences only a fraction ($1/k$) of the total external load. Furthermore, the legs are primarily loaded in **axial tension/compression** (not bending), which is the stiffest loading mode. The Stewart Platform's six legs together provide a stiffness that scales as $\sim k \cdot E \cdot A / L$, where $A$ is leg cross-section and $L$ is leg length. This is typically **10 to 100 times stiffer** than a serial arm of comparable size and weight.

**Part (b) Solution: Stewart Platform**

*(As covered in Section 4.1 above, plus Kutzbach verification already done in Section 3.1.)*

**Kutzbach Verification Recap:**

$$M = 6(14 - 18 - 1) + 36 - 0 = -30 + 36 = 6 \text{ DOF}$$

**Two Major Industrial Applications:**

1. **Flight Simulators:** Stewart Platforms provide 6-DOF motion to cockpit mockups, replicating aircraft pitch, roll, yaw, and heave for pilot training. Used by Boeing, Airbus, and military aviation training centers.

2. **Precision Machining (Hexapod Machine Tools):** Used as ultra-precision milling/polishing platforms for large optical components (telescope mirrors, lenses) and semiconductor wafer inspection. The high stiffness enables nanometer-level positioning accuracy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong $\lambda$ Value:** Students often use $\lambda = 3$ for spatial mechanisms. Remember: **planar $\Rightarrow \lambda = 3$**, **spatial $\Rightarrow \lambda = 6$**. Mixing these up leads to DOF errors of 50% or more.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Count the Base and Platform:** When counting links $n$, both the fixed base and the moving platform count as **one link each** (not zero). Missing either causes DOF errors.
>
> 3. **Joint Count Confusion:** For a 6-UPS leg, there are **3 joints per leg** (U, P, S). Total = 18, not 12 or 24.
>
> 4. **Inverse vs Forward Kinematics Reversal:** In parallel manipulators, inverse kinematics is **easy** and forward kinematics is **hard**. The opposite of serial arms. Examiners specifically test this conceptual understanding.
>
> 5. **Parallelogram Redundancy:** The Delta robot's parallelogram structure contributes 1 redundant constraint per leg ($F = 3$ total). Forgetting this leads to a DOF of $-3$ instead of the correct 3.
>
> 6. **Singularity vs Workspace:** Students often confuse these. Workspace is the *reachable region*; singularity is the *loses-stiffness/lost-DOF configurations* within the workspace boundary.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parallel Manipulator**
>
> - Parallel manipulators have **closed-loop** kinematic chains with **2+ independent legs**.
> - **Stewart Platform** = 6 legs, 6-UPS, **6 DOF** (3T + 3R).
> - **Delta Robot** = 3 legs, parallelogram, **3 DOF** (pure translation, XYZ).
> - **5-Bar Manipulator** = 2 RRR legs, **2 DOF** (XY translation only).
> - **3-RPR Planar** = 3 legs, **3 DOF** (2T + 1R in plane).
> - **Kutzbach Equation (Spatial):** $M = 6(n - j - 1) + \sum f_i - F$
> - **Kutzbach Equation (Planar):** $M = 3(n - j - 1) + \sum f_i - F$
> - **Joint DOFs:** R = 1, P = 1, U = 2, S = 3, C = 2.
> - **Key Inversion:** Parallel = Easy **Inverse**, Hard **Forward** Kinematics.
> - **Actuators** are mounted on/near the **base** (not on moving links).
> - **Stiffness:** Parallel manipulators are 10-100× stiffer than serial arms.
> - **Speed:** Delta achieves 200-300 picks/min (lightweight platform, fixed motors).
> - **Workspace:** Smaller than serial, but **highly dexterous** within the reachable region.
> - **Singularities:** Type I (configuration) and Type II (workspace boundary) are critical in design.
> - **Applications:** Pick-place (Delta), simulation (Stewart), machining (Hexapod), medical (Surgical robots), large-structure positioning (Cable-driven PMs).
> - **Redundant DOF** ($F$): Use for parallelograms and over-constrained legs; non-zero only when structural constraints exist.
> - **Always count base + platform** in the link count $n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **End Effector** is the peripheral device or tool mounted on the distal link of a robot manipulator (typically the wrist flange) that directly interacts with the workpiece or environment to perform the intended task — grasping, welding, painting, drilling, or assembly. When the wrist design incorporates **three revolute joints whose rotational axes intersect at a single common point**, the wrist is termed a **Spherical Wrist**. This concentrated intersection point decouples the orientation kinematics of the wrist from the position kinematics of the arm, producing a closed-form analytical inverse kinematics solution.

A **Mechanical End Effector** is the subclass of end effectors that physically grasps, holds, and manipulates a workpiece through direct mechanical contact using fingers, jaws, hooks, or specialised tooling — relying on mechanical force transmission (linkage, cam, screw, gear) rather than pneumatic suction or electromagnetic fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT402 – Module 1):**
> "Spherical Wrist – End effectors – Types: Mechanical grippers, Vacuum grippers, Magnetic grippers, Special purpose end effectors. Selection and design considerations of end effectors."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Hand

Think of the spherical wrist as the **human wrist joint**, where your forearm bones (radius and ulna), wrist bones (carpals), and hand bones form a structure where all the "pivots" — flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and rotation — converge near one point. This is why you can rotate your hand in any orientation without your entire arm having to reposition.

Similarly, in a robot:
- The **arm (links + elbow joints)** positions the wrist point in 3D space (X, Y, Z) — this is the *position problem*.
- The **spherical wrist (3 intersecting revolute axes)** orients the end-effector in 3D space (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) — this is the *orientation problem*.

The mechanical gripper is analogous to **your fingers and thumb** — the active mechanical device that actually grasps the object. The two opposing jaws of a gripper behave like the human thumb and index finger performing a **pinch grip**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Grasping Force (F_g)** | The clamping force exerted by the jaws on the workpiece, typically **20 N to 200 N** for industrial grippers |
| **Payload (m)** | The mass the end effector can manipulate, ranging from **0.1 kg** (light assembly) to **>50 kg** (heavy forging) |
| **Stroke (s)** | The maximum opening distance between the two jaws, typically **10 mm to 100 mm** |
| **Compliance (C)** | The degree to which the gripper deforms to accommodate positional errors, **0.01 mm/N** to **0.1 mm/N** |
| **Cycles per minute** | The actuation rate, **30 to 120 cycles/min** for high-speed applications |

> [!NOTE]
> A **spherical wrist** is also called a **concentric wrist** or **Euler wrist**, named after Leonhard Euler's three-angle rotation formalism (Z-Y-Z convention) — the natural sequence for describing orientation when three axes intersect at a point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Spherical Wrist: Kinematic Architecture

A 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist has the following Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) signature:

$$\text{DH Parameters: } \{d_4, \theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6\}$$

Where **d₄** is a translation along the wrist axis and **θ₄, θ₅, θ₆** are the three revolute joint angles. The intersection point is at the **origin of frame 4**.

### 2.1.1 Euler Angle Orientation (Z-Y-Z Convention)

Since the axes intersect, the orientation of the end-effector frame relative to frame 3 is given by the **Euler Z-Y-Z rotation matrix**:

$$R_{3}^{6} = R_z(\phi) \, R_y(\theta) \, R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c_\phi c_\theta c_\psi - s_\phi s_\psi & -c_\phi c_\theta s_\psi - s_\phi c_\psi & c_\phi s_\theta \\ s_\phi c_\theta c_\psi + c_\phi s_\psi & -s_\phi c_\theta s_\psi + c_\phi c_\psi & s_\phi s_\theta \\ -s_\theta c_\psi & s_\theta s_\psi & c_\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Euler angles** are functions only of the wrist joint variables (θ₄, θ₅, θ₆). This is why the wrist can be kinematically decoupled from the arm.

### 2.1.2 Inverse Kinematics of the Spherical Wrist

Given the desired orientation matrix $R$, and the orientation of frame 3 with respect to the base $R_0^3$, compute:

$$R_3^6 = (R_0^3)^{-1} R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:

$$\theta_4 = \text{atan2}\left( r_{23}, r_{13} \right) \quad \quad \theta_5 = \text{atan2}\left( \sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, \, r_{33} \right) \quad \quad \theta_6 = \text{atan2}\left( r_{32}, -r_{31} \right)$$

## 2.2 Mechanical Gripper Architecture

A **mechanical gripper** consists of four essential subsystems:

1. **Actuator** — Pneumatic cylinder, hydraulic piston, or servo motor that provides the input power.
2. **Transmission mechanism** — Linkages, cam-follower, rack-pinion, or lead screw that convert actuator motion into jaw motion.
3. **Fingers (Jaws)** — The contact members that physically engage the workpiece.
4. **Sensor suite** — Force sensors, position encoders, and tactile sensors for closed-loop control.

### 2.2.1 Classification of Mechanical Grippers

| Gripper Type | Driving Principle | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Linkage / Toggle Gripper** | Pin-jointed 4-bar linkage, often with **over-centre toggle lock** | High-force holding, forging |
| **Cam-Operated Gripper** | Eccentric cam rotates to translate jaw | Quick-action pick-and-place |
| **Screw-Actuated Gripper** | Lead screw converts rotary to linear motion | Precision assembly |
| **Rack-and-Pinion Gripper** | Two pinions on a common shaft engage racks on jaws | High-speed, symmetric jaw motion |
| **Angular / Wrap Gripper** | Fingers pivot in arc, conforming to cylindrical parts | Cylindrical workpiece handling |
| **Parallel-Jaw Gripper** | Two jaws translate linearly in parallel | Most common — flat/parallel faces |
| **Three-Finger Centric Gripper** | Three jaws converge on a common centre | Spherical/centred parts |

## 2.3 Force and Friction Analysis

The fundamental grasp-stability condition requires that the **frictional force at the jaw-workpiece interface** can support the weight and inertia of the payload:

$$2 \mu F_g \geq m(g + a) \cdot k_{safety}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction at jaw-workpiece interface (**0.1 for steel-rubber, 0.3 for steel-steel, 0.7 for rubber-glass**)
- $F_g$ = Gripping force per jaw
- $m$ = Mass of workpiece
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $a$ = Acceleration of the manipulator
- $k_{safety}$ = Safety factor (**1.5 to 3.0**)

### 2.3.1 Mechanical Advantage of a Toggle Linkage

For a symmetric toggle mechanism with link length $L$ and toggle angle $\alpha$ (angle between link and the line of action of the actuator force), the **mechanical advantage (MA)** is:

$$MA = \frac{F_{jaw}}{F_{actuator}} = \frac{1}{\tan(\alpha)} = \cot(\alpha)$$

> [!TIP]
> As $\alpha \to 0°$ (link nearly aligned with the actuator force line), $MA \to \infty$. This is the principle behind **toggle-lock** grippers that hold workpieces without continuous power input (energy-efficient).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13})$ | Wrist roll angle | rad |
| 2 | $\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^2+r_{23}^2}, r_{33}\right)$ | Wrist pitch angle | rad |
| 3 | $\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})$ | Wrist yaw angle | rad |
| 4 | $F_g = \dfrac{m(g+a) k_{safety}}{2\mu}$ | Required grip force per jaw | N |
| 5 | $MA = \cot(\alpha)$ | Toggle linkage mechanical advantage | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = F_g \cdot d \cdot \mu_{tip}$ | Tip-over torque (for tall workpieces) | N·m |
| 7 | $W_{actuator} = F_{actuator} \cdot s$ | Actuator work per stroke | J |
| 8 | $F_{actuator} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_c^2 P$ | Pneumatic cylinder force | N |

Where $d_c$ is the cylinder bore diameter and $P$ is the air pressure (**typical: 0.4 to 0.6 MPa**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | End Effector Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Body-panel transfer, engine block loading | Heavy-duty toggle grippers (50–200 N) |
| **Electronics PCB** | Pick-and-place of SMD components | Vacuum grippers (delicate) |
| **Food & Beverage** | Bottle handling, fruit sorting | Soft-finger compliant grippers |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Vial and syringe handling | Three-finger centric + force feedback |
| **Foundry / Forging** | Hot ingot handling | High-temperature magnetic/parallel jaws |
| **Space Robotics** | Sample collection (Mars rovers) | Multi-DOF specialised mechanical grippers |
| **Surgical Robotics** | Tissue manipulation | Miniature force-feedback grippers |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **map the choice of end effector to the workpiece material, mass, fragility, and required cycle time**. Memorise this trade-off matrix.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Gripping Force

**Problem Statement:** A robot gripper is required to lift a steel block of mass **m = 5 kg** with peak vertical acceleration **a = 2 m/s²**. The jaw-workpiece interface is **steel-on-rubber** with friction coefficient **μ = 0.5**. The safety factor is **k_safety = 2.0**. Compute the minimum gripping force per jaw.

### Step 1 — Identify the load the gripper must resist

The jaws must counteract the total downward force on the workpiece. By Newton's second law, this is:

$$F_{total} = m(g + a)$$

Substitute the values:

$$F_{total} = 5 \cdot (9.81 + 2) = 5 \cdot 11.81 = 59.05 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the friction equilibrium

Two contact points (top and bottom jaws) each provide a frictional holding force $\mu F_g$. The vertical equilibrium of the workpiece demands:

$$2 \mu F_g \geq F_{total}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the safety factor

Multiplying the right-hand side by $k_{safety}$ gives the design gripping force:

$$2 \mu F_g = m(g + a) k_{safety}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the gripping force per jaw

Rearranging:

$$F_g = \frac{m(g + a) k_{safety}}{2\mu}$$

Substitute:

$$F_g = \frac{5 \cdot 11.81 \cdot 2.0}{2 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{118.1}{1.0} = 118.1 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to engineering units and round up

$$F_g = 118.1 \text{ N} \approx 120 \text{ N (per jaw)}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always round **up** for force calculations — under-designing a grip force results in the workpiece slipping during operation, which is a major safety hazard in industrial robotics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mechanical Advantage of a Toggle Linkage

**Problem Statement:** A toggle gripper has two symmetric links of length **L = 50 mm**. The actuator pushes the toggle's apex along the symmetry axis. When the toggle angle is **α = 15°**, find the mechanical advantage and the jaw force if the actuator delivers **F_a = 50 N**.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Let the two links meet at the moving slider (apex). The slider is acted upon by the actuator force $F_a$ along the line of symmetry, and each link transmits a compressive force $F_{link}$ along its length.

### Step 2 — Force balance at the apex

The vertical (along the symmetry axis) equilibrium at the apex:

$$F_a = 2 F_{link} \cos(\alpha)$$

Hence:

$$F_{link} = \frac{F_a}{2 \cos(\alpha)}$$

### Step 3 — Resolve the link force into a jaw force

The jaw force is the component of $F_{link}$ perpendicular to the symmetry axis (i.e., the direction in which the jaw moves):

$$F_{jaw} = F_{link} \sin(\alpha) = \frac{F_a \sin(\alpha)}{2 \cos(\alpha)} = \frac{F_a}{2} \tan(\alpha)$$

### Step 4 — Derive the mechanical advantage

$$MA = \frac{F_{jaw}}{F_a} = \frac{\tan(\alpha)}{2}$$

Wait — this is for a single toggle slider. In the **symmetric four-bar toggle** configuration where both ends of the toggle connect to the jaws, the **jaw force is doubled** because both ends of the link push outward:

$$MA = \tan(\alpha)$$

Equivalent to the formula $MA = \cot(\alpha)$ if α is measured from the jaw axis (different convention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Convention check: in the **slider-crank toggle**, the mechanical advantage is $\tan(\alpha)$ where α is the half-angle of the toggle apex. In the **four-bar symmetric toggle**, it is $\cot(\alpha)$ where α is the small angle between the link and the line of actuator force. Both formulas are valid; ensure you identify the geometric definition.

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation

With **α = 15°**:

$$MA = \tan(15°) = 0.2679$$

Wait — that is **less than 1**! This means the toggle gives no advantage at 15° from the jaw axis. Recheck the convention.

> The **mechanical advantage is large when the link is nearly aligned with the line of action of the actuator force** (i.e., α small, measured from the actuator force direction). At α = 5°:

$$MA = \cot(5°) = \frac{1}{\tan(5°)} = \frac{1}{0.0875} \approx 11.43$$

So a 50 N actuator produces a jaw force of:

$$F_{jaw} = MA \cdot F_a = 11.43 \cdot 50 = 571.5 \text{ N}$$

This explains why toggle grippers are the preferred choice for **high-force holding** applications — small actuator input, very large jaw force.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics of a Spherical Wrist

**Problem Statement:** A 6-DOF manipulator's spherical wrist has reached a configuration where the desired orientation matrix of the end effector relative to frame 3 is:

$$R_3^6 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.527 & -0.574 & 0.628 \\ 0.369 & 0.819 & -0.439 \\ -0.766 & 0.000 & 0.643 \end{bmatrix}$$

Find the wrist joint angles θ₄, θ₅, θ₆.

### Step 1 — Identify the matrix elements

From the matrix:
- $r_{11} = 0.527$, $r_{12} = -0.574$, $r_{13} = 0.628$
- $r_{21} = 0.369$, $r_{22} = 0.819$, $r_{23} = -0.439$
- $r_{31} = -0.766$, $r_{32} = 0.000$, $r_{33} = 0.643$

### Step 2 — Compute θ₅

$$\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\left( \sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, \, r_{33} \right)$$

$$\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2} = \sqrt{0.628^2 + (-0.439)^2} = \sqrt{0.3944 + 0.1927} = \sqrt{0.5871} = 0.7662$$

$$\theta_5 = \text{atan2}(0.7662, 0.643) = 50.0° = 0.8727 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 3 — Compute θ₄

$$\theta_4 = \text{atan2}\left( r_{23}, r_{13} \right) = \text{atan2}(-0.439, 0.628)$$

$$\theta_4 = -34.96° = -0.6102 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 4 — Compute θ₆

$$\theta_6 = \text{atan2}\left( r_{32}, -r_{31} \right) = \text{atan2}(0.000, 0.766)$$

$$\theta_6 = 0.00° = 0.0000 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 5 — Verify using the sign of sin(θ₅)

Since $r_{33} = \cos(\theta_5) > 0$ and $\sin(\theta_5) > 0$, the solution is the **elbow-up** configuration. No branch-flip needed.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, when $\theta_5 \in (0, \pi)$ (sin θ₅ > 0), use the formulas above directly. When $\theta_5 \in (-\pi, 0)$ (sin θ₅ < 0), flip the sign of both θ₄ and θ₆ and add π to θ₅.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spherical Wrist Kinematic Schematic (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Base Frame 0"] --> B["Joint 1 Revolute Z0"]
    B --> C["Link 1 Shoulder"]
    C --> D["Joint 2 Revolute Z1"]
    D --> E["Link 2 Elbow"]
    E --> F["Joint 3 Revolute Z2"]
    F --> G["Link 3 Forearm"]
    G --> H["Wrist Centre Point O"]
    H --> I["Joint 4 Revolute Z3 Roll Axis"]
    I --> J["Link 4 Wrist Segment A"]
    J --> K["Joint 5 Revolute Z4 Pitch Axis"]
    K --> L["Link 5 Wrist Segment B"]
    L --> M["Joint 6 Revolute Z5 Yaw Axis"]
    M --> N["Link 6 End Effector Flange"]
    N --> O["Mechanical Gripper / Tool"]

    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4
    style M fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4
    style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Note:** The **gold node H (Wrist Centre Point O)** is the **single common intersection point** of the three wrist revolute axes — the defining geometric property of a spherical wrist. The three blue nodes (I, K, M) are the three orthogonal revolute axes of the wrist.

## 4.2 Mechanical Gripper Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Power Source Compressed Air 0.4 to 0.6 MPa"] --> B["Pneumatic Cylinder Actuator"]
    B --> C["Transmission Mechanism Toggle Linkage"]
    C --> D["Moving Jaw Left Finger"]
    C --> E["Moving Jaw Right Finger"]
    D --> F["Friction Contact Interface"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Workpiece"]
    H["Position Sensor Linear Encoder"] -.feedback.-> C
    I["Force Sensor Load Cell"] -.feedback.-> B
    J["Controller PLC Microcontroller"] --> B
    H --> J
    I --> J

    style A fill:#FFB6C1
    style B fill:#FFA07A
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style G fill:#D3D3D3,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Diagram Note:** This block diagram maps the **power flow** (solid arrows) and the **feedback signal flow** (dashed arrows) in a closed-loop servo-controlled mechanical gripper. The transmission mechanism (gold block) is the design heart of the gripper.

## 4.3 End Effector Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Select End Effector"] --> B{"Workpiece Material?"}
    B -->|"Metal / Rigid"| C{"Mass Range?"}
    B -->|"Glass / Ceramic Fragile"| D["Soft Finger / Vacuum / Compliant"]
    B -->|"Flat Sheet Metal"| E["Vacuum or Magnetic"]
    B -->|"Textile / Fabric"| F["Needle Gripper / Pneumatic Soft Jaw"]
    C -->|"Under 2 kg"| G["Pneumatic Parallel Jaw"]
    C -->|"2 to 20 kg"| H["Toggle Linkage Gripper"]
    C -->|"Over 20 kg"| I["Hydraulic Heavy Duty Gripper"]
    D --> G
    E --> J["Vacuum Cup Array"]
    E --> K["Electromagnetic Gripper"]

    style A fill:#4682B4,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FF6347
```

> **Diagram Note:** KTU examiners love this kind of **selection-flowchart** question. Practise drawing and justifying these branching decisions.

## 4.4 Force Resolution at the Jaw (Free-Body Diagram Representation)

```mermaid

    A --> C["Jaw 2 Contact"]

    C --> F["Normal Force F_n from Jaw 2"]
    C --> G["Friction Force F_f2 mu times F_n"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Spherical Wrist and state its kinematic advantage.

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **spherical wrist** is a wrist mechanism comprising **three revolute joints whose axes intersect at a common point**.
>
> **Kinematic advantage:**
> - The **inverse kinematics decouples** — the position of the wrist centre is determined solely by the first three joints (arm), and the orientation is determined solely by the last three joints (wrist).
> - This reduces the problem from a **6-DOF coupled nonlinear system** to **two independent 3-DOF subsystems**, yielding a **closed-form analytical solution**.
> - The Euler Z-Y-Z (or equivalent) angle set can be applied directly, avoiding iterative numerical methods.
> - Singularity analysis is simplified: the wrist singularity occurs only at θ₅ = 0 (or π), which corresponds to the two wrist axes becoming collinear.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for kinematic advantages — 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2: List any four design considerations for selecting a mechanical gripper.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Workpiece geometry** — shape, surface finish, dimensional tolerances, presence of features (holes, ribs).
> 2. **Workpiece mass and weight** — determines the required gripping force and hence the actuator size.
> 3. **Material compatibility** — friction coefficient between jaw and workpiece; risk of marring or contamination.
> 4. **Cycle time and stroke** — pick-and-place rates determine actuation speed and jaw travel.
> 5. **Environmental factors** — temperature, humidity, presence of dust, chemicals, or explosive atmospheres.
> 6. **Sensor integration** — need for force feedback, position sensing, and tactile perception.
> 7. **Compliance** — ability to absorb positional errors from the arm.

*(4 of the above, ~0.75 marks each = 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat kinematic diagram, explain the **architecture of a spherical wrist**. Derive the inverse kinematics solution to compute the wrist joint angles θ₄, θ₅, θ₆ from the desired orientation matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot gripper is required to handle an aluminium block of mass **4 kg** during a vertical pick-and-place operation with peak acceleration **1.5 m/s²**. The jaw surface is **neoprene rubber** with a coefficient of friction **μ = 0.6**. Using a safety factor of **2.0**, compute the minimum gripping force per jaw. Comment on the safety of the design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Diagram and architecture**

Refer to **Section 4.1 (Spherical Wrist Schematic)**. The spherical wrist consists of three revolute joints (4, 5, 6) whose axes all pass through a single point — the **wrist centre O**.

**Step 2 — Orientation matrix**

The end-effector orientation relative to the base is:

$$R_0^6 = R_0^3 \cdot R_3^6$$

Given a desired end-effector orientation $R_0^6$ and known $R_0^3$ (from arm joint variables), the wrist orientation is:

$$R_3^6 = (R_0^3)^T \cdot R_0^6 = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Express as Euler Z-Y-Z rotation**

$$R_3^6 = R_z(\theta_4) R_y(\theta_5) R_z(\theta_6)$$

Equating the (3,3) element:

$$r_{33} = \cos(\theta_5)$$

Equating the (1,3) and (2,3) elements:

$$r_{13} = \cos(\theta_4) \sin(\theta_5)$$
$$r_{23} = \sin(\theta_4) \sin(\theta_5)$$

Dividing the second by the first:

$$\tan(\theta_4) = \frac{r_{23}}{r_{13}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13})}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for θ₅**

$$\boxed{\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, r_{33}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for θ₆**

Equating the (3,1) and (3,2) elements with the assumption $\sin(\theta_5) > 0$:

$$r_{31} = -\sin(\theta_5)\cos(\theta_6)$$
$$r_{32} = \sin(\theta_5)\sin(\theta_6)$$

$$\tan(\theta_6) = \frac{r_{32}}{-r_{31}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})}$$

*(Diagram = 1 mark, derivation steps = 6 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**

- $m = 4$ kg, $a = 1.5$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\mu = 0.6$, $k_{safety} = 2.0$

**Step 2 — Compute the required grip force**

$$F_g = \frac{m(g + a) k_{safety}}{2\mu}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$F_g = \frac{4 \cdot (9.81 + 1.5) \cdot 2.0}{2 \cdot 0.6}$$

$$F_g = \frac{4 \cdot 11.31 \cdot 2.0}{1.2} = \frac{90.48}{1.2} = 75.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on safety**

> The minimum gripping force per jaw is **75.4 N**. For an industrial pneumatic gripper (typical capacity 100–200 N per jaw), this is **safe and achievable** with significant margin. The neoprene-aluminium interface (μ = 0.6) provides good grip without marring the aluminium surface. The safety factor of 2.0 accounts for vibration, dynamic overshoots, and surface contamination. **The design is recommended to operate at 80 N per jaw** to provide an additional 6% engineering margin over the calculated minimum.

*(Calculation = 5 marks, safety comment = 2 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With sketches, classify **mechanical grippers** based on the transmission mechanism used. Explain the toggle linkage gripper in detail, including the derivation of its mechanical advantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A toggle-jaw gripper has link length **L = 80 mm** and operates with a toggle half-angle of **α = 8°** (measured from the line of actuator force). The actuator is a pneumatic cylinder of bore **D = 40 mm** operating at **P = 0.5 MPa**. Compute:
   (i) the actuator force, and
   (ii) the gripping force per jaw. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Classification of mechanical grippers by transmission**

Refer to the **Section 2.2.1 table**. Five principal types:
- Linkage / toggle
- Cam-operated
- Screw / lead-screw
- Rack-and-pinion
- Angular / wrap-around

*(1 mark for table, 1 mark for diagram = 2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Toggle linkage gripper — working principle**

A **toggle linkage** uses two pin-jointed links that are nearly aligned in the gripping position. The actuator pushes the toggle's apex; the small angle between the links and the actuator force line creates a large **mechanical advantage** (MA → ∞ as α → 0).

**Step 3 — Derivation of mechanical advantage**

The actuator force $F_a$ pushes the slider. The force in each link is $F_{link}$. The vertical force balance at the slider:

$$F_a = 2 F_{link} \cos(\alpha)$$

$$F_{link} = \frac{F_a}{2\cos(\alpha)}$$

The jaw force is the horizontal (lateral) component:

$$F_{jaw} = 2 F_{link} \sin(\alpha) = 2 \cdot \frac{F_a}{2\cos(\alpha)} \cdot \sin(\alpha) = F_a \tan(\alpha)$$

Wait — this gives MA = tan(α), which is < 1 for small α. The classical toggle formulation uses α measured from the **jaw axis**:

$$MA = \cot(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\tan(\alpha)}$$

For α measured from the actuator force line, both formulations are geometrically equivalent; the convention differs by 90° rotation.

$$\boxed{MA = \frac{F_{jaw}}{F_a} = \frac{1}{\tan(\alpha)} = \cot(\alpha)}$$

*(Derivation = 4 marks, working explanation = 1 mark = 5 marks)*

**Step 4 — Locking property**

When the toggle reaches its **over-centre** (locked) position (α slightly past 0°), the jaw force is held without continuous actuator input — a major energy-saving feature.

*(1 mark for locking property)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the actuator force**

Pneumatic cylinder force:

$$F_a = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 P = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.040)^2 (0.5 \times 10^6)$$

$$F_a = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0016 \cdot 500{,}000 = 0.7854 \cdot 800 = 628.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mechanical advantage**

With α = 8° measured from the actuator force line, the MA (treating α as the half-angle of the apex with the convention from Part (a) where MA = cot(α) when α is the small angle from actuator axis):

$$MA = \cot(8°) = \frac{1}{\tan(8°)} = \frac{1}{0.1405} = 7.116$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gripping force per jaw**

For a symmetric four-bar toggle (two jaws, two toggles per side):

$$F_{jaw} = MA \cdot F_a = 7.116 \cdot 628.3 = 4470 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Verification and engineering comment**

> The gripper can exert a jaw force of **4.47 kN** per jaw — a substantial clamping force suitable for **heavy forging or large metal parts** with mass up to several hundred kilograms. The toggle over-centre locking means the actuator can be de-energised after closing, conserving compressed air.

*(Calculation 5 marks + comment 2 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mark-Loss Triggers on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** in the gripping force derivation — KTU examiners expect $k_{safety} = 1.5$ to $3.0$ to appear explicitly. Skipping it costs **1 to 2 marks** consistently.
>
> 2. **Confusing Euler Z-Y-Z with R-P-Y (Roll-Pitch-Yaw)** — the spherical wrist solution uses the **Z-Y-Z sequence** because the wrist axes are sequential Z-then-Y-then-Z. Using R-P-Y will give **wrong numerical answers** and lose all 7 marks of Part (a).
>
> 3. **Sign errors in atan2 arguments** — the inverse kinematics formulas use $r_{23}$ vs $r_{13}$ (not $r_{13}$ vs $r_{23}$), and $-r_{31}$ for θ₆. Transposing arguments is a **favourite KTU trap**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in toggle derivation** — if MA is computed without showing the $\cot(\alpha)$ step, partial credit is lost. Always derive the formula symbolically first, then substitute.
>
> 5. **Wrong convention for α in toggle MA** — clearly state whether α is measured from the actuator line or the jaw line. A confused derivation is marked down even if the final number is correct.
>
> 6. **Not addressing the spherical wrist singularity at θ₅ = 0** — KTU's 2024 scheme has a strong emphasis on singularity awareness. A complete answer must mention this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Spherical wrist = 3 revolute joints with axes intersecting at one point** (decouples position and orientation kinematics).
- [x] **Euler Z-Y-Z convention** is used to extract wrist angles from the desired orientation matrix.
- [x] **Inverse kinematics formulas** are closed-form: $\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13})$, $\theta_5 = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{r_{13}^2+r_{23}^2}, r_{33})$, $\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})$.
- [x] **Mechanical grippers** use direct mechanical contact — five main types: linkage/toggle, cam, screw, rack-pinion, angular.
- [x] **Toggle grippers** offer the highest mechanical advantage ($MA = \cot \alpha$) and self-locking at the over-centre position.
- [x] **Gripping force formula**: $F_g = \dfrac{m(g+a)k_{safety}}{2\mu}$ — always include the safety factor.
- [x] **Friction coefficient** depends on jaw-workpiece material pair — typical $\mu$ from 0.1 (steel-steel dry) to 0.7 (rubber-glass).
- [x] **Pneumatic cylinder force**: $F_a = (\pi/4) D^2 P$ — bore diameter D in metres, pressure in Pa.
- [x] **Design considerations** include: workpiece geometry, mass, material, cycle time, environment, sensor integration, compliance.
- [x] **Singularity**: wrist loses one DOF when θ₅ = 0 (wrist axes 4 and 6 become collinear).
- [x] **End effector selection matrix** — match gripper type to workpiece material: rigid metal → toggle; fragile glass → vacuum/soft jaw; flat sheet → vacuum/magnetic; fabric → needle/pneumatic.
- [x] **Industrial standard metrics**: 20–200 N grip force, 10–100 mm stroke, 30–120 cycles/min.
- [x] **Closed-loop control** integrates position, force, and tactile sensors for adaptive grasping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧲 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 End-Effectors in Robotic Manipulation — The Gripping Problem

In robotic manipulators, the **end-effector** is the device mounted at the wrist of the robot arm that interacts directly with the workpiece. Among the broad families of end-effectors (mechanical jaws, adhesive grippers, magnetic grippers, vacuum grippers, etc.), **non-contact or surface-contact gripping technologies** occupy a critical niche for handling delicate, flat, or geometrically complex parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCRUT402 Module 1):**
> *Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers* — their operating principles, force generation, design equations, selection criteria, and industrial deployment within a robotic cell.

---

## 1.2 Magnetic Grippers — Formal Definition

A **Magnetic Gripper** is an end-effector that generates a **holding force** through magnetic flux linkage between the gripper's magnetic circuit and a **ferromagnetic workpiece**. The attractive force between the magnet poles and the work surface is harnessed to lift, hold, or transfer the part without mechanical finger contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *"A magnetic gripper is a non-contact handling device that uses a controlled magnetic field — produced either by permanent magnets, electromagnets, or a hybrid combination — to generate an attractive force on a ferrous workpiece, enabling pick-and-place operations in robotic cells."*

### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — The Fridge Magnet

Imagine sticking a fridge magnet onto a steel refrigerator door. The magnet "holds" the paper because the magnetic flux lines loop from the magnet's north pole, through the steel sheet, and back to the south pole. The steel **completes the magnetic circuit**, dramatically amplifying the attractive force. A **magnetic gripper works on exactly the same principle**, but engineered for industrial payloads (kg to tonnes) and repeatable release cycles.

### 1.2.1 Three Sub-Families of Magnetic Grippers

| Sub-Type | Flux Source | Flux Control | Power Dependency | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Hard-magnetic material (NdFeB, SmCo) | Mechanical shunt / slide | **None** | Power-loss-safe zones, hot workpieces |
| **Electromagnetic (EM)** | Soft-iron core + DC coil | Current ON/OFF | **Continuous DC required** | Instant release, programmable |
| **Hybrid (Reed / Electro-Permanent — EPM)** | PM + EM coil | Short current pulse flips state | **Pulse only (no hold current)** | Energy-efficient industrial cells |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Fact:** *Electromagnetic grippers must be designed fail-safe (spring-loaded release on power loss) only when handling benign loads. For hot steel slabs in steel mills, **permanent magnetic grippers** are preferred because they do not lose holding force during a power outage — a critical safety consideration.*

---

## 1.3 Vacuum Grippers — Formal Definition

A **Vacuum Gripper** is an end-effector that holds a workpiece by **reducing the air pressure** between a soft elastomer cup (or pad) and the work surface, so that the **atmospheric pressure acting on the opposite face of the workpiece** pushes it firmly against the cup. The resulting **pressure differential** generates the holding (lift) force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *"A vacuum gripper is a fluid-pressure-based end-effector that creates a sub-atmospheric pressure region between an elastomer sealing element and the workpiece surface, leveraging the atmospheric pressure differential to generate a normal holding force, suitable for flat, smooth, non-porous components such as glass, sheet metal, cardboard, and PCB panels."*

### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — The Suction Cup on a Tile

Press a plastic suction cup against a smooth bathroom tile and push out the air from beneath. The atmospheric pressure above the cup (~101.3 kPa) is now far greater than the pressure beneath (~10–20 kPa). The net downward force on the cup — equal to the pressure difference multiplied by the cup's area — is what makes the cup "stick." **A vacuum gripper is the engineered, automated, robotic version of this exact phenomenon**, often using multiple cups distributed across a manifold for larger or heavier parts.

### 1.3.1 Vacuum Generation Methods

| Method | Principle | Suction Level | Industrial Use |
|--------|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Vacuum Pump (Rotary Vane / Diaphragm)** | Mechanical displacement of air | High vacuum (up to **−95 kPa** gauge) | Heavy payloads, CNC tending |
| **Venturi Ejector (Pneumatic)** | Compressed air through constriction | Medium vacuum (~**−60 to −80 kPa**) | Most common in factory automation |
| **Blower (Coanda / Multi-stage)** | High-volume low-pressure air | Low vacuum (~**−10 to −20 kPa**), high flow | Porous materials (cardboard, foam) |
| **Bernoulli Gripper** | High-velocity air creates low pressure at gap | Contactless lift | Delicate wafers, solar cells |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Magnetic Gripper — Operating Principle & Flux Circuit

The magnetic gripper's force generation is governed by the **Maxwell Stress Tensor** in the air gap of a magnetic circuit. The fundamental relation for an idealized uniform air gap of area $A$ and magnetic flux density $B$ is:

$$F_{\text{mag}} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,A}{2\,\mu_{0}}$$

where $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ is the **permeability of free space**.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind the Force

- The magnetic field stores energy in the air gap at a density $\dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$ (Joules per cubic meter).
- When the workpiece moves closer (gap $g$ decreases), this energy is **released** as mechanical work.
- The rate of energy release with respect to gap distance gives the **attractive force**.

### 2.1.2 Real-World Force Equation (with Air Gap)

In a practical electromagnet, the air gap $g$ is non-negligible, and the coil has $N$ turns carrying current $I$. Using **Hopkinson's Law** (analogue of Ohm's Law for magnetic circuits):

$$\mathcal{F} \;=\; \mathcal{R}_{c}\,\Phi \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{MMF} \;=\; \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R}_{\text{total}}$$

$$NI \;=\; \Phi \left( \frac{g}{\mu_{0}\,A} + \frac{\ell_{\text{core}}}{\mu_{r}\,\mu_{0}\,A} \right)$$

For a small gap where core reluctance $\ll$ air-gap reluctance, this simplifies to:

$$\Phi \;\approx\; \frac{\mu_{0}\,A\,N\,I}{g} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B \;=\; \frac{\mu_{0}\,N\,I}{g}$$

Substituting back into the Maxwell force:

$$F_{\text{mag}} \;\approx\; \frac{\mu_{0}\,A\,N^{2}\,I^{2}}{2\,g^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Insight — Inverse-Square Law:** The magnetic holding force is **inversely proportional to the square of the air gap** $g$. Doubling the gap reduces the force by a factor of **4**. This is why flat, clean, well-mated surfaces are mandatory for magnetic grippers.

### 2.1.3 Permanent Magnet (PM) Gripper Force

For a permanent magnet of residual flux density $B_{r}$ operating into a ferromagnetic circuit, the air-gap flux density is approximately:

$$B \;\approx\; B_{r}\,\frac{A_{m}}{A_{g}}\,\frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\ell_{g}}{\ell_{m}}\,\mu_{r,\text{rec}}}$$

where subscripts $m$ and $g$ denote magnet and gap respectively. The holding force is then obtained from the same Maxwell expression.

---

## 2.2 Vacuum Gripper — Operating Principle & Pressure Differential

The vacuum gripper exploits the **atmospheric pressure differential**. With a perfectly sealed cup of effective area $A_{\text{eff}}$ on a workpiece, the gauge vacuum pressure is $\Delta P = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vacuum}}$. The **theoretical lift force** is:

$$F_{\text{vac}} \;=\; \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$$

### 2.2.1 The "Why" Behind the Force

- Atmospheric pressure pushes on every square meter of Earth's surface with force $\approx 101.3\ \text{kPa}$ (equivalent to ~$10.3$ tonnes per m²).
- When the cup evacuates the air beneath it, this pressure on the **top face of the workpiece** remains, while the **bottom face experiences only the small residual vacuum pressure**.
- The **net upward force** equals the pressure difference times the sealed area.

### 2.2.2 Real-World Force Equation (with Safety Factor & Orientation)

The actual usable lifting force must incorporate a **safety factor** $n$ (typically $n = 2$ for horizontal pick, $n = 4$–$5$ for vertical/lift-up picks) and account for **cup efficiency** $\eta$ (typically 0.6–0.85) due to leakage, surface roughness, and cup deflection:

$$F_{\text{usable}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot \eta}{n}$$

For a **horizontal pick** (workpiece lying on a conveyor, lifted vertically):

$$F_{\text{usable}} \;\geq\; m \cdot g \quad \text{(weight to be lifted)}$$

For **vertical/inverted pick** (workpiece on ceiling or wall — friction only):

$$F_{\text{friction}} \;=\; \mu \cdot F_{\text{vac}} \;\geq\; m \cdot g$$

where $\mu$ is the **friction coefficient** between the cup elastomer and the workpiece (typically $\mu \approx 0.5$ for silicone on glass).

### 2.2.3 Effective Cup Area (annular seal correction)

A real suction cup has a sealing lip that does not contribute to the load-bearing area. The **effective area** is the area enclosed by the inner sealing lip:

$$A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\left( D_{\text{outer}}^{2} - D_{\text{seal}}^{2} \right) \;\approx\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,D_{\text{eff}}^{2}$$

where $D_{\text{eff}}$ is the **effective diameter** of the load-bearing zone.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) | Where Used |
|---|----------|----------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | $F_{\text{mag}} = \dfrac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}}$ | Max attractive force | N | All magnetic grippers |
| 2 | $F_{\text{mag}} \approx \dfrac{\mu_{0}A\,N^{2}I^{2}}{2g^{2}}$ | EM force, gap-dominated | N | Electromagnet design |
| 3 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_{0}\,N\,I}{g}$ | Air-gap flux density | T | Coil / air-gap analysis |
| 4 | $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | Permeability of free space | H/m | Fundamental constant |
| 5 | $F_{\text{vac}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$ | Theoretical lift force | N | Vacuum gripper |
| 6 | $\Delta P = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vacuum}}$ | Pressure differential | Pa | Vacuum analysis |
| 7 | $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | Pa | Reference value |
| 8 | $F_{\text{usable}} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot \eta}{n}$ | Design (usable) force | N | Design with safety factor |
| 9 | $A_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D_{\text{eff}}^{2}$ | Effective cup area | m² | Cup sizing |
| 10 | $F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{vac}}$ | Vertical/inverted holding | N | Overhead pick |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the constants:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ and $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 101.325\ \text{kPa}$. These are tested in derivations and numerical problems.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Design Philosophy

| Criterion | Magnetic Gripper | Vacuum Gripper |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| **Workpiece material** | Ferromagnetic only (steel, iron) | Any **non-porous** flat surface (glass, plastic, sheet metal, cardboard*) |
| **Surface requirement** | Flat, clean, low roughness | Flat, smooth, sealed (no through-holes) |
| **Power requirement** | PM = none; EM = continuous; EPM = pulse | Compressed air (Venturi) or electric pump |
| **Force per unit area** | Up to ~**10–20 N/cm²** (large magnets) | Limited to **~10 N/cm²** (capped by $P_{\text{atm}}$) |
| **Release method** | Current OFF (EM) / mechanical shunt (PM) | Vacuum OFF, or positive-pressure blow-off |
| **Failure mode** | Power-loss = drop (EM); hold (PM) | Air-loss = drop |
| **Cleanroom compatibility** | Yes (no air exhaust) | Excellent |
| **Typical payloads** | 1 kg – 5 tonnes | 0.1 kg – 50 kg (per cup array) |
| **Industrial examples** | Steel coil handling, scrap sorting | Glass panel pick, PCB transfer, palletizing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Maxwell Stress Force for Magnetic Gripper

**Goal:** Starting from the energy stored in an air gap, derive the attractive force $F_{\text{mag}} = \dfrac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}}$.

**Step 1 — Energy Density in a Magnetic Field**

The energy stored per unit volume in a region of magnetic flux density $B$ in free space is:

$$w \;=\; \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \quad \left[ \text{J/m}^{3} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy in the Air Gap**

If the air gap has cross-sectional area $A$ and instantaneous length $g$, the total energy stored in the gap is:

$$W_{\text{gap}} \;=\; w \cdot A \cdot g \;=\; \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Principle of Virtual Work**

The mechanical force $F$ trying to close the gap is defined as the **rate of change of stored energy with respect to gap length** (with flux held constant for a constant-current source):

$$F \;=\; \left. \frac{\partial W_{\text{gap}}}{\partial g} \right|_{\Phi = \text{const}} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,A}{2\mu_{0}}$$

**Step 4 — Result**

$$\boxed{\,F_{\text{mag}} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,A}{2\mu_{0}}\,}$$

This is the **force per pole face**; a gripper with two pole faces (one magnet, one return path) effectively doubles this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Holding Force of a Single Vacuum Cup

**Problem:** A circular vacuum cup of effective diameter $D_{\text{eff}} = 60\ \text{mm}$ is used to lift a flat glass panel weighing $m = 2.5\ \text{kg}$ vertically from a horizontal conveyor. The vacuum pump delivers $\Delta P = 70\ \text{kPa}$ gauge vacuum. The cup efficiency is $\eta = 0.75$. The recommended safety factor is $n = 2$ (horizontal lift). Verify the design is safe, and compute the maximum allowable mass for $n = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Effective Area**

$$A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}D_{\text{eff}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}(0.060)^{2} \;=\; 2.827 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Lift Force**

$$F_{\text{theor}} \;=\; \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 70{,}000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 197.9\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cup Efficiency**

$$F_{\text{eff}} \;=\; F_{\text{theor}} \cdot \eta \;=\; 197.9 \times 0.75 \;=\; 148.4\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Required Force to Lift**

$$F_{\text{req}} \;=\; m \cdot g \;=\; 2.5 \times 9.81 \;=\; 24.53\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Actual Safety Factor Achieved**

$$n_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{eff}}}{F_{\text{req}}} \;=\; \frac{148.4}{24.53} \;=\; 6.05$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $n_{\text{actual}} = 6.05 \gg 2$ (the recommended minimum for horizontal lift), the design is **safe** with a large margin.

**Step 6 — Maximum Mass for $n = 2.5$**

$$m_{\max} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{eff}}}{n \cdot g} \;=\; \frac{148.4}{2.5 \times 9.81} \;=\; 6.05\ \text{kg}$$

**Final Answer:** The design is safe; the maximum mass safely handled is approximately **6.05 kg** at the stricter $n = 2.5$ safety factor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Electromagnet Coil Design (Numerical)

**Problem:** Design a U-shaped electromagnet with pole area $A = 4\ \text{cm}^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}$ per pole, air gap $g = 1.5\ \text{mm}$ per pole, and 400 turns of coil carrying $I = 2\ \text{A}$. Compute the air-gap flux density, the total attractive force, and the force if the gap doubles.

**Step 1 — Flux Density in the Air Gap**

Using $B = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{g}$:

$$B \;=\; \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(400)(2)}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{1.005 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.670\ \text{T}$$

**Step 2 — Force per Pole**

$$F_{\text{one}} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}} \;=\; \frac{(0.670)^{2} \cdot (4 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})} \;=\; \frac{0.4489 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_{\text{one}} \;=\; \frac{1.796 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 71.4\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Total Force (Two Poles, U-shape)**

$$F_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2 \cdot F_{\text{one}} \;=\; 142.8\ \text{N} \;\approx\; 14.56\ \text{kgf}$$

**Step 4 — Force when Gap Doubles ($g = 3.0\ \text{mm}$)**

By inverse-square law:

$$F_{\text{doubled gap}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{2^{2}} \;=\; \frac{142.8}{4} \;=\; 35.7\ \text{N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Design Lesson:** Doubling the air gap **quarters** the force. Always maintain a clean, well-mated work surface for electromagnetic grippers.

---

## 3.4 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Magnetic (EM) | Vacuum (Venturi) | Selection Driver |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Force model** | $F \propto B^{2} \cdot A$ | $F \propto \Delta P \cdot A$ | Magnetic is $\propto B^{2}$, vacuum is linear in $\Delta P$ |
| **Gap sensitivity** | $F \propto 1/g^{2}$ (severe) | $F$ weakly gap-dependent | Magnetic needs surface conformity |
| **Power source** | Electrical (DC) | Compressed air (5–6 bar) | Plant utility available? |
| **Energy at hold** | Continuous (EM) or zero (PM/EPM) | Continuous (air consumption) | EPM is the most energy-efficient magnetic |
| **Release speed** | Fast (current OFF) | Fast (vent to atmosphere) | Both suitable for high-speed pick |
| **Payload scaling** | Add poles or stack magnets | Add more cups on manifold | Both modular |
| **Workpiece material** | Ferromagnetic only | Any non-porous flat | Vacuum is more universal (excluding porous) |
| **Hazardous environment** | Safe (no air exhaust) | Safe (air-driven) | Both OK; magnetic preferred in explosive dust |

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Magnetic Gripper — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of a hybrid (Electro-Permanent Magnet, EPM) magnetic gripper — the most common industrial type. It is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** rather than a physical drawing, showing how the control signal, magnetic circuit, and workpiece interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Controller PLC] --> B[24V DC Drive Module]
    B --> C[Pulse Current Generator 5ms Duration]
    C --> D[Reversible Polarity Switch H-Bridge]
    D --> E[EPM Core Soft Iron with AlNiCo Rotor]
    E --> F[Air Gap to Workpiece 0.5 to 2mm]
    F --> G[Ferromagnetic Workpiece Completes Flux Loop]
    G --> H[Maxwell Attractive Force F equals B squared A over 2 mu0]
    H --> I[Workpiece Held Payload up to 50kg]
    D --> J[Reverse Pulse or Mechanical Shunt]
    J --> K[Flux Cancellation Force equals Zero]
    K --> L[Workpiece Released]
    styleA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    styleB fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    styleC fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    styleD fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    styleE fill:#f4b183,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    styleF fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    styleG fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    styleH fill:#c00000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    styleI fill:#70ad47,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    styleJ fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    styleK fill:#f4b183,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    styleL fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 🔍 Reading the Flow

- **PLC → Drive → Pulse Generator → H-Bridge**: Control electronics that decide ON/OFF and direction of magnetization.
- **H-Bridge → EPM Core**: A short (≈ 5 ms) current pulse flips the magnetization of the **AlNiCo rotor**, magnetizing or demagnetizing the gripper — no holding current needed.
- **EPM Core → Air Gap → Workpiece**: Flux loops from N-pole, through the air gap, into the ferromagnetic workpiece, and back to S-pole.
- **Maxwell Force → Hold**: Force per Equation 1 holds the workpiece.
- **Reverse Pulse → Flux Cancellation → Release**: A pulse of opposite polarity cancels the residual flux, releasing the part instantly.

---

### 🔍 Reading the Flow

- **Controller → Solenoid → Venturi → Cups**: Vacuum is generated on demand.
- **Compressed air** (5–6 bar) flows through a constriction (Venturi) — by Bernoulli's principle, the high-velocity air creates a low-pressure region connected to the cups.
- **Multiple cups** (1–4 typical) distribute the load and conform to the workpiece surface.
- **Atmospheric pressure** acts on the top face; the evacuated region beneath is at ~ −60 to −80 kPa gauge.
- **Release**: Solenoid closes the air supply; atmospheric pressure re-enters the cups; vacuum collapses; part is released.

# 📚 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for two-pole U-shaped magnets — students write $F = B^{2}A/(2\mu_{0})$ for one pole but forget the multiplier when computing total force.
> 2. **Using gauge vs absolute pressure inconsistently** — atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**; vacuum gauge pressure is the *difference*. Use $\Delta P$ correctly.
> 3. **Ignoring the safety factor** $n$ — KTU answers that compute the *theoretical* force without applying $n$ and $\eta$ lose 2–3 marks easily.
> 4. **Confusing PM and EM behaviour** — PM does **not** lose hold on power failure; EM **does**. This is a frequent MCQ trap.
> 5. **Wrong unit conversions** — $1\ \text{cm}^{2} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$. Most numerical errors come from missing this.

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Compare permanent magnet and electromagnet grippers with respect to power dependency, release mechanism, and failure safety." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Electromagnet (EM) |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Power dependency** | No external power required to hold; flux is "frozen" in hard magnetic material | Continuous DC current required; energy consumed during entire hold |
| **Release mechanism** | Mechanical shunt lever or EPM reverse-pulse | Current OFF |
| **Failure safety** | **Fail-safe** — holds load even on power loss (preferred for hot steel handling) | **Fail-unsafe** — drops load on power loss (requires spring-loaded mechanical backup for safety) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Why are vacuum grippers unsuitable for handling porous or rough workpieces? Mention two design modifications to overcome this limitation." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Vacuum grippers rely on maintaining a **pressure differential** between the sealed region beneath the cup and the atmosphere. Porous or rough surfaces **allow air leakage** into the sealed region, rapidly equalizing the pressure and destroying the holding force.

**Two design modifications:**
1. **Use of a high-flow blower** instead of a vacuum pump — provides a continuous large volume of air evacuation to compensate for leakage through pores.
2. **Use of soft conformable foam-rubber gaskets** or large-diameter sealing lips that conform to surface roughness and form an effective seal even on textured surfaces.

*(Other valid modifications: Use of multiple smaller cups, addition of check valves, or pre-sealing with a thin film.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the working principle of an **Electro-Permanent Magnet (EPM) gripper**. State two advantages it has over a pure electromagnet gripper. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A U-shaped electromagnet has two pole faces, each of area $A = 6\ \text{cm}^{2}$, separated from a flat steel workpiece by an air gap $g = 1.2\ \text{mm}$ per pole. The coil has $N = 500$ turns carrying $I = 1.5\ \text{A}$. Compute:
   (i) the flux density in the air gap,
   (ii) the total attractive force on the workpiece.
   Use $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) EPM Gripper — Working Principle (7 Marks)**

An **Electro-Permanent Magnet (EPM) gripper** combines a **permanent magnet (AlNiCo or NdFeB rotor)** with a **pulsed electromagnetic coil** wound around a soft-iron yoke. The system has two stable magnetic states:

- **Magnetized (HOLD) state:** A short DC pulse (~5–50 ms) magnetizes the rotor in a direction that **adds** to the flux from the soft-iron pole pieces. Strong flux loops through the air gap into the workpiece → strong attractive force.
- **Demagnetized (RELEASE) state:** A reverse-polarity pulse of equivalent magnitude flips the rotor, **cancelling** the air-gap flux → zero holding force → workpiece released.

**Functional diagram:**

```
  PLC Pulse Generator
         ↓ (5 ms DC pulse)
  H-Bridge Reverser ──→ EPM Coil
                              ↕
                  ┌─── AlNiCo Rotor ───┐
                  │   (flip polarity)   │
                  └─── Soft Iron Yoke ──┘
                              ↕
                       Air Gap (g)
                              ↕
                  ┌── Workpiece (Steel) ──┐
                  │  Completes Flux Loop  │
                  └────────────────────────┘
```

**Two advantages over pure electromagnet:**
1. **Zero holding current** — no energy consumed during hold; pure electromagnet needs continuous current.
2. **Fail-safe (holds on power loss)** — unlike an EM that drops the load on power failure, the EPM retains its hold (or release) state, which is critical in steel mills, hoist applications, etc.

*(A third optional point: lower heat generation, longer coil life.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $A = 6\ \text{cm}^{2} = 6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $g = 1.2\ \text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $N = 500$, $I = 1.5\ \text{A}$, two poles.

**Step (i) — Flux density in air gap:**

$$B \;=\; \frac{\mu_{0}\,N\,I}{g} \;=\; \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(500)(1.5)}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$B \;=\; \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(750)}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{9.425 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$B \;\approx\; 0.785\ \text{T}$$

**Step (ii) — Force per pole (Maxwell):**

$$F_{\text{one}} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}} \;=\; \frac{(0.785)^{2}(6 \times 10^{-4})}{2(4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$F_{\text{one}} \;=\; \frac{(0.6162)(6 \times 10^{-4})}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{3.697 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_{\text{one}} \;\approx\; 147.1\ \text{N}$$

**Step (iii) — Total force (two poles):**

$$F_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2 \times F_{\text{one}} \;=\; 294.2\ \text{N} \;\approx\; 30.0\ \text{kgf}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $B \approx 0.785\ \text{T}$
- (ii) $F_{\text{total}} \approx 294.2\ \text{N}$ (≈ 30 kgf)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the operation of a **Venturi-type vacuum gripper**. Derive an expression for the theoretical lift force it can exert on a flat workpiece. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular vacuum cup of effective diameter $D = 80\ \text{mm}$ is used to lift a glass panel weighing $5\ \text{kg}$ from a horizontal conveyor. The vacuum pump delivers a gauge vacuum of $-65\ \text{kPa}$. Take cup efficiency $\eta = 0.8$ and acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.
   (i) Calculate the effective cup area and the theoretical holding force.
   (ii) Find the actual safety factor achieved. State whether the design is acceptable for industrial use (recommended $n \geq 2$ for horizontal lifts). **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Venturi Vacuum Gripper — Principle & Force Derivation (7 Marks)**

A **Venturi ejector** has a converging–diverging nozzle through which compressed air is forced.

```
                P_atm
                  ↓
   ┌──────────────────────────┐
   │      ↑ Atmospheric       │
   │     │ Pressure Force     │
   ├─────┴──────────────────┤
   │    ┌──┐ ←── CUp ──┐    │
   │    │  │           │    │
   │    └──┘  Sealed   │    │   ←  Workpiece
   │   Vacuum  Area    │    │
   │   Region  A_eff   │    │
   └──────────────────┴────┘
                ↑
       Compressed air (5-6 bar)
            through Venturi
```

**Working:** Compressed air at 5–6 bar enters the Venturi, accelerates through the throat, and exits to atmosphere. By **Bernoulli's principle**, the high-velocity air at the throat creates a **low-pressure region** connected via tubing to the suction cup. The cup evacuates the air beneath it, creating $\Delta P$ between atmosphere (top) and vacuum (bottom).

**Derivation of lift force:**

Let $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ act on the upper face of the workpiece, and let the cup region be at pressure $P_{\text{vac}}$. The effective sealed area is $A_{\text{eff}}$.

The net upward force on the workpiece is:

$$F_{\text{vac}} \;=\; (P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}) \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$$

For a circular cup of effective diameter $D_{\text{eff}}$:

$$A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}D_{\text{eff}}^{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_{\text{vac}} \;=\; \Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}D_{\text{eff}}^{2}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $D = 80\ \text{mm} = 0.080\ \text{m}$, $m = 5\ \text{kg}$, $\Delta P = 65\ \text{kPa} = 65{,}000\ \text{Pa}$, $\eta = 0.8$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.

**Step (i) — Effective area:**

$$A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064) \;=\; 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Theoretical holding force:**

$$F_{\text{theor}} \;=\; \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 65{,}000 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 326.7\ \text{N}$$

**Step (ii) — Effective (usable) force:**

$$F_{\text{eff}} \;=\; F_{\text{theor}} \cdot \eta \;=\; 326.7 \times 0.8 \;=\; 261.4\ \text{N}$$

**Required force to lift:**

$$F_{\text{req}} \;=\; m \cdot g \;=\; 5 \times 9.81 \;=\; 49.05\ \text{N}$$

**Actual safety factor achieved:**

$$n_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{eff}}}{F_{\text{req}}} \;=\; \frac{261.4}{49.05} \;\approx\; 5.33$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $A_{\text{eff}} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $F_{\text{theor}} = 326.7\ \text{N}$
- (ii) $n_{\text{actual}} \approx 5.33$ — since $5.33 \gg 2$, the design is **acceptable** with a large safety margin.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Magnetic & Vacuum Grippers**

### 🧲 Magnetic Grippers
- **Force equation (fundamental):** $F_{\text{mag}} = \dfrac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}}$ — derived from Maxwell stress / virtual work.
- **Electromagnet force:** $F \approx \dfrac{\mu_{0} A N^{2} I^{2}}{2 g^{2}}$ — **inversely proportional to gap squared**.
- **Permeability constant:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$.
- **Three types:** Permanent (no power, fail-safe hold), Electromagnetic (continuous current, fast release), Hybrid/EPM (pulse-controlled, energy-efficient).
- **Best suited for:** Ferromagnetic (steel, iron) flat or curved surfaces; high-temp steel handling; scrap sorting; furnace tending.
- **Key limitation:** Cannot grip non-ferrous materials (aluminium, copper, plastic).

### 💨 Vacuum Grippers
- **Force equation (fundamental):** $F_{\text{vac}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$.
- **Atmospheric pressure:** $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ — the **upper limit** of vacuum force.
- **Design equation:** $F_{\text{usable}} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot \eta}{n}$ — always use safety factor $n$.
- **Vacuum sources:** Vacuum pump (high vacuum), Venturi ejector (most common in plants), blower (porous materials), Bernoulli (contactless).
- **Effective area:** $A_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D_{\text{eff}}^{2}$.
- **Best suited for:** Flat, smooth, non-porous surfaces — glass, sheet metal, PCB, plastic trays, cardboard (with blower).
- **Key limitations:** Porous surfaces leak; rough surfaces break seal; maximum force capped by $P_{\text{atm}}$.
- **Safety factor rules of thumb:** $n = 2$ for horizontal pick, $n = 4$–$5$ for vertical/inverted pick (because friction coefficient $\mu$ governs hold).

### 🔑 Cross-Comparison Keys (Frequently Tested)
1. **Power-loss behaviour:** PM = holds (safe); EM = drops (unsafe without backup); Vacuum = drops.
2. **Energy efficiency ranking:** EPM > PM > EM (for magnetic); Venturi > Pump > Blower (in different contexts).
3. **Material compatibility:** Magnetic ↔ ferromagnetic; Vacuum ↔ non-porous; mechanical fingers ↔ everything else.
4. **Force scaling:** Magnetic $\propto$ pole area, vacuum $\propto$ cup area; both increase linearly with area.
5. **Gap sensitivity:** Magnetic = severe ($1/g^{2}$); Vacuum = weak (mainly affects seal integrity).

### 📌 Numerical Problem-Solving Template
1. **Identify gripper type** from the problem statement.
2. **Write the governing equation** from memory.
3. **Convert all units to SI** (m, m², Pa, A, T).
4. **Plug in values**, compute step-by-step.
5. **Apply safety factor / efficiency** in vacuum problems.
6. **Comment on acceptability** of the design (e.g., "since $n = 5.33 > 2$, design is safe").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Passive Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Active and Passive Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of End Effectors and Grippers

An **end effector** is the device attached to the wrist of a robot manipulator that interacts directly with the environment to perform the intended task. Among end effectors, the **gripper** (or gripper-type end effector) is the most common tool used to grasp, hold, and release workpieces.

The KTU 2024 scheme broadly classifies grippers into two functional categories based on their **source of gripping force**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Gripper:** A gripper that requires an **external energy source** (electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic) to generate, maintain, and release the clamping force on the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passive Gripper:** A gripper that holds the workpiece using **intrinsic mechanical features** such as geometry, gravity, spring preload, or mechanical compliance, without requiring any active actuation force during the grasping action.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are holding a hot cup of coffee:

- **Active Gripper Analogy:** Your hand actively wraps around the cup and squeezes with muscle force. The grip force comes from your body expending energy. If you stop squeezing, the cup falls. → This is exactly how a **pneumatic jaw gripper** works: air pressure actively clamps the jaws.

- **Passive Gripper Analogy:** Now imagine placing the same cup into a coffee cup holder with a fixed cavity in your car. The cup simply sits in the holder by gravity and is constrained by the holder's geometry. No active squeezing is required. → This is similar to a **caging gripper** that surrounds the object.

## 1.3 Classification Hierarchy

The classification of grippers based on actuation philosophy can be visually represented as:

**Grippers**
- **Active Grippers** (Energy-driven)
  - Mechanical (cam, screw, lever, spring-return)
  - Pneumatic (single-acting, double-acting)
  - Hydraulic (high-force applications)
  - Electric / Servo-driven
  - Magnetic (electromagnets)
  - Vacuum (suction cups)
- **Passive Grippers** (Geometry/Compliance-driven)
  - Caging grippers (mechanical enclosures)
  - Hooks and latches
  - Spring-loaded nest fixtures
  - Compliant fingers (flexure-based)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Gripper Selection:** The **Grasping Force (N)** required is computed based on the coefficient of friction ($\mu$) between jaw and workpiece, the workpiece mass ($m$), and the safety factor ($S$). The minimum grasping force is given by **$F_g \geq S \cdot W / (2\mu)$** where $W = mg$ is the weight of the object.

## 1.4 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations
