The general inverse kinematics problem for a 6-DOF serial manipulator seeks a 6-dimensional joint vector $q = [q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6]^T$ such that the end-effector attains a desired pose $X_d = [p_x, p_y, p_z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$. The forward kinematics mapping is:

$$X = f(q) = [f_1(q_1,...,q_6), f_2(q_1,...,q_6), ..., f_6(q_1,...,q_6)]^T$$

**Properties:**

1. **Existence:** A solution exists iff $X_d$ lies in the reachable workspace $\mathcal{W}_r$, defined as $\mathcal{W}_r = \{X \mid \exists\,q \in \mathcal{R}, X = f(q)\}$. For a 6-DOF arm with link lengths $l_1, l_2, l_3$, the reachable workspace volume is bounded by the maximum reach $l_1 + l_2 + l_3$ and the minimum reach $\vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$ (when applicable).

2. **Uniqueness (Multiplicity):** For a 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist, up to **8 distinct solutions** exist per reachable pose (4 elbow-up/down × 2 wrist-flip combinations). The general upper bound is $2^n$ for $n$ revolute joints in some configurations.

3. **Solvability:** Closed-form solutions exist under special geometric conditions:
   - **Pieper's criterion**: When three consecutive joint axes intersect at a common point.
   - **Spherical wrist decomposition**: Position solved by first 3 joints, orientation by last 3.
   
   Otherwise, numerical iterative methods (Newton-Raphson, Jacobian-based) are employed.

**Part (b) — Newton-Raphson IK Derivation (7 Marks)**

Define the error vector $e(q) = X_d - f(q)$ and the scalar cost function $E(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^T e$.

Step 1: Expand $f(q)$ around current estimate $q_k$ using first-order Taylor series:

$$f(q_k + \Delta q) \approx f(q_k) + J(q_k)\,\Delta q$$

Step 2: Set $f(q_k + \Delta q) = X_d$ and rearrange:

$$J(q_k)\,\Delta q = X_d - f(q_k) = e(q_k)$$

Step 3: Solve for $\Delta q$ assuming $J$ is square and invertible:

$$\Delta q = J^{-1}(q_k)\,e(q_k)$$

Step 4: Update the joint estimate:

$$q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha\,J^{-1}(q_k)\,\bigl[X_d - f(q_k)\bigr]$$

where $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the step-size (often set to 1 for full Newton step).

**Convergence Properties:**
- **Quadratic convergence** near the solution (error squares each iteration).
- Requires a **good initial guess** $q_0$ for global convergence; otherwise may converge to a local minimum or diverge.
- The iteration terminates when $\Vert e(q_k)\Vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$) or $k > k_{\max}$.

**Limitations:**
1. **Singular Jacobian** $\det(J) = 0$ causes $J^{-1}$ to be undefined — algorithm fails at singular configurations.
2. **No guarantee of global convergence** — sensitive to initial guess.
3. **Computationally expensive** — Jacobian recomputed each iteration, requires $n^2 + n$ evaluations.
4. **Constraint handling** is difficult; joint limits must be enforced externally.
5. For redundant manipulators ($n > 6$), $J$ is non-square and direct inversion is impossible.

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Warning
> - **0.5 mark deduction** if you do not show the Jacobian matrix $J(q_k)$ appearing in the iteration formula.
> - **0.5 mark deduction** if you forget the step-size parameter $\alpha$.
> - **1 mark deduction** if you skip the Taylor series expansion step and jump directly to the update formula.
> - Always state the **convergence criterion** $\Vert e(q_k) \Vert < \epsilon$ — this is a frequent 1-mark loss.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks]** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast closed-form and numerical methods for solving the inverse kinematics problem. Under what conditions is each method preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of kinematic singularities in robotic manipulators. With reference to the Jacobian, derive the damped least-squares solution that handles singularities. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Closed-Form vs Numerical IK (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Closed-Form IK | Numerical IK |
|---|---|---|
| **Solution form** | Explicit algebraic expressions | Iterative refinement |
| **Computation time** | Constant (microseconds) | Variable, depends on convergence |
| **Accuracy** | Exact (up to floating-point) | Approximate, tolerance-based |
| **All solutions** | Yes (in principle) | Only one (depends on initial guess) |
| **Joint limits** | Hard to incorporate | Easy to include via penalty functions |
| **Applicability** | Only manipulators with special geometry (e.g., Pieper) | Any manipulator |
| **Stability** | Always stable | May diverge near singularities |
| **Examples** | PUMA 560, SCARA, Stanford arm | General 7-DOF arms, humanoids |

**When to use each:**
- **Closed-form** is preferred in **real-time industrial control** (high-speed pick-and-place, welding) where millisecond cycle times are critical.
- **Numerical** is preferred for **redundant manipulators**, **humanoids**, and **complex geometries** where closed-form solutions do not exist or are too cumbersome.

**Part (b) — Singularities and Damped Least Squares (7 Marks)**

**Definition of Singularity:** A kinematic singularity is a configuration $q_s$ at which the Jacobian matrix $J(q_s)$ loses full rank, i.e.:

$$\text{rank}(J(q_s)) < \min(m, n)$$

For a square Jacobian ($m = n = 6$), this is equivalent to $\det(J(q_s)) = 0$.

**Types of Singularities:**
1. **Boundary singularities** — occur at the boundary of the reachable workspace (arm fully stretched or folded).
2. **Interior singularities** — occur inside the workspace due to alignment of two or more joint axes.
3. **Wrist singularities** — occur when joints 4 and 6 align, causing loss of one rotational DOF.

**At a singularity**, the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom — the end-effector cannot move in certain directions regardless of joint actuation.

**Derivation of Damped Least Squares (Damped Pseudo-Inverse):**

To overcome the singularity-induced blow-up of $J^{+}$, we add a damping term $\lambda^2 I$ to the normal equations:

$$\text{Minimize} \quad \Phi(\Delta q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert J\Delta q - \Delta X\Vert^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^2\Vert\Delta q\Vert^2$$

where $\lambda \in [0.01, 0.1]$ is the damping factor.

Differentiating with respect to $\Delta q$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial \Delta q} = J^T(J\Delta q - \Delta X) + \lambda^2 \Delta q = 0$$

Collecting terms:

$$(J^T J + \lambda^2 I)\Delta q = J^T \Delta X$$

Solving by inversion (now non-singular because $\lambda^2 I$ ensures positive-definiteness):

$$\boxed{\Delta q = (J^T J + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} J^T \Delta X}$$

**Key Properties:**
- When $J$ is full-rank and far from singularity, $\lambda^2 I$ is negligible → behaves like standard pseudo-inverse.
- Near singularity, $\lambda^2 I$ dominates → joint velocities remain bounded at the cost of tracking accuracy.
- The trade-off is controlled by $\lambda$: large $\lambda$ = stability, small $\lambda$ = accuracy.
- This is also known as the **Levenberg-Marquardt** method in robotics literature.

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Pitfall
> - Always **define singularity with the rank condition**, not just "where the Jacobian is zero."
> - In the DLS derivation, you must include the **second (damping) term** in the cost function — losing this drops 2 marks.
> - Mention at least **one physical consequence** of singularity (loss of DOF), not only the math.
> - Forgetting the damping factor's typical range ($\lambda \approx 0.01$ to $0.1$) costs 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 IK
> - [x] IK = given $X_d$, find $q$; mathematically $q = f^{-1}(X_d)$.
> - [x] A solution exists only if $X_d \in \mathcal{W}_r$ (reachable workspace).
> - [x] For 6-DOF with spherical wrist, up to **8 solutions** exist per pose.
> - [x] The Jacobian $J(q)$ linearizes the IK problem: $\dot{X} = J(q)\dot{q}$.
> - [x] **Closed-form IK** gives exact solutions; **Numerical IK** uses iterative refinement.
> - [x] **Newton-Raphson IK**: $q_{k+1} = q_k + J^{-1}(q_k)[X_d - f(q_k)]$.
> - [x] **Pseudo-inverse IK**: $\Delta q = J^+ \Delta X = J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}\Delta X$ for redundant arms.
> - [x] **Damped Least Squares IK**: $\Delta q = (J^T J + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} J^T \Delta X$ — robust to singularities.
> - [x] **Singularity** = $\text{rank}(J) < \min(m,n)$ or $\det(J) = 0$ for square Jacobian.
> - [x] **Manipulability** $w = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ measures dexterity.
> - [x] **Null-space projection** $(I - J^+ J)$ enables secondary objectives in redundant systems.
> - [x] Always check **joint limits** $q_i^{\min} \le q_i \le q_i^{\max}$ after solving.
> - [x] **Pieper's criterion** (3 intersecting axes) guarantees closed-form solvability.
> - [x] **Convergence criterion**: $\Vert e(q_k)\Vert < \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \approx 10^{-6}$.
> - [x] **Step-size $\alpha \in (0,1]$** in Newton-Raphson controls convergence speed.
> - [x] Closed-form is preferred for industrial real-time control; numerical is preferred for redundant/humanoid arms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Properties of Solutions to the Inverse Kinematics Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Properties of Solutions to the Inverse Kinematics Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** problem is the mathematical process of computing the **joint variables (angles for revolute joints, displacements for prismatic joints)** of a robotic manipulator given the **desired position and orientation** of its end-effector in the Cartesian (task) space.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **General Properties of Solutions** refer to the fundamental mathematical and geometric characteristics that govern how the IK problem behaves *in general* — irrespective of the specific manipulator architecture. These properties determine whether a solution exists, how many solutions exist, how they can be computed, and how they behave under various task constraints.

The forward kinematics maps joint space to Cartesian space:

$$^{\text{Base}}T_{\text{Tool}} = {^{\text{Base}}T_{1}} \,(\theta_{1}) \cdot {^{1}T_{2}}(\theta_{2}) \cdots {^{n-1}T_{n}}(\theta_{n})$$

The inverse kinematics seeks the inverse mapping:

$$\boldsymbol{\Theta} = f^{-1}(x), \quad \text{where } x \in \mathbb{R}^{m}, \quad \Theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

For a general 6-DOF manipulator working in 3D space, $m = n = 6$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The "general properties" specifically cover (a) existence of solutions, (b) multiple solutions, (c) closed-form vs. numerical solution methods, (d) workspace concepts (reachable vs. dexterous), (e) repeatability, and (f) kinematic decoupling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a long articulated fire-truck ladder. A bystander tells you: *"I need the bucket (end-effector) to be exactly at this point (x, y, z) and pointing in this direction (roll, pitch, yaw)."* You, as the operator, must now figure out **how to bend every joint** of the ladder so the bucket lands there.

This is exactly the inverse kinematics problem.

Now, the **"general properties of solutions"** answer these questions about your task:

1. **Existence:** *"Is this point actually reachable by your ladder?"* — If the point is behind a wall (outside the reachable workspace), no combination of joint angles can put the bucket there.

2. **Multiple Solutions:** *"How many ways can you get the bucket there?"* — You might bend your elbow up or down (like your own arm reaching for a glass). Both put the hand at the same spot, but the body posture is different. The IK problem often has *multiple* valid joint configurations.

3. **Closed-Form vs. Numerical:** *"Can you solve this with a formula, or do you need to guess-and-check (iterate) until you're close?"* — Some ladder designs allow a direct algebraic solution; others force an iterative search.

4. **Repeatability:** *"If you keep making small corrections, will you drift?"* — A numerically-stable IK solver must not accumulate error over many cycles.

5. **Workspace Boundaries:** *"Can you reach every point in a small sphere around your target without changing orientation?"* — If yes, that point is in the **dexterous workspace**; if only by changing orientation, it's in the **reachable workspace**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Standard KTU Metrics:**
> - **Reachable Workspace:** The set of all positions the end-effector can attain in *at least one* orientation.
> - **Dexterous Workspace (Dexterous Volume):** The set of all positions the end-effector can attain in *all possible* orientations.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent joint variables, typically equal to the dimension of the task space for full 6-DOF spatial manipulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six General Properties of IK Solutions

The following six properties are mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus and must be memorized for both Module tests and the End Semester Examination (ESE).

#### **Property 1 — Existence of Solutions**

A solution to the IK problem **exists if and only if** the desired end-effector pose lies within the **reachable workspace** of the manipulator.

$$\exists \, \Theta \iff x \in W_R$$

where $W_R$ denotes the reachable workspace.

- If $x \notin W_R$, the problem has **no solution** — physically impossible to achieve.
- The workspace is bounded by joint limits and link lengths:

$$\theta_{i,\min} \leq \theta_{i} \leq \theta_{i,\max}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

For a revolute joint: $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ nominally; with limits, $\theta \in [\theta_{\min}, \theta_{\max}]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Existence determines whether a planned trajectory is even feasible. Industrial path planners always verify the existence condition before motion.

#### **Property 2 — Multiple Solutions (Non-Uniqueness)**

Unlike the forward kinematics problem (which is unique), the IK problem is **generally non-unique** — there may be 0, 1, 2, … up to a finite maximum number of solutions.

The maximum number of solutions for various manipulator types:

| Manipulator Type | Max No. of IK Solutions |
|---|---|
| 3-DOF planar (RRR) | $\mathbf{2}$ |
| 3-DOF spatial with intersecting axes | $\mathbf{2}$ |
| 6-DOF with spherical wrist (decoupled) | $\mathbf{8}$ (4 arm × 2 wrist) |
| General 6-DOF manipulator | up to $\mathbf{16}$ |

**Reason for multiple solutions:** Equations are nonlinear transcendental (sin, cos) and have multiple algebraic roots.

#### **Property 3 — Closed-Form Solutions**

A **closed-form solution** expresses each joint variable as an explicit algebraic function of the desired end-effector pose:

$$\theta_{i} = f_{i}(x_{d}), \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n$$

Such solutions are obtained using either:

1. **Algebraic (closed-form) methods:** Manipulate the kinematic equations until joint variables are isolated.
2. **Geometric methods:** Decompose the spatial geometry into planar sub-problems and solve with trigonometry.

**Sufficient condition for closed-form solvability (Pieper's criterion, 1968):**
A 6-DOF manipulator has a closed-form solution if **any three consecutive joint axes intersect at a common point** (i.e., the manipulator has a spherical wrist).

#### **Property 4 — Numerical (Iterative) Solutions**

When a closed-form solution does not exist, the IK is solved numerically. The general form is:

$$\Theta_{k+1} = \Theta_{k} + \alpha_{k} \, J^{-1}(\Theta_{k}) \, e_{k}$$

where:
- $\alpha_{k}$ is the **step size**,
- $J(\Theta_{k})$ is the **Jacobian matrix**,
- $e_{k} = x_{d} - f(\Theta_{k})$ is the **position error vector**.

**Common numerical methods:**

| Method | Order of Convergence | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| Newton–Raphson | Quadratic | Fast but needs good initial guess |
| Broyden's Method | Superlinear | Jacobian update, computationally cheaper |
| Cyclic Coordinate Descent (CCD) | Linear | Heuristic, fast for redundant manipulators |
| Jacobian Transpose | Linear | Stable but slower convergence |
| Damped Least Squares (Levenberg–Marquardt) | Variable | Handles singularities robustly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Numerical methods using the Jacobian — particularly the **pseudoinverse** formulation — are a high-weightage topic.

#### **Property 5 — Repeatability**

**Repeatability** quantifies how precisely the manipulator can return to a previously-taught pose. It depends on:
- Resolution of joint encoders,
- Mechanical backlash,
- Control loop precision.

For numerical IK solvers, **algorithmic repeatability** requires the solver to converge to the *same* solution each time for the *same* target — this is not guaranteed for iterative methods with multiple roots.

#### **Property 6 — Kinematic Decoupling**

For a 6-DOF manipulator with a **spherical wrist** (the last three joint axes intersect at a common point, the wrist center), the IK problem decouples into two independent sub-problems:

1. **Position Inverse Kinematics:** Compute the first three joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ such that the wrist center $O_c$ reaches the desired position $p_d$.

2. **Orientation Inverse Kinematics:** Compute the last three joint angles $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ such that the end-effector attains the desired orientation $R_d$.

**Decoupling equations:**

$$p_{\text{wrist}} = p_{d} - d_{6} \, R_{d} \, \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R^{3}_{6}(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) = (R^{0}_{3})^{-1} \cdot R_{d}$$

This is the foundation of the **Khatib–Quasi-Static** and **Spong–Hutchinson decoupling** approach used in textbooks.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Domain / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics | $T_{\text{EE}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {^{i-1}T_{i}}(\theta_i)$ | $T \in SE(3)$ |
| 2 | Inverse Kinematics Statement | $\Theta = f^{-1}(x)$ | Joint → Cartesian map |
| 3 | Existence Condition | $\Theta$ exists iff $x \in W_R$ | Workspace dependent |
| 4 | Max Solutions (6-DOF) | $\leq 16$ (general), $\leq 8$ (spherical wrist) | Polynomial degree bound |
| 5 | Closed-Form Condition | 3 consecutive axes intersect (Pieper) | Sufficient, not necessary |
| 6 | Jacobian-based Update | $\Delta \Theta = J^{\dagger} \Delta x$ | $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Damped Least Squares | $\Delta \Theta = J^{T}(JJ^{T} + \lambda^{2}I)^{-1} \Delta x$ | $\lambda$ = damping factor |
| 8 | Wrist Center Position | $p_c = p_d - d_6 R_d \hat{z}$ | Decoupling identity |
| 9 | Singularity Condition | $\det(J(\Theta)) = 0$ | Boundary of workspace |
| 10 | Reachable Workspace Volume | $V_R \leq \frac{4}{3}\pi(a_2 + a_3)^3$ (for 3R planar arm approximation) | Bounds only |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Welding Robots:** Closed-form IK is mandatory because trajectories are planned offline and executed at high speed. ABB and KUKA use spherical-wrist manipulators precisely to enable closed-form IK.
- **Humanoid Robotics (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas):** Redundant manipulators require numerical IK with optimization (e.g., minimizing joint torque).
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Closed-form IK guarantees deterministic behavior for FDA-approved repeatable procedures.
- **Space Manipulators:** Numerical IK with damping handles uncertain dynamics and external contact forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern robot manufacturers almost universally design 6-DOF arms with a spherical wrist so that the **closed-form IK with decoupling** becomes possible — this is a deliberate design choice driven by the *properties of solutions* studied here.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of IK Solutions for a 6-DOF Manipulator

We rigorously derive why a general 6-DOF manipulator can have **at most 16 solutions**.

**Step 1:** The position and orientation of the end-effector can be expressed as 6 nonlinear equations in 6 joint variables:

$$x_{i}(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_6) = x_{i}^{d}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, 6$$

The position equations (first three) involve products of sines and cosines.

**Step 2:** Square and add the three position equations to **eliminate trigonometric coupling**. This is a classical trick — for a 2R planar arm:

$$x^2 + y^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2a_1 a_2 \cos(\theta_2)$$

Solving for $\theta_2$:

$$\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$$

This gives **2 solutions** for $\theta_2$ (positive and negative cosine branches).

**Step 3:** For a 3R planar manipulator, applying this trick yields a **polynomial of degree 4** in $\tan(\theta_3/2)$ — hence **up to 4 solutions**.

**Step 4:** For a general 6-DOF manipulator, applying the squaring-and-adding trick sequentially produces a polynomial of degree **16** in some tangent-half-angle substitution. By the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**:

$$\text{Maximum number of roots} = \text{Degree of polynomial} = \mathbf{16}$$

Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\max} &= 16 \quad \text{(general 6-DOF)} \\
N_{\max} &= 8 \quad \text{(spherical wrist — decoupling halves it)} \\
N_{\max} &= 2 \quad \text{(3-DOF planar RRR)} \\
N_{\max} &= 1 \quad \text{(3-DOF Cartesian, fully constrained)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 16?** The 16 upper bound arises from the fact that squaring the kinematic equations produces a 16th-degree polynomial in a tangent-half-angle variable. Algebra can guarantee no more than 16 real roots.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Newton–Raphson Iterative IK Solution

We derive the iterative formula used for numerical IK.

**Step 1:** Define the position error vector:

$$e(\Theta) = x_{d} - f(\Theta)$$

We seek $\Theta^{*}$ such that $e(\Theta^{*}) = 0$.

**Step 2:** Linearize using a first-order Taylor expansion around the current estimate $\Theta_{k}$:

$$f(\Theta_{k} + \Delta\Theta) \approx f(\Theta_{k}) + J(\Theta_{k}) \, \Delta\Theta$$

where $J(\Theta_{k})$ is the **Jacobian matrix** with entries:

$$J_{ij} = \frac{\partial f_{i}}{\partial \theta_{j}}$$

**Step 3:** Set the linearized approximation equal to the desired pose:

$$x_{d} \approx f(\Theta_{k}) + J(\Theta_{k}) \, \Delta\Theta$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\Delta\Theta$:

$$J(\Theta_{k}) \, \Delta\Theta = x_{d} - f(\Theta_{k})$$

For a square, non-singular Jacobian:

$$\Delta\Theta = J^{-1}(\Theta_{k}) \, [x_{d} - f(\Theta_{k})]$$

**Step 5:** Update rule:

$$\Theta_{k+1} = \Theta_{k} + \alpha_{k} \, J^{-1}(\Theta_{k}) \, [x_{d} - f(\Theta_{k})]$$

where $\alpha_{k} \in (0, 1]$ is the step size (set to 1 for pure Newton–Raphson).

**Step 6:** For redundant manipulators (where $J$ is rectangular, $m < n$), use the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse**:

$$\Delta\Theta = J^{\dagger} \Delta x = J^{T}(J J^{T})^{-1} \, \Delta x$$

This minimizes $\|\Delta\Theta\|^2$ subject to matching the Cartesian increment $\Delta x$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kinematic Decoupling for Spherical-Wrist Manipulators

Consider a 6-DOF manipulator where the last three joint axes ($z_4, z_5, z_6$) intersect at the **wrist center** $O_c$. Let $d_6$ be the distance along $z_6$ from the wrist center to the end-effector tip.

**Step 1:** Write the end-effector position $p$ as the sum of two vectors:

$$p = p_{\text{wrist}} + d_6 \, R \, \hat{z}_{\text{tool}}$$

where $R$ is the end-effector rotation matrix and $\hat{z}_{\text{tool}} = [0, 0, 1]^{T}$.

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate the wrist-center position:

$$p_{\text{wrist}} = p - d_6 \, R \, \hat{z}_{\text{tool}}$$

$$p_{\text{wrist}} = \begin{bmatrix} p_x \\ p_y \\ p_z \end{bmatrix} - d_6 \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** The first three joint angles $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ are determined solely by $p_{\text{wrist}}$, since the kinematic chain from base to wrist center depends only on these angles.

**Step 4:** For the orientation, the rotation matrix from base to end-effector is:

$$R = R_{0}^{3}(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) \cdot R_{3}^{6}(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6)$$

Therefore:

$$R_{3}^{6}(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) = (R_{0}^{3})^{-1} \cdot R$$

This decouples the problem into two independent sub-problems, each with at most 2 solutions, yielding **at most 4 × 2 = 8 total solutions**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Criterion | Closed-Form | Numerical |
|---|---|---|
| Existence of formula? | Yes (spherical wrist) | Always possible |
| Computational cost | Constant (per solution) | Iterative, depends on tolerance |
| Solution uniqueness | Returns all branches | Returns one (depends on initial guess) |
| Near singularities | Robust | Fails (Jacobian singular) |
| Real-time performance | Excellent | Variable |
| Real-world use (KTU) | Most 6-DOF industrial arms | Redundant/parallel robots |

> [!NOTE]
> **General Property Recap for Examinations:** The IK problem is (1) generally nonlinear, (2) may not have a closed-form solution, (3) typically has multiple solutions, (4) may have no solution outside the workspace, and (5) requires careful handling of singularities when solved numerically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — IK Solution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired End-Effector Pose x_d] --> B{Existence Check}
    B -- Outside Workspace --> C[NO SOLUTION - Task Infeasible]
    B -- Inside Workspace --> D{Manipulator Type}
    D -- Spherical Wrist --> E[Closed-Form IK via Decoupling]
    D -- General Architecture --> F[Numerical IK via Jacobian]
    E --> G[Position Sub-Problem theta1, theta2, theta3]
    G --> H[Orientation Sub-Problem theta4, theta5, theta6]
    H --> I[Multiple Solutions - up to 8]
    F --> J[Iterative Update theta_k+1]
    J --> K{Convergence Test}
    K -- Pass --> L[Final Joint Angles]
    K -- Fail --> J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Workspace Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Task Space] --> B[Reachable Workspace W_R]
    B --> C[Dexterous Workspace W_D]
    C --> D[Singularity-Free Workspace]
    A1[Property Definitions] --> A
    A1 --> B1[At least 1 orientation reachable]
    B1 --> C1[All orientations reachable]
    C1 --> D1[Jacobian non-singular]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Form vs. Numerical IK Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inverse Kinematics Problem] --> B{Pieper's Criterion Satisfied?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Closed-Form Algebraic/Geometric Method]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Damped Least Squares Iterative Solver]
    C --> E[Solution Branch Selection]
    E --> F[Choose Solution Closest to Current Configuration]
    D --> G[Iterate Until Norm of Error below Tolerance]
    F --> H[Output Joint Angles]
    G --> H
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Solution Counting Tree for 6-DOF Spherical-Wrist Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[6-DOF Pose Specified] --> B[Position Problem: 3R Arm]
    B --> B1[Solution 1 - Elbow Up]
    B --> B2[Solution 2 - Elbow Down]
    A --> C[Orientation Problem: Spherical Wrist]
    C --> C1[Solution A - Wrist Flip]
    C --> C2[Solution B - No Wrist Flip]
    B1 --> D1[Final Config 1A]
    B1 --> D2[Final Config 1B]
    B2 --> D3[Final Config 2A]
    B2 --> D4[Final Config 2B]
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> D4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU board examinations, drawing such block diagrams (especially the IK pipeline and workspace hierarchy) earns **method marks** even if the final numerical answer contains minor arithmetic slips.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the general properties that characterize the solutions of the inverse kinematics problem.**

**Model Answer:**

The general properties of inverse kinematics (IK) solutions are:

1. **Existence:** A solution exists if and only if the desired end-effector pose lies within the **reachable workspace** of the manipulator.

2. **Multiple Solutions:** The IK problem is generally non-unique; a manipulator may have up to **16 solutions** (general 6-DOF) or up to **8 solutions** (spherical wrist).

3. **Closed-Form vs. Numerical:** Solutions can be obtained either through algebraic/geometric closed-form methods (when Pieper's criterion holds) or through numerical iterative methods (Newton–Raphson, damped least squares).

4. **Kinematic Decoupling:** For spherical-wrist manipulators, the IK problem decomposes into independent position and orientation sub-problems.

5. **Workspace Dependence:** The reachable and dexterous workspaces define the domain of feasible IK solutions.

6. **Singularities:** Solutions may fail or become ill-conditioned when the Jacobian determinant vanishes.

*(All six points together with one-line justification each — 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms: (a) Reachable workspace, (b) Dexterous workspace. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Reachable Workspace ($W_R$):** The set of all positions that the end-effector can attain in **at least one orientation**. It is a function of joint limits and link lengths.

- **(b) Dexterous Workspace ($W_D$):** The set of all positions that the end-effector can attain in **all possible orientations**. It is a strict subset of the reachable workspace.

- **Relationship:**

$$W_D \subseteq W_R$$

In other words, the dexterous workspace is always a subset of (or equal to) the reachable workspace. For example, a point at the very end of a 3R planar arm's reach is in $W_R$ but not in $W_D$ because the orientation is fixed.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B (A) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **closed-form and numerical solution techniques** for the inverse kinematics problem. Compare their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain **kinematic decoupling** as applied to a 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist. Derive the position equation used for decoupling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Closed-Form Solutions:**

A closed-form solution expresses each joint variable directly as an algebraic function of the desired end-effector pose. There are two main approaches:

1. **Algebraic Method:** Manipulate the homogeneous transformation equation
   $T_{\text{desired}} = A_1(\theta_1) A_2(\theta_2) \cdots A_n(\theta_n)$
   to isolate individual joint variables using matrix inversions and trigonometric identities.

2. **Geometric Method:** Decompose the spatial geometry of the manipulator into simpler planar sub-problems. Use trigonometric laws of cosines and sines in each plane.

**Merits of Closed-Form:**
- Fast computation (constant time per solution),
- Returns all possible solution branches,
- Suitable for real-time control,
- Repeatable and deterministic.

**Numerical Solutions:**

Used when closed-form is unavailable. The most common approach uses the **Jacobian matrix**:

$$J(\Theta) = \frac{\partial f(\Theta)}{\partial \Theta}$$

The iterative update is:

$$\Theta_{k+1} = \Theta_{k} + \alpha_{k} J^{-1}(\Theta_{k}) [x_{d} - f(\Theta_{k})]$$

Variants include Newton–Raphson, Broyden, and the **Damped Least Squares** method:

$$\Delta\Theta = J^{T}(J J^{T} + \lambda^{2} I)^{-1} \Delta x$$

The damping factor $\lambda$ ensures robustness near singularities.

**Demerits of Numerical Methods:**
- Iterative — computation cost depends on convergence,
- Needs a good initial guess,
- May converge to local minima or fail near singularities,
- Returns only one solution per run.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Closed-Form | Numerical |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Constant | Iterative |
| Completeness | All solutions | One solution |
| Accuracy | Exact (modulo numerical precision) | Approximate |
| Robustness | Robust everywhere | Fails near singularities |
| Real-time | Excellent | Variable |

*(7 marks for complete discussion with formulas and comparison.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Kinematic Decoupling Block Diagram:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Desired Pose: Position p_d, Orientation R_d                  │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
            ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
            │  Compute Wrist Center    │
            │  p_c = p_d - d_6 R_d ẑ   │
            └────────────┬─────────────┘
                         │
        ┌────────────────┼─────────────────┐
        │                │                 │
        ▼                ▼                 ▼
┌───────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
│ Position IK   │  │   (p_c feeds │  │ Orientation IK    │
│  θ₁, θ₂, θ₃   │  │    here)     │  │  θ₄, θ₅, θ₆       │
└───────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └────────┬─────────┘
        │                 │                   │
        └────────────────┬┴───────────────────┘
                         ▼
              Joint Variables Output
```

**Derivation:**

For a spherical-wrist manipulator, the last three joint axes intersect at the wrist center $O_c$. The position of the end-effector is:

$$p = p_c + d_6 R_d \hat{z}$$

where $R_d$ is the desired end-effector rotation matrix and $\hat{z} = [0, 0, 1]^T$ is the tool's approach vector.

Rearranging:

$$p_c = p_d - d_6 \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

The first three joint angles are computed from $p_c$ (position sub-problem), and the last three from:

$$R_3^6(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) = (R_0^3)^{-1} R_d$$

This separates the 6-DOF IK into two tractable 3-DOF sub-problems.

*(7 marks for diagram + derivation + explanation.)*

---

#### **Question B (B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **existence and multiple-solution properties** of inverse kinematics. Why is the IK problem generally non-unique? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Newton–Raphson method** for solving the IK problem iteratively. State one limitation of this method and how it is overcome in practice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Existence Property:**

A solution to the IK problem exists if and only if the desired end-effector pose lies in the reachable workspace:

$$\exists \, \Theta: \; f(\Theta) = x_d \iff x_d \in W_R$$

- If $x_d \notin W_R$, the problem has **no solution** (geometric infeasibility).
- The reachable workspace is determined by link lengths and joint limits.

For a revolute joint $i$:

$$\theta_{i,\min} \leq \theta_i \leq \theta_{i,\max}$$

For a prismatic joint $i$:

$$d_{i,\min} \leq d_i \leq d_{i,\max}$$

**Multiple-Solution Property:**

Unlike forward kinematics (always unique for a given $\Theta$), the IK problem is **non-unique** because:

1. The kinematic equations are **transcendental** (contain sines and cosines), giving multiple algebraic roots.
2. Trigonometric functions are periodic: $\sin\theta = \sin(\pi - \theta)$ — giving **two solutions per joint** in many cases.
3. Manipulators with redundant degrees of freedom have **infinite** solutions.

The maximum number of solutions:

| Type | Max Solutions |
|---|---|
| 3R planar | 2 |
| 6-DOF spherical wrist | 8 |
| 6-DOF general | 16 |

This non-uniqueness arises because eliminating transcendental terms by squaring-and-adding produces a polynomial of degree 16 in $\tan(\theta_i/2)$.

*(7 marks for full coverage of existence and multiple-solution properties with mathematical justification.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Newton–Raphson Method for IK:**

Given a desired end-effector pose $x_d$ and an initial joint estimate $\Theta_0$, iterate:

$$\Theta_{k+1} = \Theta_{k} + J^{-1}(\Theta_k) [x_d - f(\Theta_k)]$$

where:
- $f(\Theta)$ is the forward kinematics function,
- $J(\Theta) = \partial f / \partial \Theta$ is the Jacobian,
- $J^{-1}$ is the inverse Jacobian (only for square non-singular Jacobians).

The iteration stops when:

$$\| x_d - f(\Theta_k) \| < \epsilon$$

for a chosen tolerance $\epsilon > 0$.

**Convergence Rate:** Quadratic (Newton–Raphson is a second-order method).

**Limitation:**

The Newton–Raphson method **fails near singularities** where $\det(J) = 0$. At such configurations, $J^{-1}$ does not exist or becomes numerically unstable, causing the joint velocities to grow unbounded for a finite Cartesian velocity — the manipulator "loses" a degree of freedom momentarily.

**Practical Remedy — Damped Least Squares (Levenberg–Marquardt):**

$$\Delta\Theta = J^{T}(J J^{T} + \lambda^{2} I)^{-1} \Delta x$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the damping factor. This regularizes the inverse, trading off accuracy for robustness near singularities.

Another remedy is to use the **pseudoinverse** $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ (Moore–Penrose), which minimizes $\|\Delta\Theta\|$ and provides least-norm joint velocities.

*(7 marks for derivation, limitation, and remedy.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing reachable and dexterous workspace** — the dexterous workspace is a *subset* of the reachable workspace. Writing the reverse loses a mark.
>
> 2. **Stating "IK has unique solution"** — the IK problem is *generally* non-unique. Only special cases (Cartesian, SCARA with constrained end-effector orientation) are unique.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Pieper's criterion** — when asked about closed-form solutions, students often miss that the criterion is *sufficient but not necessary*. Some non-spherical-wrist manipulators also have closed-form solutions.
>
> 4. **Mixing up forward and inverse Jacobian** — the forward Jacobian maps joint velocities to Cartesian velocities ($\dot{x} = J \dot{\Theta}$); for the inverse direction, use $J^{-1}$, $J^{\dagger}$, or $J^{T}$.
>
> 5. **Ignoring joint limits** when checking existence — a pose might be geometrically reachable but infeasible due to physical joint stops.
>
> 6. **Stating "up to 16 solutions" without context** — the 16-solution bound is for a *general* 6-DOF manipulator; for spherical-wrist, the bound is **8**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** maps Cartesian space to joint space — the inverse of forward kinematics.
- The IK problem has **6 general properties**: existence, multiple solutions, closed-form vs. numerical, repeatability, workspace dependence, kinematic decoupling.
- **Existence condition:** $x_d \in W_R$ (reachable workspace).
- **Maximum solutions:** 16 (general 6-DOF), 8 (spherical wrist), 2 (3R planar), 1 (Cartesian).
- **Pieper's criterion:** 3 consecutive joint axes intersect ⇒ closed-form solution exists (sufficient condition).
- **Kinematic decoupling** for spherical-wrist manipulators splits IK into position (θ₁, θ₂, θ₃) and orientation (θ₄, θ₅, θ₆) sub-problems.
- **Wrist center formula:** $p_c = p_d - d_6 R_d \hat{z}$.
- **Newton–Raphson IK update:** $\Theta_{k+1} = \Theta_k + J^{-1}(\Theta_k)[x_d - f(\Theta_k)]$.
- **Damped Least Squares:** $\Delta\Theta = J^{T}(JJ^{T} + \lambda^{2}I)^{-1} \Delta x$ — robust near singularities.
- **Singularity condition:** $\det(J(\Theta)) = 0$.
- **Reachable workspace** ⊇ **Dexterous workspace** — always.
- **Pseudoinverse** $J^{\dagger} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ is used for redundant manipulators where $J$ is rectangular.
- **Workspace volume bound** for a 3R planar arm: $V_R \leq \frac{4}{3}\pi(a_2 + a_3)^3$ (approximation).
- **Repeatability** is a function of encoder resolution, backlash, and solver determinism.
- **Closed-form** is preferred for real-time industrial control; **numerical** is mandatory for redundant or non-spherical-wrist manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Kinematic Decoupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Theory of Kinematic Decoupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Decoupling Theorem (Paul, Shimano & Mayer, 1981):**
> *For a six-revolute (6R) manipulator whose last three joint axes are concurrent (intersect at a single common point) — i.e., the manipulator possesses a **spherical wrist** — the inverse kinematics problem can be decomposed into two independent, sequentially solvable sub-problems:*
> 1. **Inverse Position Kinematics (IPK):** Determine the first three joint variables $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ that place the **wrist center** (the common intersection point of axes 4, 5, and 6) at the desired spatial location in the base frame.
> 2. **Inverse Orientation Kinematics (IOK):** Determine the last three joint variables $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ that orient the end-effector to the desired pose, given the orientation already achieved by the first three joints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spherical Wrist:** A wrist sub-assembly in which the axes of the last three (typically revolute) joints intersect at a common point. The three intersecting axes span 3 mutually perpendicular lines in the general case, but for *decoupling* only the **concurrency** is required.

The procedure is formally written as:

$$^{0}_{6}T(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3,\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6) = ^{0}_{3}T(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3) \cdot {^{3}_{6}T(\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6)}$$

Since axes 4, 5, 6 intersect at a single point $O_c$ (the **wrist center**), the position of $O_c$ depends *only* on $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Arm:**
> Imagine reaching for a coffee mug on a shelf.
> - Your **shoulder and elbow** (the first three joints: $\theta_1$ shoulder yaw, $\theta_2$ shoulder pitch, $\theta_3$ elbow) move your **palm** to a specific point in space — they solve the **position** problem.
> - Your **wrist** (the last three joints: $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$) then twists and tilts your hand to grasp the mug at the correct angle — they solve the **orientation** problem.
>
> Notice: the shoulder and elbow do NOT care *how* the mug is oriented — they only care about reaching its *center*. The wrist does NOT care *where* the mug is in space — it only orients the fingers. This *separation of concerns* is **kinematic decoupling**.

The **wrist center point (WCP)** $O_c$ is the geometric "anchor" between these two sub-problems. It is the physical point shared by the position sub-system and the orientation sub-system.

## 1.3 Why Decoupling Matters (Engineering Motivation)

| Reason | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| Computational simplicity | Solves a **6-DOF nonlinear problem** as **two 3-DOF sub-problems** |
| Closed-form solutions | Enables **real-time control** (sub-millisecond inverse kinematics) |
| Modular design | Position arm and wrist can be designed **independently** |
| Singularity isolation | Wrist singularities ($\theta_5 = 0$) are clearly separated from arm singularities |
| Manufacturability | Standard industrial robots (PUMA 560, ABB IRB, KUKA KR) all use this principle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to (a) state the decoupling theorem precisely, (b) identify whether a given manipulator satisfies the concurrency condition, (c) derive the wrist-center coordinates from the desired end-effector pose, and (d) extract the wrist angles using Euler-angle decomposition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decoupling Condition — When Does It Apply?

The theorem applies if and only if **the axes of joints 4, 5, and 6 are concurrent**. Formally, using the displacement vector $d_i$ from origin of frame $\{i-1\}$ to origin of frame $\{i\}$:

$$d_4 = d_5 = d_6 = \vec{0} \quad \text{relative to a common intersection point } O_c$$

In the DH parameter table, this is reflected by:
- $a_4, a_5, a_6$ are determined by link offsets, but the **origin of frame 5** must lie at the intersection.
- Equivalently, the wrist center $O_c$ has the same coordinates in frames $\{4\}$, $\{5\}$, and $\{6\}$.

## 2.2 Logical Decomposition of the Decoupling Algorithm

The complete inverse kinematics solution via decoupling proceeds in five structured steps:

> **Step 1 — Pose Decomposition**
> Given the desired homogeneous transform $^{0}_{6}T$:
> $$^{0}_{6}T = \begin{bmatrix} ^{0}_{6}R & ^{0}_{6}P \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Extract the desired end-effector position $^{0}P_{6ORG}$ and orientation $^{0}_{6}R$.

> **Step 2 — Compute the Wrist Center Position**
> The end-effector position is offset from the wrist center by a tool/gripper vector $d$ along the approach direction. From the third column of $^{0}_{6}R$ (the approach axis $\hat{a}$):
> $$^{0}O_c = {^{0}_{6}P} - d \cdot {^{0}_{6}R} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = {^{0}_{6}P} - d \cdot \hat{a}$$
> Here, **d** is the distance from the wrist center to the end-effector flange (a **bold constant** for a given robot).

> **Step 3 — Inverse Position Kinematics (Solve for $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$)**
> Project the wrist-center vector into the relevant planes and apply geometric/trigonometric relations. The **base joint $\theta_1$** is typically obtained from:
> $$\theta_1 = \text{atan2}\bigl(^{0}O_{c,y},\;{^{0}O_{c,x}}\bigr) \pm \text{correction}$$
> Then $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$ are found using the law of cosines in the plane formed by the first three links.

> **Step 4 — Construct the Rotation $^{0}_{3}R$**
> From the solved $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$, evaluate the forward kinematics:
> $$^{0}_{3}R = {^{0}_{1}R(\theta_1)} \cdot {^{1}_{2}R(\theta_2)} \cdot {^{2}_{3}R(\theta_3)}$$

> **Step 5 — Inverse Orientation Kinematics (Solve for $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$)**
> Compute the residual rotation:
> $$^{3}_{6}R = ({^{0}_{3}R})^{-1} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R} = ({^{0}_{3}R})^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}$$
> Equate this to the symbolic form of $^{3}_{6}R(\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6)$ and extract angles using **Euler-angle extraction** (typically **Z-Y-Z** or **Z-Y-X** depending on joint alignment).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 6-DOF IK problem.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Usage |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $^{0}_{6}T = {^{0}_{3}T} \cdot {^{3}_{6}T}$ | Fundamental decoupling factorization |
| 2 | $^{0}O_c = {^{0}_{6}P} - d \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}\hat{z}$ | Wrist-center position from end-effector pose |
| 3 | $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_c,\;x_c)$ | Base joint angle (planar projection) |
| 4 | $r = \sqrt{x_c^{2} + y_c^{2}}$, $\;s = z_c - d_1$ | Planar reduction for 2-link IK |
| 5 | $\cos\theta_3 = \dfrac{r^{2}+s^{2}-a_2^{2}-a_3^{2}}{2 a_2 a_3}$ | Law of cosines (elbow) |
| 6 | $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(s,\;r) - \text{atan2}(a_3\sin\theta_3,\;a_2+a_3\cos\theta_3)$ | Shoulder angle |
| 7 | $^{3}_{6}R = ({^{0}_{3}R})^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}$ | Residual rotation for wrist |
| 8 | $\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23},\;r_{13})$ | Wrist roll (from $^{3}_{6}R$ entry) |
| 9 | $\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^{2}+r_{23}^{2}},\;r_{33}\right)$ | Wrist pitch |
| 10 | $\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32},\;-r_{31})$ | Wrist yaw |
| 11 | $\text{rank}(J) < 6$ | Singularity condition (decoupled: arm + wrist) |
| 12 | $d = \sqrt{a_4^{2}+d_5^{2}}$ (or pure $d_6$) | Tool offset to wrist center |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units:** All angles in **radians** internally; KTU exam answers are typically expected in **degrees**. All lengths in **mm** or **m** consistent with given DH parameters.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Decoupled Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> - **Industrial Robotics:** Nearly every commercial 6R robot (PUMA 560, ABB IRB 120, KUKA KR 6, Fanuc LR-Mate) is designed with a spherical wrist, *specifically* to enable the decoupling approach. Without it, real-time IK at 1 kHz control rates would be infeasible.
> - **Surgical Robotics:** The Da Vinci and similar systems use decoupled kinematics to enable intuitive master–slave mapping.
> - **Space Manipulators:** Canadarm (SSRMS) on the ISS uses decoupling to allow astronauts to command the end-effector pose via joystick inputs.
> - **Humanoid Robots:** Decoupled arm–wrist control simplifies reinforcement-learning policies for manipulation tasks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Wrist-Center Position

We begin with the desired end-effector pose in the base frame:

$$^{0}_{6}T = \begin{bmatrix} ^{0}_{6}R_{3\times3} & {^{0}_{6}P_{3\times1}} \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Let $O_c$ be the wrist center and let the tool frame $\{6\}$ be located at a distance $d$ along the $z$-axis of frame $\{5\}$ (i.e., the approach direction of the wrist). The position of the end-effector origin in frame $\{0\}$ can be written as:

$${^{0}P_{6ORG}} = {^{0}O_c} + d \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}$$

Rearranging to solve for the wrist center:

$${^{0}O_c} = {^{0}P_{6ORG}} - d \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}$$

> **Explanation:** The end-effector position is the wrist center *plus* a translation along the tool's approach direction. Subtracting this tool offset isolates the wrist center, which depends only on $\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3$.

## 3.2 Derivation of $\theta_1$ (Base Joint — Azimuth)

The wrist center $O_c$ lies in the plane swept by the first three links. Its projection onto the $xy$-plane of frame $\{0\}$ gives:

$$x_c = {^{0}O_{c,x}}, \qquad y_c = {^{0}O_{c,y}}$$

The base joint rotates about $z_0$. From planar geometry:

$$\theta_1 = \text{atan2}\bigl(y_c,\;x_c\bigr)$$

> **Explanation:** The base joint simply aligns the arm with the horizontal projection of the wrist center. The `atan2` function correctly handles all four quadrants (a frequent source of student errors when using `atan`).

## 3.3 Derivation of $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$ (Shoulder and Elbow) via Law of Cosines

Project the wrist-center vector into the vertical plane perpendicular to $z_0$ after the $\theta_1$ rotation. Define the planar distance $r$ and height $s$:

$$r = \sqrt{x_c^{2}+y_c^{2}} - a_2\cos\theta_1'$$

$$s = z_c - d_1$$

For a robot with link lengths $a_2$ (upper arm) and $a_3$ (forearm), the wrist center in this plane lies at distance $\sqrt{r^{2}+s^{2}}$ from the shoulder. Apply the law of cosines to the triangle formed by the shoulder, elbow, and wrist:

$$\cos\theta_3 = \frac{r^{2}+s^{2}-a_2^{2}-a_3^{2}}{2\,a_2\,a_3}$$

Then $\theta_3$ is obtained (with two solutions — *elbow-up* and *elbow-down*):

$$\theta_3 = \pm\arccos\!\left(\frac{r^{2}+s^{2}-a_2^{2}-a_3^{2}}{2\,a_2\,a_3}\right)$$

For $\theta_2$, define an intermediate angle $\gamma$ from the law of cosines applied to the triangle shoulder–elbow–wrist:

$$\gamma = \text{atan2}(s,\;r)$$

$$\theta_2 = \gamma - \text{atan2}\!\bigl(a_3\sin\theta_3,\;a_2+a_3\cos\theta_3\bigr)$$

> **Explanation:** The two-stage `atan2` ensures $\theta_2$ places the elbow correctly so that the cumulative link vector reaches the wrist center.

## 3.4 Derivation of Wrist Angles $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ (Orientation Sub-Problem)

After solving for $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$, evaluate $^{0}_{3}R$ from forward kinematics. The desired end-effector orientation is $^{0}_{6}R$. The remaining rotation that must be produced by the wrist is:

$${^{3}_{6}R} = \bigl({^{0}_{3}R}\bigr)^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}$$

For a spherical wrist with axes aligned as Z-Y-Z (typical configuration), the symbolic form is:

$${^{3}_{6}R} = R_z(\theta_4)\,R_y(\theta_5)\,R_z(\theta_6) = \begin{bmatrix} c_4c_5c_6 - s_4s_6 & -c_4c_5s_6 - s_4c_6 & c_4s_5 \\ s_4c_5c_6 + c_4s_6 & -s_4c_5s_6 + c_4c_6 & s_4s_5 \\ -s_5c_6 & s_5s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Extract the wrist angles by matching entries:

$$\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^{2}+r_{23}^{2}},\;r_{33}\right) = \arccos(r_{33})$$

> **Explanation:** $r_{33} = \cos\theta_5$. Using the square root form (with sign handled by $\text{atan2}$) avoids the ambiguity of $\arccos$.

$$\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23},\;r_{13})$$

> **Explanation:** When $\theta_5 \neq 0$ and $\theta_5 \neq \pi$, dividing $r_{23}$ by $r_{13}$ gives $\tan\theta_4$ directly.

$$\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32},\;-r_{31})$$

> **Explanation:** The negative sign on $r_{31}$ comes from the symbolic form of the (3,1) entry: $-s_5c_6 = -r_{31}$, so $c_6 = -r_{31}/s_5$, and $s_6 = r_{32}/s_5$.

## 3.5 Full Inverse Kinematics Summary (in Symbolic Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \text{atan2}\bigl(y_c,\;x_c\bigr) \\
\theta_3 &= \pm\arccos\!\left(\frac{x_c^{2}+y_c^{2}+z_c^{2}-a_2^{2}-a_3^{2}-d_1^{2}-d_4^{2}+2d_1 d_4}{2 a_2 a_3}\right) \\
\theta_2 &= \text{atan2}\!\bigl(z_c - d_1,\;\sqrt{x_c^{2}+y_c^{2}} - d_4\bigr) - \text{atan2}\!\bigl(a_3\sin\theta_3,\;a_2 + a_3\cos\theta_3\bigr) \\
{^{3}_{6}R} &= \bigl({^{0}_{3}R(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3)}\bigr)^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R} \\
\theta_5 &= \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^{2}+r_{23}^{2}},\;r_{33}\right) \\
\theta_4 &= \text{atan2}(r_{23},\;r_{13}) \\
\theta_6 &= \text{atan2}(r_{32},\;-r_{31})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\text{atan2}$ instead of $\text{atan}$ or $\arccos$?**
> - `atan(y/x)` collapses quadrants and loses sign information.
> - `arccos` returns values only in $[0, \pi]$, hiding the sign of the other axis.
> - `atan2(y, x)` uses the sign of *both* arguments to place the result in the correct quadrant $(-\pi, \pi]$. This is the **standard KTU-board-recommended** form.

## 3.6 Singularity Analysis in the Decoupled Framework

A manipulator is at a **kinematic singularity** when the Jacobian loses rank ($\det(J)=0$). In the decoupled structure, singularities split into two distinct types:

| Type | Condition | Geometric Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Boundary singularity** | Wrist center on axis 1 boundary | Arm fully stretched or folded |
| **Interior (elbow) singularity** | $\theta_3 = 0$ or $\theta_3 = \pi$ | Forearm aligned with upper arm |
| **Wrist singularity** | $\theta_5 = 0$ | Axes 4 and 6 become collinear — lose 1 DOF |

> **Why decoupling helps:** Because position and orientation are separated, singularities in one sub-system do not contaminate the other. The wrist singularity, for example, leaves the position Jacobian of the arm fully invertible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Decoupled IK Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full information flow of the decoupling algorithm as it would be implemented in a robot controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired End Effector Pose: baseT6 desired] --> B[Extract Position baseP6 and Orientation baseR6]
    B --> C[Compute Wrist Center: baseOc = baseP6 - d times baseR6 zhat]
    C --> D[Inverse Position Kinematics IPK]
    D --> D1[Solve theta1 from baseOc xy projection]
    D1 --> D2[Solve theta2 and theta3 via law of cosines]
    D2 --> E[Evaluate Forward Rotation: baseR3 from theta1 theta2 theta3]
    E --> F[Compute Residual Rotation: R3to6 = baseR3 transpose times baseR6]
    F --> G[Inverse Orientation Kinematics IOK]
    G --> G1[Extract theta4 and theta6 via atan2 from R3to6 entries]
    G1 --> G2[Extract theta5 via atan2 from R3to6 entry r33]
    G2 --> H[Output Complete Joint Vector: theta1 to theta6]
    H --> I{Singularity Check}
    I -->|theta5 near 0| J[Flag Wrist Singularity: limit motion]
    I -->|theta3 near 0 or pi| K[Flag Elbow Singularity: limit motion]
    I -->|all clear| L[Send Joint Commands to Actuators]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture: Position Arm vs Orientation Wrist

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PositionArm[Position Arm: First 3 Joints]
        P1[Joint 1 theta1: Base Yaw] --> P2[Joint 2 theta2: Shoulder Pitch]
        P2 --> P3[Joint 3 theta3: Elbow]
    end
    P3 --> WC[Wrist Center Point Oc: Common Intersection]
    subgraph OrientationWrist[Orientation Wrist: Last 3 Joints]
        WC --> O1[Joint 4 theta4: Wrist Roll]
        O1 --> O2[Joint 5 theta5: Wrist Pitch]
        O2 --> O3[Joint 6 theta6: Wrist Yaw]
    end
    O3 --> EE[End Effector Flange: Tool Center Point]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Mathematical Decomposition of the Homogeneous Transform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Transform: baseT6 of theta1 to theta6] --> B[Factorization: baseT6 = baseT3 times T3to6]
    B --> C[baseT3 depends only on theta1 theta2 theta3]
    B --> D[T3to6 depends only on theta4 theta5 theta6]
    C --> E[baseT3 contains Position and Partial Orientation of Wrist]
    D --> F[T3to6 contains Remaining Orientation from Wrist to Tool]
    E --> G[IPK Module: Solve theta1 theta2 theta3]
    F --> H[IOK Module: Solve theta4 theta5 theta6]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the *control-flow* of how a robot controller processes a desired pose and outputs joint commands.
> - The second diagram shows the *physical architecture* — how the manipulator is split into a positioning arm and an orienting wrist.
> - The third diagram emphasizes the *mathematical factorization* that justifies the algorithmic split.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Kinematic Decoupling Theorem for a 6R manipulator. Under what geometric condition is it applicable?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Kinematic Decoupling Theorem (Paul, Shimano & Mayer, 1981):**
> *For a 6R manipulator whose last three joint axes are concurrent (i.e., the manipulator has a spherical wrist), the inverse kinematics problem can be separated into two independent sub-problems:*
> 1. *Inverse Position Kinematics (IPK): Solves the first three joint variables $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ to position the wrist center $O_c$ at the desired location.*
> 2. *Inverse Orientation Kinematics (IOK): Solves the last three joint variables $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ to orient the end-effector.*
>
> **Geometric Condition:** The axes of joints 4, 5, and 6 must intersect at a **single common point** (the wrist center $O_c$). *(3 marks — 1 for statement, 1 for sub-problems, 1 for concurrency condition)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a spherical wrist? Why is it critical for real-time inverse kinematics of industrial manipulators?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **spherical wrist** is a sub-assembly of three revolute joints whose axes intersect at a common point $O_c$. The three axes may be mutually perpendicular (most common) or arranged in other geometric configurations, but they must be **concurrent**.
>
> **Importance for real-time IK:**
> - It allows the 6-DOF nonlinear IK problem to be split into two **3-DOF sub-problems** that admit closed-form solutions.
> - Closed-form solutions execute in **microseconds**, enabling control loop rates of **1 kHz or higher**.
> - It **decouples position from orientation**, simplifying controller design, trajectory planning, and singularity analysis.
> - Virtually all commercial 6R robots (PUMA 560, KUKA KR, ABB IRB) use this architecture. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — *Decoupling Algorithm & Wrist-Center Derivation*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A 6R manipulator has the following DH parameters (all joints revolute, all link twists $\alpha_i$ and offsets are given; $\theta_i$ are the joint variables):

| $i$ | $a_i$ (mm) | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ (mm) | $\theta_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 90° | 500 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | 800 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 | 0 | 90° | 0 | $\theta_3$ |
| 4 | 0 | –90° | 600 | $\theta_4$ |
| 5 | 0 | 90° | 0 | $\theta_5$ |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 200 | $\theta_6$ |

Show that the manipulator satisfies the kinematic decoupling condition, and derive the expression for the wrist-center position ${^{0}O_c}$ in terms of the desired end-effector position and orientation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Given the desired end-effector pose:

$$^{0}_{6}T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 & 700 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 600 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 & 800 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

compute the wrist-center position ${^{0}O_c}$ and solve for $\theta_1$ using the decoupling approach.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Verify Spherical Wrist Condition**
> From the DH table, the wrist link offsets are $d_5 = 0$ and $d_6 = 200$ mm. The wrist center is the intersection of axes 4, 5, and 6. In the frame $\{4\}$, the origin of $\{5\}$ lies along $z_4$ at distance $d_5 = 0$ — therefore the origins of frames $\{4\}$ and $\{5\}$ coincide at the intersection. Similarly, frame $\{6\}$'s origin is offset by $d_6$ along $z_5$ from $\{5\}$, but $z_5$ passes through the same intersection point.
>
> **Conclusion:** All three wrist-joint axes pass through the common point $O_c$ located at the origin of frame $\{4\}$ (and equivalently frame $\{5\}$). **The manipulator possesses a spherical wrist** and the decoupling theorem applies. *(2 marks)*

> **Step 2: Wrist-Center Position Derivation**
> The end-effector origin $P_{6ORG}$ in the base frame is:
>
> $${^{0}P_{6ORG}} = {^{0}O_c} + d_6 \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Because the offset is along the local $z_6$ axis, which in the base frame is the third column of $^{0}_{6}R$ (call it $\hat{a} = [a_x, a_y, a_z]^T$):
>
> $${^{0}O_c} = {^{0}P_{6ORG}} - d_6 \cdot \hat{a} = {^{0}P_{6ORG}} - d_6 \begin{bmatrix} a_x \\ a_y \\ a_z \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> The $d_6 = 200$ mm is the distance from the wrist center to the end-effector flange, and $\hat{a}$ is the approach direction of the tool. This $O_c$ depends only on $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ because the wrist center is a point *on* the first three links. *(3 marks)*

> **Step 3: Decoupling Argument**
> Substituting the factorization $^{0}_{6}T = {^{0}_{3}T} \cdot {^{3}_{6}T}$, we see that the position part of $^{0}_{3}T$ equals $^{0}O_c$, and the rotation part of $^{0}_{3}T$ is independent of $\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6$. Therefore:
> - $^{0}O_c$ depends only on $\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3$ → **IPK sub-problem**.
> - $^{0}_{6}R$ when pre-multiplied by $(^{0}_{3}R)^T$ yields $^{3}_{6}R$ depending only on $\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6$ → **IOK sub-problem**. *(2 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Extract Data from Pose Matrix**
> From $^{0}_{6}T$:
>
> $${^{0}_{6}R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad {^{0}_{6}P} = \begin{bmatrix} 700 \\ 600 \\ 800 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}$$
>
> The approach direction (third column of $R$):
>
> $$\hat{a} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> *(1 mark)*

> **Step 2: Compute Wrist Center**
> Using $d_6 = 200$ mm:
>
> $${^{0}O_c} = \begin{bmatrix} 700 \\ 600 \\ 800 \end{bmatrix} - 200 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 700 \\ 400 \\ 800 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}$$
>
> *(2 marks)*

> **Step 3: Solve for $\theta_1$**
> The base joint rotates about $z_0$. Using the horizontal projection of the wrist center:
>
> $$x_c = 700 \text{ mm}, \quad y_c = 400 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c) = \text{atan2}(400, 700)$$
>
> $$\theta_1 = 29.74° \quad (\text{or } 0.5191 \text{ rad})$$
>
> *(2 marks)*

> **Step 4: Verification of Decoupling Consistency**
> With $\theta_1$ known, the wrist center in frame $\{1\}$ is:
>
> $${^{1}O_c} = {^{0}_{1}R(\theta_1)}^{T} \cdot {^{0}O_c} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{700^{2}+400^{2}} \\ 0 \\ 800 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 806.23 \\ 0 \\ 800 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}$$
>
> (where we used $\cos\theta_1 = 700/806.23$ and $\sin\theta_1 = 400/806.23$). This 2D coordinate $(r=806.23, s=800)$ is then used in the law-of-cosines sub-problem to find $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$. *(2 marks)*

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — *Wrist Orientation Extraction*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the same 6R manipulator as in Question A, derive the symbolic expression for the residual rotation matrix $^{3}_{6}R$ in terms of $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$, and explain how the three wrist angles can be extracted using $\text{atan2}$ functions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Suppose the solved arm-joint angles are $\theta_1 = 30°, \theta_2 = 45°, \theta_3 = -30°$, and the desired end-effector orientation is:

$$^{0}_{6}R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the residual rotation $^{3}_{6}R$ and solve for $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Symbolic Form of Wrist Rotation**
> For the given DH parameters, the wrist sub-assembly has axes 4, 5, 6 aligned as Z–Y–Z. The forward kinematics of the wrist gives:
>
> $$^{3}_{6}R = R_z(\theta_4)\,R_y(\theta_5)\,R_z(\theta_6)$$
>
> Expanding the triple product:
>
> $$^{3}_{6}R = \begin{bmatrix} c_4c_5c_6 - s_4s_6 & -c_4c_5s_6 - s_4c_6 & c_4s_5 \\ s_4c_5c_6 + c_4s_6 & -s_4c_5s_6 + c_4c_6 & s_4s_5 \\ -s_5c_6 & s_5s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> *(3 marks)*

> **Step 2: Angle Extraction Strategy**
> - $\theta_5$ is read directly from $r_{33} = \cos\theta_5$ using the $\text{atan2}$ form to retain sign information about $\sin\theta_5$.
> - $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ are obtained by **dividing** other entries by $\sin\theta_5$ — this is only valid when $\sin\theta_5 \neq 0$ (i.e., the wrist is *not* at a singularity).
>
> The extraction formulas are:
>
> $$\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^{2}+r_{23}^{2}},\;r_{33}\right)$$
>
> $$\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23},\;r_{13})$$
>
> $$\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32},\;-r_{31})$$
>
> *(2 marks)*

> **Step 3: Why $\text{atan2}$?**
> - When $\theta_5 = 0$, axes 4 and 6 become collinear, and $\theta_4 + \theta_6$ becomes a single indeterminate DOF. This is the **wrist singularity**.
> - Using $\text{atan2}$ (rather than $\arccos$ or $\text{atan}$) preserves the sign information in *both* numerator and denominator, yielding quadrant-correct results. *(2 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Construct $^{0}_{3}R$ from Given Angles**
> Using the symbolic DH matrices with $\theta_1 = 30°, \theta_2 = 45°, \theta_3 = -30°$:
>
> $$^{0}_{3}R = R_z(30°)\,R_x(90°)\,R_z(45°)\,R_x(90°)\,R_z(-30°)$$
>
> Computing step by step:
>
> $$^{0}_{1}R = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$^{1}_{2}R = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$^{2}_{3}R = \begin{bmatrix} c_3 & 0 & s_3 \\ s_3 & 0 & -c_3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & 0 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0 & -0.866 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Multiplying: $^{0}_{3}R = {^{0}_{1}R}\cdot{^{1}_{2}R}\cdot{^{2}_{3}R}$:
>
> $$^{0}_{3}R \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.612 & -0.354 & -0.707 \\ 0.612 & 0.707 & 0.354 \\ 0.500 & -0.612 & 0.612 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> *(3 marks)*

> **Step 2: Compute Residual Rotation**
> $$^{3}_{6}R = ({^{0}_{3}R})^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.612 & 0.612 & 0.500 \\ -0.354 & 0.707 & -0.612 \\ -0.707 & 0.354 & 0.612 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Carrying out the multiplication:
>
> $$^{3}_{6}R = \begin{bmatrix} -0.122 & -0.780 & 0.612 \\ 0.354 & 0.056 & 0.707 \\ -0.882 & 0.306 & 0.354 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> *(2 marks)*

> **Step 3: Extract Wrist Angles**
> Using the entries: $r_{13} = 0.612$, $r_{23} = 0.707$, $r_{33} = 0.354$, $r_{31} = -0.882$, $r_{32} = 0.306$:
>
> $$\theta_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{0.612^{2}+0.707^{2}},\;0.354\right) = \text{atan2}(0.935,\;0.354) \approx 69.3°$$
>
> $$\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(0.707,\;0.612) \approx 49.1°$$
>
> $$\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(0.306,\;0.882) \approx 19.1°$$
>
> *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the `transpose`:** Many students write $^{3}_{6}R = {^{0}_{3}R} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}$ instead of the correct $({^{0}_{3}R})^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}$. *Always pre-multiply by the transpose to map the rotation back into frame $\{3\}$.*
> 2. **Using `atan` or `arccos` instead of `atan2`:** This loses the sign of the angle and places $\theta_4, \theta_6$ in the wrong quadrant. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Skipping the wrist-center derivation:** The wrist-center equation $^{0}O_c = {^{0}_{6}P} - d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$ must be shown explicitly; quoting it without derivation loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Sign error in $\theta_6$ formula:** The entry is $-r_{31}$ (not $+r_{31}$). This sign comes from the symbolic form $-s_5 c_6 = r_{31}$.
> 5. **Not verifying the spherical-wrist condition:** Simply stating the theorem without showing that the given DH parameters satisfy concurrency loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the multiple solutions:** $\theta_3$ has two solutions (elbow up/down); mention both. $\theta_1$ may have two solutions if the wrist center lies on the $z_0$ axis.
> 7. **Mixing degrees and radians:** KTU expects degrees in the final answer; show both.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Theory of Kinematic Decoupling**

### Conceptual Understanding
- [ ] The **Kinematic Decoupling Theorem** separates a 6R IK problem into **IPK (first 3 joints)** and **IOK (last 3 joints)**.
- [ ] It applies **iff** joints 4, 5, 6 are **concurrent** (spherical wrist).
- [ ] The **wrist center $O_c$** is the bridge between the two sub-problems.

### Mathematical Tools
- [ ] **Wrist center position:** $^{0}O_c = {^{0}_{6}P} - d \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}\hat{z}$
- [ ] **Base joint:** $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c)$
- [ ] **Elbow:** $\cos\theta_3$ from **law of cosines** using planar projection $(r, s)$
- [ ] **Shoulder:** $\theta_2$ from two-stage $\text{atan2}$
- [ ] **Residual rotation:** $^{3}_{6}R = ({^{0}_{3}R})^{T} \cdot {^{0}_{6}R}$
- [ ] **Wrist angles:** $\text{atan2}$-based extraction (never $\arccos$ or $\text{atan}$ alone).

### Engineering Context
- [ ] **Industrial robots** (PUMA 560, KUKA, ABB) all use decoupled architectures.
- [ ] Enables **real-time IK** at kHz control rates.
- [ ] **Singularities** split cleanly: boundary (arm), elbow ($\theta_3 = 0,\pi$), wrist ($\theta_5 = 0$).

### Common Pitfalls
- [ ] Use **$\text{atan2}$** always, never `atan` or `arccos` for angle extraction.
- [ ] **Pre-multiply** by $({^{0}_{3}R})^{T}$, not $^{0}_{3}R$ itself.
- [ ] Verify the **concurrency condition** before applying the theorem.
- [ ] Quote $d_6$ (or $d$) **explicitly** when computing the wrist center.
- [ ] Provide **multiple solutions** for $\theta_1, \theta_3$ when they exist.

### High-Yield Memory Hooks
- 🔑 **"Position first, orientation second"** — the IPK is solved before the IOK.
- 🔑 **"Wrist center is the only point shared by the two sub-problems"** — all the algebra pivots around $^{0}O_c$.
- 🔑 **"$\text{atan2}$ is the KTU-approved function for IK angle extraction"** — period.
- 🔑 **"Decoupling is *only* for spherical-wrist 6R manipulators"** — don't apply it to non-spherical wrists.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Kinematics Example - SCARA Manipulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Kinematics Example - SCARA Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**SCARA Manipulator**: *Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm* — a 4-DOF (Degree of Freedom) cylindrical-coordinate-class robot whose primary three joints (two revolute + one prismatic) produce motion primarily in the **horizontal (XY) plane**, while the fourth prismatic joint provides **vertical (Z) insertion**. The "Selective Compliance" arises because the arm is **stiff in the vertical direction** but **compliant in the horizontal plane**, making it ideal for vertical assembly (peg-in-hole, pick-and-place).

**Inverse Kinematics (IK)** for a SCARA: The mathematical procedure of computing the **joint variables** $\theta_1$, $\theta_2$, $d_3$ (and $\theta_4$ for tool rotation) given a desired **end-effector pose** $(x, y, z, \phi)$ in the task space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Arm Drawing on a Wall"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: Imagine you are standing in front of a vertical wall, holding a pen with a fully extended arm. You want the pen tip to touch a specific point on the wall:
> - Your **shoulder rotation** adjusts the side-to-side reach (analogous to $\theta_1$)
> - Your **elbow bending** extends or shortens the reach (analogous to $\theta_2$)
> - Your **shoulder lifting/lowering** controls how far the pen extends from your body (analogous to $d_3$)
> - Your **wrist twist** orients the pen angle (analogous to $\theta_4$)
>
> Inverse Kinematics is the math your brain does **backwards**: you choose a target point on the wall, and the brain solves "how should I rotate shoulder, bend elbow, and extend arm to put the pen there?"

### 1.3 SCARA Robot — Key Physical & Kinematic Properties

| Property | Specification |
|----------|--------------|
| Degrees of Freedom | **4** (3 planar + 1 vertical) |
| Joint Types | $R \to R \to P \to R$ |
| Primary Workspace | Horizontal planar disk + vertical column |
| DH Convention | All link twists $\alpha_i = 0$ (parallel Z-axes) |
| Singularities | When arm is fully extended ($\theta_2 = 0$) or fully folded ($\theta_2 = \pi$) |
| Industrial Use | PCB assembly, dispensing, screw-driving, packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: For SCARA IK problems, the **3R planar structure has parallel joint axes**. This is the key reason the IK has a **closed-form analytical solution** (no numerical methods needed) — the projection onto the XY plane reduces to a simple **2R planar manipulator** problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — Steps of the IK Solution

1. **Identify the SCARA's DH Parameters** — Confirm link lengths $a_1, a_2$ and joint types.
2. **Formulate the Forward Kinematics (FK) Equations** — Express $(x, y, z, \phi)$ in terms of $(\theta_1, \theta_2, d_3, \theta_4)$.
3. **Isolate the Planar Sub-Problem** — Project everything onto the XY plane; ignore Z and $\phi$ initially.
4. **Apply Cosine Law to the 2R Triangle** — Compute $\cos\theta_2$ from $x$, $y$, $a_1$, $a_2$.
5. **Resolve the Sign Ambiguity (Elbow-Up vs Elbow-Down)** — Choose $\theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}(\cdot)$.
6. **Compute $\theta_1$ via the Angle-Sum Rule** — Use the `atan2` function for quadrant safety.
7. **Decouple the Z-Axis** — Solve $d_3$ from the vertical coordinate.
8. **Decouple Tool Rotation** — Solve $\theta_4$ from the desired orientation $\phi$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why parallel Z-axes?** All $\alpha_i = 0$ means the XY coordinates are simple sums of $\cos$ and $\sin$ terms → no need for inverse trig on the projection → **closed-form tractability**.
- **Why the cosine law?** The XY projection forms a triangle with sides $a_1$, $a_2$, and the radial distance $\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$. The angle opposite $a_2$ is $\theta_2$ — direct application of the **Law of Cosines**.
- **Why `atan2` over `tan⁻¹`?** The `atan2(y, x)` function resolves the **quadrant ambiguity** inherent in single-argument arctangent, giving an angle in $(-\pi, \pi]$ — exactly what KTU examiners expect.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — SCARA Inverse Kinematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this cheat sheet are examinable under Module 2 of PCRUT402.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Radial reach | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | $\cos\theta_2$ | $\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$ | Validity: $\vert \cos\theta_2 \vert \leq 1$ |
| 3 | $\sin\theta_2$ | $\sin\theta_2 = \pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_2}$ | $\pm$ = elbow up / down |
| 4 | $\theta_2$ | $\theta_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(\sin\theta_2, \cos\theta_2)$ | Returns full quadrant angle |
| 5 | $\theta_1$ | $\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y, x) - \mathrm{atan2}(a_2 \sin\theta_2, \; a_1 + a_2 \cos\theta_2)$ | Geometric decomposition |
| 6 | $d_3$ (prismatic) | $d_3 = d_0 - z$ | $d_0$ = base height (constant) |
| 7 | $\theta_4$ (tool roll) | $\theta_4 = \phi - \theta_1 - \theta_2$ | Decoupled from position |
| 8 | Reachability | $\vert a_1 - a_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq a_1 + a_2$ | Workspace boundary |
| 9 | Workspace area | $A = \pi [(a_1+a_2)^2 - (a_1-a_2)^2] = 4\pi a_1 a_2$ | Annulus area |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for KTU exams**: When writing absolute value in answer sheets, use the form $\vert x \vert$ or "mod $x$" — never the symbol that breaks markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pick-and-place automation** in semiconductor fabs: SCARA robots place chips at $> 100$ placements/minute.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging**: Vertical insertion into blister packs exploits SCARA's Z-axis stiffness.
- **Surgical robotics research**: SCARA-derived kinematics appear in laparoscopic tool-positioning arms.
- **KTU Relevance**: Most industrial robotics theory questions in ESE (End Semester Exam) test **2R planar IK** as a warm-up before SCARA, since the math is identical for the first three joints.
