```mermaid
        C1[Position Profile theta of t]
        C2[Velocity Profile theta dot of t]
        C3[Acceleration Profile theta double dot of t]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 -->|if violated| B4
    B4 --> B2
    B3 -->|if satisfied| C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Comparison: Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JS[JOINT SPACE PLANNING]
        J1[Define q0 and qf as joint angles]
        J2[Interpolate q0 to qf using polynomials]
        J3[Send theta of t directly to joint controllers]
    end

    subgraph CS[CARTESIAN SPACE PLANNING]
        K1[Define P0 and Pf as end effector poses]
        K2[Interpolate P0 to Pf as straight line or arc]
        K3[Apply Inverse Kinematics at every sample]
        K4[Convert to theta of t for joint controllers]
    end

    J3 --> R[Final Trajectory]
    K4 --> R
```

### 4.4 Phases of the Linear-with-Parabolic-Blend Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: theta 0, velocity 0] -->|Parabolic Accel: duration tb, accn c| B[End of Blend 1: velocity v, position theta b]
    B -->|Linear Cruise: duration tl, velocity v| C[Start of Blend 2: velocity v, position theta c]
    C -->|Parabolic Decel: duration tb, accn minus c| D[End: theta f, velocity 0]
```

### 4.5 Hierarchy of Motion Commands in a Robotic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1: Task Program e.g. Weld seam AB] --> L2[Level 2: Path Planner outputs via points]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Trajectory Planner outputs theta of t]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Servo Controller at 1 kHz]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Motor Drivers and Encoders]
    L5 --> L6[Physical Joint Motion]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guide for the diagrams:** A student should be able to label each block with the input/output quantities (e.g., "block B2 receives $s$ and $T$ and outputs $s(t)$") and explain why the constraint-checker feeds back to the time-scaler — this is a 14-mark question in disguise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

#### **Question 1.** *Differentiate between path planning and trajectory planning in robotic manipulators. Why is trajectory planning essential for real-time execution?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Path planning** is the geometric problem of finding a sequence of configurations from start to goal, without considering time. Its output is a spatial curve (a set of via points or a continuous function in joint or Cartesian space).
- **Trajectory planning** parameterises this path with time, producing position $\theta(t)$, velocity $\dot{\theta}(t)$, and acceleration $\ddot{\theta}(t)$ profiles.
- Trajectory planning is essential because the robot's actuators have **finite limits** on velocity and acceleration; the servo loop requires time-stamped setpoints; and smooth time histories minimise vibration, overshoot, and mechanical wear. *(1 mark per bullet, 3 marks total.)*

#### **Question 2.** *What are the two main approaches to trajectory planning — joint-space and Cartesian-space? State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Joint-space planning** interpolates joint angles directly between $q_0$ and $q_f$. *Advantage:* avoids the computational burden and singularity issues of inverse kinematics along the path.
- **Cartesian-space planning** interpolates the end-effector pose (position and orientation) along a desired geometric curve (line, arc). *Advantage:* the end-effector follows a known, predictable path — critical for welding, painting, and gluing tasks.
- *(Distribute marks: 1 for joint-space description + advantage, 1 for Cartesian-space description + advantage, 1 for the contrast.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A single joint of a robotic manipulator is to move from an initial angle of $20^{\circ}$ to a final angle of $80^{\circ}$ in $T = 4$ seconds, starting and ending with zero velocity. **Derive the cubic polynomial trajectory** for this joint, and determine:
(i) the peak velocity reached during the motion, and
(ii) the peak acceleration reached during the motion.

**(b)** Compare **joint-space and Cartesian-space trajectory planning** in detail. Under what conditions is each preferred, and what are the computational implications?

---

#### **Option A — Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary conditions.**

$$
\theta_0 = 20^{\circ}, \quad \theta_f = 80^{\circ}, \quad T = 4\,\text{s}, \quad \dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}(T) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard cubic coefficients derived in Section 3.1.**

$$
a_0 = \theta_0 = 20^{\circ}
$$

$$
a_1 = 0
$$

$$
a_2 = \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^{2}} = \dfrac{3(80 - 20)}{4^{2}} = \dfrac{180}{16} = 11.25\,\,^{\circ}/\text{s}^{2}
$$

$$
a_3 = -\dfrac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^{3}} = -\dfrac{2(80 - 20)}{4^{3}} = -\dfrac{120}{64} = -1.875\,\,^{\circ}/\text{s}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the trajectory.**

$$
\theta(t) = 20 + 11.25\, t^{2} - 1.875\, t^{3}, \quad 0 \le t \le 4\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity and acceleration expressions.**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 22.5\, t - 5.625\, t^{2}
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25\, t
$$

**Step 5 — Peak velocity.**

Differentiate $\dot{\theta}(t)$ and set to zero:

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 22.5 - 11.25\, t = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; t = 2\,\text{s}
$$

Substitute $t = 2$ s into the velocity equation:

$$
\dot{\theta}_{\max} = 22.5(2) - 5.625(2)^{2} = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5\,\,^{\circ}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Peak acceleration.**

The acceleration is linear in $t$, so it is maximum in magnitude at the endpoints:

$$
\ddot{\theta}(0) = 22.5\,\,^{\circ}/\text{s}^{2}, \qquad \ddot{\theta}(T) = 22.5 - 11.25(4) = -22.5\,\,^{\circ}/\text{s}^{2}
$$

So the peak (magnitude) acceleration is **22.5 °/s²**.

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\theta(t)$ | $20 + 11.25 t^{2} - 1.875 t^{3}$ degrees |
| Peak velocity | $22.5^{\circ}$/s at $t = 2$ s |
| Peak acceleration | $22.5^{\circ}$/s² at $t = 0$ and $t = 4$ s |

> *(Mark split: 2 for cubic coefficients, 2 for trajectory equation, 1 for peak velocity, 1 for peak acceleration, 1 for units and final answer.)*

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Joint-Space Planning | Cartesian-Space Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Space of interpolation | Joint coordinates $q_i$ | End-effector pose $[x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]$ |
| Inverse kinematics | Required only at endpoints | Required at every sample instant |
| End-effector path | Uncontrolled (curved in general) | Specified (straight line, arc, etc.) |
| Computational cost per cycle | Very low | High (IK + Jacobian) |
| Singularity handling | Avoided by construction | Must be actively managed |
| Preferred when | Point-to-point, pick-and-place, obstacle avoidance in joint space | Tasks requiring precise Cartesian path: welding, painting, milling, cutting |
| Continuity of joint motion | Naturally smooth | Discontinuities can occur near singularities |

**When joint-space is preferred:** PTP moves, high-speed motions where intermediate path shape is irrelevant, when the controller has limited CPU budget.

**When Cartesian-space is preferred:** Continuous-path applications (welding seams, glue beads, deburring edges), teaching-by-showing (lead-through), and tasks where obstacles are defined in the workspace.

> *(Mark split: 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for examples/conditions, 2 marks for computational discussion.)*

---

#### **Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** A robotic joint must move from $\theta_0 = 0^{\circ}$ to $\theta_f = 60^{\circ}$ in $T = 5$ seconds using a **linear segment with parabolic blends**. If the blend acceleration is fixed at $c = 30^{\circ}/\text{s}^{2}$, determine:
(i) the blend time $t_b$,
(ii) the peak velocity $v$,
(iii) the duration of the linear (constant-velocity) phase $t_l$.

**(b)** Discuss the principal **path-planning methods** used for robotic manipulators, including graph-search, cell-decomposition, and potential-field approaches. Highlight the strengths and weaknesses of each.

---

#### **Option B — Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities.**

$$
\theta_0 = 0^{\circ}, \quad \theta_f = 60^{\circ}, \quad T = 5\,\text{s}, \quad c = 30^{\circ}/\text{s}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LPB relations derived in Section 3.2.**

The peak velocity and the blend time are related by $v = c \cdot t_b$. The total time is $T = 2 t_b + t_l$. The total displacement relation is:

$$
\theta_f - \theta_0 = v(t_l + t_b)
$$

**Step 3 — Choose $t_b$ from physical reasoning.**

In the LPB profile, the blend time is the time taken to ramp velocity from 0 to $v$ at acceleration $c$. A common choice (and the one usually expected in KTU problems) is to use the **acceleration limit** directly:

$$
t_b = \dfrac{T - \dfrac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{c \cdot T_0}}{...} \quad \text{(rearranged as below)}
$$

A more direct route is to use the standard result that arises from the displacement and total-time constraints together:

$$
v = \dfrac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{T - t_b}, \qquad v = c\, t_b
$$

Equating:

$$
c\, t_b = \dfrac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{T - t_b}
$$

$$
c\, t_b (T - t_b) = \theta_f - \theta_0
$$

$$
c\, t_b T - c\, t_b^{2} = \theta_f - \theta_0
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numbers.**

$$
30\, t_b (5 - t_b) = 60
$$

$$
150\, t_b - 30\, t_b^{2} = 60
$$

$$
30\, t_b^{2} - 150\, t_b + 60 = 0
$$

Divide by 30:

$$
t_b^{2} - 5 t_b + 2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$
t_b = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 8}}{2} = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{17}}{2}
$$

$$
t_b = \dfrac{5 + 4.123}{2} = 4.56\,\text{s} \quad \text{or} \quad t_b = \dfrac{5 - 4.123}{2} = 0.438\,\text{s}
$$

The first root ($4.56$ s) is **inadmissible** because $2 t_b$ would exceed $T$, leaving no room for a linear phase. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{t_b \approx 0.438\,\text{s}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute peak velocity.**

$$
v = c\, t_b = 30 \times 0.438 = 13.15\,\,^{\circ}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute linear-phase duration.**

$$
t_l = T - 2 t_b = 5 - 2(0.438) = 5 - 0.876 = 4.124\,\text{s}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Blend time $t_b$ | $0.438$ s |
| Peak velocity $v$ | $13.15^{\circ}$/s |
| Linear-phase duration $t_l$ | $4.124$ s |

> *(Mark split: 2 for setting up equations, 1 for solving the quadratic, 1 for physical rejection of inadmissible root, 1 for peak velocity, 1 for linear-phase duration, 1 for units and final answer.)*

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three principal classical methods for path planning in robotics:**

**(1) Graph-Search / Roadmap Methods (e.g., Visibility Graphs, Voronoi Diagrams):**
- The free configuration space $C_{free}$ is reduced to a one-dimensional network of curves (a "roadmap").
- Path planning becomes a graph-search problem solved by algorithms such as $A^{\star}$ or Dijkstra.
- *Strengths:* optimality guarantees; well-suited for 2-D workspaces.
- *Weaknesses:* curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional joint spaces; difficult to construct the roadmap in cluttered 3-D environments.

**(2) Cell-Decomposition Methods:**
- The free space is partitioned into a finite set of convex cells (regular grid or adaptive decomposition).
- Adjacent free cells are connected; a path is found by a search through the connectivity graph.
- *Strengths:* complete (guaranteed to find a solution if one exists).
- *Weaknesses:* resolution-dependent; high memory usage in 3-D; the resulting path is often jagged and must be post-smoothed.

**(3) Artificial Potential-Field Methods:**
- The goal exerts an attractive potential $U_{att}$ and obstacles exert repulsive potentials $U_{rep}$.
- The robot moves along the negative gradient of the total potential: $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$.
- *Strengths:* simple, real-time implementable, naturally smooth motion.
- *Weaknesses:* susceptible to **local minima** (the robot gets stuck in a trap); oscillation in narrow passages; no optimality guarantee.

**Summary table:**

| Method | Completeness | Optimality | Real-time | Curse of Dimensionality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Graph search (roadmap) | Yes (if roadmap exists) | Yes (with $A^{\star}$) | Moderate | High impact |
| Cell decomposition | Yes | Approximate | Slow for fine grids | Very high impact |
| Potential field | No (local minima) | No | Excellent | Low impact |

> *(Mark split: 1.5 marks per method description, 1 mark per strength, 1 mark per weakness, 0.5 mark for the summary table.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up "path" and "trajectory" in definitions.** A path is geometry; a trajectory is geometry + time. Examiners deduct 1 mark instantly if these terms are used interchangeably. *(Loss: 1–2 marks.)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the inverse-kinematics step in Cartesian-space problems.** Whenever you state that a Cartesian trajectory is to be followed, you **must** mention IK; otherwise, the question is considered half-answered. *(Loss: 1 mark.)*
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistencies in polynomial coefficients.** A cubic coefficient has units of position/time³. KTU graders specifically scan for the dimensional check. Always write the unit next to each $a_i$. *(Loss: 0.5–1 mark.)*
>
> 4. **Choosing the inadmissible root of a quadratic in LPB problems.** Two roots almost always appear; the one that gives $2 t_b > T$ must be rejected with a one-line justification. Students who blindly write down the larger root lose 1 mark. *(Loss: 1 mark.)*
>
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions before starting a derivation.** Always write the four (cubic) or six (quintic) boundary conditions as a *separate line* at the top of the solution. KTU rubrics award 1 mark for this explicit listing. *(Loss: 1 mark.)*
>
> 6. **Confusing acceleration with jerk.** Acceleration is the second derivative; jerk is the third. A cubic trajectory has a **discontinuous** jerk, which is why quintics are preferred in high-performance applications. Examiners love testing this. *(Loss: 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Path & Trajectory Planning (KTU 2024, Module 4)**

- **Path planning = geometry only; trajectory planning = geometry + time.** This is the single most-tested distinction.
- **Joint-space planning** is computationally cheap and singularity-safe; **Cartesian-space planning** is geometrically accurate but IK-intensive.
- A cubic polynomial has **4 coefficients** and requires **4 boundary conditions** (two positions + two velocities).
- The canonical cubic coefficients (zero start/end velocity) are $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 3(\theta_f - \theta_0)/T^{2}$, $a_3 = -2(\theta_f - \theta_0)/T^{3}$.
- Peak velocity of a cubic (with zero end velocities) occurs at $t = T/2$ and equals $1.5 (\theta_f - \theta_0)/T$.
- Peak acceleration of a cubic (with zero end velocities) is $6(\theta_f - \theta_0)/T^{2}$ in magnitude.
- A **quintic polynomial** has 6 coefficients and adds two acceleration boundary conditions, giving $C^{2}$ continuity and bounded jerk.
- The **LPB profile** has two parabolic blends of duration $t_b$ surrounding a linear segment of duration $t_l$, with $T = 2 t_b + t_l$.
- The master LPB equations are $v = c\, t_b$ and $v = (\theta_f - \theta_0)/(T - t_b)$.
- **Three classical path-planning methods**: roadmap (graph search), cell decomposition, and potential fields.
- **Potential fields** are fast but suffer from local minima; **roadmaps** are complete and optimal but suffer from the curse of dimensionality; **cell decomposition** is complete but resolution-dependent.
- Always **list boundary conditions explicitly** before a derivation; this earns easy marks.
- Always **state units** next to polynomial coefficients and physical quantities.
- **Jerk** (third derivative) is discontinuous for cubic trajectories — this is the chief motivation for quintics in modern industrial controllers.
- A trajectory must satisfy: smoothness ($C^{2}$), bounded velocity, bounded acceleration, passage through via points, and (preferably) minimum execution time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint Space versus Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joint Space versus Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Joint Space versus Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Trajectory Planning** in robotics is the temporal (time-based) specification of a robot manipulator's motion, describing how it moves from an initial configuration to a final configuration while satisfying kinematic and dynamic constraints. The planning can be carried out in two fundamentally different mathematical spaces:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Space Trajectory Planning:** A path-planning methodology in which the trajectory is parameterized as a function of time directly in terms of the manipulator's **joint variables** $q_i(t)$, $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$. All intermediate configurations between the start and goal are specified entirely in joint coordinates. No explicit consideration of the end-effector's geometric shape in 3-D space is made during trajectory generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning:** A path-planning methodology in which the trajectory of the **end-effector pose** $\mathbf{X}(t) = [x(t), y(t), z(t), \phi(t), \theta(t), \psi(t)]^T$ is specified as a function of time in the Cartesian (task) space. The corresponding joint trajectories $q_i(t)$ are subsequently obtained by applying the **inverse kinematics** mapping $\mathbf{q}(t) = \mathcal{K}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}(t))$ at every discrete time step.

The chosen space determines the **mathematical structure of the path**, the **computational overhead**, the **kinematic smoothness**, and the **physical realizability** of the motion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tourist Map" Mental Model

Imagine you are driving from Calicut (Kozhikode) to Kochi.

- **Joint Space Planning** is like giving your driver a list of **steering-wheel rotations and gear changes** ("turn wheel 30° clockwise, shift to 3rd gear, accelerate for 12 seconds…"). The driver follows the commands directly without ever thinking about the road map. The exact path on the road map is irrelevant — the car simply responds to actuator commands.
- **Cartesian Space Planning** is like giving the driver a **GPS route** drawn directly on a city map — "follow MG Road straight for 4 km, then turn right at High Court Jn." Each landmark along the route is a Cartesian waypoint. The car has to *convert* every map instruction ("turn right") into a series of steering wheel and pedal actions in real time.

A robotic manipulator behaves the same way: its **actuators** live in joint space, but the **task** (welding, painting, pick-and-place) is naturally described in Cartesian space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Joint space = where the robot *lives*. Cartesian space = where the robot *works*. The trajectory planner is the **translator** between these two worlds.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of freedom | $n$ | typically **6** for serial manipulators |
| Task space dimension | $m$ | typically **6** (3 position + 3 orientation) |
| Joint velocity bound | $\dot{q}_{i,\max}$ | rad/s (manufacturer specified) |
| Joint acceleration bound | $\ddot{q}_{i,\max}$ | rad/s² |
| Cartesian linear velocity bound | $v_{\max}$ | typically **0.5–2.0 m/s** |
| Cartesian linear acceleration bound | $a_{\max}$ | typically **2.5–10 m/s²** |
| Trajectory sampling period | $T_s$ | typically **1 ms – 10 ms** |
| Cubic polynomial duration | $t_f$ | s (setpoint-dependent) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Joint Space Trajectory Planning — Operational Logic

The manipulator motion is governed by **scalar polynomial functions** of time for each joint independently.

**Step 1 — Specify Boundary Conditions.** At start time $t = t_0$ and end time $t = t_f$, both joint position and joint velocity are prescribed:

$$q_i(t_0) = q_{i,0}, \quad q_i(t_f) = q_{i,f}, \quad \dot{q}_i(t_0) = \dot{q}_{i,0}, \quad \dot{q}_i(t_f) = \dot{q}_{i,f}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Polynomial Family.** A polynomial of degree $n$ possesses $n+1$ coefficients. The number of boundary conditions determines the minimum polynomial degree. The most common families are:

- **Cubic polynomial** ($n = 3$) → handles 4 boundary conditions (start/end position + start/end velocity).
- **Quintic polynomial** ($n = 5$) → handles 6 boundary conditions (also start/end acceleration).
- **Parabolic blends with linear segment** → 2 parabolic sections joined to a linear section.

**Step 3 — Solve for Coefficients.** Apply the boundary conditions to the general polynomial form and solve the resulting linear system:

$$q_i(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 4 — Synchronize Across Joints.** All joints share the **same time base** $t \in [t_0, t_f]$, guaranteeing coordinated motion (the end-effector does not arrive at the goal before its base does, or vice versa).

**Step 5 — Check Actuator Limits.** Verify that $\vert \dot{q}_i(t) \vert \leq \dot{q}_{i,\max}$ and $\vert \ddot{q}_i(t) \vert \leq \ddot{q}_{i,\max}$ for all $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works well:** Each joint's trajectory is decoupled from the others. Planning is reduced to **$n$ independent scalar problems**, which is computationally trivial and inherently avoids singularities inside the joint domain.

### 2.2 Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning — Operational Logic

The end-effector pose is prescribed as a function of time in task space.

**Step 1 — Specify Cartesian Waypoints.** Define a sequence of poses:

$$\mathbf{X}_k = [x_k, y_k, z_k, \phi_k, \theta_k, \psi_k]^T, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Path Geometry.** Common geometries include:

- **Straight-line segments** between waypoints (linear interpolation of pose).
- **Circular arcs** for smooth corner transitions.
- **Spline curves** in $\mathbb{R}^3$ for free-form paths.

**Step 3 — Generate Pose as a Function of Time.** Apply scalar interpolation (linear, cubic, or quintic) to each component of $\mathbf{X}(t)$:

$$x(t) = x_0 + (x_f - x_0) s(t), \quad y(t) = \text{similar}, \quad z(t) = \text{similar}$$

where $s(t) \in [0,1]$ is the **normalized time function** $s(t) = (t - t_0)/t_f$ for linear, or a cubic profile for smooth motion.

**Step 4 — Solve Inverse Kinematics at Every Sample Time.** At each $T_s$:

$$\mathbf{q}(k T_s) = \mathcal{K}^{-1}\bigl(\mathbf{X}(k T_s)\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Check Singularity Avoidance.** Verify that the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is non-singular along the path.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Computational Burden:** For a 6-DOF manipulator, inverse kinematics must be computed at every $T_s$. This is **$O(10^3)$ – $O(10^4)$ times** more expensive than joint space planning.

### 2.3 Comparative Anatomy — Joint Space vs. Cartesian Space

| Attribute | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|---|---|---|
| **Variables used** | Joint angles $q_i(t)$ | End-effector pose $\mathbf{X}(t)$ |
| **Path shape in 3-D** | Uncontrolled (arbitrary curve) | User-defined (straight line, arc, spline) |
| **Singularity handling** | Inherently safe | Must explicitly check $\det(\mathbf{J}) \neq 0$ |
| **Inverse kinematics needed** | **No** | **Yes — every sample period** |
| **Computational cost** | Very low | High (iterative IK) |
| **Joint-limit violation** | Easy to enforce as bounds | Difficult — only visible *after* IK |
| **Natural task description** | Poor for tasks needing straight-line motion | Excellent (welding, painting, gluing) |
| **Synchronization** | Automatic (same time base) | Automatic (same time base) |
| **Redundancy exploitation** | Possible (extra DOF used to optimize) | Possible (null-space projection) |
| **Redundancy resolution** | Direct | Indirect (extra DOF gradient methods) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Cubic joint trajectory (4 BCs) |
| 2 | $\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$ | Joint velocity from cubic |
| 3 | $\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$ | Joint acceleration from cubic |
| 4 | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | Quintic trajectory (6 BCs) |
| 5 | $\ddot{q}(t) = 2a_2 + 6a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3$ | Quintic acceleration |
| 6 | $\mathbf{q}(t) = \mathcal{K}^{-1}\bigl(\mathbf{X}(t)\bigr)$ | Cartesian → joint conversion |
| 7 | $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Forward differential kinematics |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{\mathbf{X}}$ | Inverse differential kinematics |
| 9 | $s(t) = 3\left(\tfrac{t}{t_f}\right)^2 - 2\left(\tfrac{t}{t_f}\right)^3$ | Normalized cubic (Hertz) blend |
| 10 | $t_b = \tfrac{\dot{q}_0 - \dot{q}_f}{a_c}$ | Parabolic blend duration |
| 11 | $\ddot{q}(t) = \pm a_c$ (constant) | Parabolic segment acceleration |
| 12 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\max} = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\, \dot{\mathbf{X}}_{\max}$ | Joint velocity limit from Cartesian bound |
| 13 | $\sigma(t) = \sqrt{\det\bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T\bigr)}$ | Manipulability (singularity metric) |
| 14 | $a_{\max} = \dot{q}_{i,\max}^2 / \Delta q_i$ | Max allowable acceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Convention Used:** All absolute values and norms are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid corrupting markdown table syntax. Symbols like $\vert \dot{q}_i \vert$ are correctly rendered as mathematical absolute value.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Joint Space** is preferred in **high-speed pick-and-place** (SCARA, Delta robots) where path shape is irrelevant and cycle time is critical.
- **Cartesian Space** is mandatory in **welding, painting, sealing, glue dispensing, waterjet cutting, laser cutting, and CNC-style finishing**, where the tool must follow a precise geometric path.
- **Hybrid strategies** are common in industrial controllers (e.g., ABB, KUKA, FANUC): Cartesian path for the **tool-center-point (TCP)**, joint-space optimization for **redundancy resolution** and **collision avoidance**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cubic Polynomial Joint Trajectory

**Problem Statement.** A single joint must move from $q_0$ at $t = 0$ to $q_f$ at $t = t_f$. The joint is at rest at both endpoints, i.e. $\dot{q}(0) = 0$ and $\dot{q}(t_f) = 0$. Find the cubic polynomial $q(t)$.

**General Cubic Form.**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1\, t + a_2\, t^2 + a_3\, t^3
$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 1 — Initial Position.** Setting $t = 0$:

$$
q(0) = a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0)^2 + a_3(0)^3 = a_0 = q_0
$$

Therefore $a_0 = q_0$.

**Apply Boundary Condition 2 — Final Position.** Setting $t = t_f$:

$$
q(t_f) = a_0 + a_1\, t_f + a_2\, t_f^2 + a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f
$$

$$
q_0 + a_1\, t_f + a_2\, t_f^2 + a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f
$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 3 — Initial Velocity.** Differentiating $q(t)$ once:

$$
\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2\, a_2\, t + 3\, a_3\, t^2
$$

Setting $t = 0$:

$$
\dot{q}(0) = a_1 + 0 + 0 = a_1 = 0
$$

Therefore $a_1 = 0$.

**Apply Boundary Condition 4 — Final Velocity.** Setting $t = t_f$:

$$
\dot{q}(t_f) = a_1 + 2\, a_2\, t_f + 3\, a_3\, t_f^2 = 0
$$

Since $a_1 = 0$:

$$
2\, a_2\, t_f + 3\, a_3\, t_f^2 = 0
$$

**Solve the 2×2 Linear System.** Substituting $a_1 = 0$ into the position equation:

$$
q_0 + a_2\, t_f^2 + a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2\, t_f^2 + a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f - q_0
$$

From the velocity equation:

$$
2\, a_2\, t_f + 3\, a_3\, t_f^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = -\tfrac{3}{2}\, a_3\, t_f
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\left(-\tfrac{3}{2}\, a_3\, t_f\right) t_f^2 + a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f - q_0
$$

$$
-\tfrac{3}{2}\, a_3\, t_f^3 + a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f - q_0
$$

$$
-\tfrac{1}{2}\, a_3\, t_f^3 = q_f - q_0
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{2\, (q_0 - q_f)}{t_f^3}
$$

And therefore:

$$
a_2 = -\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2\, (q_0 - q_f)}{t_f^3} \cdot t_f = \frac{3\, (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}
$$

**Final Compact Cubic Trajectory.**

$$
q(t) = q_0 + \frac{3\,(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}\, t^2 - \frac{2\,(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}\, t^3
$$

The corresponding velocity and acceleration are:

$$
\dot{q}(t) = \frac{6\,(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}\, t - \frac{6\,(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}\, t^2
$$

$$
\ddot{q}(t) = \frac{6\,(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{12\,(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}\, t
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Segment with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)

**Problem Statement.** A joint must move from $q_0$ to $q_f$ in time $t_f$, with constant cruise velocity $\dot{q}_c$ in the linear middle region, and constant acceleration $a_c$ in the parabolic blend regions.

**Structure of the Trajectory.** The motion consists of three regions:

- **Region I (Blending in):** $t \in [0, t_b]$, $\ddot{q}(t) = +a_c$, linear velocity ramp-up.
- **Region II (Cruise):** $t \in [t_b, t_f - t_b]$, $\ddot{q}(t) = 0$, constant velocity.
- **Region III (Blending out):** $t \in [t_f - t_b, t_f]$, $\ddot{q}(t) = -a_c$, velocity ramp-down.

**Step 1 — Solve the Blending Region.** Integrating $\ddot{q} = a_c$ twice with $\dot{q}(0) = 0$ and $q(0) = q_0$:

$$
\dot{q}(t) = a_c\, t, \quad q(t) = q_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\, a_c\, t^2
$$

At the end of Region I ($t = t_b$):

$$
\dot{q}_c = a_c\, t_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_b = \dot{q}_c / a_c
$$

$$
q_b = q_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\, a_c\, t_b^2 = q_0 + \dot{q}_c^2 / (2 a_c)
$$

**Step 2 — Cruise Region.** Velocity remains constant at $\dot{q}_c$ for duration $(t_f - 2 t_b)$:

$$
q(t_f - t_b) = q_b + \dot{q}_c\, (t_f - 2 t_b)
$$

**Step 3 — Symmetry Condition.** For symmetric blending, the parabolic blends must meet perfectly. The total displacement is:

$$
q_f - q_0 = \dot{q}_c\, t_f - \dot{q}_c^2 / a_c
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\dot{q}_c$.**

$$
\dot{q}_c\, t_f - \frac{\dot{q}_c^2}{a_c} = q_f - q_0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{a_c}\, \dot{q}_c^2 - t_f\, \dot{q}_c + (q_f - q_0) = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
\dot{q}_c = \frac{a_c\, t_f \pm \sqrt{a_c^2 t_f^2 - 4 a_c (q_f - q_0)}}{2}
$$

The **physically realizable** (smaller positive) root is selected to keep $t_b > 0$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cartesian Linear Interpolation with Inverse Kinematics

**Problem Statement.** The end-effector must move in a straight line in Cartesian space from $\mathbf{X}_0 = [x_0, y_0, z_0]^T$ to $\mathbf{X}_f = [x_f, y_f, z_f]^T$ in time $t_f$.

**Step 1 — Linear Pose Trajectory.**

$$
\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{X}_0 + \frac{t}{t_f}\, (\mathbf{X}_f - \mathbf{X}_0), \quad t \in [0, t_f]
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate for Cartesian Velocity.**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{X}}(t) = \frac{1}{t_f}\, (\mathbf{X}_f - \mathbf{X}_0)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Inverse Differential Kinematics.** Using the Jacobian pseudo-inverse:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{\mathbf{X}}(t)
$$

For redundant systems ($n > m$):

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\, \dot{\mathbf{X}} + \bigl(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J}\bigr)\, \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Numerically.** Starting from $\mathbf{q}(0) = \mathcal{K}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_0)$:

$$
\mathbf{q}(k T_s) = \mathbf{q}\bigl((k-1) T_s\bigr) + T_s\, \dot{\mathbf{q}}\bigl((k-1) T_s\bigr)
$$

for $k = 1, 2, \dots, t_f/T_s$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cubic Trajectory Coefficients for All Joints

For an $n$-DOF manipulator, every joint has its own cubic with its own boundary conditions:

$$
q_i(t) = a_{i0} + a_{i1}\, t + a_{i2}\, t^2 + a_{i3}\, t^3, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

With the rest-to-rest cubic coefficients:

$$
a_{i0} = q_{i,0}, \quad a_{i1} = 0, \quad a_{i2} = \frac{3\,(q_{i,f} - q_{i,0})}{t_f^2}, \quad a_{i3} = \frac{-2\,(q_{i,f} - q_{i,0})}{t_f^3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When asked "show that joints arrive simultaneously," the answer is: *because all $n$ cubic polynomials are evaluated at the same terminal time $t = t_f$, every joint reaches its goal exactly at $t_f$ regardless of the magnitude of $(q_{i,f} - q_{i,0})$.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Joint Space Trajectory Planning — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User specifies start and goal joint angles q0 and qf] --> B[Choose common trajectory duration tf]
    B --> C[For each joint i from 1 to n]
    C --> D[Set boundary conditions: qi at t equals 0, qi at t equals tf, velocity zero at both ends]
    D --> E[Solve cubic polynomial coefficients a0, a1, a2, a3]
    E --> F[Store trajectory function qi of t]
    F --> G{All joints processed?}
    G -->|No| C
    G -->|Yes| H[Output vector of joint trajectories q1 of t up to qn of t]
    H --> I[Real time execution: sample every Ts milliseconds]
    I --> J[Send qi of kTs and dqi by dt to joint actuators]
    J --> K[End: Manipulator moves with synchronized joint motion]
```

### 4.2 Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: User specifies Cartesian waypoints X0, X1 up to Xn] --> Q[Choose path geometry: straight line, arc, or spline]
    Q --> R[Choose trajectory time law: linear, cubic, or quintic]
    R --> S[Generate continuous pose trajectory X of t in R6]
    S --> T[For each sampling instant kTs]
    T --> U[Compute desired Cartesian velocity Xdot of kTs]
    U --> V[Compute inverse Jacobian J inverse at current q]
    V --> W[Compute joint velocity qdot equals J inverse times Xdot]
    W --> X{Manipulability measure OK and det J nonzero?}
    X -->|No| Y[Apply singularity avoidance or null space projection]
    Y --> V
    X -->|Yes| Z[Integrate qdot to obtain q of k plus 1 Ts]
    Z --> AA[Send q and qdot to joint actuators]
    AA --> AB{kTs equals tf?}
    AB -->|No| T
    AB -->|Yes| AC[End: End effector has traced geometric path in Cartesian space]
```

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison — Joint Space vs. Cartesian Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JS[Joint Space Path]
        JS1[Start joint angles q0 vector] --> JS2[Cubic polynomial in joint angles]
        JS2 --> JS3[Direct actuator command every Ts]
        JS3 --> JS4[End joint angles qf vector]
    end
    subgraph CS[Cartesian Space Path]
        CS1[Start pose X0 in R6] --> CS2[Interpolate pose linearly in R6]
        CS2 --> CS3[Inverse kinematics at every sample]
        CS3 --> CS4[Inverse Jacobian converts Xdot to qdot]
        CS4 --> CS5[Integrate to obtain q of t]
        CS5 --> CS6[Send to joint actuators]
    end
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Data Flow — From Task to Motion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Task description in Cartesian coordinates] --> T2{Choose planning space}
    T2 -->|Joint space| T3[Convert via IK once at endpoints only]
    T2 -->|Cartesian space| T4[Interpolate pose in Cartesian frame]
    T3 --> T5[Scalar polynomial per joint]
    T4 --> T6[Inverse kinematics per sampling instant]
    T5 --> T7[Actuator command]
    T6 --> T7
    T7 --> T8[Robot motion]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Function Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Joint Space Strength: No IK required] --> F2[Joint Space Weakness: Unknown Cartesian path]
    F3[Cartesian Space Strength: User defined geometric path] --> F4[Cartesian Space Weakness: High IK computational cost]
    F2 --> F5[Tradeoff: Speed versus Path Precision]
    F4 --> F5
    F5 --> F6[Industrial practice: hybrid strategy with Cartesian outer loop and joint space inner loop]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**  
**"Differentiate between Joint Space and Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning with respect to any three parameters."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Expected Length: 3–5 lines):**

| Parameter | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|---|---|---|
| Variables used | Joint angles $q_i(t)$ | End-effector pose $\mathbf{X}(t)$ |
| Inverse kinematics required | Not required (only at endpoints) | Required at every sampling instant |
| Path shape | Uncontrolled, arbitrary | User-defined straight line, arc, or spline |

**Awarding scheme:** 1 mark per correctly identified difference with brief justification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
**"State any three advantages and any three disadvantages of Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning over Joint Space Trajectory Planning."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Expected Length: 6 lines):**

**Advantages:**  
(1) End-effector follows a user-defined geometric path (essential for welding, painting, etc.).  
(2) Singularities are explicitly detectable via manipulability index.  
(3) Task description is intuitive in Cartesian coordinates.

**Disadvantages:**  
(1) Inverse kinematics must be solved at every sample period (computationally expensive).  
(2) Joint limit violations appear *after* IK and are difficult to predict.  
(3) Path may pass through kinematic singularities, causing unbounded joint velocities.

**Awarding scheme:** 0.5 marks per correct point, 3 points for full marks.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **"[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set 2]"**
>
> **(a)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator must move its joints from $\mathbf{q}_0 = [0°, 30°]^T$ to $\mathbf{q}_f = [60°, 90°]^T$ in time $t_f = 4$ s. Both joints start and end at rest. **Derive the cubic polynomial trajectory** for each joint and obtain the velocity and acceleration at $t = 2$ s. Assume $\dot{q}_1(0) = \dot{q}_2(0) = 0$, $\dot{q}_1(t_f) = \dot{q}_2(t_f) = 0$. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** With reference to Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning, explain the **role of the inverse Jacobian $\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})$** in computing joint trajectories from a prescribed end-effector path. Discuss the case when the manipulator is redundant and how the **null-space projection** term can be used to optimize secondary objectives. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Cubic Polynomial Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — General Cubic Form for Joint 1.** Let $q_1(t) = a_{10} + a_{11} t + a_{12} t^2 + a_{13} t^3$. Boundary conditions: $q_1(0) = 0°$, $q_1(4) = 60°$, $\dot{q}_1(0) = 0$, $\dot{q}_1(4) = 0$.

$$
a_{10} = 0, \quad a_{11} = 0
$$

Solving the remaining 2×2 system (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
a_{12} = \frac{3\,(60 - 0)}{4^2} = \frac{180}{16} = 11.25\ \text{deg/s}^2
$$

$$
a_{13} = \frac{-2\,(60 - 0)}{4^3} = \frac{-120}{64} = -1.875\ \text{deg/s}^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
q_1(t) = 11.25\, t^2 - 1.875\, t^3 \quad \text{(deg)}
$$

**Step 2 — Joint 2.** Let $q_2(t) = a_{20} + a_{21} t + a_{22} t^2 + a_{23} t^3$. Boundary conditions: $q_2(0) = 30°$, $q_2(4) = 90°$, $\dot{q}_2(0) = 0$, $\dot{q}_2(4) = 0$.

$$
a_{20} = 30, \quad a_{21} = 0
$$

$$
a_{22} = \frac{3\,(90 - 30)}{4^2} = \frac{180}{16} = 11.25\ \text{deg/s}^2
$$

$$
a_{23} = \frac{-2\,(90 - 30)}{4^3} = \frac{-120}{64} = -1.875\ \text{deg/s}^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
q_2(t) = 30 + 11.25\, t^2 - 1.875\, t^3 \quad \text{(deg)}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity at $t = 2$ s.** General velocity: $\dot{q}_i(t) = 2 a_{i2} t + 3 a_{i3} t^2$.

For Joint 1:

$$
\dot{q}_1(2) = 2(11.25)(2) + 3(-1.875)(4) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5\ \text{deg/s}
$$

For Joint 2:

$$
\dot{q}_2(2) = 2(11.25)(2) + 3(-1.875)(4) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5\ \text{deg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration at $t = 2$ s.** General acceleration: $\ddot{q}_i(t) = 2 a_{i2} + 6 a_{i3} t$.

For Joint 1:

$$
\ddot{q}_1(2) = 2(11.25) + 6(-1.875)(2) = 22.5 - 22.5 = 0\ \text{deg/s}^2
$$

For Joint 2 (identical):

$$
\ddot{q}_2(2) = 0\ \text{deg/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Both joints have zero acceleration at the **midpoint**, which is characteristic of cubic polynomials with symmetric rest-to-rest boundary conditions.

**Mark Allocation (Part a):**
- BC identification and general form: 1 mark
- Correct coefficient $a_2$ for both joints: 2 marks
- Correct coefficient $a_3$ for both joints: 2 marks
- Velocity and acceleration at $t = 2$ s: 2 marks

---

#### Part (b) — Inverse Jacobian & Null-Space Projection (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Role of Inverse Jacobian.** The forward differential kinematics establishes the linear map:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Given a desired Cartesian velocity $\dot{\mathbf{X}}$, the joint velocity is recovered by:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{\mathbf{X}}
$$

for a **square Jacobian** ($n = m = 6$). This conversion must be performed **at every sampling instant** during Cartesian trajectory execution, because the relationship is configuration-dependent.

**Step 2 — Singular Configurations.** When $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$, the inverse Jacobian does not exist, and infinitesimal $\dot{\mathbf{X}}$ directions can produce unbounded $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. Manipulability index $\sigma = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ is monitored as a singularity metric.

**Step 3 — Redundant Manipulators ($n > m$).** When the manipulator has more degrees of freedom than the task dimension, the system $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is **under-determined**. The Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse $\mathbf{J}^{\dagger} = \mathbf{J}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1}$ gives the **minimum-norm solution**:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\min} = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\, \dot{\mathbf{X}}
$$

**Step 4 — Null-Space Projection.** The general solution is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\, \dot{\mathbf{X}} + \bigl(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J}\bigr)\, \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

The matrix $\bigl(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J}\bigr)$ projects any vector $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ into the **null space of $\mathbf{J}$**, where motion is produced in joint space *without* affecting the end-effector pose. This enables secondary objectives:

- **Joint-limit avoidance:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = -\nabla H(\mathbf{q})$ for potential function $H$.
- **Obstacle avoidance:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = -\nabla d(\mathbf{q})$ for distance to obstacle.
- **Singularity avoidance:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = \nabla \sigma(\mathbf{q})$.

**Mark Allocation (Part b):**
- Forward and inverse differential kinematics relation: 2 marks
- Singularity handling and manipulability: 2 marks
- Null-space projection for redundancy resolution: 3 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **"[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]"**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **linear segment with parabolic blends (LSPB)** trajectory for a single joint moving from $q_0 = 0$ rad to $q_f = 2$ rad in time $t_f = 4$ s with constant blend acceleration $a_c = 4$ rad/s². Find the cruise velocity $\dot{q}_c$ and the blend time $t_b$. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain with a suitable **real-world application example** why **Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning is mandatory** in industrial robotic welding. Discuss two limitations of Joint Space Trajectory Planning that make it unsuitable for such tasks. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — LSPB Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Displacement Constraint (derived in Section 3.2).**

$$
q_f - q_0 = \dot{q}_c\, t_f - \frac{\dot{q}_c^2}{a_c}
$$

Substituting $q_f - q_0 = 2$ rad, $t_f = 4$ s, $a_c = 4$ rad/s²:

$$
2 = 4\, \dot{q}_c - \frac{\dot{q}_c^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into Quadratic Form.**

$$
\frac{\dot{q}_c^2}{4} - 4\, \dot{q}_c + 2 = 0
$$

Multiplying by 4:

$$
\dot{q}_c^2 - 16\, \dot{q}_c + 8 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Quadratic Formula.**

$$
\dot{q}_c = \frac{16 \pm \sqrt{256 - 32}}{2} = \frac{16 \pm \sqrt{224}}{2} = \frac{16 \pm 14.97}{2}
$$

Two roots: $\dot{q}_c = 15.49$ rad/s or $\dot{q}_c = 0.515$ rad/s.

**Step 4 — Select the Smaller Realizable Root.** The larger root produces $t_b < 0$ (physically unrealizable), so we select:

$$
\dot{q}_c = 0.515\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Blend Time.**

$$
t_b = \dot{q}_c / a_c = 0.515 / 4 \approx 0.129\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify.** Check that $2 t_b < t_f$: $2 \times 0.129 = 0.258 < 4$ s. ✓ Cruise duration is positive.

**Mark Allocation (Part a):**
- Correct displacement equation: 2 marks
- Correct quadratic formulation: 1 mark
- Correct quadratic solution: 2 marks
- Blend time computation: 1 mark
- Validity check: 1 mark

---

#### Part (b) — Welding Application & Limitations (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Welding Task Description.** In arc welding, the welding torch must trace a precise geometric path (straight line, weave pattern, or curved seam) along the workpiece. The **tool-center-point (TCP)** orientation must also remain fixed relative to the seam normal (typically tilted 5°–15° in the travel direction).

**Step 2 — Why Cartesian Space is Mandatory.**

- **Geometric accuracy:** A straight weld seam in 3-D Cartesian space cannot be guaranteed by joint-space interpolation because joint angles produce non-linear end-effector paths. Only Cartesian-space path geometry provides a verifiable straight line.
- **Orientation control:** The torch orientation $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t) = [\phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ is part of the pose vector $\mathbf{X}(t)$. Joint-space planning ignores orientation entirely.
- **Seam tracking:** Modern welding systems use **laser seam trackers** that update $\mathbf{X}(t)$ in real time. The controller must convert these updates to joint commands via IK every few milliseconds — fundamentally a Cartesian-space workflow.

**Step 3 — Two Limitations of Joint Space Planning for Welding.**

**(i) Uncontrolled Cartesian Path:** Even if joint angles are interpolated smoothly, the resulting end-effector trajectory in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is an *arc of a complex curve*, not a straight line. The weld seam cannot be followed faithfully.

**(ii) Singularity Incidence Inside Joint Range:** A straight line in Cartesian space may pass arbitrarily close to or through a kinematic singularity. Joint-space planning would never detect this; the operator specifies only start and goal joint angles and is blind to intermediate Cartesian behavior.

**(iii) (Bonus) Orientation Drift:** Joint-space planning does not parameterize orientation, so even if position tracking is acceptable, the torch tilt can vary unpredictably along the path, producing defective welds.

**Mark Allocation (Part b):**
- Welding task description with geometric & orientation requirements: 3 marks
- Two well-explained limitations of joint-space planning: 4 marks (2 marks each)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Cubic polynomial derivation:** Students often forget that the **time variable** $t$ is the independent variable, not the joint angle. Writing "$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 q + \dots$" loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Inverse Jacobian derivation:** Many students confuse $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ with $\mathbf{J}^{\dagger}$ (pseudo-inverse). The square matrix inverse exists only when $n = m = 6$. For redundant manipulators, **pseudo-inverse is mandatory** — this distinction is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 3. **LSPB problem:** Students often pick the **larger quadratic root** without checking whether the resulting $t_b$ is positive. Always verify $t_b > 0$.
> 4. **Cartesian vs. Joint Space comparison:** Simply listing "Cartesian uses IK" without explaining *why* (i.e., the mapping $\mathbf{q}(t) = \mathcal{K}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}(t))$) loses the conceptual depth mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joint Space vs. Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning**

- ✅ **Joint space** plans in $q_i(t)$; **Cartesian space** plans in $\mathbf{X}(t) = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$.
- ✅ **Cubic polynomial** handles 4 boundary conditions (positions + velocities); **quintic polynomial** handles 6 (adds accelerations).
- ✅ **Rest-to-rest cubic coefficients (zero initial and final velocity):** $a_0 = q_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 3(q_f - q_0)/t_f^2$, $a_3 = -2(q_f - q_0)/t_f^3$.
- ✅ **LSPB** uses constant-acceleration parabolic blends at the start and end, with a constant-velocity cruise in between.
- ✅ **LSPB blend time** is $t_b = \dot{q}_c / a_c$, computed from the quadratic $\dot{q}_c^2/a_c - t_f \dot{q}_c + (q_f - q_0) = 0$.
- ✅ **Cartesian planning** requires inverse kinematics at every sampling instant $T_s$.
- ✅ **Differential mapping** is $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, inverted as $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{X}}$.
- ✅ **Redundant manipulator** solution uses pseudo-inverse $\mathbf{J}^{\dagger}$ plus null-space projection $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J})\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$.
- ✅ **Joint-space advantage:** low computation, no IK, no singularities.
- ✅ **Cartesian-space advantage:** user-defined geometric path (mandatory for welding, painting, gluing).
- ✅ **Singularity metric:** manipulability $\sigma = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$.
- ✅ **Industrial practice:** Hybrid — Cartesian outer loop for path, joint-space inner loop for execution.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme mark weightage:** Module 4 typically contributes 14–20 marks in End Semester Exam; expect either a full 14-mark question or two 7-mark sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cubic Polynomial Based Trajectory Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cubic Polynomial Based Trajectory Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cubic Polynomial Trajectory Planning** is a *joint-space* path-generation technique in which the position of a robot joint as a function of time, $q(t)$, is approximated by a **third-degree polynomial** of time. Because a cubic polynomial possesses exactly **four independent coefficients**, it can satisfy four independent boundary conditions — most commonly the **start position**, **end position**, **start velocity**, and **end velocity** — making it the canonical method for generating smooth, single-segment trajectories between two waypoints.

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4 Highlight:** Cubic polynomials are the *first* (and most frequently examined) trajectory-generation method taught under "Joint-Space Trajectory Planning." It is a guaranteed topic for **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Part-B questions (14 marks)** and **frequent Part-A (3 marks)** questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Picture

Imagine you are driving a car from one traffic signal (Point A) to another (Point B).

- **Position** → your *location* at any instant.
- **Velocity** → your *speed*.
- **Acceleration** → how *fast you press/release* the accelerator.

If you start at A with some speed (say **0 km/h** if you were stopped) and want to arrive at B smoothly without jerking to a halt, the *position-versus-time* graph of your journey is **NOT a straight line** (that would be a trapezoidal-velocity, jerk-prone motion). Instead, it forms a gentle, curved **S-shape** known mathematically as a **cubic polynomial**.

The cubic polynomial acts like a *mathematical cruise control* that smoothly ramps your speed up at the start and gently tapers it down at the end — exactly the four boundary conditions the polynomial shape needs to "fit" your motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "cubic" and not quadratic?** A quadratic has only 3 coefficients and can match 3 conditions (e.g., start position, end position, mid-velocity). To match *both* initial AND final velocities, we need a *fourth* degree of freedom — hence the cubic. This is the single most important conceptual leap students must internalize.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Standard Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|------------------|
| Total trajectory time | $t_f$ | **1 s – 10 s** (application dependent) | Time to move from start to end waypoint |
| Initial joint angle | $q_0$ | Joint limit dependent | Starting position (rad or m) |
| Final joint angle | $q_f$ | Joint limit dependent | Ending position (rad or m) |
| Initial velocity | $\dot{q}_0$ | Often **0** (rest-to-rest) | Starting speed |
| Final velocity | $\dot{q}_f$ | Often **0** (rest-to-rest) | Ending speed |
| Acceleration continuity | $\ddot{q}(t)$ | Continuous (no jumps) | Smooth motor torque demand |

> [!TIP]
> In **rest-to-rest** motion (the default KTU textbook assumption), $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$, which dramatically simplifies the coefficient formulas. Always check the problem statement for this assumption — it saves calculation time and avoids sign errors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Mathematical "Why"

A cubic polynomial in time has the general form:

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

Taking successive time derivatives (because *position* alone is not enough — we must control *velocity* and *acceleration* to drive robot motors smoothly):

$$\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

$$\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$

The cubic has **exactly four unknown coefficients**: $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$. Therefore, we can — and must — supply **exactly four independent boundary conditions** to solve for them uniquely.

---

## 2.2 The Four Standard Boundary Conditions

For a trajectory spanning $t \in [0, t_f]$:

| # | Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Start position | $q(0) = q_0$ | Robot starts at known angle |
| 2 | Start velocity | $\dot{q}(0) = v_0$ | Known initial speed (often 0) |
| 3 | End position | $q(t_f) = q_f$ | Robot must reach target angle |
| 4 | End velocity | $\dot{q}(t_f) = v_f$ | Known final speed (often 0) |

---

## 2.3 The Canonical Solution

Plugging the boundary conditions into $q(t)$ and $\dot{q}(t)$ yields the **4×4 linear system**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 t_f & 3 t_f^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q_0 \\ v_0 \\ q_f \\ v_f \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving via Gaussian elimination (or Cramer's rule — see Section 3 for the full derivation) gives the closed-form expressions:

$$a_0 = q_0$$

$$a_1 = v_0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 v_0}{t_f} - \frac{v_f}{t_f}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2}$$

---

## 2.4 Special Case — The Rest-to-Rest Trajectory

When $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$ (robot starts and ends at rest), the formulas simplify to:

$$a_0 = q_0, \quad a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}$$

So the trajectory collapses to:

$$q(t) = q_0 + \left( \frac{3}{t_f^2} \right)(q_f - q_0) t^2 - \left( \frac{2}{t_f^3} \right)(q_f - q_0) t^3$$

This compact form is the version that appears in 90% of KTU exam problems.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Position trajectory | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | General cubic |
| 2 | Velocity trajectory | $\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$ | First derivative |
| 3 | Acceleration trajectory | $\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$ | Second derivative (linear in $t$) |
| 4 | $a_0$ | $q_0$ | Always |
| 5 | $a_1$ | $v_0$ | Always |
| 6 | $a_2$ (general) | $\frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 v_0}{t_f} - \frac{v_f}{t_f}$ | Any $v_0, v_f$ |
| 7 | $a_3$ (general) | $-\frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2}$ | Any $v_0, v_f$ |
| 8 | $a_2$ (rest-to-rest) | $\frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}$ | $v_0 = v_f = 0$ |
| 9 | $a_3$ (rest-to-rest) | $-\frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}$ | $v_0 = v_f = 0$ |
| 10 | Acceleration at midpoint | $\ddot{q}(t_f/2) = \dfrac{6(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}$ | Always for rest-to-rest |
| 11 | Peak velocity | $\dot{q}_{\max} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(q_f - q_0)}{t_f}$ at $t = t_f/2$ | Rest-to-rest case |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** Angles in **radians**, angular velocities in **rad/s**, angular accelerations in **rad/s²**. For prismatic joints, substitute metres, m/s, m/s² respectively. Always state units in your final answer — KTU examiners award **½ mark** for unit correctness in numerical problems.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Cubic Trajectory? |
|-------------|----------------------|
| **SCARA pick-and-place robots** | Smooth motion reduces vibration during high-speed part transfer |
| **CNC milling machines** | Jerk-limited profiles protect tool life and surface finish |
| **Surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci)** | Smooth joint trajectories are critical for patient safety |
| **6-DOF industrial arms** | Each joint independently follows its own cubic to achieve Cartesian straight-line end-effector motion |
| **Humanoid robot limbs** | Cubic base profiles are interpolated into quintic splines for natural walking |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the General Cubic Coefficients

We begin with the cubic and its derivative:

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

$$\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

### Step 1 — Apply the boundary condition $q(0) = q_0$

Substitute $t = 0$ into $q(t)$:

$$q(0) = a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0)^2 + a_3(0)^3 = a_0$$

Equating to $q_0$:

$$\boxed{a_0 = q_0}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition $\dot{q}(0) = v_0$

Substitute $t = 0$ into $\dot{q}(t)$:

$$\dot{q}(0) = a_1 + 2 a_2 (0) + 3 a_3 (0)^2 = a_1$$

Equating to $v_0$:

$$\boxed{a_1 = v_0}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition $q(t_f) = q_f$

Substitute $t = t_f$ into $q(t)$:

$$q(t_f) = a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f$$

Substitute the known values $a_0 = q_0$ and $a_1 = v_0$:

$$q_0 + v_0 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f$$

Rearranging:

$$a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f - q_0 - v_0 t_f \quad \cdots (\text{Eq. A})$$

### Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition $\dot{q}(t_f) = v_f$

Substitute $t = t_f$ into $\dot{q}(t)$:

$$\dot{q}(t_f) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = v_f$$

Substituting $a_1 = v_0$:

$$v_0 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = v_f$$

Rearranging:

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = v_f - v_0 \quad \cdots (\text{Eq. B})$$

### Step 5 — Solve the 2×2 system for $a_2$ and $a_3$

From Eq. A, multiply by $\frac{2}{t_f^2}$ to align with Eq. B's $2a_2 t_f$ coefficient:

$$\frac{2}{t_f^2} \cdot \left[ a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 \right] = \frac{2}{t_f^2}(q_f - q_0 - v_0 t_f)$$

This gives:

$$2 a_2 + 2 a_3 t_f = \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 v_0}{t_f}$$

Subtract this from Eq. B:

$$\left[ 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 \right] - t_f \cdot \left[ 2 a_2 + 2 a_3 t_f \right] = (v_f - v_0) - t_f \cdot \left[ \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 v_0}{t_f} \right]$$

Left side simplification:

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 - 2 a_2 t_f - 2 a_3 t_f^2 = a_3 t_f^2$$

Right side simplification:

$$v_f - v_0 - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f} + 2 v_0 = v_f + v_0 - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f}$$

So:

$$a_3 t_f^2 = v_f + v_0 - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f}$$

Dividing by $t_f^2$:

$$\boxed{a_3 = \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2} - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}}$$

### Step 6 — Back-substitute to find $a_2$

From Eq. B:

$$2 a_2 t_f = (v_f - v_0) - 3 a_3 t_f^2$$

Dividing by $2 t_f$:

$$a_2 = \frac{v_f - v_0}{2 t_f} - \frac{3 a_3 t_f}{2}$$

Substituting the value of $a_3$:

$$a_2 = \frac{v_f - v_0}{2 t_f} - \frac{3 t_f}{2}\left[ \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2} - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3} \right]$$

$$= \frac{v_f - v_0}{2 t_f} - \frac{3(v_0 + v_f)}{2 t_f} + \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}$$

Combining the first two terms:

$$\frac{v_f - v_0 - 3 v_0 - 3 v_f}{2 t_f} = \frac{-2 v_f - 4 v_0}{2 t_f} = -\frac{2 v_0 + v_f}{t_f}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 v_0}{t_f} - \frac{v_f}{t_f}}$$

This matches the result stated in Section 2.3. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — The KTU Board Exam Style

**Problem Statement:** A single robot joint must move from $\theta_0 = 20°$ to $\theta_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 4$ seconds using a cubic polynomial trajectory. The joint starts and ends at rest. Compute all four coefficients, the velocity at $t = 2$ s, and the peak velocity.

### Step 1 — Convert units to radians (KTU convention)

$$\theta_0 = 20° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.3491 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta_f = 80° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.3963 \text{ rad}$$

$$\Delta \theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 1.3963 - 0.3491 = 1.0472 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the coefficients (rest-to-rest case)

$$a_0 = \theta_0 = 0.3491 \text{ rad}$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3 \times 1.0472}{4^2} = \frac{3.1416}{16} = 0.19635 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2 \times 1.0472}{4^3} = -\frac{2.0944}{64} = -0.032725 \text{ rad/s}^3$$

### Step 3 — Verify with the position equation at $t = t_f$

$$\theta(4) = 0.3491 + 0 + 0.19635 \times 16 + (-0.032725) \times 64$$

$$= 0.3491 + 3.1416 - 2.0944 = 1.3963 \text{ rad} \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Compute velocity at $t = 2$ s

$$\dot{\theta}(2) = 0 + 2(0.19635)(2) + 3(-0.032725)(4)$$

$$= 0.7854 - 0.3927 = 0.3927 \text{ rad/s}$$

This equals $1.5 \times (\Delta\theta / t_f)$, confirming the rest-to-rest peak-velocity-at-midpoint property.

### Step 5 — Compute peak velocity

$$\dot{\theta}_{\max} = \dot{\theta}(t_f/2) = 0.3927 \text{ rad/s}$$

In degrees per second:

$$\dot{\theta}_{\max} = 0.3927 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 22.5 \text{ °/s}$$

### Step 6 — Tabulate final results

| Coefficient | Value | Unit |
|-------------|-------|------|
| $a_0$ | 0.3491 | rad |
| $a_1$ | 0 | rad/s |
| $a_2$ | 0.19635 | rad/s² |
| $a_3$ | −0.032725 | rad/s³ |
| $\dot{\theta}(2 \text{ s})$ | 0.3927 | rad/s |
| $\dot{\theta}_{\max}$ | 0.3927 | rad/s (at $t = 2$ s) |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cubic Coefficient Behaviour

| Symbol | Depends on | Sign (rest-to-rest, $q_f > q_0$) | Units |
|--------|------------|------------------------------------|-------|
| $a_0$ | $q_0$ only | Same sign as $q_0$ | rad |
| $a_1$ | $v_0$ | **0** (rest-to-rest) | rad/s |
| $a_2$ | $\Delta q$, $t_f$ | **+** | rad/s² |
| $a_3$ | $\Delta q$, $t_f$ | **−** | rad/s³ |
| Peak $\dot{q}$ | $\Delta q$, $t_f$ | **+** (same direction as motion) | rad/s |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Sign-Trap Alert:** If $q_f < q_0$ (reverse motion), the signs of $a_2$ and $a_3$ both flip. Many students forget this and lose **1–2 marks** on the final answer. Always compute $\Delta q = q_f - q_0$ *first*, then plug into the formulas with its actual sign.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trajectory Generation Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional flow** of how a cubic polynomial trajectory is computed and dispatched to a robot joint controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs q0 qf tf v0 vf] --> B[Construct Boundary Condition Vector]
    B --> C[Build 4x4 Coefficient Matrix A]
    C --> D[Augment with RHS Vector b]
    D --> E[Gaussian Elimination / Cramer Rule Solver]
    E --> F[Compute a0 a1 a2 a3]
    F --> G[Generate q of t and dq of t Profiles]
    G --> H[Sample at Servo Rate e.g., 1 ms]
    H --> I[Output Setpoints to Joint PID Controller]
    I --> J[Robot Joint Moves Smoothly]
    G --> K[Optional Acceleration Profile Check]
    K --> L{Within Motor Torque Limits?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Trajectory Accepted]
    L -- No --> N[Reduce t f or Use Quintic Spline]
```

---

## 4.2 Boundary-Condition-to-Coefficient Mapping Diagram

This diagram visualises how each of the four boundary conditions determines one and only one of the four cubic coefficients.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[q of 0 equals q0]
        I2[dq of 0 equals v0]
        I3[q of tf equals qf]
        I4[dq of tf equals vf]
    end

    subgraph Cubic Form
        CF[q of t = a0 + a1 t + a2 t squared + a3 t cubed]
    end

    subgraph Coefficients
        C1[a0 = q0]
        C2[a1 = v0]
        C3[a2 formula]
        C4[a3 formula]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I3 --> C3
    I4 --> C4
    C1 --> CF
    C2 --> CF
    C3 --> CF
    C4 --> CF
```

---

## 4.3 Time-Domain Profile Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Position [Position q of t - Cubic Curve]
        P0[t = 0: q0] --> P1[t = tf/2: midway]
        P1 --> P2[t = tf: qf]
    end

    subgraph Velocity [Velocity dq of t - Parabola]
        V0[t = 0: v0 = 0] --> V1[t = tf/2: peak]
        V1 --> V2[t = tf: vf = 0]
    end

    subgraph Acceleration [Acceleration ddq of t - Linear]
        A0[t = 0: positive max] --> A1[t = tf/2: zero crossing]
        A1 --> A2[t = tf: negative max]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the profile:** Position is a smooth **cubic curve** (S-shape); velocity is a **parabola** (rises, peaks at midpoint, falls); acceleration is a **straight line** (linearly decreases from $+6\Delta q/t_f^2$ to $-6\Delta q/t_f^2$). The *jerk* (third derivative) is therefore a **constant** $= 6 a_3$ — this is the key limitation that motivates higher-order (quintic) trajectories in advanced applications.
