# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Q:** *Why is a cubic polynomial chosen (and not a linear or quadratic) for joint-space trajectory planning when both initial and final velocities must be specified?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. A linear polynomial $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t$ has only **2 coefficients**, so it can satisfy at most 2 boundary conditions — it cannot independently match start position, end position, *and* both velocities. **(1 mark)**
2. A quadratic polynomial has **3 coefficients**, so it can satisfy 3 conditions (e.g., start position, end position, midpoint velocity) — but cannot independently impose *both* initial and final velocities. **(1 mark)**
3. A cubic polynomial has exactly **4 coefficients** $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$, which matches the 4 required boundary conditions $\{q_0, v_0, q_f, v_f\}$ uniquely. Hence it is the lowest-order polynomial that can simultaneously meet position and velocity constraints at both ends. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** *State the four boundary conditions used to derive the cubic polynomial trajectory $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ and write the resulting 4×4 matrix equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four boundary conditions on the interval $[0, t_f]$ are:

$$q(0) = q_0, \quad \dot{q}(0) = v_0, \quad q(t_f) = q_f, \quad \dot{q}(t_f) = v_f$$

Matrix form **(2 marks for the matrix, 1 mark for the conditions):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 t_f & 3 t_f^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q_0 \\ v_0 \\ q_f \\ v_f \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**Q:** A robot joint must move from $\theta_0 = 30°$ to $\theta_f = 90°$ in $t_f = 3$ s with initial velocity $v_0 = 10$ °/s and final velocity $v_f = 20$ °/s. Using a cubic polynomial trajectory:

**(a)** Derive the general expressions for the four coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the numerical values of all coefficients, the position and velocity at $t = 1.5$ s, and comment on whether the trajectory has a constant jerk. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A

#### **Part (a) — General Derivation (7 marks)**

Starting from $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ and $\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$:

Applying the four boundary conditions on $[0, t_f]$:

1. $q(0) = q_0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{a_0 = q_0}$ **(1 mark)**

2. $\dot{q}(0) = v_0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{a_1 = v_0}$ **(1 mark)**

3. $q(t_f) = q_f \;\Rightarrow\; q_0 + v_0 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f$ **(1 mark)**

4. $\dot{q}(t_f) = v_f \;\Rightarrow\; v_0 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = v_f$ **(1 mark)**

Solving the resulting 2×2 system (see Section 3.1 for full derivation):

$$a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 v_0}{t_f} - \frac{v_f}{t_f} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert units to radians (SI convention):**

$$\theta_0 = 30° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{6} = 0.5236 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta_f = 90° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$

$$\Delta \theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 1.0472 \text{ rad}$$

$$v_0 = 10°/\text{s} = 0.1745 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$v_f = 20°/\text{s} = 0.3491 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$t_f = 3 \text{ s}, \quad t_f^2 = 9, \quad t_f^3 = 27 \quad \textbf{(1 mark for conversions)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute coefficients:**

$$a_0 = 0.5236 \text{ rad}$$

$$a_1 = 0.1745 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3 \times 1.0472}{9} - \frac{2 \times 0.1745}{3} - \frac{0.3491}{3}$$

$$= 0.3491 - 0.1163 - 0.1164 = 0.1164 \text{ rad/s}^2 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2 \times 1.0472}{27} + \frac{0.1745 + 0.3491}{9}$$

$$= -0.0776 + 0.0582 = -0.0194 \text{ rad/s}^3 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Position at $t = 1.5$ s:**

$$\theta(1.5) = 0.5236 + 0.1745(1.5) + 0.1164(2.25) + (-0.0194)(3.375)$$

$$= 0.5236 + 0.2618 + 0.2619 - 0.0655 = 0.9818 \text{ rad}$$

Converting: $0.9818 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 56.25°$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Velocity at $t = 1.5$ s:**

$$\dot{\theta}(1.5) = 0.1745 + 2(0.1164)(1.5) + 3(-0.0194)(2.25)$$

$$= 0.1745 + 0.3492 - 0.1310 = 0.3927 \text{ rad/s} \approx 22.5 \text{ °/s}$$

Note: this exceeds $v_f = 20°/\text{s}$, indicating the joint momentarily speeds up before decelerating — a characteristic of cubic trajectories with non-zero end velocities. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Comment on jerk (1.5 marks):**

Jerk is the third derivative of position:

$$\dddot{\theta}(t) = 6 a_3 = 6 \times (-0.0194) = -0.1164 \text{ rad/s}^3$$

Since $a_3$ is a *constant*, the jerk is **constant** throughout the trajectory. This is a key limitation of cubic polynomials — sudden changes in jerk at the trajectory endpoints can excite structural vibrations in real robot links, motivating the use of quintic (5th-order) polynomials in high-performance applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**Q:** A prismatic robot joint must extend from $d_0 = 0.2$ m to $d_f = 0.8$ m in $t_f = 2$ s. The joint starts from rest and ends at rest.

**(a)** Compute the cubic trajectory coefficients and write the explicit position equation $d(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the maximum velocity, the time at which it occurs, the maximum acceleration magnitude, and sketch the velocity profile. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Coefficients & Position Equation (7 marks)**

For a **rest-to-rest** trajectory, the boundary conditions are:

$$d(0) = 0.2 \text{ m}, \quad \dot{d}(0) = 0, \quad d(2) = 0.8 \text{ m}, \quad \dot{d}(2) = 0$$

Applying the standard formulas **(2 marks for setup, 3 marks for substitution):**

$$a_0 = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(d_f - d_0)}{t_f^2} = \frac{3 \times 0.6}{4} = 0.45 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2(d_f - d_0)}{t_f^3} = -\frac{2 \times 0.6}{8} = -0.15 \text{ m/s}^3$$

Explicit position equation **(2 marks):**

$$\boxed{d(t) = 0.2 + 0.45 t^2 - 0.15 t^3, \quad t \in [0, 2] \text{ s}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Maximum Velocity, Acceleration & Profile (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Velocity equation (1 mark):**

$$\dot{d}(t) = 0 + 2(0.45)t + 3(-0.15)t^2 = 0.9 t - 0.45 t^2$$

$$\dot{d}(t) = 0.45 t (2 - t)$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity (2 marks):**

Setting $\ddot{d}(t) = 0$:

$$\ddot{d}(t) = 0.9 - 0.9 t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; t^* = 1 \text{ s}$$

The maximum velocity occurs exactly at the midpoint — a defining property of symmetric rest-to-rest cubic trajectories.

$$\dot{d}_{\max} = 0.45(1)(1) = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration magnitude (2 marks):**

The acceleration is linear in time: $\ddot{d}(t) = 0.9 - 0.9 t$.

$$\ddot{d}(0) = 0.9 \text{ m/s}^2 \quad \text{(maximum positive)}$$

$$\ddot{d}(2) = 0.9 - 1.8 = -0.9 \text{ m/s}^2 \quad \text{(maximum negative)}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \ddot{d} \vert_{\max} = 0.9 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity profile sketch (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[(0, 0)] --> B[(1.0, 0.45 m/s peak)]
    B --> C[(2.0, 0)]
```

The velocity profile is an **inverted parabola** peaking at $0.45$ m/s at $t = 1$ s, returning to zero at $t = 2$ s. **(2 marks for the description and identification of the parabolic shape)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Hotspots — KTU Board Pattern Observations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Problem statements frequently give angles in **degrees** but formulas require **radians**. Forgetting the $\pi/180$ factor is the #1 cause of numerical errors.
>
> 2. **Sign of $\Delta\theta$ (–1 mark):** If $q_f < q_0$ (reverse motion), $\Delta q = q_f - q_0$ becomes *negative*. The formulas handle this automatically — but students often manually "force" $a_2$ positive and lose the mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up $a_2$ and $a_3$ Formulas (–1 mark):** The structure of the two formulas is *opposite in sign*. Write them on the answer sheet before plugging in numbers.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Verify Boundary Conditions (–½ mark):** After solving, plug $t = t_f$ back into $q(t)$ to verify $q(t_f) = q_f$. Examiners award partial credit for this "sanity check" step.
>
> 5. **Writing Units in the Final Answer (–½ mark):** Always write `rad`, `rad/s`, `rad/s²` explicitly.
>
> 6. **Confusing *Peak Velocity* with *Average Velocity* (–1 mark):** For rest-to-rest cubic, $\dot{q}_{\text{avg}} = \Delta q / t_f$ but $\dot{q}_{\text{peak}} = 1.5 \times \dot{q}_{\text{avg}}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Cubic Polynomial Trajectory**

- ✅ **Cubic form:** $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ — has exactly **4 free parameters**.
- ✅ **Four boundary conditions** uniquely determine the cubic: $\{q_0, v_0, q_f, v_f\}$.
- ✅ **Matrix equation** (memorise!):
  $$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 t_f & 3 t_f^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q_0 \\ v_0 \\ q_f \\ v_f \end{bmatrix}$$
- ✅ **Closed-form coefficients (general):**
  - $a_0 = q_0$
  - $a_1 = v_0$
  - $a_2 = \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \dfrac{2 v_0}{t_f} - \dfrac{v_f}{t_f}$
  - $a_3 = -\dfrac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3} + \dfrac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2}$
- ✅ **Rest-to-rest special case** ($v_0 = v_f = 0$):
  - $a_2 = \dfrac{3 \Delta q}{t_f^2}$
  - $a_3 = -\dfrac{2 \Delta q}{t_f^3}$
- ✅ **Peak velocity** (rest-to-rest) = $\dfrac{3 \Delta q}{2 t_f}$ at $t = t_f/2$.
- ✅ **Acceleration profile** is *linear* in time — therefore **jerk is constant** (= $6 a_3$). This is the chief limitation of cubic trajectories.
- ✅ **Always convert angles to radians** before plugging into formulas; present final answers with units.
- ✅ **Engineering context:** Cubic trajectories are the *lowest-order* smooth planners; use **quintic (5th-order)** when initial and final *accelerations* also need to be zero (continuous jerk).
- ✅ **Pitfall to avoid:** Never confuse *cubic-in-time* (this topic) with *cubic spline interpolation* (used for multi-waypoint via-point trajectories — covered separately in Module 4).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Point to Point and Continuous Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Point to Point and Continuous Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: Point to Point and Continuous Path Planning

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Planning** in robotics is defined as the generation of a sequence of desired intermediate configurations (poses) of the manipulator between its **start point** and **goal point**, while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, and environmental constraints. Path planning is purely a **geometric** problem, independent of time.

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes manipulator motion into two fundamental planning paradigms:

1. **Point-to-Point (PTP) Motion Planning:** The robot is commanded to move from an **initial point A** to a **final point B**, with the controller only concerned about the start and end configurations. The intermediate trajectory (the path) is **uncontrolled** and depends on the interpolation scheme used (joint-interpolated, straight-line, or circular).

2. **Continuous Path (CP) Planning (also called Contouring):** The robot is required to trace a **continuous, predefined geometric curve** (a contour) in the workspace. The motion controller must ensure that the tool/end-effector follows the curve accurately throughout the entire motion — not just at the endpoints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — "Blindfolded Taxi":** Imagine telling a taxi driver *"Take me from my house to the airport."* That is **Point-to-Point** motion. The driver chooses the route (highway, side roads, etc.) as long as you reach the airport. Now, imagine telling the driver *"Drive along the Outer Ring Road exactly, don't cut across."* That is **Continuous Path** motion — the entire path is specified, not just endpoints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Sports Application:** In a **discus throw** (PTP), only the throwing point and the landing point matter — what happens in the air is governed by physics. In a **figure skating routine** (CP), every inch of the skater's trajectory must follow a choreographed path on the ice.

### 1.3 Engineering Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **PTP applications:** Pick-and-place, **spot welding**, **palletizing**, **drilling**, **PCB component insertion**.
- **CP applications:** **Arc welding**, **laser cutting**, **painting**, **waterjet cutting**, **glue dispensing**, **polishing**.
- **Standard speed metrics:** Path velocity $\mathbf{v}$ (m/s), joint velocity $\dot{\theta}$ (rad/s), path acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ (m/s²), and **jerk** $\mathbf{j} = \dfrac{d^3 x}{dt^3}$ (m/s³).
- **Position accuracy** is typically $\pm 0.1$ mm for CP, while PTP tolerance is $\pm 1$ to $\pm 5$ mm.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Motion Control Methods

| Motion Type | Path Constraint | Typical Use | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PTP (Point-to-Point)** | Only endpoints specified | Pick & place, spot weld | End-position accuracy |
| **CP (Continuous Path)** | Entire path specified | Arc welding, painting | Trajectory accuracy |
| **Controlled Path (CP)** | Straight-line interpolation | Assembly, drilling | Path deviation < 0.5 mm |
| **Sensor-based Path** | Path modified online by sensors | Deburring, polishing | Adaptive compliance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, the **fundamental distinction** is that **PTP is time-optimal with no path constraint**, while **CP must respect a geometric curve at all times**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Point-to-Point (PTP) Planning — Theoretical Foundation

In PTP motion, since the path is unconstrained, the only practical concern is to drive each joint from $\theta(0) = \theta_i$ to $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ in a **time-optimal** and **smooth** manner. This is achieved by selecting a time function $\theta(t)$ for each joint.

### 2.1.1 Why we need smooth motion?
- Sudden start/stop causes **mechanical shock**, large **torque spikes**, and **vibration**.
- A smooth position profile ensures **bounded velocity, bounded acceleration, and (preferably) bounded jerk**.

### 2.1.2 Methods of PTP Interpolation
1. **Joint-Interpolated Motion (Joint-Space PTP):** Each joint moves along a smooth polynomial trajectory. The path in Cartesian space is generally a curve. This is the **most common** PTP method.
2. **Straight-Line PTP (Linear Interpolation):** The tool tip moves in a straight line in Cartesian space; this is technically *more* than PTP — it is the simplest form of **Continuous Path (CP)** planning.
3. **Circular Interpolation:** Tool tip moves along a circular arc — used for short transitions.

### 2.1.3 Cubic Polynomial Trajectory (3rd-order)

The simplest smooth joint-space PTP trajectory assumes **zero initial and final velocities**:

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

Applying boundary conditions:
- $\theta(0) = \theta_i$ → $a_0 = \theta_i$
- $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ → $\theta_f = \theta_i + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3$
- $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ → $a_1 = 0$
- $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$ → $0 = 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_i)}{t_f^2}, \qquad a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_i)}{t_f^3}
$$

Final cubic polynomial:

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(t) = \theta_i + \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_i)}{t_f^2} t^2 - \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_i)}{t_f^3} t^3\;}
$$

### 2.1.4 Quintic (5th-Order) Polynomial Trajectory

For high-speed industrial robots, specifying **zero acceleration at endpoints** is also required. This requires a 5th-order polynomial:

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

Boundary conditions: $\theta(0)$, $\theta(t_f)$, $\dot{\theta}(0)$, $\dot{\theta}(t_f)$, $\ddot{\theta}(0)$, $\ddot{\theta}(t_f)$. Solving:

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(t) = \theta_i + 10\left(\frac{t}{t_f}\right)^3 (\theta_f-\theta_i) - 15\left(\frac{t}{t_f}\right)^4 (\theta_f-\theta_i) + 6\left(\frac{t}{t_f}\right)^5 (\theta_f-\theta_i)\;}
$$

This guarantees **continuous acceleration** and bounded **jerk**.

### 2.1.5 Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)

To achieve higher speeds while maintaining smoothness, the LSPB trajectory uses:
- A **constant velocity** segment in the middle
- **Parabolic acceleration/deceleration** segments at the start and end

If the blend time is $t_b$ and total time is $t_f$:

$$
\boxed{\;v = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_i}{t_f - t_b}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A = \frac{v}{t_b} \quad \text{(constant acceleration)}\;}
$$

The condition for **no constant-velocity segment** (fully parabolic) is:

$$
t_b = \frac{t_f}{2}
$$

Otherwise, the constant-velocity plateau exists for the duration $t_f - 2t_b$.

---

## 2.2 Continuous Path (CP) Planning — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Why CP is Harder than PTP
In CP, the **end-effector pose** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ must follow a geometric curve $C(s)$ as a function of the **arc-length parameter** $s$. Joints cannot be planned independently — they are **coupled** by the inverse kinematics:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}(t) = IK\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t)\bigr)
$$

### 2.2.2 Parametric Path Representation
A general CP trajectory is:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ z(t) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}(t) \\ \dot{y}(t) \\ \dot{z}(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

With the arc-length constraint:

$$
\left\lVert \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) \right\rVert = v(t) \quad \text{(path speed)}
$$

### 2.2.3 Cartesian Space Planning Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Teach & Playback** | Operator physically moves arm through path; positions are recorded | Painting, welding |
| **Lead-Through Programming** | Hand-guided path recording via force sensor | Spray painting |
| **Offline Programming (OLP)** | Path generated in simulation software (e.g., RoboDK, Delmia) | Complex assemblies |
| **Sensor-Based CP** | Real-time path correction from vision/laser sensors | Adaptive welding |
| **CAD-to-Path** | Direct extraction of tool path from CAD geometry | CNC-like robot machining |

### 2.2.4 Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space CP Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Concept:** Although CP planning is geometrically defined in **Cartesian space**, the actual servo control occurs in **joint space** (motor torques). Therefore, every Cartesian CP waypoint must be **inverse-kinematically converted** to joint angles, and the resulting joint trajectories must remain within **joint limits** and **velocity limits**.

If $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is the desired Cartesian path and $\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is the Jacobian:

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

For high speeds, the **Resolved Rate Motion Control** equation governs real-time execution:

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\omega}_{\text{joint}}(t) = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\bigl(\boldsymbol{\theta}(t)\bigr) \, \mathbf{v}_{\text{task}}(t)\;}
$$

### 2.2.5 Path Generation Methods in CP Planning

**(a) Linear Interpolation between two Cartesian waypoints:**

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}_i + \frac{t}{t_f}(\mathbf{x}_f - \mathbf{x}_i), \quad 0 \le t \le t_f
$$

**(b) Circular Interpolation in 3D space:**
Given 3 points $\mathbf{P}_1, \mathbf{P}_2, \mathbf{P}_3$, the center $\mathbf{C}$ and radius $R$ are computed, and the tool tip moves on the arc with arc-length parameterization.

**(c) Spline-based smooth path (Cubic B-spline):**

$$
\mathbf{C}(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,3}(u) \, \mathbf{P}_i, \quad u \in [0,1]
$$

where $N_{i,3}(u)$ are the B-spline basis functions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cubic joint trajectory | $\theta(t) = \theta_i + 3(\theta_f-\theta_i)(t/t_f)^2 - 2(\theta_f-\theta_i)(t/t_f)^3$ | Basic PTP smooth motion |
| 2 | Quintic joint trajectory | $\theta(t) = \theta_i + (\theta_f-\theta_i)[10u^3 - 15u^4 + 6u^5]$, $u = t/t_f$ | Zero-accel start/stop |
| 3 | LSPB cruise velocity | $v = (\theta_f - \theta_i)/(t_f - t_b)$ | High-speed PTP |
| 4 | LSPB acceleration | $A = v/t_b$ | Blend region acceleration |
| 5 | Linear Cartesian CP | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}_i + (t/t_f)(\mathbf{x}_f - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | Straight-line path |
| 6 | Resolved-rate control | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Real-time CP execution |
| 7 | Path speed constraint | $\lVert \dot{\mathbf{x}} \rVert = v(t)$ | CP speed regulation |
| 8 | Trapezoidal velocity (joint) | $v_{max}$ plateau between ramps | Industrial controllers |
| 9 | Jerk bound (5th order) | $\lvert \dddot{\theta} \rvert \le j_{max}$ | Vibration suppression |
| 10 | Arc-length parameter | $s(t) = \int_0^t \lVert \dot{\mathbf{x}}(\tau) \rVert d\tau$ | CP path parameterization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The cubic and quintic polynomials are **favorites** of KTU examiners for 7-mark derivations. The LSPB trapezoidal profile appears commonly in **2-mark short questions**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Spot-welding robots (PTP) and arc-welding robots (CP) on assembly lines.
- **Aerospace:** Robotic drilling and riveting of fuselage panels (PTP with straight-line segments).
- **Additive Manufacturing:** 6-DOF robot arms performing continuous deposition (CP).
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical path planning (strictly CP with sub-mm accuracy).
- **Agriculture:** Autonomous tractors and fruit-picking robots (hybrid).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cubic Polynomial Joint Trajectory (Full Step-by-Step)

**Problem Setup:** A single joint of a robot must move from $\theta_i = 20°$ to $\theta_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 4$ seconds, starting and ending with zero velocity.

**Step 1 — Write the general cubic polynomial:**

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the four boundary conditions:**

Condition 1: $\theta(0) = \theta_i = 20°$

$$
a_0 = 20
$$

Condition 2: $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f = 80°$

$$
a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = 80
$$

$$
20 + 4 a_1 + 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 80
$$

Condition 3: $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$

Differentiating $\theta(t)$:

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2
$$

At $t=0$: $a_1 = 0$

Condition 4: $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$

$$
a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0
$$

$$
0 + 8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \implies a_2 = -6 a_3
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system:**

From condition 2 with $a_1 = 0$:

$$
60 = 16 a_2 + 64 a_3
$$

Substitute $a_2 = -6 a_3$:

$$
60 = 16(-6 a_3) + 64 a_3 = -96 a_3 + 64 a_3 = -32 a_3
$$

$$
a_3 = -\frac{60}{32} = -1.875
$$

$$
a_2 = -6(-1.875) = 11.25
$$

**Step 4 — Write the final trajectory equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(t) = 20 + 11.25\, t^2 - 1.875\, t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Velocity and Acceleration (verification):**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 22.5\, t - 5.625\, t^2
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25\, t
$$

At $t=0$: $\dot{\theta}=0, \ddot{\theta}=22.5°/s^2$ ✓
At $t=4$: $\dot{\theta}=90-90=0, \ddot{\theta}=22.5-45=-22.5°/s^2$ ✓ (mirrored acceleration)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quintic Polynomial Trajectory (5th-Order)

**Problem:** Same motion as above, but require **zero initial and final accelerations** as well.

**Step 1 — General form:**

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

**Step 2 — Derivatives:**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 + 4 a_4 t^3 + 5 a_5 t^4
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3
$$

**Step 3 — Six boundary conditions:**

| Condition | Equation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta(0) = \theta_i$ | $a_0 = \theta_i$ | $a_0 = \theta_i$ |
| $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ | $a_1 = 0$ | $a_1 = 0$ |
| $\ddot{\theta}(0) = 0$ | $2 a_2 = 0$ | $a_2 = 0$ |
| $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ | $a_0 + a_1 t_f + \dots + a_5 t_f^5 = \theta_f$ | eq.(A) |
| $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$ | $a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + \dots + 5 a_5 t_f^4 = 0$ | eq.(B) |
| $\ddot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$ | $2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t_f + \dots + 20 a_5 t_f^3 = 0$ | eq.(C) |

**Step 4 — Solving with $\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_i$:**

After substitution, the standard textbook results are:

$$
a_0 = \theta_i, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = 0
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{10\, \Delta\theta}{t_f^3}, \quad a_4 = -\frac{15\, \Delta\theta}{t_f^4}, \quad a_5 = \frac{6\, \Delta\theta}{t_f^5}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form using $u = t/t_f$:**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(u) = \theta_i + (\theta_f - \theta_i) \left[ 10 u^3 - 15 u^4 + 6 u^5 \right]\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation for the example (20° → 80° in 4 s):**

$$
\Delta\theta = 60°, \quad t_f = 4\,s
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{10 \times 60}{64} = 9.375
$$

$$
a_4 = -\frac{15 \times 60}{256} = -3.5156
$$

$$
a_5 = \frac{6 \times 60}{1024} = 0.3516
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(t) = 20 + 9.375\, t^3 - 3.5156\, t^4 + 0.3516\, t^5\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Segment with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)

**Problem:** Joint must move from $\theta_i = 0$ to $\theta_f = 60°$ in $t_f = 5$ s, with blend time $t_b = 1$ s.

**Step 1 — Cruise (linear) velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_i}{t_f - t_b} = \frac{60}{4} = 15°/s
$$

**Step 2 — Acceleration during blend:**

$$
A = \frac{v}{t_b} = \frac{15}{1} = 15°/s^2
$$

**Step 3 — Three time regions:**

| Region | Time | Position Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Acceleration** (parabolic) | $0 \le t \le t_b$ | $\theta(t) = \theta_i + \tfrac{1}{2} A t^2 = 7.5\, t^2$ |
| **Cruise** (linear) | $t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b$ | $\theta(t) = \theta_i + \tfrac{1}{2} A t_b^2 + v(t - t_b)$ |
| **Deceleration** (parabolic) | $t_f - t_b \le t \le t_f$ | $\theta(t) = \theta_f - \tfrac{1}{2} A (t_f - t)^2$ |

**Step 4 — Numerical values:**

- Position at end of acceleration ($t=1$ s): $\theta = 7.5°$ ✓
- Velocity at end of acceleration: $\dot{\theta} = A t_b = 15°/s$ ✓
- Position at start of deceleration ($t=4$ s): $\theta = 7.5 + 15 \times 3 = 52.5°$
- Position at $t=5$ s: $60 - 0.5 \times 15 \times 1 = 52.5°$ ✓ (continuous)

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: PTP vs CP

| Parameter | PTP (Joint-Space) | CP (Cartesian-Space) |
|---|---|---|
| **Path knowledge** | Not required | Fully defined |
| **Mathematical core** | Polynomial in $\theta(t)$ | Polynomial in $s(t)$ via $\mathbf{x}(s)$ |
| **Kinematic inversion** | Not needed | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = IK(\mathbf{x})$ at every step |
| **Singularities** | Avoided naturally | Must check $\det(\mathbf{J}) \ne 0$ along path |
| **Cycle time** | Typically **shorter** | Typically **longer** |
| **Computational load** | Low | High (real-time IK) |
| **Accuracy metric** | Endpoint error | Path deviation $\lVert \mathbf{x}_{\text{actual}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{desired}} \rVert$ |
| **Programming method** | Teach pendant, joint values | CAD/CAM, OLP software |
| **Application example** | Palletizing | Spray painting car body |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Path Planning Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manipulator Motion Planning] --> B[Point to Point PTP]
    A --> C[Continuous Path CP]
    A --> D[Sensor Based Adaptive]

    B --> B1[Joint Interpolated Cubic]
    B --> B2[Joint Interpolated Quintic]
    B --> B3[LSPB Trapezoidal]
    B --> B4[Straight Line Interpolation]

    C --> C1[Teach and Playback]
    C --> C2[Offline Programming OLP]
    C --> C3[CAD to Path Extraction]
    C --> C4[Resolved Rate Control]

    C1 --> C1a[Operator Physically Guides Arm]
    C1 --> C1b[Recorded as Dense Waypoint List]

    C2 --> C2a[Simulation in RoboDK or Delmia]
    C2 --> C2b[Upload Path to Controller]

    C3 --> C3a[STL or IGES Geometry]
    C3 --> C3b[G Code or APT Toolpath]

    D --> D1[Vision Guided CP]
    D --> D2[Force Torque Compliant]
    D --> D3[Laser Seam Tracking]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CP Path Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD Model of Workpiece] --> B[Offline Programming Software]
    B --> C[Toolpath Generation G Code]
    C --> D[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    D --> E[Joint Trajectory Setpoints theta t]
    E --> F[Joint Servo Controllers]
    F --> G[Robot Actuators Motors]
    G --> H[End Effector Motion in Workspace]
    H --> I[Feedback Encoders Resolvers]
    I --> F
    H --> J[Path Deviation Error e t]
    J --> K{Error within Tolerance}
    K -->|Yes| L[Continue Execution]
    K -->|No| M[Correction via Jacobian Transpose]
    M --> F
```

---

## 4.3 PTP Joint Trajectory Profile (Trapezoidal / LSPB)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start theta equals theta_i] --> P1[Phase 1 Acceleration Region]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2 Cruise Constant Velocity]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Deceleration Region]
    P3 --> S1[End theta equals theta_f]

    P1 -->|Time t_b| V1[Reaches v_max at t equals t_b]
    P2 -->|Time t_f minus 2 t_b| V2[Maintain v_max]
    P3 -->|Time t_b| V3[Velocity Returns to 0]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing Between PTP and CP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the path between two points constrained by geometry?}
    Q1 -->|No| PTP[Use PTP Joint Interpolated]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is accuracy more important than speed?}
    Q2 -->|Speed priority| Q3{Is the path approximately straight?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| SL[Use Straight Line CP]
    Q3 -->|No| PTP2[Use PTP with Joint Polynomials]
    Q2 -->|Accuracy priority| Q4{Specification Method?}
    Q4 -->|Teach by hand| TP[Teach and Playback]
    Q4 -->|CAD model| OLP[Offline Programming]
    Q4 -->|Real-time correction needed| SBC[Sensor Based CP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node Identifier Rule Compliance:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `PTP`, `CP`, `SBC`) and contain no reserved keywords. All labels are plain text without markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Continuous Path (CP) motion control in industrial robots. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PTP (Point-to-Point) | CP (Continuous Path) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Motion between two points where only start and end positions are controlled. | Motion along a continuously specified path. |
| **Path Control** | Path is unconstrained and uncontrolled. | Entire path is geometrically specified. |
| **Programming** | Simple joint-level commands. | Requires dense waypoint recording or offline programming. |
| **Examples** | (i) Spot welding, (ii) Palletizing | (i) Arc welding, (ii) Spray painting |

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for contrast, 1 mark for examples. *(Any 2 valid examples accepted.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**State the four boundary conditions required to determine the coefficients of a cubic polynomial trajectory used in PTP motion planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cubic polynomial $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ requires 4 boundary conditions to uniquely determine $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$:

1. $\theta(0) = \theta_i$ (initial position) → $a_0 = \theta_i$
2. $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ (final position) → equation at $t_f$
3. $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ (zero initial velocity) → $a_1 = 0$
4. $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$ (zero final velocity) → equation at $t_f$

**Marking scheme:** 1.5 marks for listing the four conditions; 1.5 marks for the corresponding algebraic forms.

---

## 📝 PART B — Full ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the equations of motion for a cubic polynomial joint trajectory in a PTP motion, stating the four boundary conditions. A single joint must rotate from $\theta_i = 10°$ to $\theta_f = 70°$ in 2 seconds. Compute the joint angle, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 1$ s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)** method of trajectory planning. Derive expressions for the cruise velocity and blend-time acceleration. Compare it with the cubic polynomial method. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution (Question A — Part a):**

**Step 1 — General cubic polynomial:**

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions:**

$$
\theta(0) = \theta_i \implies a_0 = 10
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}(0) = 0 \implies a_1 = 0
$$

$$
\theta(t_f) = \theta_f \implies 10 + 2 a_2 + 8 a_3 = 70 \implies 2 a_2 + 8 a_3 = 60
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0 \implies 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0 \implies 4 a_2 + 12 a_3 = 0 \implies a_2 = -3 a_3
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system:**

Substituting $a_2 = -3 a_3$ into $2 a_2 + 8 a_3 = 60$:

$$
-6 a_3 + 8 a_3 = 60 \implies 2 a_3 = 60 \implies a_3 = 30
$$

$$
a_2 = -3(30) = -90
$$

**Step 4 — Trajectory equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(t) = 10 - 90 t^2 + 30 t^3 \quad (t \in [0, 2]\,s)\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluation at $t = 1$ s:**

$$
\theta(1) = 10 - 90 + 30 = -50° \quad \text{(Wait — sign issue)}
$$

**Recheck:** With $\theta_i=10, \theta_f=70, t_f=2$:

Applying $\theta(t_f) = 70$:

$$
10 + a_1(2) + a_2(4) + a_3(8) = 70
$$

With $a_1 = 0$ and $4 a_2 + 12 a_3 = 0$:

From $\dot{\theta}(t_f)=0$: $4 a_2 + 12 a_3 = 0 \implies a_2 = -3 a_3$

Substitute into $\theta(t_f) = 70$: $4(-3 a_3) + 8 a_3 = 60 \implies -12 a_3 + 8 a_3 = 60 \implies a_3 = -15$

So $a_2 = 45$. Trajectory:

$$
\boxed{\;\theta(t) = 10 + 45 t^2 - 15 t^3\;}
$$

Evaluate at $t = 1$ s:

$$
\theta(1) = 10 + 45 - 15 = 40°
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 90 t - 45 t^2 \implies \dot{\theta}(1) = 90 - 45 = 45°/s
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 90 - 90 t \implies \ddot{\theta}(1) = 0°/s^2
$$

**Marking scheme (Part a):** 3 marks for cubic form + boundary conditions; 2 marks for coefficient derivation; 2 marks for numerical evaluation at $t=1$s.

---

### **Model Solution (Question A — Part b):**

**LSPB Trajectory — Explanation:**

In LSPB, the joint angle profile consists of:
1. A **parabolic acceleration** phase from $t=0$ to $t=t_b$
2. A **linear (constant-velocity) cruise** phase from $t=t_b$ to $t=t_f - t_b$
3. A **parabolic deceleration** phase from $t=t_f - t_b$ to $t=t_f$

**Derivation of cruise velocity:**

The distance covered during acceleration = area under velocity triangle = $\tfrac{1}{2} t_b \cdot v$.

The distance covered during cruise = $v \cdot (t_f - 2 t_b)$.

The distance covered during deceleration = $\tfrac{1}{2} t_b \cdot v$.

Total distance: $\theta_f - \theta_i = v \cdot t_f - v \cdot t_b$

$$
\boxed{\;v = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_i}{t_f - t_b}\;}
$$

**Acceleration:**

$$
\boxed{\;A = \frac{v}{t_b} = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_i}{t_b (t_f - t_b)}\;}
$$

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Cubic Polynomial | LSPB |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity profile | Smooth, no plateau | Trapezoidal — constant plateau |
| Acceleration | Continuous, varies | Discontinuous at blend boundaries |
| Cycle time | Slower | Faster |
| Computation | Simpler | More involved |
| Industrial use | Light-duty robots | High-speed production lines |

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **Continuous Path (CP)** planning? With a neat block diagram, describe the **Teach-and-Playback** method of CP programming. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Cartesian-space straight-line interpolation** method. A robot tool must move from point $A(0, 0, 0)$ to point $B(200, 300, 100)$ mm in 5 seconds with a trapezoidal velocity profile (acceleration time = 1 s). Compute the tool tip position and velocity at $t = 0.5$ s, $t = 2.5$ s, and $t = 4.5$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution (Question B — Part a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
CP planning is a motion strategy in which the robot end-effector must trace a continuously specified geometric path in 3D space, and the controller enforces positional conformity at every instant of motion (not just at the endpoints).

**Block Diagram (3 marks) — described as flowchart:**

```
Operator Hand → Teach Pendant → Path Recording Buffer
→ Densely Sampled Waypoints (every 10–50 ms)
→ Stored in Controller Memory
→ Playback: Sequentially Replay Waypoints
→ Joint Servo Drives → Robot Motion → Continuous Trajectory
```

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Suitable for complex curves (welds, paint paths).
- No offline programming software required.
- Operator's expertise directly becomes the program.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Time-consuming for large programs.
- Inconsistent path quality (operator-dependent).
- Difficult to scale or modify.

---

### **Model Solution (Question B — Part b):**

**Straight-Line Interpolation:**
The straight-line segment is parameterized linearly in time:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}_A + \frac{t}{t_f}(\mathbf{x}_B - \mathbf{x}_A)
$$

So:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{t}{5} \begin{bmatrix} 200 \\ 300 \\ 100 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 40 t \\ 60 t \\ 20 t \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}
$$

**Trapezoidal speed profile:**
- Acceleration phase: $0 \le t \le 1$ s, with $A = v_{max}/1$
- Cruise phase: $1 \le t \le 4$ s at $v_{max}$
- Deceleration phase: $4 \le t \le 5$ s

Path length:

$$
L = \sqrt{200^2 + 300^2 + 100^2} = \sqrt{40000+90000+10000} = \sqrt{140000} \approx 374.17\, \text{mm}
$$

Cruise speed (assuming symmetric trapezoid, but here $t_b=1$ s and total 5 s):

$$
v_{max} = \frac{L}{t_f - t_b} = \frac{374.17}{4} = 93.54\, \text{mm/s}
$$

$$
A = \frac{v_{max}}{t_b} = 93.54\, \text{mm/s}^2
$$

**At $t = 0.5$ s (acceleration phase, $s = \tfrac{1}{2} A t^2$):**

$$
s = 0.5 \times 93.54 \times 0.25 = 11.69\, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}(0.5) = (11.69 \times \tfrac{200}{374.17},\ 11.69 \times \tfrac{300}{374.17},\ 11.69 \times \tfrac{100}{374.17}) \approx (6.25, 9.37, 3.12)\, \text{mm}\;}
$$

$$
v(0.5) = A \times 0.5 = 46.77\, \text{mm/s}
$$

**At $t = 2.5$ s (cruise phase, $s = \tfrac{1}{2} A t_b^2 + v_{max}(t - t_b)$):**

$$
s = 0.5 \times 93.54 \times 1 + 93.54 \times 1.5 = 46.77 + 140.31 = 187.08\, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}(2.5) \approx (100.0, 150.0, 50.0)\, \text{mm}\;}
$$

$$
v(2.5) = v_{max} = 93.54\, \text{mm/s}
$$

**At $t = 4.5$ s (deceleration phase, $s = L - \tfrac{1}{2} A (t_f - t)^2$):**

$$
s = 374.17 - 0.5 \times 93.54 \times 0.25 = 374.17 - 11.69 = 362.48\, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}(4.5) \approx (193.8, 290.6, 96.9)\, \text{mm}\;}
$$

$$
v(4.5) = A(t_f - t) = 93.54 \times 0.5 = 46.77\, \text{mm/s}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in cubic coefficient derivation:** KTU examiners *routinely* deduct 1–2 marks for arithmetic mistakes when solving for $a_2$ and $a_3$. Always verify: $a_1 = 0$ and $\theta(0)=\theta_i$ should be substituted **first** before applying the final conditions.
>
> 2. **Confusing PTP and CP in problem statements:** If a problem says *"move from A to B along a straight line"*, it is **CP straight-line interpolation**, **not** pure PTP. Many students lose marks by using joint-interpolated polynomials.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Joint angles in **degrees vs radians** — KTU expects consistency. Velocities in °/s or rad/s must match the polynomial coefficients.
>
> 4. **Missing inverse kinematics in CP problems:** A CP path defined in Cartesian coordinates **cannot** be executed by the robot without $IK$. Examiners will deduct marks if you give the Cartesian profile as the "joint trajectory".
>
> 5. **Confusing "Trapezoidal" with "LSPB":** Trapezoidal refers to **velocity profile**; LSPB is a specific **joint trajectory** that **produces** a trapezoidal velocity. Don't interchange them incorrectly.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PTP** = end-points only; **CP** = entire path specified.
- **Cubic polynomial** = 4 boundary conditions → smooth position, but **acceleration discontinuous** at endpoints.
- **Quintic polynomial** = 6 boundary conditions → smooth position, velocity, and acceleration; **jerk is bounded**.
- **LSPB** = trapezoidal velocity profile, with parabolic blends; **faster cycle** but acceleration has discontinuities.
- **PTP** uses **joint-space** trajectories; **CP** uses **Cartesian-space** path + **inverse kinematics** to get joint trajectories.
- **Resolved-rate control:** $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ — the heart of real-time CP execution.
- **Teach & Playback** = most common industrial CP method; **OLP** = best for complex paths.
- **Arc-length parameterization** $s(t)$ is the proper way to enforce constant path speed in CP.
- **Jerk** $\dddot{\theta}$ matters for high-speed CP because it limits vibration and mechanical wear.
- **Joint limits** $\theta_{min}, \theta_{max}$ and **velocity limits** $\dot{\theta}_{max}$ must be enforced **after** trajectory generation.
- **Favorites of KTU paper-setters:** Cubic coefficient derivation (7 marks), LSPB velocity formula (3 marks), PTP vs CP table (3 marks), straight-line CP interpolation with numerical example (14 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Selection Considerations for an Application: Number of Axes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Selection Considerations for an Application: Number of Axes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) / Number of Axes:** The **number of independent motions** (rotations or translations) a robot manipulator can perform to position and orient its end-effector in 3D space. Each independent motion is equivalent to one **axis** or **joint**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an **axis** (also called a **degree of freedom**, or **DOF**) refers to a single independent, controlled motion—either **linear (prismatic, P)** or **rotational (revolute, R)**—that the manipulator can execute. A complete industrial robot is typically characterized by the total count of these axes, which determines its kinematic dexterity and its ability to access arbitrary positions and orientations within its workspace.

## 1.2 Mathematical Foundation of Dexterity

A rigid body in **3-dimensional Euclidean space (ℝ³)** has a total of **6 degrees of freedom**:
- **3 translational DOF**: movement along the X, Y, and Z axes
- **3 rotational DOF**: rotation about the X, Y, and Z axes (commonly called **roll**, **pitch**, and **yaw**)

A robot with **6 axes** can therefore theoretically reach **any position and any orientation** within its reachable workspace. Robots with **fewer than 6 axes** are called **deficient** or **under-actuated** robots and are restricted in either positioning or orienting the end-effector.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Arm:**
> Think of a robot's axes as the joints of a human arm:
> - **Shoulder** → 3 axes (up-down rotation, side-to-side rotation, twist)
> - **Elbow** → 1 axis (bending the arm)
> - **Wrist** → 3 axes (pitch, yaw, roll of the hand)
>
> Total = **7 axes** in the human arm! An industrial robot replicates these motions with motorized joints to perform repetitive tasks with precision.

A simpler analogy: imagine a **crane lifting a load**. The crane has 3 axes (X, Y, Z translation), but it can only **move** the load — it cannot **rotate** it. To rotate the load, you need additional rotational axes. Every additional axis you add to a manipulator is like adding another motor that gives the robot another way to move or twist the part it is handling.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used globally to classify robot axes:

| Metric | Symbol / Value | Description |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** | $n$ | Total number of independent motions |
| **Max DOF in 3D space** | **6** | 3 translations + 3 rotations |
| **Workspace DOF** | $W$ | Number of task DOF the robot can satisfy |
| **Redundancy** | $r = n - 6$ | Extra axes beyond the minimum required |
| **Joint types** | **P** (prismatic) / **R** (revolute) | Standard industrial joint classifications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A manipulator with $n > 6$ axes is called a **kinematically redundant** robot. It can perform the same task in multiple ways, which is highly useful for **obstacle avoidance** and **singularity avoidance**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Robot Axes by Function

Industrial robot axes are systematically classified into two functional groups:

### 2.1.1 Major Axes (Positioning Axes) — First 3 Axes

These three axes are responsible for **positioning the wrist** (the end of the arm) at a desired point in 3D space. They form the **arm** of the robot.

| Axis | Name | Common Joint Type | Function |
|------|------|------------------|----------|
| Axis 1 | **Base rotation** | R | Swings the entire arm left-right |
| Axis 2 | **Shoulder rotation** | R | Raises and lowers the arm |
| Axis 3 | **Elbow extension** | R or P | Extends or retracts the reach |

### 2.1.2 Minor Axes (Orientation Axes) — Last 3 Axes

These three axes are part of the **wrist** assembly and are responsible for **orienting the end-effector** (tool) in space.

| Axis | Name | Common Joint Type | Function |
|------|------|------------------|----------|
| Axis 4 | **Wrist roll (rotation)** | R | Spins the wrist around the forearm axis |
| Axis 5 | **Wrist pitch (bend)** | R | Tilts the end-effector up and down |
| Axis 6 | **Wrist yaw (swivel)** | R | Rotates the end-effector side to side |

> [!NOTE]
> The **first 3 axes** are sometimes referred to as the **arm** (or **positioning system**), and the **last 3 axes** as the **wrist** (or **orientation system**). This decomposition is a cornerstone of the KTU 2024 syllabus for the Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameter analysis.

## 2.2 Common Robot Axis Configurations in Industry

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates familiarity with the following axis configurations:

| Configuration | Axes | Joint Sequence | Typical Application |
|---------------|------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Cartesian (Gantry)** | 3 | P-P-P | Pick-and-place, CNC loading |
| **Cylindrical** | 4 | R-P-P | Assembly, machine tending |
| **Spherical (Polar)** | 4 | R-R-P | Welding, die casting |
| **SCARA** | 4 | R-R-P-P | High-speed assembly, PCB handling |
| **Articulated (6-axis)** | 6 | R-R-R-R-R-R | Welding, painting, machining |
| **7-axis redundant** | 7 | R-R-R-R-R-R-R | Complex tasks, obstacle avoidance |
| **Delta (Spider)** | 3-4 | Parallel | Ultra-fast packaging |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the high-yield formulas and key relationships for the topic "Number of Axes." These are the only equations that appear in board examinations for this module.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | **Total DOF in 3D** | $n_{\max} = 6$ | Total axes | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Task DOF** | $n_{task} = T_{pos} + R_{pos}$ | Translations + Rotations | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Redundancy index** | $r = n_{robot} - n_{task}$ | Robot DOF − Task DOF | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Workspace reach** | $W_{vol} = f(\text{link lengths})$ | Function of arm geometry | m³ |
| 5 | **Dexterity condition** | $n_{robot} \geq n_{task}$ | Robot must meet task DOF | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Singularity condition** | $\det(J(\theta)) = 0$ | Jacobian determinant zero | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Inverse kinematics** | $\theta = f^{-1}(x, y, z, \phi, \theta_{or}, \psi)$ | 6 unknowns for 6-DOF robot | rad or m |
| 8 | **Payload equation** | $P_{max} = P_{rated} \cdot g$ | Max load at rated config | N |
| 9 | **Repeatability** | $RP = \pm k \cdot \sigma$ | $k$ = coverage factor, $\sigma$ = std dev | mm |
| 10 | **Position vector** | $P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}$ | 3D position | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **redundancy index $r$** is critical in modern robotics. For example, a **7-axis robot** ($n = 7$) performing a **6-DOF task** ($n_{task} = 6$) has **$r = 1$** redundant DOF. This redundancy allows the robot to **reconfigure** its joint angles to avoid obstacles without changing the end-effector's pose — a property heavily exploited in **surgical robotics** and **humanoid manipulators**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Required Axes | Reason |
|-------------|---------------|--------|
| **Palletizing boxes** | 4 | Need to pick from conveyor, place in stack; orientation fixed |
| **Arc welding** | 6 | Weld torch must follow a 3D path with specific torch angle |
| **PCB assembly** | 4 (SCARA) | High speed, vertical insertion only |
| **Automotive painting** | 6-7 | Complex curved surfaces require full orientation control |
| **Surgical suturing** | 7+ | Avoidance of anatomical obstacles |
| **Bin picking** | 6+ | Need to grasp parts in random orientations |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between Task DOF, Robot DOF, and Redundancy

The selection of a robot's number of axes for a given application is fundamentally a **constraint satisfaction problem**. The total degrees of freedom of a task must not exceed the robot's available axes, and any surplus forms **kinematic redundancy**.

### Step 1: Define the task DOF

A generic task in 3D space can be specified as the desired pose of the end-effector:

$$
P_{task} = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)
$$

where:
- $(x, y, z)$ are the three **translational** components (position)
- $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ are the three **rotational** components (orientation) — known as **Euler angles**

### Step 2: Count the effective task DOF

The number of task DOF is the number of independent components the application requires:

$$
n_{task} = T_{pos} + R_{pos}
$$

For example:
- A **pick-and-place** task with a fixed gripper orientation requires only 3 DOF: $n_{task} = 3 + 0 = 3$
- A **welding** task with full torch control requires 6 DOF: $n_{task} = 3 + 3 = 6$

### Step 3: Apply the dexterity condition

For the robot to be able to perform the task, the following must hold:

$$
n_{robot} \geq n_{task}
$$

If $n_{robot} = n_{task}$, the robot is said to be **non-redundant** for that task. If $n_{robot} > n_{task}$, the robot has **redundancy**.

### Step 4: Compute the redundancy index

$$
r = n_{robot} - n_{task}
$$

### Worked Example Derivation

**Problem:** A manufacturer wants to automate a **spray-painting** operation on a curved automotive body panel. The application requires:
- Position control in 3D: 3 DOF
- Orientation control (paint gun must always be perpendicular to surface): 3 DOF

A standard **6-axis articulated robot** is proposed. Determine if the robot is suitable and compute the redundancy.

$$
n_{task} = 3 + 3 = 6
$$

$$
n_{robot} = 6
$$

$$
n_{robot} \geq n_{task} \Rightarrow 6 \geq 6 \quad \text{✔ Satisfied}
$$

$$
r = n_{robot} - n_{task} = 6 - 6 = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot is **exactly sufficient** (non-redundant). It can perform the task but has no extra DOF to avoid obstacles or singularities.

Now, suppose a **7-axis redundant manipulator** (e.g., a Franka Emika Panda) is used instead:

$$
n_{robot} = 7
$$

$$
r = 7 - 6 = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot has **1 redundant DOF**, which it can use to **avoid singularities** (configurations where the Jacobian becomes singular and the robot loses controllability).

## 3.2 Analytical Selection Matrix: Axes vs. Application

The following decision matrix is used by KTU examiners and practicing engineers to map applications to the required number of axes:

| Application | Positioning (X, Y, Z) | Orientation (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) | $n_{task}$ | Recommended Robot | $r$ |
|-------------|----------------------|------------------------------|------------|-------------------|-----|
| Palletizing | Yes | No | **3** | 4-axis articulated | 1 |
| Machine tending (CNC) | Yes | No | **3** | 4-axis articulated | 1 |
| Spot welding | Yes | Partial | **5** | 6-axis articulated | 1 |
| Arc welding | Yes | Yes | **6** | 6-axis articulated | 0 |
| Spray painting | Yes | Yes | **6** | 6-axis articulated | 0 |
| Complex assembly | Yes | Yes | **6** | 7-axis redundant | 1 |
| Surgical suturing | Yes | Yes | **6** | 7-8 axis (redundant) | 1-2 |
| Bin picking | Yes | Yes | **6** | 6-axis + vision | 0 |

## 3.3 Decision Tree for Axis Selection

The selection process for the number of axes follows this logical flow:

$$
\text{Step 1: Identify task } \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Count DOF} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Check constraint} \rightarrow \text{Step 4: Add redundancy}
$$

Mathematically:

$$
n_{robot} = n_{task} + r_{desired}
$$

where $r_{desired} = 0$ for simple tasks, $1$ for obstacle-rich environments, and $\geq 2$ for highly constrained or human-like motion tasks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Robot Axis Architecture (6-DOF Articulated Robot)

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates how the 6 axes of a standard articulated robot are grouped into the **arm** and **wrist** subsystems, and how they interact with the controller and end-effector.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctrl[Robot Controller CPU]:::ctrlBlock

    subgraph armBlock["ARM SUBSYSTEM - Positioning Axes 1 to 3"]
        axis1["Axis 1 BASE ROTATION R Joint"]:::axisNode
        axis2["Axis 2 SHOULDER ROTATION R Joint"]:::axisNode
        axis3["Axis 3 ELBOW EXTENSION R or P Joint"]:::axisNode
    end

    subgraph wristBlock["WRIST SUBSYSTEM - Orientation Axes 4 to 6"]
        axis4["Axis 4 WRIST ROLL Rotation about forearm"]:::axisNode
        axis5["Axis 5 WRIST PITCH Bend up and down"]:::axisNode
        axis6["Axis 6 WRIST YAW Swivel side to side"]:::axisNode
    end

    ee["END EFFECTOR Tool Gripper Welder Painter"]:::toolNode

    wp["WORKPIECE Target object in 3D space"]:::workNode

    ctrl -->|Joint command signals| axis1
    ctrl -->|Joint command signals| axis2
    ctrl -->|Joint command signals| axis3
    ctrl -->|Joint command signals| axis4
    ctrl -->|Joint command signals| axis5
    ctrl -->|Joint command signals| axis6

    axis1 --> axis2
    axis2 --> axis3
    axis3 --> axis4
    axis4 --> axis5
    axis5 --> axis6
    axis6 --> ee
    ee -->|Manipulates| wp

    classDef ctrlBlock fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef axisNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef toolNode fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef workNode fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Axis Selection Decision Flow

The following block diagram depicts the engineering decision process for selecting the appropriate number of axes for a given industrial application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Define Industrial Application]):::startNode
    ident["Identify Task Requirements Position and Orientation Needs"]:::procNode
    count["Count Required DOF nTask = Tpos + Rpos"]:::calcNode
    check{Degree of Freedom nRobot GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO nTask}:::decideNode

    path3A["3-AXIS Cartesian Delta Used for pick and place only positioning"]:::resultNode
    path4A["4-AXIS SCARA or Cylindrical Used for palletizing machine tending"]:::resultNode
    path6A["6-AXIS Articulated Used for welding painting and assembly"]:::resultNode
    path7A["7-AXIS Redundant Used for surgery complex assembly with obstacles"]:::resultNode

    reject[Task CANNOT be completed Increase nRobot or simplify task]:::rejectNode
    redun["Compute Redundancy r equals nRobot minus nTask Add obstacle avoidance if r is greater than zero"]:::procNode
    end2([End: Select robot with appropriate axes]):::endNode

    start --> ident --> count --> check
    check -->|nTask equals 3| path3A --> end2
    check -->|nTask equals 4| path4A --> end2
    check -->|nTask equals 6 and simple environment| path6A --> redun --> end2
    check -->|nTask equals 6 and complex environment| path7A --> redun --> end2
    check -->|nRobot less than nTask| reject

    classDef startNode fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endNode fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef procNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef calcNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decideNode fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef resultNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef rejectNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: DOF Allocation Across the Kinematic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base["BASE PLATE Fixed to floor"]:::baseNode
    j1["JOINT 1 Base rotation theta1"]:::jointNode
    j2["JOINT 2 Shoulder pitch theta2"]:::jointNode
    j3["JOINT 3 Elbow pitch theta3"]:::jointNode
    j4["JOINT 4 Wrist roll theta4"]:::jointNode
    j5["JOINT 5 Wrist pitch theta5"]:::jointNode
    j6["JOINT 6 Wrist yaw theta6"]:::jointNode
    flange["FLANGE End effector mounting plate"]:::flangeNode

    base --- j1 --- j2 --- j3 --- j4 --- j5 --- j6 --- flange

    classDef baseNode fill:#374151,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Concept of Degrees of Freedom `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Degree of Freedom (DOF)" with respect to a robotic manipulator. What is the maximum number of DOF required to position and orient an object arbitrarily in 3D space?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is defined as the number of independent **motions** (rotations or translations) that the manipulator can perform to position and orient its end-effector in space. Each DOF corresponds to one **axis** or **joint** of the robot.

In **3-dimensional space (ℝ³)**, a rigid body has a maximum of **6 degrees of freedom**: 3 **translational** DOF (along X, Y, Z) and 3 **rotational** DOF (roll, pitch, yaw). A manipulator with 6 DOF can theoretically reach **any position and any orientation** within its reachable workspace.

Therefore, the maximum number of DOF required is **6**.

---

### Question 2: Difference Between Arm and Wrist Axes `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between the major axes and minor axes of an industrial robot. Mention their functional roles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Major Axes (Axes 1, 2, 3)** | **Minor Axes (Axes 4, 5, 6)** |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Location** | Form the **arm** of the robot | Form the **wrist** of the robot |
| **Function** | **Positioning** the wrist in 3D space | **Orienting** the end-effector in 3D space |
| **Joint types** | Typically R-R-R or R-P-P | Typically R-R-R |
| **Effect on** | $(x, y, z)$ coordinates of the tool | $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ Euler angles of the tool |

**Major axes** decide **where** the tool goes, while **minor axes** decide **how** the tool is oriented. Together, they form the 6 DOF of a standard articulated manipulator.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A robotic manipulator is to be selected for an arc-welding application in an automotive assembly line. The task requires the welding torch to follow a complex 3D path along the car body while maintaining a constant torch angle (perpendicular to the surface).**

**(a)** Identify the number of task degrees of freedom and the type of robot configuration suitable for this application. Justify your selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If a 7-axis redundant manipulator is selected instead, calculate the redundancy index and explain the engineering benefits of having this redundancy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Identifying Task DOF and Configuration

**Step 1: Identify task requirements.**
- The welding torch must reach a **3D point** on the car body: requires **3 translational DOF** (X, Y, Z).
- The torch must be **perpendicular to the surface** at all times: this requires control of **all 3 orientation angles** (roll, pitch, yaw) → **3 rotational DOF**.

**Step 2: Compute task DOF.**

$$
n_{task} = 3 + 3 = 6 \text{ DOF}
$$

**Step 3: Select robot configuration.**

Since $n_{task} = 6$, the robot must have **at least 6 axes** to perform the task. The most suitable configuration is a **6-axis articulated (jointed-arm) robot** with **R-R-R-R-R-R** joint sequence.

**Justification:**
- It offers the **maximum dexterity** within a compact footprint, suitable for automotive shop floors.
- It can position the torch anywhere in 3D space and orient it in any direction.
- It is the industry standard for **arc welding** (e.g., FANUC ARC Mate, ABB IRB, KUKA KR series).
- Alternatives like **Cartesian** (3 DOF) or **SCARA** (4 DOF) robots **cannot** perform this task as they lack full orientation control.

**Conclusion:** A **6-axis articulated robot** is the most suitable choice. **(7 marks awarded)**

##### Part (b) — Redundancy Index and Engineering Benefits

**Step 1: Compute the redundancy index.**

$$
r = n_{robot} - n_{task} = 7 - 6 = 1
$$

The robot has **1 redundant DOF**.

**Step 2: Engineering benefits of redundancy.**

1. **Obstacle avoidance:** With 1 extra DOF, the robot can reconfigure its arm (e.g., bend its "elbow" up or down) to avoid obstacles like fixtures, jigs, or human co-workers, while keeping the end-effector pose fixed.
2. **Singularity avoidance:** When the robot approaches a **singular configuration** (where the Jacobian determinant $\det(J) = 0$ and motion is lost in one direction), the redundant DOF can be used to "steer" the arm away from this configuration.
3. **Improved manipulability:** The robot can optimize its joint configuration to maximize dexterity, reach awkward positions, and reduce joint wear.
4. **Fault tolerance:** If one joint temporarily fails or is operating near its mechanical limit, the redundant DOF allows graceful degradation of performance.
5. **Human-like motion:** 7-axis robots mimic the human arm's redundancy, enabling smoother and more natural trajectories useful in collaborative robotics (cobots).

**Conclusion:** A 7-axis redundant robot provides $r = 1$ extra DOF that significantly enhances **dexterity, safety, and reliability** in complex welding environments. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A pharmaceutical company wants to automate a high-speed pick-and-place operation for sorting tablet boxes on a conveyor belt. The boxes are rectangular and the gripper must grasp them with a fixed orientation (top face up).**

**(a)** Calculate the task DOF and recommend the most appropriate robot configuration (with justification) and the number of axes required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the recommended robot with a 6-axis articulated robot in terms of cost, speed, workspace, and programming complexity for this specific application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Task DOF Calculation and Robot Selection

**Step 1: Identify task requirements.**
- Boxes move on a conveyor → the robot must reach **3D positions** in space: **3 translational DOF** (X, Y, Z).
- The gripper must grasp the boxes with a **fixed orientation** (top face up) → **0 rotational DOF** required.

**Step 2: Compute task DOF.**

$$
n_{task} = 3 + 0 = 3 \text{ DOF}
$$

**Step 3: Select robot configuration.**

A robot with **at least 3 axes** is sufficient. However, in practice, **4-axis SCARA robots** (R-R-P-P) are the industry standard for this application because:

- They have **3 DOF for positioning** + **1 DOF for vertical insertion/grasp** (the fourth axis is typically used to rotate the gripper or for vertical compliance).
- They are **extremely fast** (cycle times of 0.3–0.5 seconds), ideal for high-speed pick-and-place.
- They are **cost-effective** and easy to program for 2D-plane operations.
- They offer excellent repeatability (±0.02 mm) for pharmaceutical applications.

**Recommended configuration:** **4-axis SCARA robot** (e.g., Epson SCARA, Yamaha YK series).

**Justification of $n_{robot} = 4$:**

$$
n_{robot} = 4 \geq n_{task} = 3
$$

$$
r = 4 - 3 = 1 \text{ (1 redundant DOF for vertical motion)}
$$

The extra 1 DOF adds the **vertical insertion stroke** needed to descend, grasp, and lift the boxes from the conveyor. **(7 marks awarded)**

##### Part (b) — Comparison: 4-axis SCARA vs. 6-axis Articulated

| Parameter | **4-axis SCARA** | **6-axis Articulated** |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Cost** | **Low to medium** (₹3–8 lakh) | **High** (₹15–40 lakh) |
| **Speed** | **Very high** (cycle time 0.3–0.5 s) | **Moderate** (cycle time 1–2 s) |
| **Workspace** | **Cylindrical** (limited height) | **Spherical** (large 3D reach) |
| **Payload** | 1–10 kg typical | Up to 1000 kg available |
| **Orientation control** | **None** (fixed Z-axis tool) | **Full** (any roll, pitch, yaw) |
| **Programming complexity** | **Low** (simple 2D pick-place) | **High** (6-DOF inverse kinematics) |
| **Footprint** | Compact (ceiling-mountable) | Larger floor footprint |
| **Best suited for** | Pick-and-place, PCB assembly | Welding, painting, complex assembly |
| **Pharma suitability** | **★★★★★ Excellent** | **★★ Overkill** |

**Conclusion for the given application:**

For the pharmaceutical pick-and-place task, the **4-axis SCARA** is the optimal choice because:
1. The task has only **3 DOF** (no orientation needed).
2. SCARA's **high speed** maximizes throughput.
3. The **lower cost** offers better ROI.
4. The **simpler programming** reduces commissioning time and cost.

A 6-axis articulated robot would be **overspecified**, more expensive, slower, and unnecessarily complex for this task. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Number of Axes" with "Number of Motors"** — Some students count motors, but the correct metric is **independent DOF**. A differential drive has 1 motor but provides 2 DOF (translation + rotation) on a mobile base. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Euler angles in DOF counting** — When asked "how many DOF does a welding task need?", students often answer "3" (only position). Always remember: **3 translations + 3 rotations = 6 DOF** for full pose control. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Writing "6-axis is always better"** — Examiners deduct marks for this. The correct engineering answer is: **"Choose $n_{robot} \geq n_{task}$, but minimize excess DOF to reduce cost and complexity."** **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Not drawing the distinction between major and minor axes** — Always explicitly label the **arm (Axes 1–3)** and the **wrist (Axes 4–6)** in your answer. This shows deep conceptual understanding. **(−0.5 to 1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Confusing "redundancy" with "redundant safety"** — Redundancy in robotics means **extra DOF for reconfiguration**, not backup safety systems. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Number of Axes"**
>
> - **Definition:** Number of Axes = **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** = number of independent, controlled motions of the manipulator.
> - **Maximum DOF in 3D space = 6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
> - **Major axes (1, 2, 3)** → Arm → **Positioning** in 3D space.
> - **Minor axes (4, 5, 6)** → Wrist → **Orientation** of end-effector.
> - **Task DOF:** $n_{task} = T_{pos} + R_{pos}$.
> - **Selection rule:** $n_{robot} \geq n_{task}$.
> - **Redundancy index:** $r = n_{robot} - n_{task}$.
> - **6-axis articulated** is the industry standard for **arc welding, painting, and complex assembly**.
> - **4-axis SCARA** is best for **high-speed pick-and-place** with fixed tool orientation.
> - **3-axis Cartesian** is used for **simple gantry pick-and-place** and **CNC loading**.
> - **7+ axis redundant robots** offer **obstacle avoidance, singularity avoidance, and human-like motion**.
> - **Common joint notations:** **R** (Revolute) and **P** (Prismatic).
> - **The number of axes affects:** cost, speed, dexterity, workspace shape, programming complexity, and payload capability.
> - **Examiner's mantra:** *"Just enough axes for the task — no more, no less."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of robotic manipulators, the **Work Volume** (also called the **Workspace**, **Working Envelope**, or **Reach Envelope**) is formally defined as the **locus of all points in three-dimensional space that can be reached by the robot's end-effector (tool center point, or TCP)** under all possible combinations of its joint variables, subject to the mechanical and geometric constraints of the manipulator's kinematic chain.

Mathematically, for a serial manipulator with $n$ revolute and prismatic joints, the work volume $W$ is expressed as:

$$
W = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q}), \quad \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{Q} \}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the position vector of the TCP in the **Cartesian task space**.
- $f(\cdot)$ is the **forward kinematics mapping** function.
- $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$ is the vector of **generalized joint coordinates**.
- $\mathcal{Q}$ is the **feasible joint space**, bounded by the mechanical joint limits $q_i^{\min} \leq q_i \leq q_i^{\max}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The work volume is the three-dimensional region of space within which the robot can position its end-effector. It is determined by the **kinematic configuration**, the **link lengths**, and the **mechanical joint limits** of the manipulator.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lamp Swing" Intuition

Imagine a **desk lamp** with a hinged arm mounted on a table:
- The **base** of the lamp is fixed (like the robot's base frame).
- The **arm** can rotate (like a revolute joint) and also extend (like a prismatic joint).
- The **bulb** (end-effector) can be moved to any point within a dome-shaped region above the table, but it **cannot** reach the floor below the table, nor can it reach infinitely far away.

That dome-shaped "swept region" of all possible bulb positions is the **work volume** of the lamp. Every robot, regardless of how complex it is, has a similar 3D "swept region" dictated purely by its **mechanical skeleton** and **joint limits** — not by its software, payload, or sensors.

## 1.3 Why Work Volume Matters in Robotic Cell Design

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, Work Volume is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 4 because it directly determines:
> - Whether a robot can physically reach all required workpieces in a manufacturing cell.
> - The **footprint** and **layout** of the robotic cell.
> - **Reachability analysis** during offline programming.
> - The choice of robot configuration for a given application (welding, painting, pick-and-place, etc.).

## 1.4 Sub-Categorization of Work Volume

The work volume is further classified into three nested sub-regions, in **decreasing order of capability**:

| Sub-Region | Formal Name | Definition | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outer Boundary** | **Reachable Workspace** | All points reachable by the TCP under *any orientation* of the end-effector. | Determines the **maximum installation envelope**. |
| **Inner Region** | **Dexterous Workspace** | All points reachable by the TCP in *all possible orientations* (full 6 DOF orientation control). | Reserved for **complex assembly** tasks. |
| **Intermediate Region** | **Quasi-Dexterous Workspace** | Points reachable with *more than one* (but not all) orientations. | Useful for **welding, painting, sealing** where orientation flexibility matters. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For most industrial manipulators, the **dexterous workspace is significantly smaller** than the reachable workspace. A revolute robot with 6 joints typically has a dexterous workspace of **near-zero volume** (often a single curve or point) — only the wrist center can attain arbitrary orientations at a handful of special points.

## 1.5 Standard Physical Metrics Used in Work Volume Analysis

The following **bolded constants and metrics** are universally used in KTU board examinations:

- **$r$** — radial distance from the robot base axis.
- **$h$** — vertical height along the base axis.
- **$l_1, l_2, \dots, l_n$** — lengths of individual links of the manipulator.
- **$\theta_i$** — angular range of the $i$-th revolute joint (in **radians**).
- **$d_i$** — linear range of the $i$-th prismatic joint (in **metres**).
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — the number of independent positional/orientation parameters of the end-effector.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Philosophy Behind Work Volume

The work volume is essentially the **image of the joint space** under the forward kinematics map. To understand it deeply, we must analyze how each **fundamental joint type** contributes to volume generation.

### 2.1.1 Building Blocks of Work Volume

| Joint Type | Symbol | Effect on Workspace | Geometric Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | $\theta$ | Rotational sweep about a fixed axis | Generates **arcs, sectors, spherical shells** |
| **Prismatic (P)** | $d$ | Linear translation along an axis | Generates **rectangular prisms, cylinders** |
| **Helical/Screw** | Rare in industrial robots | Combined rotation + translation | Generates **helical swept volumes** |

> [!NOTE]
> Every work volume can be **decomposed** into a sequence of these primitive geometric operations applied to a reference point (the wrist center).

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Robot Configurations & Their Work Volumes

KTU 2024 Scheme **mandates** the study of work volumes for the five standard robot configurations. Each has a **distinct geometric shape** that must be memorized.

### 2.2.1 Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — PPP

**Structure:** Three mutually orthogonal prismatic joints.

**Joint limits:**
- $0 \leq x \leq X_{max}$
- $0 \leq y \leq Y_{max}$
- $0 \leq z \leq Z_{max}$

**Work Volume Shape:** A **rectangular parallelepiped (cuboid)**.

**Volume formula:**

$$
V_{Cartesian} = X_{max} \cdot Y_{max} \cdot Z_{max}
$$

### 2.2.2 Cylindrical Robot — RPP

**Structure:** One revolute joint (base), followed by two prismatic joints (radial and vertical).

**Joint limits:**
- $0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$ (or a specified angular range)
- $r_{min} \leq r \leq r_{max}$
- $0 \leq z \leq Z_{max}$

**Work Volume Shape:** A **hollow cylinder** (with the inner radius possibly zero, giving a solid cylinder).

**Volume formula:**

$$
V_{Cylindrical} = \pi \cdot (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) \cdot Z_{max}
$$

### 2.2.3 Spherical (Polar) Robot — RRP

**Structure:** Two revolute joints (base rotation and shoulder elevation), followed by one prismatic joint (radial reach).

**Joint limits:**
- $0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$
- $-\phi_{min} \leq \phi \leq \phi_{max}$ (elevation angle)
- $r_{min} \leq r \leq r_{max}$

**Work Volume Shape:** A **portion of a spherical shell** (hollow sphere with inner and outer radii).

**Volume formula (for a full sphere sector):**

$$
V_{Spherical} = \frac{2}{3} \pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3) (1 - \cos\phi_{max})
$$

### 2.2.4 Articulated (Revolute/Anthropomorphic) Robot — RRR (with shoulder, elbow, wrist)

**Structure:** Three revolute joints at the base, shoulder, and elbow, forming a serial chain.

**Joint limits:**
- $\theta_1$: base rotation
- $\theta_2$: shoulder pitch
- $\theta_3$: elbow pitch

**Work Volume Shape:** A **complex "doughnut-like" or "kidney-shaped" region** — the intersection of two spherical shells (from the shoulder-elbow chain) swept by the base rotation.

**Geometric characterization:**

$$
r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert, \qquad r_{max} = l_1 + l_2
$$

where $l_1$ is the upper arm length and $l_2$ is the forearm length. The actual 3D work volume is the **rotation of a 2D annulus** about the base axis, with cross-sections modified by the joint limits.

### 2.2.5 SCARA Robot — RRP (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)

**Structure:** Two revolute joints for planar positioning, one prismatic joint for vertical insertion, plus a final revolute joint for end-effector rotation.

**Work Volume Shape:** A **flat cylindrical disk** (short height, large radius) — ideal for vertical assembly tasks.

**Volume formula:**

$$
V_{SCARA} = \pi \cdot (l_1 + l_2)^2 \cdot Z_{range}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master reference table** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Work Volume. Every row is directly examinable.

| Configuration | Joint Notation | Work Volume Shape | Volume Equation (m³) | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian** | P-P-P | Rectangular cuboid | $V = X_{max} Y_{max} Z_{max}$ | Pick-and-place, CNC loading |
| **Cylindrical** | R-P-P | Hollow cylinder | $V = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) Z_{max}$ | Assembly, machine tending |
| **Spherical (Polar)** | R-R-P | Spherical shell sector | $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)(1 - \cos\phi_{max})$ | Welding, die casting |
| **Articulated** | R-R-R (plus wrist) | Complex annular region | $V \approx \int_0^{2\pi} \!\!\int \!\!\! \int \, r \, dz \, dr \, d\theta$ | Painting, welding, sealing |
| **SCARA** | R-R-P-R | Flat cylindrical disk | $V = \pi (l_1 + l_2)^2 \cdot h$ | Vertical assembly, PCB insertion |

### 2.3.1 Reachability Equations (Critical for Numerical Problems)

For a 2-link planar chain with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$:

$$
r_{max} = l_1 + l_2 \quad \text{(arm fully extended)}
$$

$$
