r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert \quad \text{(arm fully folded)}
$$

$$
\text{Reach} = l_1 + l_2
$$

The reachable area in the 2D plane (annulus area) is:

$$
A = \pi \left[ (l_1 + l_2)^2 - (l_1 - l_2)^2 \right] = 4\pi l_1 l_2
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding work volume is **not academic** — it is the very first calculation a robotics engineer performs when designing a manufacturing cell. Mistakes in this analysis lead to **catastrophic cell failures** (robot cannot reach critical fixtures).

**Industrial Use Cases:**

1. **Automotive Welding Cell:** The robot must reach every weld point on a car body. The work volume must envelop the car body's geometry with at least **20% margin** for torch orientation.
2. **Palletizing Cell:** The work volume must cover the **pallet surface** at floor level and the **infeed conveyor height** at the top.
3. **Painting Cell:** The work volume must envelope the entire part from multiple angles — articulated robots are preferred because their curved work envelopes match car body contours.
4. **Injection Molding Machine Tending:** The robot must reach **inside the mold** (low $z$), **across the conveyor** (medium $z$), and **onto the inspection station** (high $z$) — favouring cylindrical or articulated robots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Volume of a 2-Link Planar Manipulator (Articulated Robot in 2D)

This derivation is the **single most repeated numerical problem** in KTU ESE papers.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a 2-link planar manipulator in the $xy$-plane with:
- **Link 1** (upper arm) of length $l_1$ at shoulder angle $\theta_2$.
- **Link 2** (forearm) of length $l_2$ at elbow angle $\theta_3$ (relative to Link 1).
- **Base joint** angle $\theta_1$ rotates the entire arm about the $z$-axis.

The TCP position is given by the standard 2-link forward kinematics.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the position of the elbow joint $E$ in the base frame.

$$
E_x = l_1 \cos\theta_2, \qquad E_y = l_1 \sin\theta_2
$$

**Text:** The elbow lies at the tip of Link 1, which is rotated by the shoulder angle $\theta_2$ from the base $x$-axis.

**Step 2:** Write the position of the end-effector $P$ relative to the elbow.

$$
P_x^{rel} = l_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3), \qquad P_y^{rel} = l_2 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

**Text:** The forearm adds Link 2 in the direction of the absolute angle $(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$, which is the shoulder angle plus the elbow angle relative to the upper arm.

**Step 3:** Apply the base rotation $\theta_1$ to obtain the absolute TCP position.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_x &= \cos\theta_1 \left[ l_1 \cos\theta_2 + l_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \right] \\
P_y &= \sin\theta_1 \left[ l_1 \cos\theta_2 + l_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \right] \\
P_z &= l_1 \sin\theta_2 + l_2 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text:** The base rotation $\theta_1$ projects the planar position onto a circle in the $xy$-plane, while the $z$-coordinate is determined solely by the sum of vertical projections of both links.

**Step 4:** Compute the radial distance $r$ from the base axis.

$$
r = \sqrt{P_x^2 + P_y^2} = l_1 \cos\theta_2 + l_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

**Text:** Taking the square root of the sum of squares, the $\cos\theta_1$ and $\sin\theta_1$ factors collapse to unity, giving the planar distance.

**Step 5:** Determine the **maximum reach** when the arm is fully extended.

**Condition:** $\theta_3 = 0$ (forearm aligned with upper arm, "elbow straight").

$$
r_{max} = l_1 \cos\theta_2 + l_2 \cos\theta_2 = (l_1 + l_2)\cos\theta_2
$$

**Text:** When $\theta_3 = 0$, the cosine of the sum simplifies, and $r$ is maximized when $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm horizontal):

$$
\boxed{r_{max} = l_1 + l_2}
$$

**Step 6:** Determine the **minimum reach** when the arm is fully folded.

**Condition:** $\theta_2 + \theta_3 = \pi$ (forearm folded back on the upper arm).

$$
r_{min} = l_1 \cos\theta_2 + l_2 \cos(\pi) = l_1 \cos\theta_2 - l_2
$$

**Text:** Maximizing the magnitude of $r_{min}$ (in the negative sense) gives the inner boundary. With $\theta_2 = 0$:

$$
\boxed{r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the 3D work volume by integrating over the base rotation $\theta_1 \in [0, 2\pi]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \int_0^{2\pi} \!\!\! \int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} \!\!\! \int_{z_{min}(\theta_1, r)}^{z_{max}(\theta_1, r)} r \, dz \, dr \, d\theta_1 \\
  &= 2\pi \int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r \cdot h(r) \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text:** The $r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta_1$ term is the cylindrical Jacobian volume element, and $h(r)$ is the local height of the work volume at radius $r$. For a symmetric articulated robot with unrestricted shoulder joint:

$$
h(r) = 2\sqrt{r_{max}^2 - r^2} \quad \text{at each radial distance}
$$

**Step 8 (Simplified form for unrestricted joints):**

$$
V_{articulated} = 2\pi \int_{\vert l_1 - l_2 \vert}^{l_1 + l_2} 2r \sqrt{r_{max}^2 - r^2} \, dr
$$

This evaluates to:

$$
\boxed{V_{articulated} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \left[ 2(l_1^3 + l_2^3) - (l_1 + l_2)^3 + \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert^3 \right] \cdot \sin\phi_{max}}
$$

where $\phi_{max}$ is the half-elevation range of the shoulder joint.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Robot for an Application

| Application | Required Work Volume Shape | Recommended Configuration | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PCB assembly (vertical insertion)** | Flat, wide cylinder | SCARA | Short vertical reach, large planar reach, high stiffness vertically |
| **Car body painting** | Large, curved, enveloping | Articulated (6 DOF) | Curved work envelope matches car body contour |
| **CNC machine tending** | Rectangular box with door access | Cartesian or Gantry | Cuboid envelope covers machine door and pallet positions |
| **Die casting extraction** | Sphere sector above the machine | Spherical/Polar | Vertical extraction with horizontal swing |
| **Stacking boxes on a pallet** | Cylinder between conveyor and pallet | Cylindrical or Articulated | Vertical stacking with horizontal reach |
| **Arc welding of pipe joints** | Toroidal around the pipe | Articulated with positioner | Curved trajectory around the cylindrical workpiece |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A 14-mark question often asks you to **justify** the choice of robot configuration for a given application. Always link the **work volume shape** to the **workpiece geometry** and the **process path**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Work Volume Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical pipeline** used by a robotics engineer to analyse work volume during cell design. This is the **safe block-level representation** requested in the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Robot Configuration PPP RPP RRP RRR SCARA] --> B[Extract Kinematic Parameters l1 l2 l3 theta1 theta2 theta3]
    B --> C[Define Joint Limits qmin qmax for each joint]
    C --> D[Apply Forward Kinematics Map x equals f of q]
    D --> E{Workspace Sub-Region}
    E --> F1[Reachable Workspace Boundary]
    E --> F2[Dexterous Workspace Interior]
    E --> F3[Quasi-Dexterous Annulus]
    F1 --> G[Compute Geometric Shape Cuboid Cylinder Sphere Annulus Disk]
    F2 --> H[Validate Application Reachability with 20 percent Margin]
    F3 --> H
    H --> I[Final Robot Cell Layout Selection]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Output Work Volume Specification to Cell Design Document]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Robot Configuration Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Application Analysis] --> S2{What is the workpiece geometry?}
    S2 -->|Rectangular box| S3[Select Cartesian PPP Robot]
    S2 -->|Cylindrical| S4[Select Cylindrical RPP Robot]
    S2 -->|Spherical tank| S5[Select Spherical RRP Robot]
    S2 -->|Complex curved surface| S6[Select Articulated RRR Robot]
    S2 -->|Flat 2D pattern vertical insert| S7[Select SCARA RRP Robot]
    S3 --> S8[Verify cuboid work volume covers all task points]
    S4 --> S9[Verify cylindrical envelope with 20 percent safety margin]
    S5 --> S10[Verify spherical sector with proper elevation range]
    S6 --> S11[Verify annular region with full orientation at center]
    S7 --> S12[Verify flat disk geometry for assembly tolerance]
    S8 --> S13[Finalize Cell Layout]
    S9 --> S13
    S10 --> S13
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S13
```

## 4.3 Comparative Work Volume Map (Schematic Description)

The following block diagram illustrates the **cross-sectional comparison** of the five work volumes when viewed in the $xz$-plane (passing through the base axis):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CartesianCuboid[Cartesian PPP Work Volume]
        C1[Rectangular block X by Y by Z]
    end
    subgraph CylindricalEnv[Cylindrical RPP Work Volume]
        C2[Hollow cylinder with height Z and radius rmax]
    end
    subgraph SphericalEnv[Spherical RRP Work Volume]
        C3[Spherical shell sector with inner radius rmin and outer radius rmax]
    end
    subgraph ArticulatedEnv[Articulated RRR Work Volume]
        C4[Annular kidney shaped region l1 plus l2 outer minus l1 minus l2 inner]
    end
    subgraph SCARAEnv[SCARA RRP Work Volume]
        C5[Flat disk with radius l1 plus l2 and short height h]
    end
    C1 --> D[Application Matching]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Optimal Robot Selection]
```

## 4.4 Joint Limit Influence on Work Volume (Sub-Region Classification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Work Volume from Joint Limits] --> B[Reachable Workspace outer shell]
    A --> C[Dexterous Workspace interior core]
    A --> D[Quasi Dexterous Workspace annulus between core and shell]
    B --> E[Achievable with any orientation]
    C --> F[Achievable with all 6 DOF orientations]
    D --> G[Achievable with multiple but not all orientations]
    E --> H[Used for Reach Envelope Sizing]
    F --> I[Used for Complex Assembly Tasks]
    G --> J[Used for Welding Painting Sealing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Work Volume of a robotic manipulator. List the three sub-regions of the work volume in increasing order of capability.**

**Model Answer:**

The **work volume** of a robotic manipulator is defined as the three-dimensional region of space that can be reached by the **end-effector (TCP)** of the robot under all possible combinations of joint motions, subject to the mechanical joint limits.

The three sub-regions in **increasing order of capability** are:

1. **Reachable Workspace** — all points reachable in **any orientation**.
2. **Quasi-Dexterous Workspace** — all points reachable in **more than one** (but not all) orientations.
3. **Dexterous Workspace** — all points reachable in **all possible orientations** (full 6-DOF orientation control).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the work volume shapes of a Cartesian robot and a SCARA robot.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cartesian Robot | SCARA Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Configuration** | P-P-P | R-R-P-R |
| **Work Volume Shape** | Rectangular cuboid | Flat cylindrical disk |
| **Vertical Extent** | Full cuboid height | Very short (limited by prismatic joint) |
| **Lateral Extent** | Defined by X and Y travel | Defined by sum of link lengths $(l_1 + l_2)$ |
| **Typical Application** | CNC loading, pick-and-place | Vertical PCB assembly, screw insertion |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — 14 Marks]`

**(a)** A 2-link planar articulated robot has upper arm length $l_1 = 0.5$ m and forearm length $l_2 = 0.4$ m. Determine: **(i)** the maximum reach, **(ii)** the minimum reach, and **(iii)** the planar reachable area. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the work volume of a spherical (polar) robot with $r_{max} = 1.2$ m, $r_{min} = 0.4$ m, and elevation range $\phi_{max} = 90°$. Calculate its work volume. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1: Maximum reach (arm fully extended).**

$$
r_{max} = l_1 + l_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Minimum reach (arm fully folded).**

$$
r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.4 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Planar reachable area (annulus between $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$).**

$$
A = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) = \pi (0.9^2 - 0.1^2) = \pi (0.81 - 0.01) = 0.80\pi
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 2.513 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Verification using $4\pi l_1 l_2$ formula:**

$$
A = 4\pi l_1 l_2 = 4\pi (0.5)(0.4) = 0.8\pi \approx 2.513 \text{ m}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Sketch the work volume** — a **spherical shell sector** with inner radius 0.4 m, outer radius 1.2 m, opening upward through 90° (from the horizontal plane to the vertical).

**Step 2: Apply the spherical sector volume formula.**

$$
V = \frac{2}{3} \pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)(1 - \cos\phi_{max})
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.** With $\phi_{max} = 90°$, $\cos\phi_{max} = 0$, so:

$$
V = \frac{2}{3} \pi (1.2^3 - 0.4^3)(1 - 0) = \frac{2}{3} \pi (1.728 - 0.064)
$$

$$
V = \frac{2}{3} \pi (1.664) = \frac{3.328\pi}{3} \approx 3.485 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{spherical} \approx 3.485 \text{ m}^3}
$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the work volumes of the five standard robot configurations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical robot has a maximum radial reach $r_{max} = 1.5$ m, minimum radial reach $r_{min} = 0.3$ m, and vertical travel $Z_{max} = 2.0$ m. Calculate its work volume. If the application requires covering a **rectangular table of 2.5 m × 1.5 m at floor level**, can this robot service the entire table from a single base location? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

Refer to the table in Section 2.2 and the comparative diagram in Section 4.3. The five standard work volumes are:

1. **Cartesian (PPP):** Rectangular cuboid of dimensions $X_{max} \times Y_{max} \times Z_{max}$.
2. **Cylindrical (RPP):** Hollow cylinder of radius range $[r_{min}, r_{max}]$ and height $Z_{max}$.
3. **Spherical (RRP):** Spherical shell sector.
4. **Articulated (RRR):** Complex annular / kidney-shaped region.
5. **SCARA (RRP):** Flat cylindrical disk of radius $(l_1 + l_2)$ and short height $h$.

**Sketch (textual representation for board exam):**

```
Cartesian:   [Cuboid outline with X, Y, Z axes labelled]
Cylindrical: [Cylinder with dashed inner cylinder, height Z, radius r]
Spherical:   [Quarter-sphere shell with r_min and r_max]
Articulated: [Two arcs of radius l1+l2 and |l1-l2| with l1, l2 labelled]
SCARA:       [Flat disk of radius l1+l2, very small thickness h]
```

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Compute the cylindrical work volume.**

$$
V = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) Z_{max} = \pi (1.5^2 - 0.3^2)(2.0)
$$

$$
V = \pi (2.25 - 0.09)(2.0) = \pi (2.16)(2.0) = 4.32\pi \approx 13.57 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{cylindrical} \approx 13.57 \text{ m}^3}
$$

**Step 2: Verify whether the table can be serviced.**

**Table dimensions:** 2.5 m (length) × 1.5 m (width).

**Step 3: Calculate the minimum and maximum reach requirements to cover the table corners.**

Place the robot base at the centre of one edge of the table. The **diagonal** of the table is:

$$
d_{diag} = \sqrt{2.5^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{8.5} \approx 2.915 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with the robot's reach envelope.**

- The robot can reach up to **1.5 m radially** from its base.
- The farthest corner of the 2.5 m × 1.5 m table is at a radial distance of **~2.915 m** (when base is at the centre of an edge).

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

$$
r_{max} = 1.5 \text{ m} \;\; < \;\; 2.915 \text{ m} = d_{diag}
$$

The robot **cannot service the entire table from a single base location** because the table's diagonal exceeds the maximum radial reach. The table is too large. **Either a larger cylindrical robot, a gantry robot, or relocating the robot base to multiple positions would be required.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (always state m, m², or m³ explicitly).
> 2. **Confusing $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$** in cylindrical and spherical volume formulas.
> 3. **Using $\cos 90° = 1$ instead of 0** in spherical sector problems.
> 4. **Neglecting to justify** the application feasibility — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the *justification* paragraph.
> 5. **Drawing 2D sketches instead of 3D** — a work volume sketch must show depth; even a simple isometric block diagram earns more marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Work Volume**

- [ ] **Work volume = reachable region of TCP** in 3D Cartesian space, dictated by kinematics + joint limits.
- [ ] **Three sub-regions:** Reachable (largest) ⊃ Quasi-Dexterous ⊃ Dexterous (smallest).
- [ ] **Five canonical configurations** with their distinct work volume shapes must be memorized:
  - Cartesian → **Cuboid**
  - Cylindrical → **Hollow cylinder**
  - Spherical → **Spherical shell sector**
  - Articulated → **Annular / kidney-shaped region**
  - SCARA → **Flat disk**
- [ ] **Reach formulas for 2-link planar arm:** $r_{max} = l_1 + l_2$, $r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$.
- [ ] **Planar reachable area:** $A = 4\pi l_1 l_2$ (also equals $\pi(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$).
- [ ] **Cylindrical volume:** $V = \pi(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) Z_{max}$.
- [ ] **Spherical volume:** $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi(r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)(1 - \cos\phi_{max})$.
- [ ] **Cartesian volume:** $V = X_{max} Y_{max} Z_{max}$.
- [ ] **SCARA volume:** $V = \pi(l_1 + l_2)^2 h$.
- [ ] **Joint symbols:** P = Prismatic (linear), R = Revolute (rotational).
- [ ] **Application matching** is the most common 14-mark KTU question pattern.
- [ ] Always include a **20% safety margin** when matching a work volume to a real workspace.
- [ ] **Units** must accompany every numerical answer (m, m², or m³).
- [ ] **Drawings** must be 3D isometric or labelled 2D cross-sections — never flat unscaled boxes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacity and Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotic manipulators, **Capacity** and **Speed** are the two primary kinematic-dynamic performance specifications that quantitatively define a robot's operational envelope and productivity. Together, they form the basis for robot selection, cycle-time estimation, and manufacturing cell throughput analysis.

- **Capacity (Payload Capacity):** The maximum mass, inclusive of the end-effector and the workpiece, that the robot manipulator can repeatedly handle at a specified pose (typically at full reach, worst-case wrist orientation) without exceeding any of the structural, thermal, or servo-drive limits of its joints. It is measured in **kilograms (kg)** or **Newtons (N)**.

- **Speed (Rated Speed):** The maximum linear or angular velocity attainable by the Tool Center Point (TCP) or by an individual joint axis respectively, under a specified payload and along a specified trajectory. It is measured in **m/s (linear)** or **rad/s / deg/s (angular)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In *Robotic Manipulators and Cell Design*, capacity and speed are *not* treated as independent parameters. A reduction in payload results in a higher permissible speed, and vice versa. The rated specifications are always quoted as a **coupled pair** (e.g., "5 kg at 1.5 m/s").

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) super-fast express bus** climbing the steep ghat road from Kalpetta to Wayanad.

- The **Capacity** of the bus is analogous to the *number of passengers* it can carry up the ghats. If you overload it, the engine overheats, brakes fade, and the journey becomes unsafe — exactly like a robot whose joint motors will fault (over-current / over-torque trip) if the payload exceeds the rated capacity.
- The **Speed** of the bus is analogous to the *kilometers per hour* it can sustain on that gradient. An empty bus can fly up the hill at 80 km/h; a fully loaded bus must crawl at 25 km/h. Similarly, an unloaded robot joint can reach its maximum RPM, but under full payload, the controller automatically derates the velocity to maintain torque margins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Capacity and Speed are *trade-off partners*. The robot's datasheet curve is a **hyperbola** — as one goes up, the other must come down to stay within the motor's power envelope $P = \tau \cdot \omega$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems

- **Payload (P):** kg
- **Reach (R):** mm
- **TCP Speed (v):** mm/s or m/s
- **Joint Speed (ω):** rad/s
- **Repeatability:** ± mm
- **Cycle Time (t_c):** seconds per cycle
- **Throughput (Th):** parts per hour (pph)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Capacity

#### 2.1.1 Static Capacity (Rated Payload)
The rated payload $m_{rated}$ is the maximum mass the manufacturer guarantees under **quasi-static** conditions (i.e., no acceleration). It is determined by three limiting factors:

1. **Structural Limit** — Deflection at the end-effector must remain below the repeatability tolerance.
2. **Servo Limit** — Each joint motor must be able to hold the gravity-induced torque with a safety margin (typically 60–80 % of continuous stall torque).
3. **Drive Transmission Limit** — Gears, harmonic drives, or RV reducers have a published torque capacity.

The **worst-case joint torque** for a serial manipulator holding a payload at full reach $R$ is:

$$\tau_{joint,i} = m \cdot g \cdot r_i \cdot \cos(\theta_i)$$

where $r_i$ is the moment arm of joint $i$ for the given configuration. The rated capacity is the largest $m$ such that $\tau_{joint,i} \le \tau_{max,i}$ for *all* $i$.

#### 2.1.2 Dynamic Capacity (Effective Payload)
Under motion, the effective load on the joints increases due to **inertial forces**. The dynamic payload capacity $m_{dyn}$ is always less than $m_{rated}$:

$$m_{dyn} = \frac{\tau_{max} / \alpha - I_{link} \cdot \ddot{q}}{r_{eff}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the servo safety factor (typically 0.5–0.7), $I_{link}$ is the link inertia, $\ddot{q}$ is the joint acceleration, and $r_{eff}$ is the effective moment arm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** KTU problems often ask students to *derive* the dynamic reduction. A common pitfall is forgetting the inertial term $I_{link} \cdot \ddot{q}$ and treating capacity as purely static.

### 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown of Speed

#### 2.2.1 Joint Speed
Each joint has a rated maximum angular velocity $\omega_{max,i}$, dictated by the **no-load RPM of the motor** divided by the **gear ratio $N$** of the transmission:

$$\omega_{max,i} = \frac{\omega_{motor,no\text{-}load}}{N_i}$$

#### 2.2.2 TCP Speed
The Tool Center Point speed is the vector sum of contributions from all active joints:

$$\vec{v}_{TCP} = J(q) \cdot \dot{q}$$

where $J(q)$ is the **Jacobian matrix** of the manipulator evaluated at the current configuration $q$, and $\dot{q}$ is the joint velocity vector.

#### 2.2.3 Acceleration Profile
Most industrial robots use a **trapezoidal velocity profile** to limit jerk. The average speed over a motion segment of length $L$ with acceleration $a$ and maximum speed $v_{max}$ is:

- If $L \le \dfrac{v_{max}^2}{a}$ (triangular profile):
$$t_{tri} = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{a}}$$
- If $L > \dfrac{v_{max}^2}{a}$ (trapezoidal profile):
$$t_{trap} = \frac{L}{v_{max}} + \frac{v_{max}}{a}$$

### 2.3 Cycle Time and Throughput

The **cycle time** $t_c$ is the total time for one complete pick-and-place or machining operation:

$$t_c = t_{move,1} + t_{process} + t_{move,2} + t_{settle}$$

The **throughput** is the inverse, scaled to an hour:

$$Th = \frac{3600}{t_c} \quad \text{[parts/hour]}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Static joint torque | $\tau = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta$ | N·m | Worst case at $\theta = 0$ |
| 2 | Dynamic payload | $m_{dyn} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}/\alpha - I \ddot{q}}{r_{eff}}$ | kg | $\alpha \in [0.5, 0.7]$ |
| 3 | Motor joint speed | $\omega_{joint} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{N}$ | rad/s | $N$ = gear ratio |
| 4 | TCP velocity | $\vec{v}_{TCP} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | m/s | $J$ = Jacobian |
| 5 | Triangular time | $t_{tri} = 2\sqrt{L/a}$ | s | Short moves |
| 6 | Trapezoidal time | $t_{trap} = L/v_{max} + v_{max}/a$ | s | Long moves |
| 7 | Throughput | $Th = 3600/t_c$ | pph | parts/hour |
| 8 | Power limit | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | Hyperbolic trade-off |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** In automotive body shop design, capacity and speed jointly determine the *takt time* of a robotic welding cell. A robot with $m = 200$ kg payload and $v_{TCP} = 2$ m/s can sustain a takt of 60 seconds on a sedan underbody line, which is the industry benchmark for high-volume production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Dynamic Reduction of Capacity

**Problem Context:** A 6-axis robot has a joint whose maximum permissible torque is $\tau_{max} = 350$ N·m, with a safety factor $\alpha = 0.6$. The link inertia reflected to this joint is $I = 12$ kg·m². The joint must accelerate at $\ddot{q} = 2$ rad/s². The payload is held at an effective moment arm of $r_{eff} = 0.5$ m. Find the dynamic capacity of this joint.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing dynamic equilibrium at the joint.**

The torque demanded by the joint must overcome the gravity of the payload *and* accelerate its own inertia:

$$\tau_{demand} = m \cdot g \cdot r_{eff} + I \cdot \ddot{q}$$

This equation states that the total motor torque is the sum of (a) torque to support the weight ($m g r_{eff}$) and (b) torque to produce angular acceleration ($I \ddot{q}$).

**Step 2 — Apply the servo safety constraint.**

The continuous rated torque with safety factor $\alpha$ must exceed demand:

$$\alpha \cdot \tau_{max} \ge m \cdot g \cdot r_{eff} + I \cdot \ddot{q}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.6 \times 350 &\ge m \times 9.81 \times 0.5 + 12 \times 2 \\
210 &\ge 4.905 \, m + 24 \\
186 &\ge 4.905 \, m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the payload mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &\le \frac{186}{4.905} \\
m &\le 37.92 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $m_{dyn} \approx 37.9$ kg, which is the dynamic capacity of this single joint. The robot's *rated* capacity (datasheet) would be lower because all six joints must simultaneously satisfy this constraint.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycle Time of a Pick-and-Place Operation

**Problem Context:** A SCARA robot must pick a part, move it 800 mm horizontally, place it, and return. The maximum TCP speed is $v_{max} = 2$ m/s, and the controller uses a trapezoidal profile with $a = 4$ m/s². Process time at the placement station is 1.2 s. Settle time is 0.3 s. Compute the cycle time.

**Step 1 — Determine the profile type.**

Compute the transition distance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{trans} &= \frac{v_{max}^2}{a} = \frac{2^2}{4} = 1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L = 0.8$ m $< d_{trans} = 1$ m, the motion uses a **triangular velocity profile**.

**Step 2 — Compute the triangular motion time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tri} &= 2 \sqrt{\frac{L}{a}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.8}{4}} \\
t_{tri} &= 2 \sqrt{0.2} = 2 \times 0.4472 \\
t_{tri} &= 0.8944 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total cycle time for one-way motion (outbound).**

The full outbound motion takes $0.8944$ s. The return motion (also 0.8 m) takes the same time (assuming symmetric profile).

**Step 4 — Add process and settle times.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= t_{out} + t_{process} + t_{return} + t_{settle} \\
t_c &= 0.8944 + 1.2 + 0.8944 + 0.3 \\
t_c &= 3.289 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $t_c \approx 3.29$ s, and the throughput is:

$$
Th = \frac{3600}{3.289} \approx 1094 \text{ pph}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Capacity–Speed Trade-off

| Payload (% of rated) | Permissible Speed (% of $v_{max}$) | Reason |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------|--------|
| 0 % (no load) | 100 % | No torque consumption, full motor RPM |
| 50 % | ~85 % | Half torque available for acceleration |
| 100 % | ~60–70 % | Full torque for gravity, little for accel |
| 110 % (overload) | Trip / fault | Safety interlock activates |

This matrix is **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 module-end assessments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Capacity-Speed Coupling

The following Mermaid diagram depicts how the controller, motor, transmission, and mechanical structure interact to enforce the capacity–speed relationship. The architecture follows the standard industrial robot servo chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trajectory Planner] --> B[Inverse Dynamics Module]
    B --> C[Torque Demanded per Joint]
    C --> D{Servo Safety Check}
    D -->|Within Limit| E[Current / Voltage Command]
    D -->|Exceeds Limit| F[Derate Speed or Trip]
    E --> G[Servo Motor]
    G --> H[Gear Reducer Ratio N]
    H --> I[Joint Shaft]
    I --> J[Link Mechanism]
    J --> K[Payload mass m]
    K --> L[Reaction Torque and Inertia]
    L --> D
    F --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The trajectory planner requests a desired motion. The inverse dynamics module computes the required joint torques. The servo safety check compares the demand against the maximum available torque (which depends on motor thermal limits and the gear reducer rating). If demand is within limits, the command flows to the motor. The motor, through the reducer, drives the joint, which moves the link, which carries the payload. The payload, in turn, generates a reaction torque and inertia that is fed back into the safety check — closing the loop. If the safety check fails, the controller either derates the speed or triggers a fault.

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Robot Operational Mode

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateIDLE[Idle Standby]
    stateMOVING[Moving at Rated Speed]
    stateDERATE[Derated Speed Mode]
    stateTRIP[Overload Trip]
    stateIDLE --> stateMOVING : Command received
    stateMOVING --> stateDERATE : Payload > 80 percent rated
    stateDERATE --> stateMOVING : Payload reduced
    stateDERATE --> stateTRIP : Payload > 100 percent rated
    stateTRIP --> stateIDLE : Operator reset
    stateMOVING --> stateIDLE : Motion complete
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Speed Profile Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Start of Move Segment] --> Q[Compute L and v_max and a]
    Q --> R{Check L vs v_max_sq over a}
    R -->|L small| S[Triangular Profile Selected]
    R -->|L large| T[Trapezoidal Profile Selected]
    S --> U[Compute t_tri formula]
    T --> V[Compute t_trap formula]
    U --> W[Output Cycle Time]
    V --> W
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **Payload Capacity** of an industrial robot. Why is it always specified as a function of reach and wrist orientation?

**Model Answer:**
Payload capacity is the maximum mass (including the end-effector and workpiece) that a robot can manipulate repeatedly at a specified pose without exceeding the structural, thermal, or servo-torque limits of any joint. It is specified as a function of reach because the moment arm about each joint grows linearly with reach — at full reach, the static torque is maximum. Wrist orientation matters because it determines the projection of the payload gravity vector onto each joint axis. Hence, the same robot can lift 10 kg close to its base but only 6 kg at full horizontal reach.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **Rated Speed** and **TCP Speed** of a robotic manipulator.

**Model Answer:**
Rated speed is the maximum angular velocity achievable by an individual joint axis (in rad/s or deg/s), governed by the motor's no-load RPM and the gear ratio. TCP speed (Tool Center Point speed) is the resultant linear velocity of the end-effector in Cartesian space (in m/s or mm/s), obtained by mapping joint velocities through the Jacobian matrix. Rated speed is a *joint-space* property; TCP speed is a *task-space* property and depends on the current configuration of the manipulator.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain how the **rated payload** of a 6-axis articulated robot is determined from joint torque limits. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pick-and-place SCARA robot has a horizontal reach of 600 mm. The maximum joint acceleration is $3$ rad/s², and the rated speed of the primary shoulder joint is $2\pi$ rad/s. The payload is 3 kg and is held at the end-effector at 500 mm from the shoulder axis. Compute (i) the time taken for a triangular-velocity move of 0.5 m, and (ii) the throughput in parts per hour if the process time is 1.5 s and the return move is identical. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**
For a 6-axis robot, the rated payload is the maximum mass that can be held *simultaneously* at a *given configuration* such that **no joint** exceeds its continuous-rated torque.

- For each joint $i$, compute the static torque:
$$\tau_{i,static} = m \cdot g \cdot r_i(q)$$
- Apply safety factor $\alpha$:
$$\tau_{i,static} \le \alpha \cdot \tau_{i,rated}$$
- The rated payload is the minimum over all joints:
$$m_{rated} = \min_i \left( \frac{\alpha \cdot \tau_{i,rated}}{g \cdot r_i(q_{max})} \right)$$
The *worst configuration* (full reach, horizontal arm) is used. The sketch should show the arm extended with payload at the tip and arrows indicating reaction torque at each joint.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Triangular time:**

The motion is governed by the shoulder joint. The arc length covered for a 0.5 m linear displacement at 0.5 m radius is $\theta = L/r = 0.5/0.5 = 1$ rad.

First, check profile type. The maximum angular velocity needed without acceleration limit is $\omega_{pk} = \sqrt{2 a \theta} = \sqrt{2 \times 3 \times 1} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449$ rad/s.

Since $2.449 < 2\pi \approx 6.283$ rad/s (rated), the velocity is *acceleration-limited* (triangular profile).

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tri} &= 2\sqrt{\frac{\theta}{a}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \\
t_{tri} &\approx 1.155 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Throughput:**

The total cycle consists of outbound + process + return.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= 1.155 + 1.5 + 1.155 = 3.81 \text{ s} \\
Th &= \frac{3600}{3.81} \approx 945 \text{ pph}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain, with relevant equations, the **trade-off between payload capacity and speed** in an industrial robot. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 4-axis palletizing robot has a rated payload of 50 kg at full reach of 1.2 m. If the same robot is operated at 50 % of its reach (0.6 m) and 80 % of rated payload (40 kg), estimate (i) the percentage increase in permissible TCP speed, assuming the motor torque limit is unchanged, and (ii) the new cycle time if the original cycle time at full reach was 4.0 s and the trapezoidal motion segment was 60 % of the cycle. Use the relation $v_{max} \propto \tau_{available} / m$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**
The motor of each joint can deliver a maximum continuous torque $\tau_{max}$. This torque must support the weight of the payload (proportional to $m$ and moment arm $r$) **and** provide acceleration torque (proportional to $I_{eff} \cdot \ddot{q}$). Since $\tau_{max}$ is fixed, if $m$ increases, the residual torque available for acceleration decreases, and therefore the maximum velocity and acceleration must be reduced. The hyperbola $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ encapsulates this: for a fixed power envelope, increasing $\tau$ (high payload) requires reducing $\omega$ (lower speed). The manufacturer's datasheet plots this as a **capacity-speed curve**.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Percentage increase in permissible speed:**

At full reach, $r_1 = 1.2$ m, $m_1 = 50$ kg.
At half reach, $r_2 = 0.6$ m, $m_2 = 40$ kg.

The torque consumed by payload support at full reach:
$$
T_1 = 50 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 = 588.6 \text{ N·m}
$$
At half reach:
$$
T_2 = 40 \times 9.81 \times 0.6 = 235.44 \text{ N·m}
$$
Available torque for acceleration scales as $(T_{max} - T_{payload})$. Assuming $T_{max} = 1000$ N·m (illustrative):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avail,1} &= 1000 - 588.6 = 411.4 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{avail,2} &= 1000 - 235.44 = 764.56 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speed scales as $T_{avail}/m$ (proportional to acceleration budget):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_2}{v_1} &= \frac{T_{avail,2}/m_2}{T_{avail,1}/m_1} = \frac{764.56/40}{411.4/50} = \frac{19.114}{8.228} \approx 2.323
\end{aligned}
$$

So permissible TCP speed increases by approximately **132 %**.

**(ii) New cycle time:**

The original trapezoidal motion time was $0.6 \times 4.0 = 2.4$ s. If speed increases by a factor of $2.323$, the motion time decreases by the same factor:

$$
t_{motion,2} = \frac{2.4}{2.323} \approx 1.033 \text{ s}
$$

The non-motion portion (process + settle) remains $0.4 \times 4.0 = 1.6$ s. New total cycle time:

$$
t_{c,new} = 1.033 + 1.6 = 2.633 \text{ s}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Many students compute payload capacity using only the *static* formula and forget the inertial term $I \ddot{q}$. For dynamic cycles, this leads to over-estimation of capacity by 20–30 %.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 2:** In trapezoidal vs. triangular profile questions, students often apply the trapezoidal formula even when the distance is short. Always compute $L$ vs. $v_{max}^2/a$ first.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 3:** For throughput problems, students sometimes forget to include *both* the outbound and return moves, leading to a cycle time that is too low by a factor of 2.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 4:** In capacity–speed trade-off derivations, students write "speed is inversely proportional to payload" without the moment arm — losing 2 of the 7 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capacity** is governed by three limits: structural, servo (torque), and transmission. All three must be satisfied *simultaneously*.
- **Speed** at the joint is $\omega_{motor} / N$ (gear ratio). At the TCP it is $J(q) \dot{q}$.
- The **Capacity–Speed curve** is a hyperbola in the $m$–$v$ plane, derived from the power equation $P = \tau \cdot \omega$.
- **Triangular profile** for short moves: $t = 2\sqrt{L/a}$.
- **Trapezoidal profile** for long moves: $t = L/v_{max} + v_{max}/a$.
- **Throughput** $Th = 3600 / t_c$ in parts per hour.
- Dynamic capacity is always **less than** static capacity because of inertial loads.
- Worst-case payload analysis is performed at **full reach** and **horizontal configuration**.
- KTU problems always couple capacity and speed — never treat them independently.
- Always include the **return move** in cycle-time computations.
- Use **safety factors** $\alpha = 0.5$–$0.7$ in servo-torque calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroke and Reach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stroke and Reach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stroke** is the distance (or range of motion) that a specific joint or linear axis of a robot can travel between its two extreme limits (minimum and maximum positions). It is the **active, controllable movement** along a single degree of freedom (DOF).
> **Reach** is the **maximum radial distance** from the base center of the robot to the **farthest point of the end-effector (or tool center point, TCP)** when the arm is fully extended along any trajectory in its workspace.

In simpler board-exam language:
- **Stroke = Length of the arrow of motion** (the "play" available in a joint).
- **Reach = The biggest circle your hand can draw from the shoulder** (the radius of the workspace envelope).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the center of a room with your right arm fitted with a measuring tape:
1. **Stroke** of your shoulder is how far you can move your arm *forward–backward*, *up–down*, or *left–right* — each of those individual movements is a stroke.
2. **Reach** is what happens when you **stretch your hand to its absolute maximum** in any direction — the distance from your shoulder (the robot base) to your fingertips (the end-effector).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Board Exams**
> - **Stroke** is a *per-joint* parameter (measured in mm or degrees).
> - **Reach** is a *global* parameter of the manipulator (measured in mm).
> - Stroke values **add up** to contribute to the total Reach.

### Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Reach (R):** expressed in **millimeters (mm)**, typically in the range **500 mm – 3500 mm** for industrial robots.
- **Stroke (S):** expressed in **mm (linear)** or **degrees (rotational)**.
- **Working Envelope / Workspace:** the 3-D volume swept when all joints traverse their strokes simultaneously.
- **Payload (W):** the maximum load the robot can carry at **rated reach** — usually **decreases** as reach increases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Reach in Articulated Robots

For a **multi-link articulated (RRR / 6-DOF) manipulator**, the reach is bounded by the geometry of the links. The following structural reasoning applies:

- **Minimum Reach ($R_{min}$):** Distance from the base to the end-effector when the arm is **fully retracted** (folded back on itself).
- **Maximum Reach ($R_{max}$):** Distance from the base to the end-effector when the arm is **fully extended** in a straight line.
- **Rated Reach ($R_{rated}$):** The reach at which the manufacturer guarantees the full **payload**, **repeatability**, and **speed**.

$$
R_{min} \;\leq\; R_{rated} \;\leq\; R_{max}
$$

### 2.2 Stroke of Linear and Rotary Joints

| Joint Type | Symbol | Stroke Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prismatic (Linear) | $S_L$ | mm | Slide length of the axis |
| Revolute (Rotary) | $S_R$ | degrees | Angular sweep about the joint axis |
| Cylindrical | Mixed | mm + deg | Combined linear & rotary |
| Spherical | 3 Rotary | deg | Pitch, yaw, roll |

### 2.3 Geometric Reach Formula (2-Link Planar Arm)

For a planar RR arm with link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$ (the most common KTU derivation):

**Step (a) — Fully Extended Configuration:**
When both links are aligned in the same direction ($\theta_2 = 0^\circ$), the end-effector lies along the X-axis at a distance equal to the **sum of link lengths**.

$$
R_{max} \;=\; a_1 \;+\; a_2
$$

**Step (b) — Fully Retracted Configuration:**
When the second link folds back on the first ($\theta_2 = 180^\circ$), the end-effector is at the **difference of link lengths**.

$$
R_{min} \;=\; \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert
$$

**Step (c) — General End-Effector Position:**
Using the **forward kinematic position equation** of a 2-link planar arm with joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$:

$$
x \;=\; a_1 \cos\theta_1 \;+\; a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y \;=\; a_1 \sin\theta_1 \;+\; a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

The instantaneous reach (radial distance from the base to TCP) is therefore:

$$
R(\theta_1, \theta_2) \;=\; \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
R(\theta_1, \theta_2) \;=\; \sqrt{a_1^{2} + a_2^{2} + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The reach is **independent of $\theta_1$** (base rotation) and depends **only on $\theta_2$** (elbow angle). This is a very common KTU viva question!

### 2.4 The Workspace (Working Envelope)

The full workspace is the **annular region** swept by the reach as $\theta_2$ varies across its stroke.

$$
R_{min} \;\leq\; R(\theta_2) \;\leq\; R_{max}, \quad \forall \theta_1 \in [0, 2\pi], \; \theta_2 \in [\theta_{2,min}, \theta_{2,max}]
$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max Reach (2-link) | $R_{max} = a_1 + a_2$ | mm | Links collinear |
| 2 | Min Reach (2-link) | $R_{min} = \mid a_1 - a_2 \mid$ | mm | Links folded |
| 3 | Instantaneous Reach | $R = \sqrt{a_1^{2} + a_2^{2} + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2}$ | mm | Depends only on $\theta_2$ |
| 4 | Workspace Area (2-link) | $A = \pi (R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2})$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Annular region |
| 5 | Workspace Volume (3-link spherical) | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi (R_{max}^{3} - R_{min}^{3})$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Spherical shell |
| 6 | Stroke of Joint $i$ | $S_i = q_{i,max} - q_{i,min}$ | mm or deg | Per joint |
| 7 | Total Linear Reach (n prismatic) | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$ | mm | When fully extended |
| 8 | Rated Reach Payload Trade-off | $W_{actual} = W_{rated} \cdot \frac{R_{rated}}{R_{actual}}$ (approx.) | kg | Inverse relation |

> **Engineering Utility:** Reach determines **cell layout, fixture positioning, and conveyor spacing** in a robotic cell. Stroke determines whether a robot can perform tasks like **inserting, palletizing, or deep-cavity machining** without singularities. Together, they govern **cycle-time feasibility** in any automated production line.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reach Equation of a 2-Link Planar RR Manipulator

**Given:**
- Link 1 length = $a_1$, oriented at angle $\theta_1$ from the X-axis.
- Link 2 length = $a_2$, oriented at angle $\theta_2$ **relative to Link 1**.
- Joint angles measured counter-clockwise (right-hand rule).

**To Derive:** $R = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ in closed form.

#### Step 1 — Position of the Elbow Joint (Point A)

The elbow is at the end of Link 1. Its coordinates are simply the polar-to-Cartesian conversion:

$$
A_x \;=\; a_1 \cos\theta_1
$$

$$
A_y \;=\; a_1 \sin\theta_1
$$

*Explanation:* Since Link 1 starts at the origin and points in the direction $\theta_1$ for a length $a_1$, the elbow lies at these coordinates.

#### Step 2 — Position of the End-Effector (Point B = TCP)

The end-effector is offset from the elbow by Link 2, which is at absolute angle $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ from the X-axis:

$$
x \;=\; A_x \;+\; a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \;=\; a_1 \cos\theta_1 \;+\; a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y \;=\; A_y \;+\; a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \;=\; a_1 \sin\theta_1 \;+\; a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

*Explanation:* We translate the elbow coordinates by the vector components of Link 2.

#### Step 3 — Compute Reach as Radial Distance

$$
R^2 \;=\; x^2 + y^2
$$

Expanding $x^2$:

$$
x^2 \;=\; (a_1 \cos\theta_1)^2 + (a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

Expanding $y^2$:

$$
y^2 \;=\; (a_1 \sin\theta_1)^2 + (a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Trigonometric Identity

Add $x^2 + y^2$. The squared terms combine to give $a_1^{2} + a_2^{2}$. The cross terms combine to give $2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2$ using the identity:

$$
\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \;=\; \cos\theta_2
$$

So:

$$
R^2 \;=\; a_1^{2} + a_2^{2} + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

#### Step 5 — Final Reach Expression

$$
R \;=\; \sqrt{a_1^{2} + a_2^{2} + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2}
$$

*Physical Insight:* Because $\cos\theta_2$ is the only term containing joint angle, $R$ is **constant for any base rotation** $\theta_1$ and is purely a function of **elbow folding**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 2-link planar arm has $a_1 = 500\,\text{mm}$, $a_2 = 400\,\text{mm}$. The elbow joint has a stroke of $0^\circ \leq \theta_2 \leq 150^\circ$. Compute (i) $R_{max}$, (ii) $R_{min}$, (iii) $R$ at $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$, and (iv) the workspace area.

**Solution (i) — $R_{max}$:**
At $\theta_2 = 0^\circ$ (links fully extended):

$$
R_{max} = 500 + 400 = 900\,\text{mm}
$$

**Solution (ii) — $R_{min}$:**
At $\theta_2 = 150^\circ$ (most folded position within stroke):

$$
R_{min} = \sqrt{500^{2} + 400^{2} + 2(500)(400)\cos 150^\circ}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \sqrt{250000 + 160000 + 400000(-0.8660)}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \sqrt{410000 - 346410} = \sqrt{63590}
$$

$$
R_{min} \approx 252.17\,\text{mm}
$$

**Solution (iii) — $R$ at $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$:**

$$
R = \sqrt{500^{2} + 400^{2} + 2(500)(400)\cos 90^\circ} = \sqrt{250000 + 160000 + 0}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{410000} \approx 640.31\,\text{mm}
$$

**Solution (iv) — Workspace Area:**

$$
A = \pi (R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}) = \pi (900^{2} - 252.17^{2})
$$

$$
A = \pi (810000 - 63590) = \pi (746410) \approx 2.345 \times 10^{6}\,\text{mm}^{2}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Configuration | $\theta_2$ | $\cos\theta_2$ | Reach Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fully Extended | $0^\circ$ | $+1$ | $R_{max} = a_1 + a_2$ |
| Perpendicular (L-shape) | $90^\circ$ | $0$ | $R = \sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}}$ |
| Slightly Folded | $60^\circ$ | $+0.5$ | $R = \sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+a_1 a_2}$ |
| Folded (Right-Angle Reverse) | $150^\circ$ | $-0.866$ | $R$ approaches $R_{min}$ |
| Fully Retracted (Ideal) | $180^\circ$ | $-1$ | $R_{min} = \mid a_1 - a_2 \mid$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Stroke, Reach & Workspace Interact

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot Base Origin] --> B[Joint 1: Base Rotation theta1]
    B --> C[Link L1]
    C --> D[Joint 2: Shoulder theta2]
    D --> E[Link L2]
    E --> F[Joint 3: Elbow theta3]
    F --> G[Link L3]
    G --> H[End Effector / TCP]

    I[Stroke S_i per joint] --> J[Range Limits q_min, q_max]
    J --> K[Forward Kinematics Solver]
    K --> L[Cartesian Coordinates x, y, z]
    L --> M[Reach R = sqrt x2 + y2 + z2]
    M --> N[Working Envelope / Workspace]

    O[Manufacturer Specs] --> P[Rated Reach]
    O --> Q[Rated Payload]
    P --> N
    Q --> N
    N --> R[Cell Layout Design]
    N --> S[Cycle Time Planning]
    N --> T[Singularity Avoidance]
```

### 4.2 Stroke vs Reach — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Joint Stroke S1 mm or deg] --> R[Total Reach R mm]
    J2[Joint Stroke S2 mm or deg] --> R
    J3[Joint Stroke S3 mm or deg] --> R
    J4[Joint Stroke S4 mm or deg] --> R
    R --> W[Workspace Envelope]
    W --> APP1[Material Handling]
    W --> APP2[Welding]
    W --> APP3[Palletizing]
    W --> APP4[Assembly]
```

### 4.3 Reach Envelope for a 2-Link Planar Arm (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph W[Annular Workspace]
        direction TB
        OUT[R_max = a1 + a2, Outer Boundary]
        IN[R_min = a1 - a2, Inner Boundary]
        BASE[Base Origin, Origin 0 0]
        P1[Configuration 1, theta2 = 0 deg, R_max]
        P2[Configuration 2, theta2 = 90 deg, R = sqrt a1 sq + a2 sq]
        P3[Configuration 3, theta2 = 180 deg, R_min]
        OUT --> P1
        P2 --> OUT
        P3 --> IN
    end
    BASE --> P1
    BASE --> P2
    BASE --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The annulus (ring) between $R_{min}$ and $R_{max}$ is the **complete planar workspace** of a 2-link arm. The base can rotate $\theta_1$ freely, sweeping this annulus into a 3-D disk-like volume in real 3-D space.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**  
*Differentiate between **Stroke** and **Reach** of a robotic manipulator. Give one industrial example where each parameter is the critical design constraint.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stroke | Reach |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Range of motion of an individual joint | Maximum distance from base to farthest point of end-effector |
| Measured in | mm or degrees | mm |
| Scope | Per joint (local) | Whole manipulator (global) |
| Critical for | Tasks requiring deep linear insertion, e.g., **PCB component insertion** | Tasks requiring wide coverage, e.g., **palletizing or welding large car bodies** |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
*State and explain the relationship between **reach** and **payload** in industrial robots. Why is reach alone insufficient for robot selection?*

**Model Answer:**
The **payload capacity of a robot decreases as reach increases** because the cantilevered load creates a larger moment about the base joints. The relationship is approximately:

$$
W_{actual} \approx W_{rated} \cdot \frac{R_{rated}}{R_{actual}}
$$

Reach alone is insufficient because **payload, repeatability, speed, and inertia** ratings are all **reach-dependent**. A robot rated for 10 kg at 500 mm reach may only handle 5 kg at its maximum reach. Hence, a complete selection requires the **payload–reach–speed–repeatability** quartet.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 (Model)]**  
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **reach** of a 2-link planar RR manipulator in terms of its link lengths $a_1$, $a_2$ and the elbow joint angle $\theta_2$. Show clearly that the reach is independent of the base joint angle $\theta_1$. **(7 Marks)**  
> **(b)** A 6-axis articulated robot has a **rated reach of 1500 mm** and a **rated payload of 12 kg** at that reach. Compute the expected payload when the robot is extended to **(i) 1200 mm** and **(ii) 1700 mm**. Comment on the practical implications for cell design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**  
Already fully derived in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps the examiner expects:

1. Forward kinematics giving $x = a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ and $y = a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$. **(2 marks)**
2. Computing $R^2 = x^2 + y^2$. **(2 marks)**
3. Using the cosine subtraction identity to obtain $R^2 = a_1^{2} + a_2^{2} + 2a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2$. **(2 marks)**
4. Stating explicitly that $\theta_1$ cancels out — reach depends only on $\theta_2$. **(1 mark)**

**(b) — Payload Computation (7 marks):**  
Using $W_{actual} \approx W_{rated} \cdot (R_{rated}/R_{actual})$:

**Part (i) at $R_{actual} = 1200$ mm:**

$$
W_{1200} = 12 \cdot \frac{1500}{1200} = 12 \cdot 1.25 = 15.0\,\text{kg}
$$

*Implication:* The robot can carry **more than rated payload** at shorter reach, useful for **close-fixture assembly** tasks.

**Part (ii) at $R_{actual} = 1700$ mm:**

$$
W_{1700} = 12 \cdot \frac{1500}{1700} = 12 \cdot 0.8824 \approx 10.59\,\text{kg}
$$

*Implication:* At maximum extension, payload **drops by ~12 %**. Designers must ensure that **end-of-arm tooling (EOAT) + part weight ≤ 10.59 kg** when the robot is fully extended — a common source of overload failures and joint-motor burnout.

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 (Model)]**  
> **(a)** Define **working envelope** (workspace) of a robot. With neat sketches, describe the workspace of **(i)** Cartesian, **(ii)** Cylindrical, **(iii)** Spherical, and **(iv)** Articulated robot configurations. **(7 Marks)**  
> **(b)** A 2-link planar arm has $a_1 = 600$ mm and $a_2 = 500$ mm. If the elbow stroke is restricted to $30^\circ \leq \theta_2 \leq 135^\circ$, determine (i) the maximum and minimum reach, (ii) the area of the working envelope. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Workspace Types (7 marks):**

1. **Cartesian (PPP):** Rectangular cuboid — $L_x \times L_y \times L_z$. Each stroke adds linearly to one dimension. Used in **pick-and-place, CNC loading**.
2. **Cylindrical (RPP):** A cylinder of radius $(R_{max} - R_{min})$ and height equal to vertical stroke. **Stroke** of the prismatic joint = vertical reach.
3. **Spherical (RRP):** A section of a sphere, bounded by inner radius $R_{min}$ and outer radius $R_{max}$, and vertical limits. **Reach** is radial.
4. **Articulated (RRR):** Highly irregular, complex 3-D envelope. **Reach** dominates the outer boundary; **stroke** of each joint carves out the inner dead zones.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i):** $R_{max}$ occurs at $\theta_2 = 30^\circ$ (closest to full extension):

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt{600^{2} + 500^{2} + 2(600)(500)\cos 30^\circ}
$$

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt{360000 + 250000 + 600000(0.8660)} = \sqrt{1129600} \approx 1062.83\,\text{mm}
$$

$R_{min}$ occurs at $\theta_2 = 135^\circ$ (most folded):

$$
R_{min} = \sqrt{360000 + 250000 + 600000\cos 135^\circ}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \sqrt{610000 + 600000(-0.7071)} = \sqrt{610000 - 424264} = \sqrt{185736} \approx 430.97\,\text{mm}
$$

**Part (ii) — Workspace Area:**

$$
A = \pi (R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}) = \pi (1062.83^{2} - 430.97^{2})
$$

$$
A = \pi (1129606 - 185735) = \pi (943871) \approx 2.965 \times 10^{6}\,\text{mm}^{2}
$$

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Stroke & Reach Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing Stroke with Reach** in definitions — *Stroke = per joint; Reach = global*. Examiners deduct 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine subtraction identity** when simplifying $x^2 + y^2$. Always show the identity explicitly.
> 3. **Using $R_{min} = a_1 - a_2$ blindly** without checking the elbow stroke limits. If $\theta_{2,min} \neq 180^\circ$, you **must** plug it into the reach formula — examiners *love* this trap.
> 4. **Sign error in $\cos\theta_2$** for folded configurations ($\theta_2 > 90^\circ$ gives negative cosine — students often miss this).
> 5. **Forgetting the payload–reach inverse relationship** in design questions. Always mention it for full marks.
> 6. **Skipping units (mm, deg, mm²)** — KTU examiners deduct half a mark per missing unit.

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stroke** = range of motion of a **single joint**; **Reach** = maximum **base-to-TCP** distance.
- ✅ Reach is computed from the **forward kinematics** of the arm.
- ✅ For a 2-link planar arm: $R = \sqrt{a_1^{2} + a_2^{2} + 2a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2}$ — **independent of $\theta_1$**.
- ✅ $R_{max} = a_1 + a_2$ (links collinear, $\theta_2 = 0^\circ$).
- ✅ $R_{min} = \mid a_1 - a_2 \mid$ (links folded, $\theta_2 = 180^\circ$).
- ✅ **Workspace area (planar)** = $\pi (R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2})$.
- ✅ **Workspace volume (spherical)** = $\frac{4}{3}\pi (R_{max}^{3} - R_{min}^{3})$.
- ✅ Payload is **inversely related** to reach — heavier loads only possible at **closer** working distances.
- ✅ Workspace **shape** depends on robot configuration: cuboid (Cartesian), cylinder (cylindrical), spherical shell (spherical/articulated), irregular 3-D blob (articulated 6-axis).
- ✅ **Rated reach** is the manufacturer-guaranteed operating distance; **maximum reach** is the geometric limit at reduced payload.
- ✅ Always quote **units** (mm for linear, deg for rotary) — KTU is strict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeatability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repeatability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeatability** is formally defined as the **closeness of agreement** between the **positioning and pose states** achieved by a robot's end-effector when repeatedly commanded to approach the same target location from the **same direction**, under identical environmental and load conditions.

In the context of the **ISO 9283** standard (which KTU 2024 follows), *Position Repeatability (RP)* is quantified as the radius of a sphere (or circle in 2D) that contains a specified percentage (typically **95%** or **99%** based on the 3-sigma rule) of all measured end-effector positions when the robot is commanded to the same pose repeatedly.

**Repeatability is a measure of consistency, not correctness.** A robot can be highly repeatable but completely inaccurate (e.g., it returns to the "wrong" spot every time, but the "wrong" spot is exactly the same).

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Drunk Archer"

Imagine a skilled but inebriated archer shooting at a target:

| Archer Type | What Happens | Robotic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Accurate but not repeatable** | Aims perfectly, but the arrow lands scattered around the bullseye | Robot reaches commanded pose incorrectly each time |
| **Repeatable but not accurate** | All arrows land in a tight cluster, but in the wrong part of the target | Robot returns to the *same wrong pose* every cycle |
| **Accurate AND repeatable** | A tight cluster centered on the bullseye | Industrial-grade precision robot |
| **Neither** | Arrows scattered randomly across the field | Faulty/uncalibrated system |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In manufacturing, **repeatability is often more valuable than accuracy**. If a part needs to be welded at 100 mm from a reference, we can simply *program* the robot to go to 101.5 mm if we know its systematic bias — but if the robot is inconsistent, every part will be slightly different, leading to rejects.

## 1.3 Why Repeatability Matters in Robotic Cells

In a **KTU 2024 aligned robotic cell design**, repeatability directly determines:

1. **Defect Rate (DPMO):** Variation in pick-and-place positions causes misalignment in assembly.
2. **Tool Wear Compensation:** Consistent path execution ensures uniform machining.
3. **Quality Control Tolerance:** Sub-millimeter repeatability (±0.02 mm) is required for electronics assembly.
4. **Economic Justification:** Robots justify capital cost only if they outperform human variance.

## 1.4 Standardized Numerical Benchmark (Industry Reference)

| Robot Class | Typical Repeatability (mm) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **SCARA / Delta** | ±0.01 to ±0.05 | Electronics, pharmaceuticals |
| **Articulated 6-axis** | ±0.02 to ±0.10 | Welding, painting |
| **Heavy-payload industrial** | ±0.05 to ±0.50 | Palletizing, foundry |
| **Collaborative (Cobot)** | ±0.05 to ±0.10 | Assembly, polishing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Nature of Repeatability

Robot repeatability is **not a single deterministic value** — it emerges from a **distribution** of positions around a central tendency. This is because each joint contributes:

1. **Encoder quantization errors** (digital resolution)
2. **Backlash** in gear trains
3. **Mechanical hysteresis** in joints
4. **Thermal expansion** of links
5. **Compliance** under varying payloads

The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** states that the combined effect of many small independent error sources produces a position error distribution that approximates a **multivariate normal (Gaussian) distribution**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Bivariate Gaussian Model

For a planar (2D) measurement, the end-effector position $(x_i, y_i)$ at the $i^{th}$ cycle is modeled as:

$$p(x, y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma_x \sigma_y \sqrt{1-\rho^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2(1-\rho^2)}\left[\frac{(x-\mu_x)^2}{\sigma_x^2} - \frac{2\rho(x-\mu_x)(y-\mu_y)}{\sigma_x\sigma_y} + \frac{(y-\mu_y)^2}{\sigma_y^2}\right]\right)$$

where:
- $\mu_x, \mu_y$ = mean position (the "center" of the cluster)
- $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ = standard deviations along each axis
- $\rho$ = correlation coefficient between $x$ and $y$ errors

For most industrial robots, errors are **approximately isotropic and uncorrelated**, simplifying to $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma$ and $\rho = 0$.

## 2.3 The 3-Sigma Rule (Why ±3σ?)

In a Gaussian distribution:
- **68.27%** of points fall within $\pm 1\sigma$
- **95.45%** of points fall within $\pm 2\sigma$
- **99.73%** of points fall within $\pm 3\sigma$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Robot repeatability is officially quoted as the radius containing **99.73%** of measured points — this corresponds to **$3\sigma_R$** from the centroid, where $\sigma_R$ is the **radial standard deviation** in 3D space.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $d_i$ | $\sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$ | Radial distance of $i^{th}$ point from centroid (mm) |
| $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$ | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$ | Centroid of repeated positions (mm) |
| $\sigma_R$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2}$ | Radial standard deviation (mm) |
| $\bar{d}$ | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i$ | Mean radial distance (mm) |
| **ISO 9283 Repeatability** $RP$ | $\bar{d} + 3\sigma_R$ | Position repeatability (mm) |
| **3-Sigma Repeatability** $RP_{3\sigma}$ | $3\sigma_R$ | 99.73% confidence bound (mm) |
| **2-Sigma Repeatability** $RP_{2\sigma}$ | $2\sigma_R$ | 95.45% confidence bound (mm) |
| **Resolution** $R$ | $\min(\Delta q_i \cdot \vert J^{-1} \vert)$ | Smallest commanded displacement (mm) |
| **Number of cycles** $N$ | Typically 30 (ISO) or 50 | Statistically significant sample |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ISO 9283** standard uses $RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R$ (not just $3\sigma_R$) because $\bar{d} \neq 0$ in practice — the cluster's centroid rarely coincides with the sample mean of *distances*. Many textbook simplifications use only $3\sigma_R$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Tolerance Stack-up Analysis:** Repeatability defines the smallest repeatable feature size the robot can produce.
2. **Cycle Time Optimization:** Tight repeatability allows faster accelerations without overshoot penalties.
3. **Predictive Maintenance:** Gradual increase in $\sigma_R$ over time signals joint bearing wear.
4. **Fixture Design:** Knowing $RP$ lets engineers design compliant grippers and part fixtures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Radial Standard Deviation $\sigma_R$

### Step 1: Define the measured position vector
Let the $i^{th}$ measured end-effector position in 3D Cartesian space be:
$$\mathbf{p}_i = \begin{bmatrix} x_i \\ y_i \\ z_i \end{bmatrix}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

### Step 2: Compute the centroid (sample mean)
The centroid $\bar{\mathbf{p}}$ is the average of all measured points:
$$\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{p}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{x} \\ \bar{y} \\ \bar{z} \end{bmatrix}$$

Expanded in scalar form:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$$

**Logic:** The centroid is the single point that minimizes the sum of squared distances to all measured points (this is the *least-squares* optimum).

### Step 3: Compute the radial distance $d_i$
For each measurement, find the Euclidean distance from the centroid:
$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

**Logic:** This converts 3D scatter into a 1D scalar — the "radial error magnitude" — which is what we use to define a *sphere* of repeatability.

### Step 4: Compute the mean radial distance $\bar{d}$
$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i$$

**Logic:** For a Gaussian scatter, the mean radial distance is **not zero**. For an isotropic 2D Gaussian, $\bar{d} = \sigma\sqrt{\pi/2}$. For 3D, $\bar{d} = 2\sigma/\sqrt{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} = 2\sigma\sqrt{2/\pi} \approx 1.596\sigma$.

### Step 5: Compute the radial variance and standard deviation
$$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2, \quad \sigma_R = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2}$$

**Logic:** Squaring the distances and averaging gives the *second moment* of the radial distribution. Taking the square root yields $\sigma_R$ — a measure of the spread of points around the centroid.

### Step 6: Combine to get ISO 9283 repeatability
$$RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R$$

**Logic:** The mean radial distance $\bar{d}$ captures the *bias* of the radial distribution, while $3\sigma_R$ captures the *spread* at the 99.73% confidence level. Adding them gives a conservative upper bound on the repeatability sphere radius.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A KTU-style 6-axis robot is commanded 30 times to reach the same pose. The measured Cartesian positions yield the following summary statistics (in mm):
$$\bar{x} = 250.012, \quad \bar{y} = 100.005, \quad \bar{z} = 50.001$$
$$\sigma_x = 0.015, \quad \sigma_y = 0.012, \quad \sigma_z = 0.018$$

Assuming the errors are isotropic and uncorrelated, determine:
**(a)** The 3-sigma repeatability bound $RP_{3\sigma}$.
**(b)** The ISO 9283 repeatability $RP$.
**(c)** The probability that the next measurement falls within $RP$.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Isotropic radial standard deviation**

For an isotropic Gaussian, the radial standard deviation is:
$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}{?}}$$

Wait — careful! For an *isotropic* scatter, all axis variances are equal in expectation, so:
$$\sigma_R^2 = \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2$$

Wait — this is true only if we treat $d_i^2 = (x_i-\bar{x})^2 + (y_i-\bar{y})^2 + (z_i-\bar{z})^2$ and compute the expectation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[d_i^2] &= \mathbb{E}[(x_i-\bar{x})^2] + \mathbb{E}[(y_i-\bar{y})^2] + \mathbb{E}[(z_i-\bar{z})^2] \\
&= \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2
\end{aligned}$$

(The cross-terms vanish because errors are uncorrelated.)

Substituting numerical values:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_R^2 &= (0.015)^2 + (0.012)^2 + (0.018)^2 \\
&= 0.000225 + 0.000144 + 0.000324 \\
&= 0.000693 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{0.000693} = 0.02633 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:
$$RP_{3\sigma} = 3 \times 0.02633 = 0.0790 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.079 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b): ISO 9283 repeatability**

For a 3D isotropic Gaussian, the mean radial distance is:
$$\bar{d} = 2\sigma_R \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} = \sigma_R \cdot 2 \cdot 0.7979 \approx 1.5958 \, \sigma_R$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{d} &= 1.5958 \times 0.02633 \\
&= 0.04201 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Now applying the ISO formula:
$$RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R = 0.04201 + 0.0790 = 0.1210 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c): Probability within $RP$**

By construction, $RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R$ corresponds to the **99.73%** confidence sphere centered on the centroid. Therefore:
$$P(\text{next point inside } RP) \approx 99.73\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Notice that $RP_{3\sigma} = 0.079$ mm is the *radius* of the 99.73% sphere, while the ISO formula $RP = 0.121$ mm is the *outer bound* of the cluster. Always clarify which convention the question is using.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Repeatability vs. Accuracy

| Parameter | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ability to return to the *same* position | Ability to reach the *commanded* position |
| **Depends on** | Joint precision, mechanical errors, resolution | Calibration, kinematic model, payload compensation |
| **Statistical measure** | $\sigma_R$ (spread) | $\vert \bar{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}_{cmd} \vert$ (bias) |
| **Typical industrial value** | ±0.02 mm | ±0.5 mm (often much worse) |
| **Improvement method** | Better bearings, encoders, temperature control | Better calibration, error compensation algorithms |
| **Position cluster visualization** | Tight cluster anywhere | Cluster centered on target |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Repeatability vs. Accuracy — Cluster Visualization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Acc1["Case A: High Repeatability, High Accuracy"]
        T1["Target Pose (Commanded)"]
        C1["Tight Cluster centered on Target"]
        T1 --- C1
    end

    subgraph Acc2["Case B: High Repeatability, Low Accuracy"]
        T2["Target Pose (Commanded)"]
        C2["Tight Cluster, Offset from Target"]
        T2 -.- C2
    end

    subgraph Acc3["Case C: Low Repeatability, High Accuracy"]
        T3["Target Pose (Commanded)"]
        C3["Scattered Points around Target"]
        T3 --- C3
    end

    subgraph Acc4["Case D: Low Repeatability, Low Accuracy"]
        T4["Target Pose (Commanded)"]
        C4["Scattered Cluster, Far from Target"]
        T4 -.- C4
    end
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ISO 9283 Repeatability Measurement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Commanded Pose P_cmd (Robot Controller)"] --> B["Robot Joint Actuators"]
    B --> C["Mechanical Linkage + Gear Train"]
    C --> D["End-Effector Actual Pose P_i"]
    D --> E["Metrology System (Laser Tracker / CMM)"]
    E --> F["Data Acquisition (N=30 cycles)"]
    F --> G["Statistical Processor (Mean, Variance)"]
    G --> H["Centroid p_bar Calculation"]
    G --> I["Radial Distances d_i"]
    I --> J["Radial Standard Deviation sigma_R"]
    H --> K["Mean Radial Distance d_bar"]
    J --> L["ISO 9283 Repeatability RP = d_bar + 3 sigma_R"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Repeatability Certificate (mm)"]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Statistical Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1: Define commanded pose P_cmd"] --> S2["Step 2: Execute N cycles (N >= 30)"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Record N position samples"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute centroid p_bar"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute radial distances d_i"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Compute d_bar and sigma_R"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Apply RP = d_bar + 3 sigma_R"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Report Repeatability"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term 'Repeatability' of a robotic manipulator as per ISO 9283. Distinguish it from 'Accuracy'.**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Repeatability** is defined by ISO 9283 as the closeness of agreement between the positions/orientations reached by the end-effector after a number of repeated attempts to command the same pose, under the same conditions (load, speed, direction of approach).
>
> Mathematically, it is quantified as:
> $$RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R$$
> where $\bar{d}$ is the mean radial distance and $\sigma_R$ is the radial standard deviation of $N$ measured positions.
>
> **Distinction from Accuracy:**
> - **Accuracy** = ability to reach the *commanded* position (closeness to the true target).
> - **Repeatability** = ability to return to the *same* position consistently (closeness of repeated readings to each other).
>
> A robot may be highly repeatable but inaccurate (consistent at the wrong spot), or vice versa.

*(Distribution: 1 mark – definition, 1 mark – distinction, 1 mark – mathematical form)*

---

### **Q2. State the formula for position repeatability and explain why the coefficient '3' appears in the expression.**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The position repeatability per ISO 9283 is:
$$RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R$$

**Why the factor 3?** The '3' arises from the **3-sigma (3σ) rule** of the Gaussian (normal) distribution. Robot end-effector position errors, being the cumulative result of many small independent error sources (backlash, encoder resolution, thermal drift, compliance), follow a normal distribution by the **Central Limit Theorem**. In a normal distribution, the interval $[\mu - 3\sigma, \mu + 3\sigma]$ contains **99.73%** of all observations. Therefore, $3\sigma_R$ provides a **99.73% confidence bound** on the radial error — the industry-standard way to quote repeatability.

*(Distribution: 1 mark – formula, 2 marks – 3-sigma justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module-level internal choice is mandatory. *Attempt any ONE full question.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the **Position Repeatability (RP)** of a robotic manipulator as specified in the ISO 9283 standard. Clearly define each variable in the final expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-axis articulated robot is tested for position repeatability at a specified pose. The controller commands the end-effector to position $(300.00, 200.00, 150.00)$ mm. After $N = 30$ repeated trials, the following data are recorded (units in mm):
$$\sum x_i = 9000.40, \quad \sum y_i = 6000.18, \quad \sum z_i = 4500.22$$
$$\sum x_i^2 = 2{,}700{,}024.12, \quad \sum y_i^2 = 1{,}200{,}010.80, \quad \sum z_i^2 = 675{,}005.27$$

Calculate:
(i) The centroid of the measured positions.
(ii) The position repeatability $RP$ using the ISO 9283 formula.
(iii) The 3-sigma repeatability bound $RP_{3\sigma}$.
**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Data Acquisition**
Collect $N$ Cartesian position samples $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)^T$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ by repeatedly commanding the same pose.

**Step 2: Centroid Computation**
The cluster centroid is:
$$\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i\right)$$

**Step 3: Radial Distance**
For each sample, the radial distance from the centroid is:
$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

**Step 4: Mean Radial Distance and Standard Deviation**
$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i, \quad \sigma_R = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2}$$

**Step 5: ISO 9283 Formula**
$$\boxed{RP = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_R}$$

**Variable Definitions:**
- $N$ = number of test cycles (typically 30)
- $\bar{\mathbf{p}}$ = mean position of all samples
- $d_i$ = radial distance of $i^{th}$ sample from centroid
- $\bar{d}$ = mean of all radial distances
- $\sigma_R$ = radial standard deviation

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Centroid Calculation (2 marks):**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{N} = \frac{9000.40}{30} = 300.0133 \text{ mm}$$
$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i}{N} = \frac{6000.18}{30} = 200.0060 \text{ mm}$$
$$\bar{z} = \frac{\sum z_i}{N} = \frac{4500.22}{30} = 150.0073 \text{ mm}$$

So the centroid is $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = (300.0133, 200.0060, 150.0073)$ mm.

**(ii) Variance Computation (3 marks):**
Using the identity:
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i^2 - \bar{x}^2$$

For $x$:
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{2{,}700{,}024.12}{30} - (300.0133)^2 = 90{,}000.804 - 90{,}008.000 = -7.196 \text{ mm}^2$$
