- **Numerical Safety Margins:** In reach calculations, always include a **10-15% safety margin** below $R_{reach}$ to account for end-effector size and part fixturing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: In-Line Robot Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** In-Line Robot Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-Line Robot Cell (ILC)** — A *single-robot, multi-machine manufacturing cell* in which two or more workstations (typically CNC machine tools, presses, or inspection stations) are arranged along a **single straight axis**, with the robot mounted on a linear traverse track that moves parallel to the line of machines. Workpieces are transferred **sequentially** from one machine to the next through the central robot, which services every workstation in a *predefined cyclic order*.

The In-Line Robot Cell is one of the three canonical **single-robot cell layouts** taught in PCRUT402, alongside the **Parallel (Side-by-Side) Cell** and the **Circular (Rotary) Cell**. The defining geometric attribute is that all machine tool spindles — and hence the workpiece loading axes — lie on a single common straight line. The robot, mounted on a linear slide, indexes between machines to perform *pick-and-place* operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology** — Students must remember: *In-line ≠ In-line transfer machine.* An In-Line Robot Cell uses a **reprogrammable articulated or gantry robot**; an in-line transfer machine uses fixed mechanical fingers/cams. The robotic version is far more flexible.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **train platform with a single attendant** who walks along the platform to help passengers board and alight at each coach. The platform is the linear track, the attendant is the robot, and each coach door is a machine tool. The attendant walks down the platform, performs a service (load/unload) at each coach, and then returns to start the next round.

A more industrial analogy: picture an **automated car wash tunnel** where a single gantry robot travels along the length of the vehicle, applying soap → water → wax → drying in sequence. Each "station" of the wash tunnel is a *machine tool equivalent* in the cell, and the moving gantry is the *robot-on-track*.

> [!TIP]
> The **single-robot** service architecture is what distinguishes an in-line cell from a *transfer line* (which has many dedicated mechanisms) and from a *flexible manufacturing system* (which has multiple robots and automated guided vehicles).

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The metrics used to evaluate any robotic cell are quantified as follows:

- **Cell Cycle Time ($T_c$)** — Time to complete **one full processing cycle** on one workpiece, expressed in **seconds (s)**.
- **Throughput ($\dot{n}$)** — Number of workpieces completed per unit time, given by $\dot{n} = 3600 / T_c$ in **parts per hour (pph)**.
- **Robot Reach ($R$)** — Minimum distance from robot base mounting flange to the farthest machine tool chuck, in **millimetres (mm)**.
- **Linear Track Length ($L_{track}$)** — Total traverse distance the robot must cover, in **metres (m)**.
- **Machine Utilisation ($U_i$)** — Fraction of time machine $i$ is actively cutting, typically $\geq \mathbf{0.85}$ for economic viability.

These four metrics are the "vital signs" of any robotic cell design and will recur throughout the module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of an In-Line Cell

The In-Line Robot Cell comprises the following functional sub-systems:

1. **Workpiece Input Conveyor (WIC)** — Delivers raw blanks to the first machine station.
2. **Machine Tools (M₁, M₂, …, M_n)** — Typically CNC turning centres or machining centres arranged colinearly with their work spindles facing the robot's working envelope.
3. **Linear Traverse Track (LTT)** — A precision ground rail (often with rack-and-pinion or ballscrew drive) carrying the robot base.
4. **Articulated Robot Arm (6-axis or 4-axis SCARA)** — Performs load/unload operations.
5. **Workpiece Output Conveyor (WOC)** — Removes finished parts from the last station.
6. **Fixture / Pallet Exchange System** — Holds the workpiece during transfer.
7. **Safety Enclosure with Light Curtains** — Compliant with **ISO 10218 / ANSI/RIA R15.06** standards.

### 2.2 Operational Sequence (Cyclic Logic)

The robot executes the following logical steps per cycle:

- **Step 1 — Index:** Robot translates along LTT to Machine $M_1$.
- **Step 2 — Unload:** Gripper extracts finished part from $M_1$ chuck.
- **Step 3 — Load:** Robot loads raw blank into $M_1$, signals cycle start.
- **Step 4 — Re-Index:** Robot traverses to $M_2$ (or to WIC for parts pickup).
- **Step 5 — Repeat:** Steps 2–4 are repeated for $M_2, M_3, \dots, M_n$.
- **Step 6 — Deliver Output:** Finished part from $M_n$ is placed on WOC.
- **Step 7 — Return Home:** Robot returns to start position; cycle restarts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Decision — *Pallet Pooling* vs *Sequential Transfer*.** In a true in-line cell, the robot physically moves the **same workpiece** from one machine to the next. This is called *sequential transfer*. In a *pallet-pool* in-line cell, the robot only does load/unload at each machine, and parts move between machines on a separate conveyor — this is essentially a hybrid layout.

### 2.3 Why Choose an In-Line Cell? — Engineering Rationale

- **Floor Space Efficiency:** Linear arrangement fits well in narrow factory bays.
- **Material Flow Simplicity:** Unidirectional flow, easy to integrate with upstream/downstream lines.
- **Easy Expansion:** New machines can be added at the end of the line.
- **Predictable Cycle Time:** Because all motions are along one axis, cycle time estimation is highly accurate (deterministic kinematics).
- **Cost-Effective for Low/Medium Mix:** Excellent for **high-volume, low-variety** production (e.g., automotive gearbox shafts, hydraulic cylinder rods).

### 2.4 Limitations

- **Single Point of Failure:** If the robot fails, the entire cell halts (no redundancy).
- **Reach Constraints:** As more machines are added, robot reach may become insufficient — usually $n \leq 4$ machines in a practical in-line cell.
- **Uneven Machine Utilisation:** The first and last machines may have longer idle times if processing times vary widely.
- **Long Cycle Time for Many Stations:** Sequential servicing inherently increases $T_c$ with $n$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table contains **every high-yield formula** you need to solve in-line cell design problems in the KTU ESE. Memorise the symbols and the *form* of each equation — not just the final expression.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Cell Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{traverse} + T_{process}^{max}$ | s | $T_{process}^{max}$ = longest machine cycle |
| 2 | Robot Traverse Time | $T_{traverse} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_{track}}{v_{robot}}$ | s | Round trip between first and last station |
| 3 | Cell Length | $L_{cell} = n \cdot L_{machine} + (n-1) \cdot d_{gap}$ | m | $d_{gap}$ = clearance between machines |
| 4 | Minimum Robot Reach | $R_{min} = \dfrac{L_{cell}}{2} + r_{fixture} + s_{safety}$ | mm | $s_{safety} = 100$–$200$ mm |
| 5 | Throughput | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | pph | Parts per hour |
| 6 | Machine Utilisation | $U_i = \dfrac{T_{process,i}}{T_c}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\geq 0.85$ |
| 7 | Balanced Utilisation | $T_c \geq T_{process}^{max} + (n-1) \cdot T_{load\_unload}$ | s | Bottleneck condition |
| 8 | Number of Stations Served | $n \leq \left\lfloor \dfrac{R_{max} - s_{safety} - r_{fixture}}{L_{machine} + d_{gap}} \right\rfloor$ | integer | Reach-limited design |
| 9 | Robot Joint Speed Check | $\omega_j = \dfrac{v_{tcp}}{r_{link}} \leq \omega_{j,max}$ | rad/s | For each joint $j$ |
| 10 | Production Time per Batch | $T_{batch} = N \cdot T_c$ | s | $N$ = batch size |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by **manufacturing systems engineers** in automotive, aerospace, and heavy-engineering industries to *bid* for new contracts — accurate cell sizing directly determines factory ROI (return on investment).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cell Cycle Time for an $n$-Station In-Line Cell

**Statement:** A robot serves $n$ machines arranged in a straight line. The robot performs load ($T_L$) and unload ($T_U$) at each station, and traverses between adjacent stations in time $T_{step}$. The longest machine processing time is $T_P^{max}$. Derive the steady-state cycle time $T_c$.

**Assumptions:**
- Each machine $M_i$ has a processing time $T_{P,i}$.
- Robot traverse between adjacent machines is uniform with time $T_{step}$.
- Robot returns to the home (first) station after serving the last machine.
- Time to traverse from $M_n$ back to $M_1$ is $(n-1) \cdot T_{step}$.

**Derivation:**

The robot visits each of the $n$ stations in sequence, performing load and unload at every one. The total "robot active time" per cycle is:

$$
T_{robot}^{active} = n \cdot (T_L + T_U) + (n-1) \cdot T_{step}
$$

This is the time the robot spends **moving and handling parts** during one full circuit of the cell. However, the cell's cycle time is *not* this — it is the **bottleneck** between the robot's cycle and the slowest machine's processing time.

For steady state, the workpiece emerges from the last machine every $T_c$ seconds. Between two consecutive visits of the robot to $M_n$, the machine $M_n$ must have enough time to finish its operation on the part that was just loaded. Hence:

$$
T_c \geq T_P^{max} + (n-1) \cdot T_{step}
$$

This is because the robot takes $(n-1) \cdot T_{step}$ to reach $M_n$ after loading $M_1$, and during that time, $M_1$ has already started processing. The *binding constraint* is the longest machine cycle, plus the transit delay to the bottleneck station. The complete cell cycle time is therefore:

$$
\boxed{T_c = \max \left[\, T_P^{max} + (n-1) \cdot T_{step}\,,\ \ n(T_L + T_U) + (n-1) \cdot T_{step} \,\right]}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- The **first term** represents the time until the slowest machine completes a part.
- The **second term** represents the time the robot takes to complete one full circuit.
- The cell's true cycle time is whichever is *larger* — this is the classic *max-of-bottlenecks* formulation that appears throughout manufacturing systems engineering.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** An in-line robot cell has **3 CNC turning centres** ($M_1, M_2, M_3$). Robot load time $T_L = 8$ s, unload time $T_U = 6$ s. Traverse time between adjacent machines $T_{step} = 4$ s. Machine processing times are $T_{P,1} = 45$ s, $T_{P,2} = 60$ s, $T_{P,3} = 50$ s. Calculate the cell cycle time, throughput, and machine utilisation of $M_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the longest processing time.**

$$
T_P^{max} = \max(45,\, 60,\, 50) = 60 \text{ s}
$$

This is the cycle time of $M_2$, the bottleneck.

**Step 2 — Compute robot active time per circuit.**

$$
T_{robot}^{active} = 3 \cdot (8 + 6) + (3-1) \cdot 4 = 3 \cdot 14 + 2 \cdot 4 = 42 + 8 = 50 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bottleneck cycle time term.**

$$
T_P^{max} + (n-1) \cdot T_{step} = 60 + 2 \cdot 4 = 68 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the maximum of the two candidate cycle times.**

$$
T_c = \max(68,\, 50) = 68 \text{ s}
$$

The bottleneck is the **slowest machine's processing time** plus traverse delay, not the robot's busy time.

**Step 5 — Compute throughput.**

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{68} = 52.94 \approx 53 \text{ pph}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute utilisation of $M_2$.**

$$
U_2 = \frac{T_{P,2}}{T_c} = \frac{60}{68} = 0.882 = 88.2\,\%
$$

This is **acceptable** (above the 85% threshold). If $U_2$ were less than 85%, the cell would be considered *under-utilised* and would need rebalancing.

### 3.3 Derivation of Minimum Robot Reach

**Statement:** Derive the minimum horizontal reach $R_{min}$ of a robot mounted on a linear track such that it can service the first and last machines in an in-line cell.

**Geometric Setup:** Place the track along the $x$-axis. Let the first machine centre be at $x_1 = 0$ and the last at $x_n = L_{cell}$. The track is centred such that the robot base can move from $x = -L_{cell}/2$ to $x = +L_{cell}/2$ (mid-stroke positioning for symmetry).

The robot arm has a horizontal reach $R$ from the centre of its base mounting flange. To reach a machine at distance $x$ from the track's left end, the distance from the robot base is:

$$
d = \left\vert x - x_{base} \right\vert + r_{fixture}
$$

The maximum $d$ occurs at the extremities, $x_1 = 0$ and $x_n = L_{cell}$. With the base at mid-track ($x_{base} = L_{cell}/2$), the maximum distance is:

$$
d_{max} = \frac{L_{cell}}{2} + r_{fixture}
$$

Adding a safety margin $s_{safety}$:

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{L_{cell}}{2} + r_{fixture} + s_{safety}}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** This equation tells the design engineer *which catalogue robot to select.* A typical 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., **KUKA KR 16 R2010**, **ABB IRB 1660ID**) has a reach of **$1.5$ to $2.0$ m**, which limits a symmetric in-line cell to roughly $L_{cell} \leq 3$ m when $r_{fixture} \approx 250$ mm.

### 3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix

The following table compares the In-Line Cell with the two other canonical single-robot layouts:

| Parameter | In-Line Cell | Parallel (Side-by-Side) Cell | Circular Cell |
|-----------|--------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| Floor Footprint | Long and narrow | Compact square | Compact circular |
| Number of Machines Served | $n \leq 4$ typical | $n \leq 3$ | $n \leq 6$ |
| Cycle Time Scaling | Linear in $n$ | Linear in $n$ | Sub-linear (rotational) |
| Robot Reach Required | Large (extends along track) | Moderate | Moderate (radial) |
| Material Flow | Unidirectional | Cross-flow | Radial |
| Cost of Track | High (linear guide) | Low (fixed mount) | Medium (rotary indexer) |
| Best For | Shaft-type, prismatic parts | Box-type casings | Family of parts |
| Example | Crankshaft machining | Engine block machining | Bearing ring machining |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 14-mark question, the examiner expects a **labelled sketch + comparative table + 2-line justification** for full marks. Memorise this matrix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-View Layout of a 3-Station In-Line Robot Cell

The following Mermaid block renders the floor-plan architecture of a typical in-line cell with workpiece flow paths annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph infeed["Workpiece Infeed Conveyor WIC"]
        WIC1["Raw Blank In"]
    end

    subgraph cell["In-Line Cell  L track approx 4.0 m"]
        M1["M1 CNC Lathe"]
        M2["M2 CNC Lathe"]
        M3["M3 CNC Lathe"]
        TR["Robot on Linear Track LTT"]
    end

    subgraph outfeed["Workpiece Outfeed Conveyor WOC"]
        WOC1["Finished Part Out"]
    end

    WIC1 -- raw blank --> M1
    M1 -- rough part --> M2
    M2 -- semi finished --> M3
    M3 -- finished --> WOC1

    TR -. load unload M1 .-> M1
    TR -. load unload M2 .-> M2
    TR -. load unload M3 .-> M3
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the In-Line Cell Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PLC["Cell PLC Master CPU"] --> IO1["Digital I/O Modules"]
    PLC --> NET1["Profinet Industrial Ethernet"]
    PLC --> SAF["Safety PLC SIL3"]

    NET1 --> R1["Robot Controller RC"]
    NET1 --> MC1["M1 CNC Controller"]
    NET1 --> MC2["M2 CNC Controller"]
    NET1 --> MC3["M3 CNC Controller"]
    NET1 --> CV1["Conveyor VFD Drives"]

    R1 --> RV["Robot Servo Drives 6 axis"]
    R1 --> RG["Robot Gripper Pneumatic"]

    SAF --> LC1["Light Curtain Zone 1"]
    SAF --> LC2["Light Curtain Zone 2"]
    SAF --> EMO["Emergency Stop Loop"]
```

### 4.3 Cyclic Operation Timeline (Gantt-Style Block Diagram)

The block diagram below illustrates the time-multiplexed activity of the robot (top three rows) and the three machines (bottom three rows) over one full cell cycle of **68 seconds** (from the worked example).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph tline["Time Axis 0s to 68s"]
        direction LR
        t0["0s"] --- t14["14s"] --- t28["28s"] --- t42["42s"] --- t56["56s"] --- t68["68s"]
    end

    R0["RBT at M1 load"] --> R1["RBT traverse to M2 4s"] --> R2["RBT at M2 load unload 14s"] --> R3["RBT traverse to M3 4s"] --> R4["RBT at M3 load unload 14s"] --> R5["RBT return home 8s"]

    M1P["M1 process 45s overlap allowed"] --> M1I["M1 idle 23s"]
    M2P["M2 process 60s bottleneck"]
    M3P["M3 process 50s"] --> M3I["M3 idle 18s"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $M_1$ and $M_3$ have **idle intervals** while the robot is busy at other stations. This is the *fundamental inefficiency* of single-robot cells and is the most common 14-mark design question: *"How would you rebalance the cell to improve throughput?"* The answer involves either (a) adding a parts buffer between machines, (b) using a dual-gripper robot, or (c) splitting the cell into two smaller in-line sub-cells.

### 4.4 Robot Work-Envelope Coverage Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE["Robot Base on LTT"] -->|reach R| ENV["Spherical Work Envelope"]
    ENV --> P1["P1 Reach M1 chuck"]
    ENV --> P2["P2 Reach M2 chuck"]
    ENV --> P3["P3 Reach M3 chuck"]
    ENV --> P4["P4 Reach infeed conveyor"]
    ENV --> P5["P5 Reach outfeed conveyor"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define an In-Line Robot Cell. List any **two** advantages of this layout over a circular robot cell.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** An In-Line Robot Cell is a manufacturing cell in which two or more workstations are arranged in a single straight line and serviced by **one robot** mounted on a linear traverse track, which performs sequential load/unload operations at each station.
- **Any two advantages (1 mark):** (i) Simple unidirectional material flow. (ii) Easy to extend by adding machines at the line ends. (iii) Lowest floor area for long prismatic parts. (iv) Predictable deterministic cycle time.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the formula for the **minimum robot reach** required in a 4-station in-line cell with machines of length 1.2 m spaced 0.8 m apart, fixture radius 0.25 m, and safety margin 0.15 m.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cell length is:

$$
L_{cell} = n \cdot L_{machine} + (n-1) \cdot d_{gap} = 4 \cdot 1.2 + 3 \cdot 0.8 = 4.8 + 2.4 = 7.2 \text{ m}
$$

Applying the reach formula:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{L_{cell}}{2} + r_{fixture} + s_{safety} = \frac{7.2}{2} + 0.25 + 0.15 = 3.6 + 0.40 = 4.00 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** $R_{min} = \mathbf{4.0\ m}$. (The student should conclude that a standard 6-axis industrial robot is insufficient, justifying a **gantry robot** for this cell.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning**
> 
> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — In-Line Cell Questions:**
> > 1. Students often **forget the traverse time** $(n-1) \cdot T_{step}$ when computing cycle time — this costs **3 marks**.
> > 2. Students frequently assume the robot is the bottleneck — *wrong*. The **machine with the longest processing time** is the bottleneck in 90% of cases.
> > 3. Students omit the **safety margin** $s_{safety}$ in the reach formula, leading to an unrealistically small $R_{min}$ and losing **2 marks**.
> > 4. Labelled diagrams **must** include the workpiece flow arrows, robot track, and machine identifiers. A bare sketch without labels fetches **zero** diagram marks.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cell Design + Cycle Time**

**(a)** A 3-station in-line robot cell has machines $M_1, M_2, M_3$ arranged collinearly. Robot load time $T_L = 5$ s, unload time $T_U = 5$ s. Traverse time between consecutive machines $T_{step} = 3$ s. Machine processing times are $T_{P,1} = 30$ s, $T_{P,2} = 50$ s, $T_{P,3} = 35$ s. Determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) the cell cycle time $T_c$,
- (ii) the throughput in parts per hour,
- (iii) the utilisation of the bottleneck machine.

**(b)** Each machine has length $L_{machine} = 1.5$ m, the inter-machine gap is $d_{gap} = 1.0$ m, fixture radius $r_{fixture} = 0.2$ m, and safety margin $s_{safety} = 0.15$ m. Determine the **minimum robot reach** and suggest a **suitable robot type** from the following catalogue: *KUKA KR 10 R1420 (reach 1.42 m)*, *KUKA KR 16 R2010 (reach 2.01 m)*, *FANUC M-2000iA/1700L (reach 4.68 m)*. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**(a)(i) Cell cycle time [3 marks]:**

Identify the bottleneck machine: $T_P^{max} = \max(30, 50, 35) = 50$ s, which is $M_2$.

Robot active time per circuit:

$$
T_{robot}^{active} = 3 \cdot (5+5) + 2 \cdot 3 = 30 + 6 = 36 \text{ s}
$$

Apply the cycle-time formula:

$$
T_c = \max\bigl[T_P^{max} + (n-1) T_{step},\ T_{robot}^{active}\bigr] = \max[50 + 6,\, 36] = \max[56,\, 36] = 56 \text{ s}
$$

**$\therefore T_c = 56$ seconds.**

**(a)(ii) Throughput [2 marks]:**

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{56} = 64.29 \approx 64 \text{ pph}
$$

**(a)(iii) Utilisation of $M_2$ [2 marks]:**

$$
U_2 = \frac{T_{P,2}}{T_c} = \frac{50}{56} = 0.893 = 89.3\,\%
$$

This is **acceptable** (above 85%).

**(b) Minimum robot reach [4 marks]:**

Compute cell length:

$$
L_{cell} = 3 \cdot 1.5 + 2 \cdot 1.0 = 4.5 + 2.0 = 6.5 \text{ m}
$$

Apply reach formula:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{6.5}{2} + 0.20 + 0.15 = 3.25 + 0.35 = 3.60 \text{ m}
$$

**(b) Robot selection [3 marks]:**

Comparing with the catalogue:

- KUKA KR 10 R1420: reach 1.42 m → **Insufficient** (3.60 m needed).
- KUKA KR 16 R2010: reach 2.01 m → **Insufficient** (3.60 m needed).
- FANUC M-2000iA/1700L: reach 4.68 m → **Suitable**, with a margin of $4.68 - 3.60 = 1.08$ m.

**Recommended robot: FANUC M-2000iA/1700L** (a heavy-payload gantry-style arm, ideal for large in-line cells).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Comparative Layout + Rebalancing**

**(a)** With the help of a **labelled diagram**, explain the working principle of a 3-station in-line robot cell. Compare the in-line layout with the **circular robot cell** on five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In the cell described in Q1 of Part A, the bottleneck machine $M_2$ is upgraded and its processing time reduces from 50 s to 28 s. Recalculate the **new cycle time, throughput, and utilisation of each machine**. Comment on whether the cell is now **balanced**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Working Principle [3 marks]:**

A 3-station in-line robot cell consists of three CNC machines $M_1, M_2, M_3$ arranged on a common straight line with their work-spindle axes facing the robot. A 6-axis articulated robot is mounted on a **linear traverse track** running parallel to the line of machines. The cycle proceeds as follows:
- The robot moves to $M_1$, unloads the finished part, loads a new blank, and signals $M_1$ to start.
- The robot traverses to $M_2$, performs load/unload, and so on.
- After servicing $M_3$, the finished part is placed on the output conveyor and the robot returns to the home position.

*(A labelled top-view diagram showing the three machines, the linear track, the robot, the infeed and outfeed conveyors, and the work-flow arrows is required for full marks.)*

**Comparison Table [4 marks]:**

| Parameter | In-Line Cell | Circular Cell |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Floor plan | Long, narrow | Compact, square |
| Robot motion | Linear (1-DOF traverse) | Rotary indexer |
| Typical reach | Large (3–5 m) | Moderate (1.5–2.5 m) |
| Material flow | Unidirectional | Radial inward/outward |
| Ease of expansion | Easy — add at line end | Moderate — add a station on the periphery |
| Best application | Long prismatic parts | Disc/ring-shaped parts |
| Cell cycle time | Linear in $n$ | Sub-linear in $n$ |

**(b) Rebalancing calculation [7 marks]:**

New processing times: $T_{P,1} = 30$ s, $T_{P,2} = 28$ s, $T_{P,3} = 35$ s. Bottleneck becomes $M_3$ with $T_P^{max} = 35$ s.

Robot active time remains:

$$
T_{robot}^{active} = 3 \cdot 10 + 2 \cdot 3 = 36 \text{ s}
$$

Cycle time:

$$
T_c = \max\bigl[T_P^{max} + (n-1)T_{step},\ T_{robot}^{active}\bigr] = \max[35 + 6,\, 36] = \max[41,\, 36] = 41 \text{ s}
$$

Throughput:

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{41} = 87.8 \approx 88 \text{ pph}
$$

Utilisations:

$$
U_1 = \frac{30}{41} = 73.2\,\%, \quad U_2 = \frac{28}{41} = 68.3\,\%, \quad U_3 = \frac{35}{41} = 85.4\,\%
$$

**Comment:** The cell is now **moderately balanced**. The bottleneck $M_3$ is at 85.4% utilisation (acceptable), but $M_1$ and $M_2$ are under-utilised. To improve balance, the engineer may either: (i) **assign auxiliary tasks** to $M_1$ and $M_2$ (e.g., deburring, washing), (ii) introduce a **small buffer** between $M_1$ and $M_2$ to decouple them, or (iii) move the robot's home position to favour $M_3$ access.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — In-Line Robot Cell**

- **Definition:** Single robot on a linear track serving $n$ collinear machines.
- **Layout:** Unidirectional flow; machines $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_n$ on a straight line; robot base translates along LTT.
- **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = \max\bigl[T_P^{max} + (n-1)T_{step},\ n(T_L+T_U) + (n-1)T_{step}\bigr]$ — **memorise verbatim.**
- **Cell Length:** $L_{cell} = n \cdot L_{machine} + (n-1) \cdot d_{gap}$.
- **Minimum Reach:** $R_{min} = L_{cell}/2 + r_{fixture} + s_{safety}$ — **always include safety margin.**
- **Throughput:** $\dot{n} = 3600 / T_c$ in pph.
- **Utilisation:** $U_i = T_{P,i} / T_c$; target $U \geq 85\%$.
- **Bottleneck Identification:** Sort $T_{P,i}$ in descending order; the largest is the bottleneck.
- **Comparison with Circular Cell:** In-line has linear flow + long reach; circular has radial flow + moderate reach.
- **Typical Applications:** Crankshafts, cylinder rods, gear blanks, hydraulic shafts.
- **Practical Limit:** $n \leq 4$ machines in a standard 6-axis robot cell; beyond that, switch to a gantry or AGV-based FMS.
- **Safety Standards:** ISO 10218-1/-2; ANSI/RIA R15.06; light curtains and SIL-3 emergency stop.
- **Examiner Hot-Spots:** (i) Forgetting traverse time in $T_c$. (ii) Forgetting safety margin in $R_{min}$. (iii) Confusing in-line robot cell with mechanical transfer line. (iv) Skipping the labelled diagram.
- **Common Rebalancing Tools:** Dual gripper, parts buffer pallet, task reassignment, robot speed-up, parallel sub-cells.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Mobile Robot Cell** is a flexible manufacturing work cell in which one or more *autonomous or semi-autonomous mobile platforms* (such as Automated Guided Vehicles, Autonomous Mobile Robots, or mobile manipulators) operate alongside — or as part of — fixed robotic manipulators, sensors, and peripheral equipment, executing material handling, part transfer, and collaborative assembly tasks under a unified supervisory control architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A mobile robot cell is an *integrated robotic workstation* where **mobility**, **manipulation**, and **perception** are fused to provide reconfigurable production capacity, in contrast to the rigid transfer lines of classical automation.

### 1.2 Core Components of a Mobile Robot Cell

A standard mobile robot cell is composed of the following subsystems:

1. **Mobile Base / Platform** – The locomotion subsystem (wheeled, legged, tracked, or hybrid).
2. **On-board Manipulator (Optional)** – A robotic arm fixed to the mobile base to perform in-situ operations.
3. **End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT)** – Grippers, suction cups, welding torches, etc.
4. **Perception Suite** – LIDAR, ultrasonic sensors, RGB-D cameras, IMU, encoders.
5. **Navigation & Guidance System** – Algorithms + hardware (magnetic tape, QR codes, SLAM).
6. **Central Supervisory Controller** – PLC, SCADA, or ROS-based fleet manager.
7. **Communication Network** – Wi-Fi, 5G, Ethernet/IP, Profinet for V2X (vehicle-to-everything).
8. **Safety Infrastructure** – LiDAR safety scanners, e-stops, light curtains, fenced zones.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Hospital Floor Analogy"**
>
> Imagine a **hospital ward** where a **wheeled nurse's trolley** moves between beds carrying medicine (mobile base) and uses a **small robotic arm** to deliver a cup of water to a specific patient (manipulation). The trolley knows the layout of the ward (map), avoids patients walking around (obstacle avoidance), and reports to a central nurse station (supervisor) which dispatches the trolley based on requests.
>
> The **mobile robot cell is exactly this** — but in a factory. The trolley is the **AGV/AMR**, the robotic arm is the **on-board manipulator**, the patients are **machines/workstations**, and the nurse station is the **central controller**.

### 1.4 Why Mobile Robot Cells Matter (Engineering Motivation)

- **Flexibility:** Routes can be reprogrammed in software without physical reconfiguration (unlike conveyors).
- **Scalability:** New cells or routes can be added incrementally.
- **Labor Safety:** Replaces human operators in hazardous zones (e.g., forging, painting).
- **Industry 4.0 Integration:** Native compatibility with IoT, digital twins, and MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted:** Industry-standard mobile robots typically operate with a **positioning accuracy of ±10 mm (for AGVs)** and **±5 mm (for AMRs with SLAM + fiducial markers)** in industrial settings.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Mobile Robots Used in Cells

| Class | Full Form | Navigation Method | Typical Use |
|------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **AGV** | Automated Guided Vehicle | Fixed path (magnetic tape, wire, QR) | Repetitive, structured routes |
| **AGC** | Automated Guided Cart | Similar to AGV, lighter load | Light assembly transfer |
| **AMR** | Autonomous Mobile Robot | SLAM, LiDAR, vision-based | Dynamic, unstructured environments |
| **MMR** | Mobile Manipulator Robot | AMR + 6-DOF / 7-DOF arm | Mobile manipulation tasks |
| **Hybrid** | Mobile Robot + Conveyor | Combines mobile + fixed | Cell-to-cell handoff |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot Cell

The cell operates on a **layered control hierarchy** (analogous to the 5-level automation model):

1. **Field Level (Level 0):** Sensors, motors, encoders on the mobile base.
2. **Control Level (Level 1):** PLCs, motor drivers, low-level motion controllers.
3. **Supervisory Level (Level 2):** SCADA, ROS Master, fleet manager.
4. **Planning Level (Level 3):** MES, route optimizer, job dispatch.
5. **Enterprise Level (Level 4):** ERP integration (SAP, Oracle).

### 2.2 Dead-Reckoning Position Estimation (Core Algorithm)

A mobile robot estimates its pose $(x, y, \theta)$ using **wheel odometry**. For a differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase $b$:

The linear velocity of each wheel $v_L$ and $v_R$ produces:
- Linear velocity of the robot $v$.
- Angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the wheelbase axis. The robot moves along a circular arc of radius $R = v / \omega$ around the ICR.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition |
|--------------------|---------|----------------------------|
| $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Linear velocity of robot center | m/s |
| $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | Angular velocity about ICR | rad/s |
| $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{b}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | Radius of circular path | m (valid when $v_R \neq v_L$) |
| $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos(\theta_k)$ | X-update (odometry) | m |
| $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin(\theta_k)$ | Y-update (odometry) | m |
| $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ | Heading update | rad |
| $\sigma_d^2 = k_d \cdot d$ | Odometric position variance (grows with distance) | m² |
| $T_c = \dfrac{L}{v_{\text{avg}}}$ | Cycle time for distance $L$ | s |
| $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Cell efficiency ratio | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $n_{\text{robots}} = \dfrac{\lambda T_s}{\rho}$ | Fleet sizing (queueing theory) | integer, rounded up |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint:** The wheel odometry equations assume **no wheel slip** and **rigid body motion**. In real industrial cells, slip introduces drift, which is why modern AMRs **fuse odometry with IMU + LiDAR** using a Kalman filter.

### 2.4 Path Planning Methods in Mobile Cells

Mobile robot cells use three hierarchical planning methods:

- **Global Planner (Off-line):** A* or Dijkstra's algorithm on a static map → optimal top-level route.
- **Local Planner (Real-time):** Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) or Vector Field Histogram (VFH) → obstacle avoidance.
- **Behavior-based:** Subsumption architecture (Brooks) for reactive control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Hierarchy?** Global planning is computationally heavy (run every few seconds); local planning must run at **>20 Hz** to avoid moving obstacles. Splitting the two allows the cell to remain responsive.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Tesla, BMW, and Mercedes-Benz operate mobile robot cells in body-shop assembly, where AMRs deliver sub-assemblies to stationary welding robots.
- **Warehousing:** Amazon's Kiva/Hercules robots form massive mobile cells (called "drives") inside fulfillment centers.
- **Semiconductor Fabs:** AMRs transport FOUPs (Front-Opening Unified Pods) in cleanrooms where human access is restricted.
- **Hospitals & Pharma:** AGCs deliver medications and sterilized instruments in automated hospitals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dead-Reckoning Kinematics of a Differential-Drive Mobile Robot

#### Step 1: Define the geometric setup

Consider a differential-drive mobile robot with:
- Two independently driven wheels of radius $r$ on a common axle.
- Wheel separation (wheelbase) $b$.
- Left and right wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ (rad/s).

The linear velocity of each wheel at the contact point is:
$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \qquad v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

#### Step 2: Compute the linear velocity of the robot's center

Since the two wheels are rigidly connected by the chassis, the center of the robot moves with the average of the two wheel velocities (along the wheelbase axis):

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

**Explanation:** The midpoint of the wheelbase moves with the arithmetic mean because the chassis is rigid and the rotation about the ICR shifts the contact points at equal and opposite tangential rates.

#### Step 3: Compute the angular velocity of the robot

The difference in wheel velocities causes the chassis to rotate. The angular velocity is the difference in linear velocities divided by the wheelbase:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$$

**Explanation:** A small time interval $\Delta t$ produces a small arc of length $v_R \Delta t$ on the right and $v_L \Delta t$ on the left. The differential arc length divided by the wheelbase gives the rotation angle per unit time.

#### Step 4: Compute the radius of curvature

The radius of the circular arc on which the robot's center moves is:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{\frac{v_R + v_L}{2}}{\frac{v_R - v_L}{b}} = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$$

**Special Case:** If $v_R = -v_L$ (pivot turn), then $R = 0$ — the robot rotates about its own center, which is the tightest possible turn.

**Special Case:** If $v_R = v_L$, then $\omega = 0$ and $R \to \infty$ — the robot moves in a straight line.

#### Step 5: Integrate over time to obtain the pose

Starting from an initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$, the pose update over a small time step $\Delta t$ is:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_k)$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta_k)$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

**Explanation:** The X and Y positions advance in the direction of the current heading $\theta_k$, while the heading itself evolves according to the angular velocity. This is a forward-Euler integration of the unicycle model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"What happens to the position estimate if wheel slip occurs?"* Answer: The encoder-integrated pose **drifts** away from the true pose, and the error grows approximately as $\sigma_d^2 = k_d \cdot d$, where $d$ is the distance traveled. This is why AMR cells use sensor fusion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cell Cycle Time for a Mobile Robot Serving $n$ Stations

#### Step 1: Define the service problem

A single mobile robot serves $n$ workstations in a closed-loop cycle. The distance between consecutive stations is $d_i$, and the time spent loading/unloading at station $i$ is $t_i$.

#### Step 2: Total travel time

The total travel time is the sum of all inter-station travel times:

$$T_{\text{travel}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{d_i}{v_{\text{avg}}}$$

#### Step 3: Total dwell (service) time

$$T_{\text{dwell}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

#### Step 4: Total cycle time

$$T_c = T_{\text{travel}} + T_{\text{dwell}}$$

#### Step 5: Cell efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = \frac{T_{\text{dwell}}}{T_c}$$

**Explanation:** A well-designed mobile cell aims for $\eta_{\text{cell}} \ge 0.6$, meaning at least 60% of the robot's time is spent on productive loading/unloading tasks rather than traveling. If $\eta_{\text{cell}} < 0.4$, the cell is *transportation-bound* and needs route optimization or an additional robot.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.4$ m, and wheel angular velocities $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. Find the linear velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature of the path. Also compute the position update after 1 second starting from $(0, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1: Linear velocity of each wheel**

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.05 \cdot 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.05 \cdot 12 = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Linear velocity of robot center**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.6 + 0.5}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Angular velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{0.6 - 0.5}{0.4} = 0.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Radius of curvature**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.55}{0.25} = 2.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Pose update after 1 second**

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.55 \cdot 1 \cdot \cos(0) = 0.55 \text{ m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 + 0.55 \cdot 1 \cdot \sin(0) = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.25 \cdot 1 = 0.25 \text{ rad} = 14.32^\circ$$

**Final Pose:** $(0.55 \text{ m}, \ 0 \text{ m}, \ 14.32^\circ)$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot Cell

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the layered control architecture and data flow of a typical mobile robot cell.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ERP Layer] --> B[MES Scheduler]
    B --> C[Fleet Supervisor]
    C --> D[Cell Controller]
    D --> E[Mobile Base PLC]
    D --> F[Manipulator Controller]
    E --> G[Drive Motors]
    E --> H[Wheel Encoders]
    E --> I[IMU Sensor]
    F --> J[Robot Arm Joints]
    F --> K[End Effector]
    C --> L[WiFi 5G Network]
    L --> E
    L --> F
    D --> M[LiDAR Safety Scanner]
    D --> N[Vision Camera]
    D --> O[Ultrasonic Array]
    M --> P[Safety PLC]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[E Stop Circuit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The top of the graph (A → B → C → D) is the *planning hierarchy*, while the bottom (E → G, F → J) is the *real-time control layer*. The right-side branch (M/N/O → P → Q) is the *safety chain*, which is independent of the motion controllers for fail-safe operation.

---

### 4.2 State Machine: Mobile Robot Cell Operating Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Idle Standby] --> S1[Receive Job Request]
    S1 --> S2[Plan Global Path A Star]
    S2 --> S3[Traverse to Pickup]
    S3 --> S4{Obstacle Detected}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Local Planner DWA Replan]
    S5 --> S3
    S4 -- No --> S3
    S3 --> S6[Arrived at Pickup]
    S6 --> S7[Align Using Fiducial Markers]
    S7 --> S8[Manipulator Picks Payload]
    S8 --> S9[Traverse to Drop off]
    S9 --> S10[Place Payload via Manipulator]
    S10 --> S11[Job Complete Return to Charger]
    S11 --> S0
    S3 --> S12[Low Battery]
    S12 --> S13[Navigate to Charging Dock]
    S13 --> S0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The `Low Battery` branch shows a **self-rescue** behavior, which is a key feature of modern AMR cells and is often asked as a 3-mark question on *autonomy levels* in mobile robots.

---

### 4.3 Comparison: AGV vs AMR — Cell Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need Fixed Repetitive Route} --> A1[Choose AGV]
    Q1 --> Q2{Dynamic Environment with Humans}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Choose AMR with SLAM]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Budget Constraint}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A1
    Q3 -- No --> A2
    A1 --> A4[Use Magnetic Tape or QR Floor]
    A2 --> A5[Use LiDAR and 2D Map]
    A4 --> A6[Low Autonomy Level 1 to 2]
    A5 --> A7[High Autonomy Level 4 to 5]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Mobile Robot Cell. List its major subsystems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Mobile Robot Cell is an integrated manufacturing workstation in which one or more mobile platforms operate alongside fixed manipulators and sensors under a unified control architecture.

**Major subsystems (2 marks):**
1. Mobile base / locomotion platform.
2. On-board manipulator (optional).
3. Perception suite (LiDAR, cameras, IMU).
4. Central supervisory controller.
5. Communication network and safety infrastructure.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between an AGV and an AMR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AGV | AMR |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Navigation | Fixed path (tape/wire/QR) | Free path (SLAM, LiDAR) |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Environment | Structured, predictable | Dynamic, mixed with humans |
| Intelligence | Reactive only | Autonomous decision-making |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a mobile robot cell. Describe the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equations of motion (linear velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature) for a differential-drive mobile robot. A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.08$ m, $b = 0.5$ m, $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s, and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. Calculate the linear velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Field Level (Level 0):** Contains the actual sensors and actuators — wheel encoders, IMU, drive motors, LiDAR safety scanners, cameras. Generates raw data and executes motion commands. (1.5 marks)

2. **Control Level (Level 1):** Hosts PLCs and motor drivers that close the low-level control loops (current, velocity, position) at high frequency. (1.5 marks)

3. **Supervisory Level (Level 2):** SCADA/ROS Master coordinates all mobile robots, manages task queues, and handles communication. (2 marks)

4. **Planning Level (Level 3):** MES dispatches jobs, optimizes routes, and balances workload across the fleet. (1 mark)

5. **Enterprise Level (Level 4):** ERP integration for order-driven production. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

The derivations from **Section 3.1** are reproduced:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}, \qquad R = \frac{b(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

**Numerical substitution (4 marks):**

$$v_L = 0.08 \cdot 8 = 0.64 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = 0.08 \cdot 12 = 0.96 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v = \frac{0.64 + 0.96}{2} = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\omega = \frac{0.96 - 0.64}{0.5} = 0.64 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$R = \frac{0.8}{0.64} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers:** $v = 0.8$ m/s, $\omega = 0.64$ rad/s, $R = 1.25$ m.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What are the different navigation and guidance methods used in mobile robot cells? Compare them in terms of accuracy, cost, and flexibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot serves 5 stations in a closed loop. The inter-station distances are 8 m, 6 m, 10 m, 7 m, and 9 m. The dwell time at each station is 15 seconds. The robot's average speed is 1 m/s. Compute the total cycle time, total travel time, and cell efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Navigation Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Magnetic Tape/Wire:** A physical guide is laid on the floor. Simple, cheap, but inflexible. Accuracy: ±10 mm. (1.5 marks)
2. **QR/Barcode Floor:** Robot reads fiducial markers. Good accuracy (±5 mm), moderate cost. (1.5 marks)
3. **Inertial Navigation:** Uses IMU + gyroscope. Drift accumulates over time. (1 mark)
4. **Laser Triangulation:** Reflectors placed on walls. Accuracy ±5 mm, mid-to-high cost. (1.5 marks)
5. **LiDAR SLAM:** Builds a 2D/3D map in real time. Highest flexibility, highest cost. (1.5 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Total travel distance:**
$$L = 8 + 6 + 10 + 7 + 9 = 40 \text{ m}$$

**Total travel time:**
$$T_{\text{travel}} = \frac{40}{1} = 40 \text{ s}$$

**Total dwell time:**
$$T_{\text{dwell}} = 5 \cdot 15 = 75 \text{ s}$$

**Total cycle time:**
$$T_c = 40 + 75 = 115 \text{ s}$$

**Cell efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = \frac{75}{115} = 0.652 \approx 65.2\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *cell efficiency* with *overall equipment effectiveness (OEE)*. Cell efficiency here is purely the ratio of productive (dwell) time to total cycle time. Including availability, performance, and quality components will be marked wrong in this question. (Common loss: **2 marks**)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mobile Robot Cells**

- [ ] **Definition:** A mobile robot cell = *mobility* + *manipulation* + *perception* + *centralized control*.
- [ ] **AGV vs AMR:** AGV = fixed path; AMR = free path (SLAM-based).
- [ ] **5-Level Architecture:** Field → Control → Supervisory → Planning → Enterprise.
- [ ] **Differential Drive Equations:**
  - $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$
  - $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}$
  - $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega}$ (special cases: $R = 0$ for pivot, $R \to \infty$ for straight).
- [ ] **Pose Update:** Use forward-Euler integration of unicycle model.
- [ ] **Cycle Time:** $T_c = T_{\text{travel}} + T_{\text{dwell}}$.
- [ ] **Cell Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{dwell}}}{T_c}$; target $\ge 60\%$.
- [ ] **Navigation Methods:** Magnetic tape → QR → Laser → LiDAR SLAM (in order of increasing flexibility and cost).
- [ ] **Odometry Drift:** Position error variance grows with distance — fuse with IMU/LiDAR.
- [ ] **Safety:** LiDAR scanners + e-stops form an *independent* safety chain (not part of motion loop).
- [ ] **Industry 4.0:** Mobile robot cells natively integrate with MES/ERP — a frequent 3-mark question.
- [ ] **Fleet Sizing:** Use queueing theory $n = \lceil \lambda T_s / \rho \rceil$ for multi-robot cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Robots and Machine Interference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiple Robots and Machine Interference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MULTIPLE ROBOTS AND MACHINE INTERFERENCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multi-Robot Systems (MRS) in Manufacturing Cells

A **Multi-Robot Manufacturing Cell (MRMC)** is a work-cell layout in which two or more robotic manipulators operate in a shared or partially shared workspace, executing either **cooperative** (jointly handling a single workpiece) or **competitive** (independent parallel tasks sharing common resources) operations under a unified control architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (verbatim from syllabus):**
> *"A work-cell may contain more than one robot. Multiple robots may be used to handle large parts, perform operations too complex for a single robot, increase throughput, and provide redundancy. Machine interference occurs when the cycle time of a robot is greater than the cycle time of the machine it services, causing the machine to remain idle."*

### 1.2 Machine Interference (MI) — Formal Definition

**Machine Interference** is the productive time lost by a machine because the **server** (human operator, robot, or AGV) responsible for its tending is currently engaged in servicing another machine. In robotic cells, this manifests when a robot's service cycle time exceeds the machine's processing time, forcing the machine into an unproductive waiting state.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bus Stop" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Bus Stop Problem):**
> Imagine a single ticket-counter clerk serving **N = 4** passengers who arrive at random times. When the clerk is helping passenger A, passengers B, C, and D must **wait (interfere)** — they cannot do anything productive. The clerk's **service rate (μ)** is fixed, but the **arrival rate (λ)** from all N passengers combined is Nλ. The probability that *all N passengers are simultaneously idle and the clerk is also idle* is very small — this *lost waiting time* is the **interference**. Similarly, in a robotic cell, a single robot tending **N** CNC machines creates interference when one machine finishes its cycle and the robot is busy elsewhere.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

| Driver | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Throughput Maximization** | Cell output (parts/hr) depends on bottleneck identification across multiple machines and robots |
| **Capital Cost Optimization** | Each robot costs **₹ 8–25 lakhs** — minimizing robots per machine is critical for ROI |
| **Cycle-Time Matching** | Mismatched cycle times cause machine starvation or robot starvation |
| **NEP 2020 Outcome** | Maps to **CO4**: *Design robotic cells with multiple robots and analyze interference effects* |

### 1.5 Classification of Multi-Robot Cell Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> **Three canonical KTU cell configurations (per Groover, 2008 — adopted in KTU reference texts):**
> 1. **Single Robot – Multiple Machines (SR-MM):** One robot tends N machines → dominant interference scenario.
> 2. **Multiple Robots – Single Machine (MR-SM):** N robots tend one machine → e.g., large-part welding.
> 3. **Multiple Robots – Multiple Machines (MR-MM):** General flexible cell with M robots and N machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Machine Interference Problem (MIP) — Conceptual Foundation

The MIP is fundamentally a **finite-source queuing system**. Unlike infinite-customer queues, the population of machines is bounded (N machines). When a machine is being serviced, it **withdraws from the source population** — hence the arrival rate into the queue is *state-dependent*.

### 2.2 Birth-Death Process Modelling of the SR-MM Cell

The system can be modelled as a **birth-death process** with the following state definitions:

- **State n:** n machines are in the queue (i.e., n machines are waiting for service or being serviced, while N−n machines are running)
- **Transition rates:**
  - **Birth rate λ_n:** When (N−n) machines are running and any one of them fails/stops, the birth rate is **(N−n)·λ**
  - **Death rate μ_n:** When n ≥ 1, the server is busy, so the death rate is **μ** (constant, single server)

### 2.3 State Transition Diagram — Single Server, N Machines

```
State 0  ---(N·λ)-->  State 1  ---((N-1)·λ)-->  State 2 ... State N
   ^                    ^                            ^
   |                    |                            |
   (μ)                (μ)                          (μ)
```

The system is in **State 0** when the robot is idle and all N machines are running. As machines fail, the system transitions to higher states. State transitions are **governed by exponential inter-event times**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are **examination-critical** and appear frequently in KTU Module-4 questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-state probability $P_n$ | $P_n = \dfrac{N!}{(N-n)!} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}\right)^n \cdot P_0$ | Probability of being in state n (n machines in queue) |
| 2 | Normalization constant $P_0$ | $P_0 = \left[\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N}\dfrac{N!}{(N-n)!}\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}\right)^n\right]^{-1}$ | Probability that robot is idle & all machines running |
| 3 | Service factor $x$ | $x = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless ratio (also called *traffic intensity per machine*) |
| 4 | Expected machines waiting $E(W)$ | $E(W) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{N}(n-1)\,P_n$ | Mean queue length beyond the one being served |
| 5 | Expected machines being serviced $E(S)$ | $E(S) = 1 - P_0$ | Mean number of machines the robot is working on (= 1 − idle prob) |
| 6 | Expected machines running $E(R)$ | $E(R) = N - \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{N} n\,P_n$ | Mean productive machines |
| 7 | Machine efficiency $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{E(R)}{N}$ | Fraction of time machines are productive (0 ≤ η ≤ 1) |
| 8 | Robot utilization $\rho$ | $\rho = 1 - P_0$ | Fraction of time the robot is busy |
| 9 | Service factor per server $r$ (multiple servers) | $r = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\,\mu}$ where c = number of servers | For MR-SM / MR-MM cells |
| 10 | Optimum number of machines $N^*$ | $\dfrac{d\,\eta(N)}{dN} = 0$ or minimize total cost | $N^* \approx \dfrac{1}{x}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_r}{C_m}}$ (approximate) |
| 11 | Total cost $TC$ | $TC = C_m \cdot (N - E(R)) + C_r \cdot \rho$ | $C_m$ = machine cost, $C_r$ = robot cost |
| 12 | Utilization Balance (multi-robot) | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{M}\rho_i = \text{cell throughput factor}$ | For M robots in MR-MM cell |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention (used in all derivations below):**
> - $\lambda$ = machine failure rate (or service-completion rate) per machine (jobs/hr)
> - $\mu$ = robot service rate (jobs/hr)
> - $N$ = total number of machines in the cell
> - $P_n$ = steady-state probability that exactly n machines are in the system
> - All rates are in **reciprocal time units** (per minute, per hour, or per shift)

### 2.5 Why This Math is Used in Real Engineering

In real factories, **process engineers** use these equations to:
- **Decide the robot-to-machine ratio:** Should we buy 1 robot for 3 CNCs or 1 robot for 5 CNCs?
- **Estimate throughput** before the cell is built (avoiding expensive trial-and-error)
- **Bid on contracts:** Knowing exact parts-per-hour is mandatory for RFQs (Requests for Quotation)
- **Simulate FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems):** These closed-form solutions are the *analytical backbone* that simulators like FlexSim, Plant Simulation, or AnyLogic validate against

### 2.6 Multi-Robot Coordination Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three coordination paradigms (commonly asked in 2-mark questions):**
> 1. **Centralized Control:** A single master controller arbitrates all robot motions. Pros: globally optimal. Cons: single point of failure, computationally heavy.
> 2. **Decentralized Control:** Each robot has local intelligence; coordination via message-passing (e.g., blackboard architecture). Pros: fault-tolerant, scalable.
> 3. **Hierarchical Control:** Combination — local controllers supervised by a cell-level coordinator. Standard in KUKA, FANUC, ABB cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Steady-State Probabilities of the Single-Robot, N-Machine Cell

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider **N = 3** identical CNC machines tended by a **single robot**. Each machine, after completing a cycle, becomes unavailable for an average time $1/\lambda$ (loading + machining) before requiring the robot. The robot's service time is exponentially distributed with mean $1/\mu$. Find the steady-state probability that the robot is idle.

### 3.1.2 Governing Equations (Balance Equations)

For a **birth-death process** at steady state, the flow into each state must equal the flow out:

$$\lambda_n \cdot P_n = \mu_{n+1} \cdot P_{n+1}$$

In our cell, the state-dependent rates are:

$$\lambda_n = (N - n)\,\lambda, \quad \mu_n = \mu \;\; (\text{for } n \geq 1)$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Recursion

**Step 1:** Write balance for State 0 → State 1:

$$N\lambda \cdot P_0 = \mu \cdot P_1 \implies P_1 = \frac{N\lambda}{\mu}\,P_0 = N \cdot x \cdot P_0$$

where $x = \lambda/\mu$.

**Step 2:** Balance for State 1 → State 2:

$$(N-1)\lambda \cdot P_1 = \mu \cdot P_2 \implies P_2 = \frac{(N-1)\lambda}{\mu}\,P_1 = N(N-1)\,x^2\,P_0$$

**Step 3:** Balance for State 2 → State 3:

$$(N-2)\lambda \cdot P_2 = \mu \cdot P_3 \implies P_3 = \frac{(N-2)\lambda}{\mu}\,P_2 = N(N-1)(N-2)\,x^3\,P_0$$

**Step 4:** General pattern (by mathematical induction):

$$P_n = \frac{N!}{(N-n)!}\,x^n\,P_0 \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** (already shown in Formula Sheet, item 1).

**Step 5:** Apply normalization $\sum_{n=0}^{N} P_n = 1$:

$$P_0 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N} \frac{N!}{(N-n)!}\,x^n = 1$$

$$P_0 = \left[\sum_{n=0}^{N} \frac{N!}{(N-n)!}\,x^n\right]^{-1}$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** $N = 3$, $\lambda = 4$ jobs/hr, $\mu = 12$ jobs/hr → $x = \lambda/\mu = 1/3$

**Compute each $P_n$:**

$$P_0 = \left[1 + 3\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + 6\left(\tfrac{1}{9}\right) + 6\left(\tfrac{1}{27}\right)\right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[1 + 1 + 0.6667 + 0.2222\right]^{-1} = (2.8889)^{-1} = 0.3462$$

$$P_1 = 3 \times \tfrac{1}{3} \times 0.3462 = 0.3462$$

$$P_2 = 6 \times \tfrac{1}{9} \times 0.3462 = 0.2308$$

$$P_3 = 6 \times \tfrac{1}{27} \times 0.3462 = 0.0769$$

**Verification:** $0.3462 + 0.3462 + 0.2308 + 0.0769 = 1.0001$ ✓ (rounding)

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Machine Efficiency and Robot Utilization

### 3.2.1 Robot Utilization $\rho$

The robot is busy whenever the system is in **any state $n \geq 1$**:

$$\rho = 1 - P_0$$

For the example above: $\rho = 1 - 0.3462 = 0.6538$ → **65.38 % busy**.

### 3.2.2 Expected Number of Running Machines $E(R)$

$E(R)$ is the expected number of machines *not* in the system. Since in state $n$ exactly $(N-n)$ machines are running:

$$E(R) = \sum_{n=0}^{N}(N-n)\,P_n = N - \sum_{n=0}^{N} n\,P_n = N - \bar{n}$$

where $\bar{n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N} n\,P_n$ is the mean number of machines in the system (waiting + being served).

**For the example:**

$$\bar{n} = 0(0.3462) + 1(0.3462) + 2(0.2308) + 3(0.0769) = 0.3462 + 0.4616 + 0.2307 = 1.0385$$

$$E(R) = 3 - 1.0385 = 1.9615 \text{ machines running on average}$$

### 3.2.3 Machine Efficiency $\eta$

$$\eta = \frac{E(R)}{N} = \frac{1.9615}{3} = 0.6538 \;\; (\text{65.38 \%})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** For $x = 1/3$ and $N = 3$, the efficiency **equals** the robot utilization. This is **not a coincidence** — it follows from the conservation equation $\rho + \eta = 1$ in single-server finite-source queues (only true when the server services one machine at a time and service time is negligible compared to machine time).

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Optimum Number of Machines per Robot

### 3.3.1 Cost-Based Optimization

The total operating cost of the cell per unit time is:

$$TC(N) = C_r + C_m \cdot N - C_m \cdot E(R)$$

where:
- $C_r$ = hourly cost of the robot (amortized)
- $C_m$ = hourly cost per machine
- $E(R)$ = expected productive machines (revenue-producing)

The first derivative condition for optimum $N^*$ is:

$$\frac{d\,TC}{dN} = 0 \implies C_m \cdot \left(1 - \frac{dE(R)}{dN}\right) = 0$$

### 3.3.2 Approximate Closed-Form (KTU Shortcut)

For **large N** and **small x**, an engineering approximation (asymptotic expansion) gives:

$$N^* \approx \frac{1}{x} \sqrt{\frac{C_r}{C_m}}$$

**Numerical check:** If $C_r/\text{hr} = ₹500$, $C_m/\text{hr} = ₹200$, $x = 0.5$:

$$N^* \approx \frac{1}{0.5}\sqrt{\frac{500}{200}} = 2 \times 1.581 = 3.16$$

Thus **$N^* = 3$ machines per robot** (always rounded down to avoid overload).

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Cycle Time of a Multi-Robot Workcell

### 3.4.1 Bottleneck Identification

In a **Multi-Robot – Multi-Machine (MR-MM)** cell with M robots and N machines, the cell cycle time is:

$$T_{cell} = \max\left(T_{R1}, T_{R2}, \ldots, T_{RM}\right)$$

where $T_{Ri}$ is the cycle time of robot $R_i$ computed from its assigned workload.

### 3.4.2 Robot Cycle Time Formula

For robot $R_i$ assigned to $N_i$ machines, by the **palmgren-miner** (M/M/1) result, the average time between successive service requests is:

$$\bar{T}_{R_i} = \frac{1}{(N_i - \bar{n}_i)\lambda}$$

The robot's own cycle (time to visit all assigned machines once) is:

$$T_{R_i} = N_i \cdot \frac{1}{\mu} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_i}$$

### 3.4.3 Cell Throughput

The cell throughput $\phi$ (parts/hr) for the **MR-MM** configuration:

$$\phi = \frac{1}{T_{cell}} = \frac{1}{\max_i(T_{R_i})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Adding the robot times instead of taking the **maximum** (bottleneck). If $T_{R1} = 30$ s and $T_{R2} = 45$ s, throughput is governed by $T_{R2}$ — *not* the sum (75 s) or the average.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Multi-Robot Interference Zone Analysis

When two robots share a common workspace region, **collision-avoidance zones** are defined. Let $A$ and $B$ be the swept volumes of robots $R_1$ and $R_2$. The interference region is:

$$I = A \cap B$$

The probability of **simultaneous occupation** in steady state is:

$$P_{coll} = P(R_1 \in I) \cdot P(R_2 \in I) = \rho_1 \cdot \rho_2 \cdot \frac{\vert I \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

In a well-designed KTU cell, $P_{coll}$ should be **< 0.01** to be considered safe for hard-real-time industrial control.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Scenarios to Equations

| Scenario | Model Type | Governing Equation | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 robot, 1 machine | Degenerate (no interference) | $\rho = \lambda/\mu$ | 2 |
| 1 robot, N machines | M/M/1 finite-source | Birth-death recursion | 7–14 |
| 2 robots, N machines | M/M/2 finite-source | Erlang-C variant | 14 |
| Multiple robots, shared workspace | Collision-zone overlap | $P_{coll} = \rho_1 \rho_2 \cdot (\vert I\vert / \vert A \cup B\vert)$ | 7 |
| Optimum N* selection | Cost-minimization | $N^* \approx (1/x)\sqrt{C_r/C_m}$ | 7 |
| Cycle-time bottleneck | Max-of-T approach | $T_{cell} = \max(T_{R_i})$ | 3 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Robot Cell — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a multi-robot manufacturing cell, showing data flow and physical interactions between sub-systems. (Adapted to a functional block view rather than a literal 3-D layout, as Mermaid cannot render physical CAD drawings.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CC[Cell Controller / PLC Master] --> R1[Robot 1 Controller]
    CC --> R2[Robot 2 Controller]
    CC --> R3[Robot 3 Controller]
    CC --> S1[Sensor Bus Vision and Force]
    CC --> CB[Conveyor Buffer]
    CC --> TS[Tool Changer Station]
    R1 --> M1[Machine 1 CNC Mill]
    R1 --> M2[Machine 2 CNC Lathe]
    R2 --> M3[Machine 3 Press Brake]
    R2 --> M4[Machine 4 Welding Cell]
    R3 --> M5[Machine 5 Inspection Station]
    R3 --> M6[Machine 6 Packaging Unit]
    M1 --> CB
    M2 --> CB
    M3 --> CB
    M4 --> CB
    M5 --> CB
    M6 --> CB
    S1 --> CC
    classDef ctrl fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef robot fill:#059669,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef machine fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sensor fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    class CC,R1,R2,R3 ctrl
    class M1,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6 machine
    class S1,CB,TS sensor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Blue blocks** = control hierarchy (PLC + robot controllers)
> - **Green blocks** = robotic manipulators (the *servers* in MI terminology)
> - **Red blocks** = machines (the *customers* in MI terminology)
> - **Orange blocks** = shared resources (conveyor, sensors, tool changer)
> Each red machine waits on a green robot → this is the **interference problem** in graphical form.

---

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Machine Interference Birth-Death Process

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> state0
    state0 --> state1 : N times lambda
    state1 --> state2 : (N-1) times lambda
    state2 --> state3 : (N-2) times lambda
    state3 --> stateN : 1 times lambda
    state1 --> state0 : mu
    state2 --> state1 : mu
    state3 --> state2 : mu
    stateN --> stateNm1 : mu
    state0 : State 0 - Robot idle, all machines running
    state1 : State 1 - Robot busy, 1 machine waiting
    state2 : State 2 - Robot busy, 2 machines waiting
    state3 : State 3 - Robot busy, 3 machines waiting
    stateN : State N - All N machines waiting
    stateNm1 : State N-1 - Robot busy, N-1 machines waiting
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU exam:**
> - Arrows leaving the *left* of each state represent **births** (machine failures/requests arriving) at rate $(N-n)\lambda$
> - Arrows entering from the *right* represent **deaths** (services completed) at rate $\mu$
> - At steady state, $\pi_n \cdot \lambda_n = \pi_{n+1} \cdot \mu_{n+1}$ → use this to derive all $P_n$ formulas in Section 3.1

---

## 4.3 Flowchart — Multi-Robot Cell Design Methodology (KTU Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define part family and operations] --> B[Identify required machines and processes]
    B --> C[Estimate cycle times T_machine and T_robot]
    C --> D{Is single robot sufficient?}
    D -->|Yes| E[SR-MM configuration: Apply M/M/1 MIP model]
    D -->|No| F[MR-MM configuration: Apply M/M/c model]
    E --> G[Compute P0, Pn, eta, rho]
    F --> H[Compute cell throughput phi]
    G --> I{Meets target throughput?}
    H --> I
    I -->|No| J[Add robot OR reduce machines per robot]
    J --> D
    I -->|Yes| K[Compute cost TC and select optimum N star]
    K --> L[Layout cell with collision-free zones]
    L --> M[Validate via simulation FlexSim or AnyLogic]
    M --> N[Final KTU design report]
```

---

## 4.4 Multi-Robot Interference Zone — Topological View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ws[Shared Workspace]
        direction LR
        z1[Robot 1 Swept Volume A] 
        z2[Robot 2 Swept Volume B]
        z3[Interference Zone I equals A intersect B]
        z1 --- z2
        z3 --- z1
        z3 --- z2
    end
    o1[Operation 1 - only Robot 1] --> z1
    o2[Operation 2 - only Robot 2] --> z2
    o3[Cooperative operation - both robots] --> z3
    classDef zoneA fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    classDef zoneB fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    classDef zoneI fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    class z1 zoneA
    class z2 zoneB
    class z3 zoneI
```

> [!NOTE]
> - **Blue zone** = exclusive to Robot 1
> - **Green zone** = exclusive to Robot 2
> - **Red zone** = the **interference region** where collision-avoidance protocols (e.g., mutual-exclusion semaphores, dead-lock-free Petri nets) must be enforced

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Define Machine Interference. State the factors that affect the magnitude of interference in a robotic work cell.**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Interference is the productive time lost by a machine while its serving robot is engaged in tending another machine. It arises in a **Single-Robot–Multiple-Machine (SR-MM)** configuration when the robot's service demand exceeds its service capacity.

**Factors affecting interference magnitude:**

1. **Service factor $x = \lambda/\mu$:** Higher $x$ → higher interference (machine processing time approaches robot service time).
2. **Number of machines N:** More machines per robot → higher interference (more competition for the server).
3. **Variability in service times:** Greater CV (coefficient of variation) of $\mu$ → more waiting.
4. **Robot's working envelope & travel time:** Long travel between machines amplifies interference.
5. **Machine reliability:** Lower MTTF → more frequent service requests → higher interference.
6. **Layout geometry:** Distance between machines directly affects robot transit time.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Distinguish between cooperative and competitive multi-robot tasks with suitable industrial examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cooperative Multi-Robot Task | Competitive Multi-Robot Task |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Jointly accomplish a single task | Independent parallel tasks |
| **Workspace** | Shared, often with overlap | Partitioned or fully shared |
| **Synchronization** | Tight, real-time coordination | Loose, resource-mediated |
| **Example 1** | Two robots lifting a car body for welding (e.g., FANUC R-2000iC pair) | Two robots in parallel tending two CNC mills (independent queues) |
| **Example 2** | Dual-arm assembly of a gearbox (e.g., ABB YuMi) | Two SCARA robots placing SMD components on a PCB line |
| **Control** | Centralized, force-coupled | Decentralized with deadlock prevention |
| **Interference type** | Geometric (workspace overlap) | Resource (machine/buffer) contention |

> [!NOTE]
> Both forms of interference are *exam-relevant*; KTU frequently asks for the distinguishing table in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A single robot services $N = 4$ CNC machines. The processing time of each machine is exponentially distributed with mean $1/\lambda = 15$ minutes. The robot's service time is exponentially distributed with mean $1/\mu = 5$ minutes. Determine:
(i) The steady-state probability that the robot is idle.
(ii) The probability that all 4 machines are waiting.
(iii) The machine efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the above parameters, determine the **optimum number of machines per robot** if the robot's amortized hourly cost is $C_r = ₹600$ and each machine costs $C_m = ₹250$ per hour to operate.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Steady-State Probabilities

**Step 1: Compute the service factor**

$$x = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{1/15}{1/5} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2: List all $P_n$ for $N = 4$**

Using the canonical formula $P_n = \dfrac{N!}{(N-n)!}\,x^n\,P_0$:

$$P_0 = 1 \cdot x^0 \cdot P_0 = 1 \cdot P_0$$

$$P_1 = \frac{4!}{3!}\,x^1\,P_0 = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}\,P_0 = 1.3333\,P_0$$

$$P_2 = \frac{4!}{2!}\,x^2\,P_0 = 12 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9}\,P_0 = 1.3333\,P_0$$

$$P_3 = \frac{4!}{1!}\,x^3\,P_0 = 24 \cdot \tfrac{1}{27}\,P_0 = 0.8889\,P_0$$

$$P_4 = \frac{4!}{0!}\,x^4\,P_0 = 24 \cdot \tfrac{1}{81}\,P_0 = 0.2963\,P_0$$

**Step 3: Apply normalization**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{4} P_n = P_0\,(1 + 1.3333 + 1.3333 + 0.8889 + 0.2963) = 4.8518\,P_0 = 1$$

$$P_0 = \frac{1}{4.8518} = 0.2061$$

**Step 4: Back-substitute individual probabilities**

$$P_1 = 0.2748, \quad P_2 = 0.2748, \quad P_3 = 0.1832, \quad P_4 = 0.0611$$

**Verification:** $0.2061 + 0.2748 + 0.2748 + 0.1832 + 0.0611 = 1.0000$ ✓

**(i) Robot idle probability:** $\boxed{P_0 = 0.2061}$ **(20.61 %)**

**(ii) All 4 machines waiting:** $\boxed{P_4 = 0.0611}$ **(6.11 %)**

**(iii) Machine efficiency:**

Mean number of machines in system:

$$\bar{n} = 0 + 0.2748 + 2(0.2748) + 3(0.1832) + 4(0.0611) = 0.2748 + 0.5496 + 0.5496 + 0.2444 = 1.6184$$

Mean running machines:

$$E(R) = N - \bar{n} = 4 - 1.6184 = 2.3816$$

Machine efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{E(R)}{N} = \frac{2.3816}{4} = 0.5954$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 59.54 \%}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Optimum Number of Machines

Using the KTU approximation formula:

$$N^* \approx \frac{1}{x}\sqrt{\frac{C_r}{C_m}}$$

$$N^* \approx \frac{1}{1/3}\sqrt{\frac{600}{250}} = 3 \times \sqrt{2.4} = 3 \times 1.5492 = 4.65$$

**Engineering judgment:** Round **down to $N^* = 4$** machines (going to 5 would exceed the server's capacity and reduce efficiency).

**Validate by computing efficiency for $N = 4$ vs $N = 5$:**

For $N = 5$, recompute $P_0$ and $\eta$:

$$P_0(N=5) = \left[1 + 5x + 20x^2 + 60x^3 + 120x^4 + 120x^5\right]^{-1}$$

$$= \left[1 + 1.6667 + 0.5556 + 0.1235 + 0.0206 + 0.0027\right]^{-1} = (3.3691)^{-1} = 0.2968$$

$$\bar{n}(N=5) = 0 + 1(0.4947) + 2(0.3298) + 3(0.1465) + 4(0.0488) + 5(0.0130) = 1.4300$$

$$\eta(N=5) = (5-1.4300)/5 = 0.7140 \;(71.40\%)$$

| $N$ | $\eta$ (%) |
|---|---|
| 3 | 65.4 |
| **4** | **59.5 (optimum by cost)** |
| 5 | 71.4 (efficiency ↑ but more machine cost) |

> [!NOTE]
> The cost-based optimum balances $\eta$ against $C_m \cdot N$. With high $C_r$ (expensive robot), the cell tolerates more machines per robot to spread the robot's fixed cost.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A flexible manufacturing cell consists of **2 robots** servicing **5 machines**. Each machine fails on average every **20 minutes** ($\lambda = 3$ per hr per machine). The mean service time per machine by a robot is **6 minutes** ($\mu = 10$ per hr per robot). Model the system as an **M/M/2/5** queue and determine the **robot utilization** and the **probability that a machine has to wait** for a robot.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain any **three multi-robot coordination strategies** used in modern manufacturing cells. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with respect to **fault-tolerance, scalability, and real-time response**.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — M/M/2/5 Multi-Server Interference

**System parameters:**
- $N = 5$ machines (finite source)
- $c = 2$ robots (servers)
- $\lambda = 3$ per hr per machine
- $\mu = 10$ per hr per robot
- $x = \lambda/\mu = 0.3$

**Step 1: State-dependent birth rates (finite population)**

When the system is in state $n$ (n machines waiting or being serviced), the number of *running* machines is $(N-n)$. The birth rate (entry of new request) is:

$$\lambda_n = (N - n)\lambda$$

The death rate is **$n\mu$ when $n \leq c$** (all servers busy) and **$c\mu$ when $n > c$** (queue forms).

**Step 2: Steady-state balance equations**

For $n = 0, 1$ (fewer requests than servers):

$$(N-n)\lambda \cdot P_n = (n+1)\mu \cdot P_{n+1}$$

For $n \geq 2$ (queue forms):

$$(N-n)\lambda \cdot P_n = c\mu \cdot P_{n+1}$$

**Step 3: Recursion to compute $P_n$**

$$P_1 = \frac{5\lambda}{1\mu}\,P_0 = \frac{5 \times 3}{10}\,P_0 = 1.5\,P_0$$

$$P_2 = \frac{4\lambda}{2\mu}\,P_1 = \frac{4 \times 3}{20}\,(1.5\,P_0) = 0.9\,P_0$$

$$P_3 = \frac{3\lambda}{2\mu}\,P_2 = \frac{3 \times 3}{20}\,(0.9\,P_0) = 0.405\,P_0$$

$$P_4 = \frac{2\lambda}{2\mu}\,P_3 = \frac{2 \times 3}{20}\,(0.405\,P_0) = 0.1215\,P_0$$

$$P_5 = \frac{1\lambda}{2\mu}\,P_4 = \frac{3}{20}\,(0.1215\,P_0) = 0.01823\,P_0$$

**Step 4: Normalization**

$$\sum P_n = P_0(1 + 1.5 + 0.9 + 0.405 + 0.1215 + 0.01823) = 3.94473\,P_0 = 1$$

$$P_0 = 0.2535$$

**Step 5: Individual probabilities**

$$P_1 = 0.3802, \quad P_2 = 0.2281, \quad P_3 = 0.1027, \quad P_4 = 0.0308, \quad P_5 = 0.00462$$

**Step 6: Robot utilization**

Both robots are busy when $n \geq 2$ (state 2 and above). When $n = 1$, only one robot is busy. So:

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Expected busy robots}}{c} = \frac{E(\text{busy})}{2}$$

Expected busy robots:

$$E(\text{busy}) = 0(P_0) + 1(P_1) + 2(P_2 + P_3 + P_4 + P_5)$$

$$= 0 + 0.3802 + 2(0.2281 + 0.1027 + 0.0308 + 0.00462)$$

$$= 0.3802 + 2(0.3662) = 0.3802 + 0.7324 = 1.1126$$

Per-robot utilization:

$$\rho = \frac{1.1126}{2} = 0.5563 \;\; (\textbf{55.63\%})$$

**Step 7: Probability that a machine must wait**

A machine waits if **both robots are busy when it requests service** → system in state $n \geq 2$:

$$P(\text{wait}) = P_2 + P_3 + P_4 + P_5 = 0.2281 + 0.1027 + 0.0308 + 0.00462$$

$$\boxed{P(\text{wait}) = 0.3662 \;\; (36.62\%)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Three Multi-Robot Coordination Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> This is a 7-mark descriptive question. The KTU-recommended structure is to **tabulate** the comparison for clarity.

**Strategy 1: Centralized Control**

- **Architecture:** A single master controller (PLC/industrial PC) computes all robot trajectories and dispatches commands.
- **Real-time response:** Highest (deterministic cycle times, no inter-controller comm delay).
- **Fault-tolerance:** **Low** — single point of failure; if master fails, entire cell halts.
- **Scalability:** **Poor** — adding robots overloads the master.
- **Example:** Legacy automotive welding lines with Siemens SINUMERIK master.

**Strategy 2: Decentralized Control**

- **Architecture:** Each robot has its own controller; coordination is achieved via a **blackboard** (shared memory) or **contract-net protocol** (CNP).
- **Real-time response:** Medium (subject to network latency in distributed systems).
- **Fault-tolerance:** **High** — one robot's failure does not stop the others.
- **Scalability:** **High** — new robots can be added modularly.
- **Example:** KUKA KR-C5 cells using EtherCAT + blackboard.

**Strategy 3: Hierarchical Control**

- **Architecture:** Combines centralized planning (cell-level) with decentralized execution (robot-level). Cell-level handles scheduling, robot-level handles motion.
- **Real-time response:** High (real-time control at lower level, soft-real-time at upper level).
- **Fault-tolerance:** **Medium-High** — upper failure isolates cell, but local control preserves safety.
- **Scalability:** **Very High** — supports hundreds of robots (e.g., FANUC R-30iB Plus cells).
- **Example:** ABB RobotStudio + IRC5 controller hierarchies.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Centralized | Decentralized | Hierarchical |
