> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:** When asked "Draw the architecture of a work cell controller," sketch a **3-tier pyramid** — (i) MES/ERP at top, (ii) WCC in the middle as a thick red bar, (iii) Field devices at the bottom. Label the **fieldbus** connecting the WCC to the devices. This simple sketch alone is worth **3–4 marks** out of 14.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define a Work Cell Controller. List any four functions it performs in a robotic manufacturing cell. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A Work Cell Controller (WCC) is a real-time industrial control system (PLC/industrial PC) that synchronizes and supervises all equipment within a robotic manufacturing cell, acting as the bridge between the MES (top) and the field devices (bottom).

**Four key functions:**
1. **Recipe/Part-Program Management** — stores and dispatches production recipes.
2. **Sequencing and Dispatching** — orchestrates the order of robot and machine operations.
3. **I/O and Fieldbus Coordination** — exchanges signals with robots, CNCs, and conveyors via Profinet/EtherCAT.
4. **Alarm, Safety, and Reporting** — handles E-stops, logs faults, and pushes OEE data to the MES.

*(Word-limit tip: Aim for ~80–100 words; one diagram of the WCC hierarchy can earn an extra mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Hard Real-Time and Soft Real-Time control with one robotic-cell example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Deadline missing consequence | **System failure / safety risk** | Degraded quality, not catastrophic |
| Jitter tolerance | **< 50 µs** | **< 10 ms** |
| Robotic example | Spot-welding gun synchronization with robot motion | Vision-based defect inspection feedback |
| Typical protocol | EtherCAT, Profinet IRT | Standard Ethernet/IP, OPC UA |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Work Cell Controller in a robotic manufacturing cell. Discuss its role in the ISA-95 hierarchy. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Work Cell Controller occupies **Level 2** of the ISA-95 hierarchy, sitting between the **MES (Level 3)** above and the **PLC/Robot/CNC controllers (Level 1)** below.

**Block Diagram:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  LEVEL 4 — ERP (SAP, Oracle)   │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             │ OPC UA / Web Services
┌────────────▼────────────────────┐
│  LEVEL 3 — MES                 │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             │ Production Orders / Recipes
┌────────────▼────────────────────┐
│  LEVEL 2 — WORK CELL CONTROLLER│  ◄── THE BRAIN
│  • Recipe Database             │
│  • SFC Sequencer               │
│  • Fieldbus Master             │
│  • Safety Logic (SIL 2/3)      │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             │ Profinet / EtherCAT / DeviceNet
┌────────────▼────────────────────┐
│  LEVEL 1 — PLCs, Robot, CNC    │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             │ Discrete / Analog I/O
┌────────────▼────────────────────┐
│  LEVEL 0 — Sensors, Actuators  │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Role of WCC in the hierarchy:**

1. **Translation Layer:** Converts high-level MES orders (e.g., "Make 50 part-A's") into low-level I/O signals.
2. **Coordination Layer:** Synchronizes heterogeneous devices operating on different time scales.
3. **Data Aggregation:** Collects cycle data, downtime, OEE, and pushes it upward to the MES.
4. **Safety Enforcement:** Implements SIL 2/3 safety functions (per IEC 61508) and E-stop logic.
5. **Recipe Management:** Maintains version-controlled part programs and process parameters.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for diagram, 3 for ISA-95 explanation, 2 for WCC roles.)*

---

**(b) A robotic work cell has the following cycle: Load (10 s) → Robot Pick-and-Place (5 s) → CNC Machining (45 s) → Robot Transfer (4 s) → Inspection Station (12 s) → Unload (6 s). The WCC implements partial overlap such that loading of the next blank starts during inspection, saving 8 seconds of effective cycle time. Calculate: (i) Original cycle time, (ii) Overlapped cycle time, (iii) Throughput in parts/hour for both cases, (iv) Percentage improvement. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original (serial) cycle time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 10 + 5 + 45 + 4 + 12 + 6 \\
&= 82 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Original throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p^{orig} &= \frac{3600}{82} \\
&= 43.90 \text{ parts/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overlapped cycle time** (with 8 s savings during inspection):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c^{overlap} &= 82 - 8 \\
&= 74 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Overlapped throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p^{overlap} &= \frac{3600}{74} \\
&= 48.65 \text{ parts/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage improvement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta &= \frac{48.65 - 43.90}{43.90} \times 100 \\
&= 10.82 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Original | Overlapped | Improvement |
|-----------|----------|------------|-------------|
| Cycle Time | 82 s | 74 s | −8 s |
| Throughput | 43.90 parts/hr | 48.65 parts/hr | +10.82 % |

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the communication protocols used by a Work Cell Controller to interface with field devices. Compare Profinet, EtherCAT, and DeviceNet on the basis of cycle time, bandwidth, and typical application. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The WCC uses industrial fieldbuses to communicate with robots, CNCs, sensors, and actuators. Three major families dominate:

**1. Profinet (IEC 61784)**
- **Cycle Time:** 1 ms (IRT class)
- **Bandwidth:** 100 Mbps
- **Topology:** Line, star, ring
- **Application:** Automotive body shop, large discrete manufacturing

**2. EtherCAT (Ethernet for Control Automation Technology)**
- **Cycle Time:** ≤ 100 µs (the fastest)
- **Bandwidth:** 100 Mbps, full duplex
- **Topology:** Daisy-chain ("processing on the fly")
- **Application:** High-speed packaging, semiconductor, precision assembly

**3. DeviceNet (CIP-based)**
- **Cycle Time:** 5–10 ms
- **Bandwidth:** 500 kbps
- **Topology:** Trunk-line/drop-line
- **Application:** Legacy installations, sensor-actuator networks in mid-size cells

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Profinet | EtherCAT | DeviceNet |
|-----------|----------|----------|-----------|
| Cycle Time | 1 ms | ≤ 100 µs | 5–10 ms |
| Bandwidth | 100 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 500 kbps |
| Max Nodes | ~256 | ~65535 | 64 |
| Determinism | High (IRT) | Very High | Moderate |
| Typical Use | Automotive | Semiconductor | Sensor networks |

**WCC's Role:** The WCC hosts a **fieldbus master card** (e.g., Siemens CP 343-1, Beckhoff EK1100) that polls all slaves cyclically, exchanges process data, and handles diagnostics.

---

**(b) Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). For a robotic cell, the following data is collected over a 30-day month: Planned Production Time = 720 hours, Downtime = 60 hours, Ideal Cycle Time = 90 s, Total Parts Produced = 24,000, Defective Parts = 480. Compute the OEE and identify the largest contributor to losses. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Availability (A).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \\
&= \frac{720 - 60}{720} \\
&= \frac{660}{720} \\
&= 0.9167 \quad (91.67\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Performance (P).**

First, the actual cycle time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Total Parts Produced}} \\
&= \frac{660 \times 3600}{24000} \\
&= 99 \text{ s/part}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, Performance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Actual Cycle Time}} \\
&= \frac{90}{99} \\
&= 0.9091 \quad (90.91\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality (Q).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts Produced}} \\
&= \frac{24000 - 480}{24000} \\
&= \frac{23520}{24000} \\
&= 0.9800 \quad (98.00\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
&= 0.9167 \times 0.9091 \times 0.9800 \\
&= 0.8167 \quad (81.67\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the largest loss contributor.**

- Availability loss: $1 - A = 8.33\%$
- Performance loss: $1 - P = 9.09\%$ ◄ **LARGEST**
- Quality loss: $1 - Q = 2.00\%$

**Conclusion:** The cell's biggest drag is **Performance Loss** — the actual cycle time (99 s) is 10% slower than ideal (90 s). To improve OEE toward the world-class benchmark of 85%, the plant should focus on **reducing minor stoppages and speed losses**, e.g., by retuning the WCC's SFC sequencer and robot acceleration profiles.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps in Work Cell Controller Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PLC with WCC** — A PLC is a *single machine* controller; the WCC coordinates *multiple* machines. Students frequently lose **1–2 marks** by using these interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the safety aspect** — Forgetting to mention **SIL levels, E-stop wiring, and ISO 13849-1** is the most common reason for losing 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Forgetting units in cycle-time problems** — Always write "**seconds**" or "**parts/hour**" beside the numerical answer. Bare numbers are penalized.
> 4. **Mixing up ISA-95 levels** — WCC is **Level 2**, MES is **Level 3**, ERP is **Level 4**. Reversing these costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the fieldbus name** — A WCC answer without naming **Profinet/EtherCAT/DeviceNet** is considered incomplete. Examiners specifically test this in **2-mark sub-parts**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Work Cell Controller (WCC)** = Level 2 of ISA-95; the *brain* of a robotic manufacturing cell.
- ✅ **Core Functions:** Recipe management, sequencing, I/O coordination, safety, alarm handling, MES reporting.
- ✅ **Standard:** **IEC 61131-3** for programming (LD, ST, FBD, SFC, IL).
- ✅ **Real-time requirement:** Hard real-time ≤ 1 ms; soft real-time ≤ 10 ms.
- ✅ **Cycle time formula:** $T_c = T_{load} + T_{process} + T_{transfer} + T_{unload}$.
- ✅ **Throughput formula:** $T_p = 3600 / T_c$ (parts/hour).
- ✅ **OEE formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ — world-class benchmark = **85%**.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- ✅ **Major Fieldbuses:** **Profinet** (1 ms), **EtherCAT** (≤ 100 µs), **DeviceNet** (5–10 ms).
- ✅ **Communication with MES:** **OPC UA (IEC 62541)** — the modern standard.
- ✅ **Safety Standard:** **ISO 13849-1 PL d/e**, response time **≤ 25 ms**.
- ✅ **Bottleneck station** has the largest processing time — the WCC schedules around it.
- ✅ **Overlap scheduling** can boost throughput by **10–40%** in serial cells.
- ✅ **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$ — used for buffer sizing in front of each station.
- ✅ **Always draw the 3-tier hierarchy (ERP → MES → WCC → PLC → Field)** in 7-mark questions.
- ✅ **Always name a fieldbus** — never leave the WCC as a "black box."
- ✅ **Always state units** — never write bare numerical answers in cycle/throughput problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Analysis for Robotics: Basic Data Required - Type of Robot Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economic Analysis for Robotics: Basic Data Required - Type of Robot Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Analysis for Robotics** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation of all costs, benefits, and financial risks associated with the acquisition, installation, operation, and maintenance of an industrial robotic system, performed **before** the capital investment is committed, to determine the financial viability and optimal configuration of the proposed automation.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT402), economic analysis is the **financial backbone** of the *Cell Design* decision process. A robot is never purchased because it is "cool"; it is purchased because the **projected financial returns** justify the capital expenditure. This involves collecting **basic data** (cost elements, production data, labor data) and then mapping them against the **type of robot installation** being contemplated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT402 - Module 4):**
> Economic justification is treated as an **engineering decision**, not a purely accounting exercise. Students must understand that the *type of installation* (Standalone, Cell, Line, or Mixed-Model) directly dictates which cost parameters are dominant and which economic model is applicable.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Robot Economic Analysis

Every economic study in robotics rests on three pillars:

1. **Cost Data (The Outflow):** How much money goes out?
2. **Benefit / Savings Data (The Inflow):** How much money comes back?
3. **Time / Production Data (The Multiplier):** How fast does the recovery happen?

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Auto-Rickshaw vs. The Company Car"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you run a small business.** You have two choices:
> - **Option A:** Buy an **auto-rickshaw** for ₹3 lakhs. It will replace one delivery boy (saving ₹15,000/month in salary). It runs solo, single-shift, and only does one task.
> - **Option B:** Buy a **company car** for ₹15 lakhs. It will replace three delivery boys, work two shifts, and integrate with a warehouse management system.
>
> Before you buy *either*, you **must collect basic data**: fuel cost, EMI, driver's salary, insurance, maintenance, expected life, daily kilometers. The **type of installation** (rickshaw = Standalone Robot; car = Robotic Cell in a Line) determines *what* data matters most. For the rickshaw, fuel and tires dominate. For the car, fleet integration, insurance, and downtime dominate. **This is exactly the logic of robotic economic analysis.**

### 1.4 Standard Economic Metrics Used in Robotics

The following metrics are the **standard language** of KTU board questions on this topic:

- **Payback Period (PBP)** — measured in **months** or **years**.
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** — measured in **% per year**.
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** — measured in monetary units (₹, $, €).
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — measured in **%**.
- **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** — measured in monetary units per year.

These five metrics are the "vital signs" of any robotic project proposal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Basic Data Required for Economic Analysis

The KTU syllabus and standard industrial engineering textbooks classify the **basic data** into **four master categories**. Missing data in any category invalidates the entire study.

#### Category A: Capital Investment Data (One-Time Costs)

These are paid **once** at the time of acquisition.

- **Robot purchase price** ($C_r$) — base cost of the manipulator and controller.
- **End-effector / Gripper cost** ($C_g$) — custom or standard tooling.
- **Peripheral equipment** ($C_p$) — conveyors, fixtures, sensors, safety cages.
- **Installation & Commissioning** ($C_i$) — labor for setup, mounting, wiring.
- **Engineering & Integration** ($C_e$) — cell design, programming, simulation.
- **Training of personnel** ($C_t$) — operator and maintenance staff training.
- **Working capital** ($C_w$) — spare parts inventory, initial consumables.

$$
C_{capital} = C_r + C_g + C_p + C_i + C_e + C_t + C_w
$$

#### Category B: Operating Cost Data (Recurring Costs)

These repeat **every year** (or every shift/month).

- **Direct labor** ($L$) — operator wages, supervisor wages, benefits.
- **Maintenance & Spare parts** ($M$) — preventive and corrective.
- **Energy / Utilities** ($U$) — electricity, compressed air, cooling.
- **Consumables** ($C_{ns}$) — welding wire, paint, adhesives, cutting fluids.
- **Insurance & Taxes** ($I_x$) — annual premium and property tax on the asset.

$$
C_{annual\_operating} = L + M + U + C_{ns} + I_x
$$

#### Category C: Production / Throughput Data

- **Annual production volume** ($Q$) — units per year.
- **Cycle time** ($T_c$) — seconds per unit (robot + peripheral).
- **Available work hours** ($H$) — typically 2,000 hours/year (1 shift), 4,000 (2 shifts), 6,000 (3 shifts).
- **Machine uptime / OEE** ($\eta$) — Overall Equipment Effectiveness, typically 85–95%.
- **Scrap rate reduction** ($\Delta S$) — improvement in quality.
- **Throughput increase** ($\Delta Q$) — additional units produced due to faster cycle.

#### Category D: Benefit / Savings Data (The Inflows)

- **Labor savings** — displaced workers × loaded hourly wage × hours.
- **Increased productivity** — value of additional units produced.
- **Quality savings** — reduced scrap, reduced rework, reduced warranty claims.
- **Inventory savings** — faster throughput = lower WIP (Work-In-Progress).
- **Safety / Ergonomic savings** — reduced insurance premiums, reduced injury costs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Indirect benefits (safety, ergonomics, employee morale) are **real but hard to quantify**. KTU examiners often test whether students *acknowledge* them, even if the formula does not include them directly.

### 2.2 The Five Key Formulas — KTU Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU 2024 question on economic analysis is a variation of one of these five formulas.**

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|--------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{C_{annual\_saving} - C_{annual\_operating}}$ | Years | Quick screening, simple projects |
| 2 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{C_{annual\_saving} - C_{annual\_operating}}{C_{capital}} \times 100$ | % / year | Comparing competing projects |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = -C_{capital} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | ₹, $, € | Long-life, multi-year projects |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $0 = -C_{capital} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | % | Finding the "true" discount rate |
| 5 | **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** | $EUAC = C_{capital} \times \left[ \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \right] + C_{annual\_operating}$ | ₹/year | Comparing alternative lifespans |

**Where the symbols mean:**

- $C_{capital}$ = Total one-time capital investment (₹).
- $C_{annual\_saving}$ = Total annual cash inflow from labor + productivity + quality savings.
- $C_{annual\_operating}$ = Total annual cash outflow (labor, energy, maintenance).
- $NCF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$ (savings − operating cost).
- $i$ = Discount rate (cost of capital), typically 8–15% in industry.
- $n$ = Project life in years (robot useful life = typically 10–15 years).
- $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** — a key engineering economics constant.

### 2.3 The Role of the Discount Rate ($i$)

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we discount future money?** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee next year because of **inflation, opportunity cost, and risk**. The discount rate $i$ captures all three. A higher $i$ makes long-term savings look smaller, which is why robotic projects with long payback periods are *punished* by high discount rates.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Manufacturing Strategy:** Economic analysis tells the plant manager *whether* to automate, *where* to automate, and *when* to automate.
- **Vendor Selection:** Used to compare bids from different robot OEMs (ABB, KUKA, FANUC, Yaskawa).
- **Capacity Planning:** Determines if a robot cell justifies a 2-shift or 3-shift operation.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Sometimes the analysis says "do not automate — outsource the process."
- **Government Subsidies:** Many KTU-style case problems involve tax breaks or subsidies that must be added to savings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Payback Period Formula

The **Payback Period** is the simplest metric. It answers the question: *"How long until I recover my money?"*

**Step 1 — Define the Net Annual Cash Flow.**

$$
NCF = C_{annual\_saving} - C_{annual\_operating}
$$

> *Logic:* What I earn (savings) minus what I spend (operating) = what I net per year. This is the actual amount available to "pay back" the initial investment.

**Step 2 — Define Payback Period as the time required to recover the capital.**

By definition, the cumulative cash flow equals the capital investment at the payback point:

$$
C_{capital} = NCF \times PBP
$$

> *Logic:* If I save $NCF$ every year, I need $PBP$ years to save up $C_{capital}$.

**Step 3 — Solve for PBP.**

$$
PBP = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{NCF} = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{C_{annual\_saving} - C_{annual\_operating}}
$$

> *Logic:* A simple algebraic rearrangement. **Units check:** ₹ / (₹/year) = years. ✓

**Step 4 — Practical refinement (KTU-style).**

In real problems, savings grow each year due to wage inflation. The formula then becomes:

$$
PBP = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{NCF_1} \quad \text{(if savings are constant)}
$$

$$
PBP = \dfrac{\ln\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{capital} \cdot g}{NCF_1}\right)}{\ln(1+g)} \quad \text{(if savings grow at rate } g \text{ per year)}
$$

> *Logic:* When wages rise 5% per year, displaced labor savings rise 5% per year, so recovery happens faster than the simple formula suggests.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Net Present Value (NPV) Formula

The NPV accounts for the **time value of money**. It is the gold standard in KTU 14-mark problems.

**Step 1 — Recognize that future money is worth less.**

A cash flow of $NCF_t$ received $t$ years from now is equivalent to receiving $\dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ today. The factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t}$ is the **Present Value Factor (PVF)**.

**Step 2 — Sum all discounted future cash flows.**

$$
PV_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the initial capital outlay.**

The capital $C_{capital}$ is spent at $t=0$, so its present value is itself:

$$
NPV = -C_{capital} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule (KTU standard).**

$$
\text{Accept the project if } NPV > 0.
$$

> *Logic:* If NPV is positive, the project earns more than the discount rate (cost of capital). It is financially attractive. If NPV is negative, the money is better invested elsewhere.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

The CRF converts a present capital cost into an equivalent annual cost — crucial for comparing robots with different lifespans.

**Step 1 — Start with a present value $P$ that must be recovered over $n$ years at interest $i$.**

We seek an annual payment $A$ such that:

$$
P = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the right side as a finite geometric series.**

First term: $a_1 = \dfrac{A}{1+i}$, common ratio: $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$, number of terms: $n$.

**Step 3 — Apply the geometric series sum formula.**

$$
S_n = a_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} = \dfrac{A}{1+i} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$
1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i} = \dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and invert.**

$$
P = \dfrac{A}{1+i} \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i / (1+i)} = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for A.**

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}} = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**. It tells you what uniform annual payment $A$ is equivalent to the present capital $P$ over $n$ years at rate $i$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Complete Economic Analysis (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **A KTU-style 14-mark question, solved step-by-step.**

**Problem Statement:**
> A manufacturing company is evaluating a robotic welding cell. The following data is collected:
> - Robot + welding torch + fixture: **₹ 25,00,000**
> - Installation, training, safety cage: **₹ 5,00,000**
> - Displaced labor: 2 workers × **₹ 30,000/month** (loaded wage)
> - Annual maintenance: **₹ 2,00,000/year**
> - Annual energy: **₹ 1,00,000/year**
> - Scrap reduction benefit: **₹ 1,50,000/year**
> - Productivity gain (extra units): **₹ 3,00,000/year**
> - Discount rate $i = 10\%$, project life $n = 10$ years.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute total capital investment.**

$$
C_{capital} = 25{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute annual labor savings.**

$$
L_{saved} = 2 \times 30{,}000 \times 12 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total annual operating cost.**

$$
C_{annual\_operating} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total annual savings (inflows).**

$$
C_{annual\_saving} = L_{saved} + \text{Scrap} + \text{Productivity} = 7{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{annual\_saving} = 11{,}70{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Net Annual Cash Flow.**

$$
NCF = 11{,}70{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}70{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Payback Period.**

$$
PBP = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{8{,}70{,}000} = 3.45 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute ROI.**

$$
ROI = \dfrac{8{,}70{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 29\% \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute NPV (assume constant NCF for simplicity).**

The present value annuity factor for $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ is:

$$
PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} = 6.1446
$$

$$
NPV = -30{,}00{,}000 + (8{,}70{,}000 \times 6.1446)
$$

$$
NPV = -30{,}00{,}000 + 53{,}45{,}802 = +23{,}45{,}802 \text{ ₹}
$$

> **Conclusion:** $NPV > 0$, $PBP = 3.45$ years (well within the 10-year life), and $ROI = 29\%$ (excellent). **The robotic welding cell is strongly recommended for installation.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Metric

| Project Characteristic | Best Metric | Why |
|------------------------|-------------|-----|
| Short life (< 3 years), small cost | **Payback Period** | Speed of recovery matters most |
| Comparing two competing projects | **ROI** | Easy side-by-side percentage comparison |
| Multi-year, long-life, high capital | **NPV** | Properly accounts for time value |
| Finding the "break-even" discount rate | **IRR** | Useful when cost of capital is unknown |
| Robots with different useful lives | **EUAC** | Normalizes to annual basis for fair comparison |
| High uncertainty, volatile savings | **Sensitivity Analysis** | Tests "what if" scenarios |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Economic Analysis Workflow for Robot Installation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `startA`, `dataB`), no reserved keywords used as node names, all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or italics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START: ROBOTIC PROJECT PROPOSAL] --> idenB[IDENTIFY TYPE OF INSTALLATION]
    idenB --> dataC[COLLECT BASIC DATA]
    dataC --> capD[CAPITAL COST DATA]
    dataC --> opeE[OPERATING COST DATA]
    dataC --> proF[PRODUCTION DATA]
    dataC --> savG[SAVINGS AND BENEFIT DATA]
    capD --> calcH[COMPUTE NCF AND METRICS]
    opeE --> calcH
    proF --> calcH
    savG --> calcH
    calcH --> payI[COMPUTE PAYBACK PERIOD]
    calcH --> roiJ[COMPUTE ROI]
    calcH --> npvK[COMPUTE NPV AND IRR]
    calcH --> euaL[COMPUTE EUAC]
    payI --> sensM[PERFORM SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS]
    roiJ --> sensM
    npvK --> sensM
    euaL --> sensM
    sensM --> deciN{ACCEPT PROJECT?}
    deciN -->|NPV GREATER THAN ZERO AND PBP LESS THAN N| apprO[APPROVE INSTALLATION]
    deciN -->|NPV LESS THAN ZERO OR PBP TOO LONG| rejeP[REJECT OR RESTRUCTURE]
    apprO --> docQ[DOCUMENT FINAL REPORT]
    rejeP --> revR[REVISE ASSUMPTIONS AND RE ANALYZE]
    docQ --> stopS[END]
    revR --> dataC
    stopS([END])
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Four Types of Robot Installation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    typeA[TYPES OF ROBOT INSTALLATION] --> typeB[STANDALONE ROBOT]
    typeA --> typeC[ROBOTIC CELL]
    typeA --> typeD[ROBOTIC LINE]
    typeA --> typeE[MIXED MODEL CELL]
    typeB --> costB1[LOW CAPITAL COST]
    typeB --> econB2[PAYBACK 1 TO 2 YEARS]
    typeB --> appB3[SINGLE TASK REPLACEMENT]
    typeC --> costC1[MEDIUM CAPITAL COST]
    typeC --> econC2[PAYBACK 2 TO 4 YEARS]
    typeC --> appC3[MULTIPLE OPERATIONS IN ONE STATION]
    typeD --> costD1[HIGH CAPITAL COST]
    typeD --> econD2[PAYBACK 3 TO 6 YEARS]
    typeD --> appD3[FULL PRODUCTION LINE AUTOMATION]
    typeE --> costE1[VERY HIGH CAPITAL COST]
    typeE --> econE2[PAYBACK 4 TO 8 YEARS]
    typeE --> appE3[FLEXIBLE HIGH MIX LOW VOLUME]
```

---

### 4.3 Detailed Description of Each Installation Type

#### Type 1 — Standalone Robot Installation

- **Description:** A single robot performs **one specific task** (e.g., pick-and-place, single welding seam) in an otherwise manual workstation.
- **Capital Cost:** **Lowest** (typically ₹10–30 lakhs).
- **Data Required:** Simple — labor displaced, robot cost, energy, maintenance.
- **Economic Profile:** Fastest payback, lowest NPV risk.
- **KTU Exam Cue:** Often appears in 3-mark questions testing data-collection scope.

#### Type 2 — Robotic Cell Installation

- **Description:** A robot plus its peripherals (fixtures, conveyors, safety) forms an **integrated work cell** that performs a complete operation sequence (e.g., welding an entire sub-assembly).
- **Capital Cost:** **Medium** (typically ₹30 lakhs – ₹1.5 crore).
- **Data Required:** Full cell data — robot, grippers, fixtures, conveyors, safety, programming.
- **Economic Profile:** Moderate payback, strong NPV.
- **KTU Exam Cue:** Most common in 14-mark questions.

#### Type 3 — Robotic Line Installation

- **Description:** Multiple robotic cells **chained together** to automate an entire production line (e.g., automotive body shop).
- **Capital Cost:** **Highest** (several crores).
- **Data Required:** Line-wide data — synchronized cycle times, buffer storage, line balancing, downtime propagation.
- **Economic Profile:** Long payback but very high NPV at scale.
- **KTU Exam Cue:** Tests understanding of *line balancing* and *downtime cost*.

#### Type 4 — Mixed-Model / Flexible Cell Installation

- **Description:** A cell designed to handle **multiple product variants** on the same equipment.
- **Capital Cost:** **Very high** (sophisticated vision, quick-change tooling).
- **Data Required:** Changeover time, programming overhead, mixed-model scheduling.
- **Economic Profile:** Justified only at high product variety.
- **KTU Exam Cue:** Tests understanding of *flexibility premium*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the basic categories of data required to perform an economic analysis for a robot installation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The basic data required for economic analysis of a robot installation is grouped into **four categories**:
1. **Capital investment data** — robot, end-effector, peripherals, installation, training.
2. **Operating cost data** — labor, maintenance, energy, consumables, insurance.
3. **Production data** — cycle time, annual volume, uptime, OEE.
4. **Benefit data** — labor savings, productivity gain, scrap reduction, inventory savings.

> *Award 1 mark for naming each of 3 categories; 0.5 mark each for partial list of items.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Standalone and Cell type robot installations from an economic viewpoint.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Standalone | Robotic Cell |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Capital cost | Low | Medium to High |
| Payback period | 1–2 years | 2–4 years |
| Complexity of data | Simple | Detailed (peripherals, fixtures) |
| Typical application | Single task | Complete sub-assembly |
| Risk | Low | Moderate |

> *Award 1 mark for each correct comparison point (3 points × 1 mark).*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

A company is evaluating the installation of a robotic painting cell. The collected data is as follows:

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Robot + spray gun + fixture cost | ₹ 18,00,000 |
| Safety enclosure, conveyor, installation | ₹ 4,50,000 |
| Training and engineering | ₹ 1,50,000 |
| Displaced labor | 3 workers × ₹ 25,000/month |
| Annual maintenance | ₹ 1,80,000/year |
| Annual energy & consumables | ₹ 1,20,000/year |
| Scrap reduction benefit | ₹ 2,00,000/year |
| Discount rate $i$ | 12% per year |
| Project life $n$ | 8 years |

**(a)** Compute the **Payback Period** and **Return on Investment (ROI)** for the project. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** and state whether the project should be accepted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — PBP and ROI

**Step 1 — Total capital investment.**

$$
C_{capital} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual labor savings.**

$$
L_{saved} = 3 \times 25{,}000 \times 12 = 9{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Total annual operating cost.**

$$
C_{annual\_operating} = 1{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Total annual savings.**

$$
C_{annual\_saving} = 9{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Annual Cash Flow.**

$$
NCF = 11{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Payback Period.**

$$
PBP = \dfrac{24{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 7 — Return on Investment.**

$$
ROI = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\% \text{ per year}
$$

> *Marking: Step 1 = 1m, Step 2 = 1m, Step 3 = 1m, Step 4 = 1m, Step 5 = 1m, Step 6 = 1m, Step 7 = 1m.*

##### Part (b) — NPV

**Step 1 — Present Value Annuity Factor for $i = 12\%$, $n = 8$.**

$$
PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1.12)^{-8}}{0.12}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^8$:

$$
(1.12)^2 = 1.2544
$$

$$
(1.12)^4 = (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735
$$

$$
(1.12)^8 = (1.5735)^2 = 2.4760
$$

Therefore:

$$
PVAF = \dfrac{1 - \dfrac{1}{2.4760}}{0.12} = \dfrac{1 - 0.4039}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.5961}{0.12} = 4.9676
$$

**Step 2 — Present value of all future cash flows.**

$$
PV = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 4.9676 = 39{,}74{,}080 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Present Value.**

$$
NPV = -24{,}00{,}000 + 39{,}74{,}080 = +15{,}74{,}080 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since $NPV = +₹15.74$ lakhs $> 0$, and the payback of 3 years is well within the 8-year project life, **the robotic painting cell should be accepted**.

> *Marking: Step 1 = 2m, Step 2 = 2m, Step 3 = 2m, Step 4 (decision with justification) = 1m.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **four types of robot installations** and the basic data required for the economic analysis of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is considering a robotic machine-tending cell. The total capital cost is ₹ 40 lakhs. The annual savings are ₹ 14 lakhs and the annual operating cost is ₹ 4 lakhs. The useful life is 10 years and the discount rate is 10%. Compute the **EUAC** and determine if the project is acceptable using the EUAC method if the alternative (manual operation) has an EUAC of ₹ 13 lakhs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Four Types and Their Data

**Block Diagram (Text Representation for Answer Sheet):**

```
[Type of Installation]
        |
        +---> [Standalone] ----> Data: Robot cost, labor displaced, energy
        |
        +---> [Cell] ----------> Data: Robot + gripper + fixture + safety + programming
        |
        +---> [Line] ----------> Data: Multiple cells, line balancing, buffer storage, downtime
        |
        +---> [Mixed-Model] ---> Data: Changeover time, vision system, scheduling
```

**Explanation:**

- **Standalone:** Simplest data set — robot price, single-task labor savings, energy.
- **Cell:** Adds data on gripper, fixture, conveyor, safety, integration labor.
- **Line:** Adds data on cycle-time synchronization, buffer inventory, downtime cost, line balancing.
- **Mixed-Model:** Adds data on changeover cost, vision cost, scheduling software, programming overhead.

> *Marking: Diagram = 3m, Explanation of each type with data = 1m each (4m total).*

##### Part (b) — EUAC Computation

**Step 1 — Compute the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF).**

For $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$:

$$
(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937
$$

$$
CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \dfrac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.16275
$$

**Step 2 — Compute capital recovery (annualized capital cost).**

$$
A_{capital} = C_{capital} \times CRF = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16275 = 6{,}51{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute EUAC.**

$$
EUAC = A_{capital} + C_{annual\_operating} = 6{,}51{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}51{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

- Robotic cell EUAC = ₹ 10.51 lakhs/year
- Manual alternative EUAC = ₹ 13.00 lakhs/year

Since $EUAC_{robot} < EUAC_{manual}$, the **robotic cell is more cost-effective** and the project is **acceptable**.

> *Marking: Step 1 = 2m, Step 2 = 2m, Step 3 = 2m, Step 4 (decision with comparison) = 1m.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract operating cost from savings before computing PBP/ROI.** Many students write $PBP = C_{capital} / C_{annual\_saving}$, which is **wrong** — you must net out operating cost first. *(Lose 1–2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Using the wrong discount rate or project life.** A $n = 8$ year problem solved with $n = 10$ year annuity factors is a **fatal error**. Always re-read the data table. *(Lose 2 marks)*
>
> 3. **Forgetting to add installation, training, and engineering to capital cost.** Students often quote only the robot price. Capital cost = **robot + gripper + peripherals + installation + training + engineering**. *(Lose 1 mark)*
>
> 4. **Computing PBP and ROI but not stating the decision.** KTU examiners require an **explicit accept/reject statement with justification**. Just numbers = incomplete answer. *(Lose 1 mark)*
>
> 5. **In EUAC problems, omitting the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) calculation.** Skipping the CRF derivation and directly writing the EUAC value shows no work. *(Lose 1–2 marks)*
>
> 6. **Ignoring "type of installation" context.** A 14-mark question asking about a *cell* installation but answered as a *standalone* is conceptually inconsistent. Always **align the data set with the installation type** in your introduction. *(Lose 1 mark)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ Economic analysis = collecting **capital, operating, production, and savings** data.
- ☐ The **type of installation** (Standalone, Cell, Line, Mixed-Model) determines the **scope of data** and the **dominant cost components**.
- ☐ **Payback Period** = $C_{capital} / (Savings - Operating)$ — fastest screening tool.
- ☐ **ROI** = $(Savings - Operating) / C_{capital} \times 100$ — best for comparing projects.
- ☐ **NPV** uses the discount rate $i$ to convert future money to present value; accept if $NPV > 0$.
- ☐ **EUAC** = $C_{capital} \times CRF + C_{operating}$ — best when comparing alternatives with different lifespans.
- ☐ **Discount rate** $i$ accounts for inflation, opportunity cost, and risk.
- ☐ **Acceptance rule:** $PBP <$ project life AND $NPV > 0$ AND $ROI >$ minimum attractive rate of return (MARR).
- ☐ Always **subtract operating cost from savings** before using PBP/ROI formulas.
- ☐ Always include **installation, training, and engineering** in the capital cost.
- ☐ Standalone = simple, fast payback; Cell = most common exam scenario; Line = high capital, high NPV; Mixed-Model = flexibility premium.
- ☐ In KTU answers, **always end with a clear decision statement**: "The project is accepted/rejected because..."
- ☐ **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — memorize this; it appears in every EUAC problem.
- ☐ **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** = $\dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ — memorize this; it appears in every NPV problem.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Economic Analysis for Robotics: Basic Data Required - Type of Robot Installation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Data Required for the Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Data Required for the Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Data for Robotic Cell Analysis (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Cost data required for analysis refers to the systematic collection, categorization, and quantification of all financial inputs and economic parameters necessary to perform a comprehensive **economic justification** and **cost-benefit evaluation** of installing, operating, and maintaining a robotic work cell or flexible manufacturing system.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module-4 (Cell Design Economics), the cost data forms the **foundational layer** of any feasibility study. Without accurate cost data, no economic analysis (Annual Worth, Net Present Value, Payback Period, Return on Investment) can be mathematically validated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Family Car Purchase Decision"**
>
> Imagine you are planning to buy a car for your family. Before you visit the showroom, you make a *mental list* of all the costs:
> - **One-time costs:** Car price, registration, insurance
> - **Recurring costs:** Petrol, servicing, EMI, parking
>
> Similarly, before a company invests **₹50 Lakhs in a robotic cell**, the management needs to collect a similar comprehensive "cost dossier." This dossier must capture *every rupee* that will flow in and out over the life of the robot (typically 7-15 years). If you miss a cost, the entire investment decision becomes risky. **Cost Data Collection = The homework before the big purchase.**

### 1.3 Primary Categories of Cost Data

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly divides cost data into the following mandatory categories:

1. **Investment (Capital) Costs** — One-time, paid at installation
2. **Operating (Revenue) Costs** — Recurring, paid during operation
3. **Savings & Revenue Generation Data** — Money earned by the cell
4. **Productivity & Performance Metrics** — Output rates, uptime, scrap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** All cost data must be normalized to a common time base — typically **per year (annual basis)** or **per part (unit basis)** — before any economic comparison is made between manual and robotic alternatives.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used

- **Effective Robot Hourly Rate (ERHR)** in **₹/hr** or **$/hr**
- **System Availability / Uptime** as a percentage
- **Tool Life** in number of parts
- **Standard Payback Period** target of **24–36 months** (industry norm)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Cost Data Categories

#### A. Investment Costs (Capital Expenditure / CAPEX)

These are **one-time, non-recurring** costs incurred at the time of acquisition and installation.

- **Robot Equipment Cost (C_R):** Base price of the manipulator + controller + teach pendant + end-effector.
- **End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT) Cost (C_EOAT):** Grippers, weld torches, paint sprayers, vacuum cups, force/torque sensors.
- **Auxiliary Equipment Cost (C_AUX):** Indexing tables, conveyors, part feeders, positioners, safety fencing, light curtains.
- **Installation Cost (C_INS):** Foundation, cabling, plumbing (air/hydraulic), power drops, robotic cell integration.
- **Engineering & Programming Cost (C_ENG):** Process engineering, offline programming, fixture design, cycle-time optimization studies.
- **Initial Training Cost (C_TR):** Operator and maintenance staff training, manuals, certification.
- **Spare Parts Inventory (C_SP):** Recommended stock of motors, drives, encoders, PCB cards kept on shelf.
- **Building Modification (C_BLD):** Floor strengthening, ceiling height, ventilation, lighting upgrades.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Investment costs are depreciated over the project life using **Straight-Line** or **Double Declining Balance** methods. A typical project life is **L = 7 to 10 years** for KTU numerical problems.

#### B. Operating Costs (Revenue Expenditure / OPEX)

Recurring, year-on-year costs to keep the cell productive.

- **Direct Labor Cost (C_L):** Wages, salaries, benefits, overtime of operators, loaders/unloaders, inspectors.
- **Maintenance Cost (C_M):** Preventive (PM) + Corrective (CM) + Predictive maintenance.
- **Energy Cost (C_E):** Electrical power consumption of the robot, controller, auxiliaries. (Typical robot: **3–7 kW**).
- **Tooling Wear & Replacement (C_T):** Welding tips, grinding wheels, drill bits, gripper pads.
- **Consumables (C_CON):** Coolant, weld shielding gas, paint, adhesives, lubricants.
- **Indirect / Overhead Cost (C_OH):** Factory burden — rent, taxes, insurance, supervision, administration, typically **150%–200%** of direct labor.
- **Floor Space Cost (C_FS):** Area occupied × cost per square meter per year.
- **Downtime Cost (C_DT):** Lost production × profit margin per unit during breakdowns.

#### C. Savings & Revenue Generation Data

The "income side" of the equation.

- **Labor Savings (S_L):** Number of workers displaced × annual loaded wage.
- **Increased Throughput Savings (S_T):** Additional parts produced per year × contribution margin.
- **Quality Improvement Savings (S_Q):** Reduction in scrap, rework, warranty claims.
- **Material Savings (S_M):** Optimized weld deposition, reduced paint wastage, less material handling damage.
- **Inventory Savings (S_I):** Reduced WIP due to faster cycle times and just-in-time flow.

#### D. Productivity & Performance Data

- **Cycle Time (T_c)** in **seconds per part**
- **Availability (A)** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
- **Utilization (U)** = Actual production hours / Available hours
- **Annual Production Volume (Q)** = units/year
- **Product Life (L_p)** in years

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Symbol** | **Name of Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| C_TOTAL | Total Investment Cost | $$C_{TOTAL} = C_R + C_{EOAT} + C_{AUX} + C_{INS} + C_{ENG} + C_{TR} + C_{SP} + C_{BLD}$$ | ₹ or $ |
| C_ANNUAL | Annualized Operating Cost | $$C_{ANNUAL} = C_L + C_M + C_E + C_T + C_{CON} + C_{OH} + C_{FS} + C_{DT}$$ | ₹/year |
| S_ANNUAL | Total Annual Savings | $$S_{ANNUAL} = S_L + S_T + S_Q + S_M + S_I$$ | ₹/year |
| NCF | Net Annual Cash Flow | $$NCF = S_{ANNUAL} - C_{ANNUAL}$$ | ₹/year |
| C_EOAT | Effective Operating Time | $$C_{EOAT} = H_{SHIFT} \times N_{SHIFT} \times D_{WORK} \times A \times U$$ | hours/year |
| ERHR | Effective Robot Hourly Rate | $$ERHR = \dfrac{C_{TOTAL} \times CRF}{C_{EOAT}} + \dfrac{C_{ANNUAL}}{C_{EOAT}}$$ | ₹/hour |
| CRF | Capital Recovery Factor | $$CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^L}{(1+i)^L - 1}$$ | dimensionless |
| PB | Payback Period | $$PB = \dfrac{C_{TOTAL}}{NCF}$$ | years |
| ROI | Return on Investment | $$ROI = \dfrac{NCF}{C_{TOTAL}} \times 100\%$$ | % |
| NPV | Net Present Value | $$NPV = -C_{TOTAL} + \sum_{t=1}^{L} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$ | ₹ |
| C_PC | Cost Per Part (Unit Cost) | $$C_{PC} = \dfrac{C_{ANNUAL}}{Q_{ANNUAL}}$$ | ₹/part |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing ROI Decisions:** Every automotive OEM (Tata Motors, Maruti, Hyundai) uses this cost data to decide whether to automate a welding or painting cell.
- **Tender & Vendor Evaluation:** Industrial robot vendors (FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa) provide this data during turnkey project bids.
- **Government Subsidy Justification:** In India's **PLI (Production Linked Incentive)** scheme, companies must submit this cost data to claim automation subsidies.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** In-house robotic cell vs. outsourcing — cost data is the decisive factor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Robot Hourly Rate (ERHR)

The **ERHR** is the single most important cost metric that KTU asks in 14-mark problems. It represents the *true* per-hour cost of running a robot, combining both investment recovery and operating expenses.

**Starting Point:** The total annual cost of operating the robot cell must be recovered through productive hours. Mathematically, the annual cost has two components:

**(i) Annualized Investment Recovery** — We must recover the lump-sum capital cost over the project life *L*. This is done using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**.

**(ii) Annual Operating Cost** — All OPEX items summed.

The number of *productive* hours the robot actually works per year is:

$$
H_{EFF} = H_{SHIFT} \times N_{SHIFT} \times D_{WORK} \times A \times U
$$

Where:
- $H_{SHIFT}$ = hours per shift
- $N_{SHIFT}$ = number of shifts per day
- $D_{WORK}$ = working days per year
- $A$ = availability (MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR))
- $U$ = utilization (often combined with availability as **OEE factor**)

**The full derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Cost Recovery} &= C_{TOTAL} \times CRF \\[6pt]
\text{Annual Operating Cost} &= C_{ANNUAL} \\[6pt]
\text{Total Annual Cost} &= C_{TOTAL} \times CRF + C_{ANNUAL} \\[6pt]
ERHR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Annual Cost}}{\text{Effective Productive Hours}} \\[6pt]
ERHR &= \dfrac{C_{TOTAL} \times CRF}{H_{EFF}} + \dfrac{C_{ANNUAL}}{H_{EFF}} \\[6pt]
ERHR &= \underbrace{\dfrac{C_{TOTAL} \times CRF}{H_{EFF}}}_{\text{Fixed Component}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{C_{ANNUAL}}{H_{EFF}}}_{\text{Variable Component}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The first term is a **fixed cost per hour** (depreciation) and the second is a **variable cost per hour** (consumables, energy). For high-volume production ($H_{EFF}$ large), the ERHR drops, making the robot more attractive.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

The CRF converts a present value (PV) into a uniform annual series (A) over *L* years at interest rate *i*.

Let $A$ be the uniform annual payment over $L$ years. The present value of this annuity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \sum_{t=1}^{L} \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^t} \\[6pt]
PV &= A \left[ \dfrac{1}{(1+i)} + \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^L} \right] \\[6pt]
PV &= A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-L}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

The term in brackets is the **Present Worth Factor (PWF)** of an annuity. Its reciprocal is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \dfrac{1}{PWF} = \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-L}} \\[6pt]
CRF &= \dfrac{i(1+i)^L}{(1+i)^L - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Payback Period (PB)

The Payback Period is the simplest economic metric — how long until cumulative savings equal the initial investment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cumulative Savings after } t \text{ years} &= t \times NCF \\[6pt]
\text{Payback Condition:} \quad t \times NCF &= C_{TOTAL} \\[6pt]
PB &= \dfrac{C_{TOTAL}}{NCF}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often use **undiscounted** NCF in the payback formula. For a *precise* payback, use the cumulative discounted cash flow and solve iteratively. The simple formula above gives the **nominal payback**, which is what KTU 14-mark problems typically require.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Manual vs. Robotic

| **Cost Element** | **Manual Operation** | **Robotic Operation** | **Net Difference** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Labor | High (3 workers/shift) | Low (1 supervisor) | Negative saving |
| Energy | Low | High (robot + auxiliaries) | Negative saving |
| Tooling | Manual hand tools | EOAT + spares | Neutral/Slight saving |
| Quality / Scrap | High scrap rate | Low scrap rate | Positive saving |
| Throughput | Variable | Consistent & high | Positive saving |
| Floor Space | Larger | Compact cell | Positive saving |
| Initial Investment | NIL (only fixtures) | High (CAPEX) | Negative saving |
| Maintenance | Low | High (PM + CM) | Negative saving |

**Decision Rule (KTU Standard):**
- If **S_ANNUAL > C_ANNUAL** and **PB < Company Target (typically 3 years)** → **ACCEPT** the project
- Else → **REJECT** or **Re-engineer** the cell

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six categories of investment cost data required for robotic cell analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The six categories of investment (CAPEX) cost data are:
1. **Robot equipment cost (C_R)** — base price of manipulator, controller, and teach pendant.
2. **End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT) cost (C_EOAT)** — grippers, weld torches, paint sprayers.
3. **Auxiliary equipment cost (C_AUX)** — conveyors, indexing tables, part feeders, safety fences.
4. **Installation cost (C_INS)** — foundation, power, cabling, plumbing.
5. **Engineering and programming cost (C_ENG)** — process design, fixture design, simulation.
6. **Initial training cost (C_TR)** — operator and maintenance training programs.

*(Any six from Section 2.1A accepted.)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Effective Robot Hourly Rate (ERHR) and state its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Effective Robot Hourly Rate (ERHR)** is the *true per-hour* cost of operating a robotic work cell, obtained by distributing the total annualized cost (capital recovery + operating cost) over the **effective productive hours** of the robot per year.

$$
ERHR = \dfrac{C_{TOTAL} \times CRF}{H_{EFF}} + \dfrac{C_{ANNUAL}}{H_{EFF}}
$$

It is used to compare robotic cell cost with manual operation cost on an *equivalent per-hour basis*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various categories of cost data required for the economic analysis of a robotic work cell. Classify them into investment, operating, and savings data with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic welding cell requires the following cost data:
- Robot cost: **₹25,00,000**
- EOAT and fixtures: **₹4,00,000**
- Installation and engineering: **₹3,00,000**
- Annual operating cost: **₹8,00,000/year**
- Annual savings: **₹14,00,000/year**
- Effective working hours: **3,800 hr/year**
- Project life: **8 years**
- Interest rate: **12%**

Compute the (i) Capital Recovery Factor, (ii) Effective Robot Hourly Rate, (iii) Payback Period.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Cost data categories for robotic cell economic analysis:**

**1. Investment Cost Data (CAPEX)** — One-time costs at installation:
- Robot manipulator and controller
- End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT)
- Auxiliary equipment (conveyors, positioners, safety)
- Installation, foundation, and engineering
- Training and spare parts
- Building modification

**2. Operating Cost Data (OPEX)** — Recurring annual costs:
- Direct labor (operators, inspectors)
- Maintenance (preventive + corrective)
- Energy (kWh × tariff)
- Tooling replacement and consumables
- Floor space and overhead burden
- Downtime cost

**3. Savings / Revenue Data** — Income side:
- Labor cost savings (workers displaced)
- Increased throughput savings
- Quality and scrap reduction savings
- Material savings (less wastage)
- Inventory reduction savings

**4. Productivity Data** — Used to convert costs/savings to a time base:
- Cycle time, MTBF, MTTR, OEE, annual production volume.

*(Each category carries 1–2 marks; a clean tabular presentation fetches full marks.)*

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical)

**Given Data:**
- $C_R = ₹25,00,000$
- $C_{EOAT} = ₹4,00,000$
- $C_{INS+ENG} = ₹3,00,000$
- $C_{ANNUAL} = ₹8,00,000$/year
- $S_{ANNUAL} = ₹14,00,000$/year
- $H_{EFF} = 3,800$ hr/year
- $L = 8$ years
- $i = 12\% = 0.12$

**Step 1: Total Investment Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TOTAL} &= C_R + C_{EOAT} + C_{INS+ENG} \\
&= 25,00,000 + 4,00,000 + 3,00,000 \\
&= ₹32,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Net Annual Cash Flow (NCF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCF &= S_{ANNUAL} - C_{ANNUAL} \\
&= 14,00,000 - 8,00,000 \\
&= ₹6,00,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: (i) Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \dfrac{i(1+i)^L}{(1+i)^L - 1} \\
&= \dfrac{0.12 \times (1.12)^8}{(1.12)^8 - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.2544^2 = 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^8 &= 1.5735^2 = 2.4760
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{2.4760 - 1} \\
&= \dfrac{0.29712}{1.4760} \\
&= 0.20130
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{CRF \approx 0.2013}
$$

**Step 4: (ii) Effective Robot Hourly Rate (ERHR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ERHR &= \dfrac{C_{TOTAL} \times CRF}{H_{EFF}} + \dfrac{C_{ANNUAL}}{H_{EFF}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{32,00,000 \times 0.2013}{3800} + \dfrac{8,00,000}{3800} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{6,44,160}{3800} + 210.53 \\[6pt]
&= 169.52 + 210.53 \\[6pt]
&= ₹380.05 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{ERHR \approx ₹380/\text{hr}}
$$

**Step 5: (iii) Payback Period (PB)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB &= \dfrac{C_{TOTAL}}{NCF} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{32,00,000}{6,00,000} \\[6pt]
&= 5.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{PB \approx 5.33 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss #1:** Forgetting to add **EOAT and Installation** to the robot cost. Always sum **all CAPEX** items.
> - **Mark Loss #2:** Computing $(1.12)^8$ incorrectly. A common error is using $1.12 \times 8 = 8.96$ instead of compounding. **Always use the step-by-step squared expansion** as shown.
> - **Mark Loss #3:** Using $(1.12)^8 \approx 2.48$ is acceptable; using 2.0 is a mark loss.
> - **Mark Loss #4:** Reporting ERHR without **units** (₹/hr) — a 0.5 mark deduction.
> - **Mark Loss #5:** Payback of 5.33 years **exceeds the typical 3-year target**, so in a viva the student should conclude that the project is **NOT financially attractive** unless NCF can be improved.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the procedure for collecting and processing cost data for a robotic cell economic analysis. What is the role of CRF and OEE in this process?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the cost per part between a manual operation and a robotic operation using the following data:

| **Parameter** | **Manual Cell** | **Robotic Cell** |
|---|---|---|
| Operators per shift | 3 | 1 |
| Loaded wage per operator | ₹500/day | ₹700/day |
| Shifts per day | 2 | 2 |
| Working days/year | 250 | 250 |
| Annual tooling cost | ₹50,000 | ₹1,50,000 |
| Annual overhead (200% of labor) | 200% | 200% |
| Annual production volume | 80,000 | 1,20,000 |
| Annual investment cost (depreciation) | ₹0 | ₹12,00,000 |
| Annual energy cost | ₹20,000 | ₹3,00,000 |
| Annual maintenance cost | ₹5,000 | ₹1,20,000 |

Which system is more economical on a **per-part** basis?

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Flowchart of cost data collection procedure:**

```
Project Definition → Identify Cost Elements → Collect Historical Data
        ↓
Tabulate Investment (CAPEX) and Operating (OPEX) Costs
        ↓
Estimate Effective Hours = H × N × D × A × U  (OEE-based)
        ↓
Compute CRF = [i(1+i)^L] / [(1+i)^L − 1]
        ↓
Compute NCF, PB, ROI, NPV
        ↓
Decision: Accept / Reject / Re-engineer
```

**Role of CRF:** Converts the present lump-sum investment into a uniform annual series so it can be added to annual operating costs.

**Role of OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. It determines the **effective productive hours** $H_{EFF}$ over which the annualized cost is distributed. A higher OEE reduces the ERHR and improves project viability.

*(A well-labeled flowchart on the answer sheet fetches 4 marks; 3 marks for the conceptual role of CRF and OEE.)*

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Comparative Cost Analysis)

**Manual Cell — Annual Costs:**

**(i) Direct Labor:**
$$
3 \text{ operators} \times 2 \text{ shifts} \times 250 \text{ days} \times ₹500 = ₹7,50,000
$$

**(ii) Overhead (200% of labor):**
$$
2.0 \times 7,50,000 = ₹15,00,000
$$

**(iii) Tooling:** ₹50,000

**(iv) Energy:** ₹20,000

**(v) Maintenance:** ₹5,000

**(vi) Depreciation (Investment):** ₹0

**Total Annual Cost (Manual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{MANUAL} &= 7,50,000 + 15,00,000 + 50,000 + 20,000 + 5,000 + 0 \\
&= ₹23,25,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost per part (Manual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PC,MANUAL} &= \dfrac{23,25,000}{80,000} \\
&= ₹29.06 \text{ per part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Robotic Cell — Annual Costs:**

**(i) Direct Labor:**
$$
1 \text{ operator} \times 2 \text{ shifts} \times 250 \text{ days} \times ₹700 = ₹3,50,000
$$

**(ii) Overhead (200% of labor):**
$$
2.0 \times 3,50,000 = ₹7,00,000
$$

**(iii) Tooling:** ₹1,50,000

**(iv) Energy:** ₹3,00,000

**(v) Maintenance:** ₹1,20,000

**(vi) Depreciation (Investment Recovery):** ₹12,00,000

**Total Annual Cost (Robotic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ROBOT} &= 3,50,000 + 7,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 3,00,000 + 1,20,000 + 12,00,000 \\
&= ₹28,20,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost per part (Robotic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PC,ROBOT} &= \dfrac{28,20,000}{1,20,000} \\
&= ₹23.50 \text{ per part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparative Result:**

| **Metric** | **Manual** | **Robotic** | **Advantage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost per part | ₹29.06 | ₹23.50 | **Robotic (saves ₹5.56/part)** |
| Annual volume | 80,000 | 1,20,000 | **Robotic (50% higher)** |
| Annual saving | — | — | ₹(29.06 − 23.50) × 1,20,000 = **₹6,67,200/year** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss #1:** Computing labor for **only 1 shift** and forgetting to multiply by $N_{SHIFT}=2$. Always re-read the data carefully.
> - **Mark Loss #2:** Forgetting the **200% overhead** on direct labor. This is a common cost component in Indian KTU problems.
> - **Mark Loss #3:** Mixing up the *production volumes* when computing cost per part. Use the **own volume** of each system.
> - **Mark Loss #4:** Not writing the **conclusion** in words. The examiner expects a sentence like: *"Despite higher total cost, the robotic cell delivers a 19% lower cost per part and 50% higher throughput — hence it is the more economical choice."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Cost Data Required for Analysis**

- ✅ **CAPEX = One-time cost** at installation; **OPEX = Recurring annual cost**; **Savings = Income side** of the analysis.
- ✅ Total CAPEX formula: $C_{TOTAL} = C_R + C_{EOAT} + C_{AUX} + C_{INS} + C_{ENG} + C_{TR} + C_{SP} + C_{BLD}$.
- ✅ Effective productive hours: $H_{EFF} = H_{SHIFT} \times N_{SHIFT} \times D_{WORK} \times A \times U$ — **multiply all five factors**.
- ✅ **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):** $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^L}{(1+i)^L - 1}$ — always derived from first principles in the exam.
- ✅ **ERHR formula** = [Capital recovery per year + Annual OPEX] / Effective hours — measured in **₹/hr**.
- ✅ **Net Cash Flow:** $NCF = S_{ANNUAL} - C_{ANNUAL}$.
- ✅ **Payback Period:** $PB = C_{TOTAL} / NCF$ — accept if **PB < 3 years** (industry benchmark).
- ✅ **ROI = (NCF / C_TOTAL) × 100%**; **NPV** requires **discounting** each year's cash flow.
- ✅ **Cost per part** ($C_{PC} = C_{ANNUAL} / Q_{ANNUAL}$) is the **most decisive metric** for high-volume production.
- ✅ For KTU numericals: state all given data, show **all** intermediate calculations, and end with a **clear conclusion** in a sentence.
- ✅ Common cost components often missed: **overhead at 150–200% of labor**, **floor space cost**, **downtime cost**, **EOAT replacement**.
- ✅ **Real-world context:** Cost data is mandatory for any **Industry 4.0 / PLI / Smart Manufacturing** project sanction in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
