# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Payback Method (also called Payback Period Method)** is the simplest and most widely used technique of capital budgeting and economic analysis in engineering projects. It is defined as the **length of time required to recover the original cost of an investment from the net cash inflows (or net annual savings) generated by that investment**.

Mathematically, it is the period (in years, months, or production units) at which the **cumulative net cash flow equals the initial investment**. If the payback period is less than or equal to a pre-determined cutoff period (acceptable maximum), the project is accepted; otherwise, it is rejected.

The two principal variants studied in KTU 2024 are:
1. **Simple (Undiscounted) Payback Period (SPP)**
2. **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a coffee vending machine for your college canteen for **₹60,000**, and it generates a **net daily profit of ₹500** (after deducting coffee, milk, sugar, electricity, and maintenance). 

A simple question arises: **"When will I get back my ₹60,000?"**

$$\text{Days to recover} = \frac{60{,}000}{500} = 120 \text{ days} \approx 4 \text{ months}$$

That "4 months" is your **Payback Period**. If you had decided in advance that any investment must recover its cost within **6 months** to be worthwhile, the vending machine is a **good investment** because 4 < 6. 

The Payback Method is the engineering economist's way of answering this universal question: *"How quickly does my money come back to me?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Time Value of Money (TVM)**: In *Simple Payback*, cash flows are **NOT** discounted. In *Discounted Payback*, they are discounted using a cost of capital rate (usually the **MARR** — Minimum Attractive Rate of Return, often **10%–15%** in KTU problems).
> - **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)**: The lowest return an investor is willing to accept. It serves as the benchmark discount rate.
> - **Cutoff Period (N)**: A pre-set maximum allowable payback time. Typical values in industrial robotics are **3 to 5 years**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Payback Method is part of the broader topic of *Methods of Economic Analysis*, which also includes Net Present Value (NPV), Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC), Benefit-Cost Ratio, and Internal Rate of Return (IRR). However, Payback is the most intuitive and frequently tested method in KTU examinations for engineering-economics modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Payback Method

The Payback Method rests on three operational pillars:

- **Liquidity Orientation:** It measures how *quickly* the invested capital becomes liquid again, addressing the risk of long-term uncertainty.
- **Simplicity:** It requires only the initial investment and a sequence of cash flows — no complex discount rate calculations (in the simple version).
- **Risk Screening Tool:** A project that recovers its cost fast is considered less risky, even if long-term profitability is ignored.

### 2.1.1 Simple (Conventional) Payback Period — Operational Logic

**Step 1:** Identify the initial investment $I_0$ (a negative cash outflow at year 0).

**Step 2:** List the expected net annual cash inflows $CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$ for each year.

**Step 3:** Compute the **cumulative net cash flow** year by year.

**Step 4:** The payback period is the year in which cumulative cash flow first becomes **non-negative** (≥ 0).

**Step 5:** If payback occurs *exactly* within a year (not at the year-end), interpolate as:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{SPP}} = A + \frac{B}{C}}$$

where:
- $A$ = number of full years just before recovery
- $B$ = unrecovered amount at the start of year $A+1$
- $C$ = cash flow during year $A+1$

### 2.1.2 Discounted Payback Period — Operational Logic

**Step 1:** Apply the discount rate $i$ (MARR) to each cash flow:
$$DCF_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 2:** Compute cumulative **discounted** cash flows.

**Step 3:** Identify the year in which cumulative DCF ≥ 0; interpolate if needed.

**Step 4:** Compare with the cutoff period.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** Vertical bars `|` are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table structure.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | When to Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Payback (Equal Cash Flows) | $P = I_0 \div CF$ | When annual cash flow is **constant** | Years |
| 2 | Simple Payback (Unequal Cash Flows) | $P_{\text{SPP}} = A + \frac{B}{C}$ | When annual cash flow **varies** | Years |
| 3 | Discount Factor | $DF_t = \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$ | To convert future cash flow to present value | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Discounted Cash Flow | $DCF_t = CF_t \cdot DF_t$ | Discount each year's inflow at MARR | Currency |
| 5 | Discounted Payback (Interpolated) | $P_{\text{DPP}} = A + \frac{B_{\text{disc}}}{C_{\text{disc}}}$ | When DCF recovery occurs mid-year | Years |
| 6 | Decision Rule (Accept) | $P \leq N_{\text{cutoff}}$ | If payback ≤ maximum acceptable period | — |
| 7 | Decision Rule (Reject) | $P > N_{\text{cutoff}}$ | If payback > maximum acceptable period | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **robotic cell design**, the Payback Method is routinely used by manufacturing managers to:
- Justify the procurement of an industrial robot (typically ₹15 lakh to ₹1 crore) by computing how many production shifts or years are needed to recover its cost through labour savings, scrap reduction, and quality improvements.
- Compare alternative robot models (e.g., 6-axis vs. SCARA) on the basis of **speed of capital recovery** — a critical metric in small and medium enterprises (SMEs) where cash flow liquidity is paramount.
- Make go/no-go decisions on **automation upgrades** when the engineering team has limited access to full life-cycle cost data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Simple Payback with Equal Annual Cash Flow

**Problem Setup:** A firm invests $I_0$ in a robotic arm that generates an identical net cash flow $CF$ every year for $n$ years. Find the payback period $P$.

**Step 1:** Define cumulative cash flow at the end of year $k$:

$$\text{Cumulative}(k) = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{k} CF_t$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $CF_t = CF$ (constant) for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, k$:

$$\text{Cumulative}(k) = -I_0 + k \cdot CF$$

**Step 3:** Payback occurs when $\text{Cumulative}(P) = 0$:

$$-I_0 + P \cdot CF = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $P$:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{I_0}{CF}}$$

This is the most-cited formula in KTU exams for the equal-cash-flow variant.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Simple Payback with Unequal Cash Flows

**Step 1:** Build the cumulative table year by year.

**Step 2:** Identify the last year ($A$) where $\text{Cumulative}(A) < 0$ and the first year where $\text{Cumulative}(A+1) \geq 0$.

**Step 3:** The unrecovered amount at the **start** of year $A+1$ is:

$$B = \lvert \text{Cumulative}(A) \rvert = -\text{Cumulative}(A)$$

**Step 4:** The cash flow recovered during year $A+1$ is $C = CF_{A+1}$.

**Step 5:** Assuming **linear recovery** within that year, the fraction of the year needed is $B / C$:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{SPP}} = A + \frac{B}{C} = A + \frac{-\text{Cumulative}(A)}{CF_{A+1}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Discounted Payback Period

The logic is identical to the simple payback, but every cash flow is first discounted:

$$DCF_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

The discounted payback period $P_{\text{DPP}}$ satisfies:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{P_{\text{DPP}}} DCF_t = 0$$

When recovery occurs mid-year, interpolate:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{DPP}} = A + \frac{-\text{CumDCF}(A)}{DCF_{A+1}}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> A small-scale industry is considering installing a CNC robotic cell. The initial investment is **₹8,00,000**. The expected net cash inflows (savings in labour, material, and quality) are:
> - Year 1: ₹2,00,000
> - Year 2: ₹2,50,000
> - Year 3: ₹3,00,000
> - Year 4: ₹3,50,000
>
> The firm's maximum acceptable payback period is **3 years**. Determine whether the project should be accepted using (a) the Simple Payback Method and (b) the Discounted Payback Method at $i = 10\%$.

### Solution (a) — Simple Payback

**Step 1:** Tabulate cumulative cash flow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 0: } & \quad \text{Cumulative} = -8{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 1: } & \quad \text{Cumulative} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = -6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 2: } & \quad \text{Cumulative} = -6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = -3{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Year 3: } & \quad \text{Cumulative} = -3{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = -50{,}000 \\
\text{Year 4: } & \quad \text{Cumulative} = -50{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 = +3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Recovery occurs between Year 3 and Year 4.

- $A = 3$ (last negative year)
- $B = \lvert -50{,}000 \rvert = 50{,}000$
- $C = 3{,}50{,}000$

**Step 3:** Apply interpolation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{SPP}} &= 3 + \frac{50{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 3 + 0.1429 \\
&= 3.14 \text{ years} \;\; \approx \;\; 3 \text{ years and } 52 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Since $P_{\text{SPP}} = 3.14 \leq 3$? **No**, 3.14 > 3. **Project is borderline-rejected** under the simple method (strict comparison). However, if compared to a 3.5-year cutoff, it would be **accepted**.

### Solution (b) — Discounted Payback at $i = 10\%$

**Step 1:** Compute discount factors:

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1.10)^t}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \frac{1}{1.10} = 0.9091 \\
DF_2 &= \frac{1}{1.21} = 0.8264 \\
DF_3 &= \frac{1}{1.331} = 0.7513 \\
DF_4 &= \frac{1}{1.4641} = 0.6830
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute discounted cash flows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_1 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 1{,}81{,}818 \\
DCF_2 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 2{,}06{,}612 \\
DCF_3 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 2{,}25{,}394 \\
DCF_4 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 2{,}39{,}050
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute cumulative discounted cash flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 0: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -8{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 1: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}81{,}818 = -6{,}18{,}182 \\
\text{Year 2: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -6{,}18{,}182 + 2{,}06{,}612 = -4{,}11{,}570 \\
\text{Year 3: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -4{,}11{,}570 + 2{,}25{,}394 = -1{,}86{,}176 \\
\text{Year 4: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -1{,}86{,}176 + 2{,}39{,}050 = +52{,}874
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Recovery occurs between Year 3 and Year 4.

- $A = 3$
- $B_{\text{disc}} = 1{,}86{,}176$
- $C_{\text{disc}} = 2{,}39{,}050$

**Step 5:** Interpolate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{DPP}} &= 3 + \frac{1{,}86{,}176}{2{,}39{,}050} \\
&= 3 + 0.779 \\
&= 3.78 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Since $P_{\text{DPP}} = 3.78 > 3$, the project is **rejected** under the discounted method. The time value of money makes the project less attractive than the simple method suggests.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Observation:** The Discounted Payback Period (3.78 years) is always **longer than or equal to** the Simple Payback Period (3.14 years), because discounting reduces the present value of future cash flows.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision-Flow Block Diagram for Payback Method

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire logical flow of a Payback analysis — from initial investment to final accept/reject decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Capital Investment Decision] --> A1[Identify Initial Investment I0]
    A1 --> A2[List Annual Net Cash Flows CF1, CF2, ..., CFn]
    A2 --> A3{Choose Method}
    A3 -- Simple Payback --> B1[Compute Cumulative Undiscounted Cash Flow]
    A3 -- Discounted Payback --> B2[Apply MARR i to Get DCFt = CFt / 1+i to the power t]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow]
    B1 --> C1{Cumulative >= 0?}
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- No --> C2[Continue to Next Year]
    C2 --> C1
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Identify Last Negative Year A and Cash Flow C in Year A+1]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Payback P = A + Unrecovered Amount / C]
    D2 --> E1{Compare P with Cutoff Period N}
    E1 -- P <= N --> F1[ACCEPT Project]
    E1 -- P > N --> F2[REJECT Project]
    F1 --> G1[End: Proceed with Implementation]
    F2 --> G2[End: Abandon or Redesign]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Simple vs. Discounted Payback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Simple Payback Pipeline]
        S1[I0] --> S2[CFt]
        S2 --> S3[Cumulative Sum]
        S3 --> S4[Find Recovery Point]
        S4 --> S5[Interpolate P_SPP]
    end
    subgraph DP[Discounted Payback Pipeline]
        D1[I0] --> D2[CFt]
        D2 --> D3[Apply Discount Factor 1 over 1+i to the power t]
        D3 --> D4[Cumulative DCF Sum]
        D4 --> D5[Find Recovery Point]
        D5 --> D6[Interpolate P_DPP]
    end
    SP --> CMP[Compare P_SPP vs Cutoff]
    DP --> CMP
    CMP --> OUT[Decision Accept or Reject]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Payback Analysis in a Robotic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Robot System Proposal] --> M1[Market and Technical Feasibility]
    M1 --> M2[Estimate Initial Investment: Robot + End Effector + Installation]
    M2 --> M3[Project Annual Cash Inflows: Labour Savings, Quality Gains, Throughput Increase]
    M3 --> M4[Construct Cash Flow Table Year 0 to Year n]
    M4 --> M5{Payback Method Selected}
    M5 -- Simple --> M6[Compute Undiscounted Cumulative Cash Flow]
    M5 -- Discounted --> M7[Apply MARR and Compute Discounted Cumulative Cash Flow]
    M6 --> M8[Determine Payback Period P]
    M7 --> M8
    M8 --> M9{Is P less than or equal to Cutoff N}
    M9 -- Yes --> M10[ACCEPT and Procure Robot]
    M9 -- No --> M11[REJECT or Re negotiate ROI]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the term *Payback Period*. State its main limitation as a method of investment appraisal.

**Model Answer:**
The Payback Period is the length of time required for the cumulative net cash inflows from an investment to equal the original cost of the investment. Mathematically, it is the smallest integer $P$ such that:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{P} CF_t \geq I_0$$

**Main Limitation:** The Simple Payback Method ignores the **time value of money** and disregards all cash flows that occur *after* the payback period. Hence, a project with high long-term returns may be rejected simply because it does not recover its cost quickly, while a short-term profitable but ultimately inferior project may be wrongly selected.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for limitation with explanation)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between *Simple Payback Period* and *Discounted Payback Period*. Why is DPP always greater than or equal to SPP?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Simple Payback (SPP) | Discounted Payback (DPP) |
|---|---|---|
| Discounting | Not applied | Cash flows discounted at MARR |
| Time Value of Money | Ignored | Accounted for |
| Result | Shorter period | Longer or equal period |

**Why DPP ≥ SPP:** Discounting reduces the present value of future cash flows. Since smaller PVs take *longer* to accumulate to the value of $I_0$, the recovery point shifts to a later year in the discounted scenario, ensuring $P_{\text{DPP}} \geq P_{\text{SPP}}$.

*(1 mark for distinction table, 2 marks for explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, a *Module Internal Choice* is provided. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4]`

A manufacturing firm is evaluating the purchase of an automated welding robot.

**Data given:**
- Initial investment: **₹12,00,000**
- Estimated net cash inflows:
  - Year 1: ₹3,00,000
  - Year 2: ₹4,00,000
  - Year 3: ₹3,50,000
  - Year 4: ₹4,50,000
- MARR = **12%** per annum
- Maximum acceptable payback period = **3 years**

**(a)** Compute the **Simple Payback Period** and state whether the project is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Discounted Payback Period** at $i = 12\%$. Compare with the cutoff and comment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Cumulative undiscounted cash flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 0: } & \quad \text{CumCF} = -12{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 1: } & \quad \text{CumCF} = -12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = -9{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 2: } & \quad \text{CumCF} = -9{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = -5{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 3: } & \quad \text{CumCF} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 = -1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Year 4: } & \quad \text{CumCF} = -1{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = +3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Recovery occurs between Year 3 and Year 4. With $A = 3$, $B = 1{,}50{,}000$, $C = 4{,}50{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{SPP}} &= 3 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 3 + 0.333 \\
&= 3.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $P_{\text{SPP}} = 3.33 > 3$, so the project is **rejected** under the simple method.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Discount factors at $i = 12\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \frac{1}{1.12} = 0.8929 \\
DF_2 &= \frac{1}{1.12^2} = 0.7972 \\
DF_3 &= \frac{1}{1.12^3} = 0.7118 \\
DF_4 &= \frac{1}{1.12^4} = 0.6355
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Discounted cash flows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 2{,}67{,}857 \\
DCF_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 3{,}18{,}878 \\
DCF_3 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 2{,}49{,}123 \\
DCF_4 &= 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6355 = 2{,}85{,}975
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Cumulative discounted cash flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 0: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -12{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 1: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -9{,}32{,}143 \\
\text{Year 2: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -6{,}13{,}265 \\
\text{Year 3: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -3{,}64{,}142 \\
\text{Year 4: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -78{,}167
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Even after Year 4, the cumulative DCF is still negative (-₹78,167). The project **never recovers** its cost in present-value terms within the 4-year horizon. Hence:

$$P_{\text{DPP}} > 4 \text{ years} \implies \text{Project is REJECTED}$$

**Comparison:** The DPP is even more unfavourable than the SPP, confirming the project's poor viability. The time value of money erodes the value of future cash inflows substantially at 12% MARR.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4]`

A robotic material-handling system costs **₹6,00,000** to install. It is expected to generate constant net annual savings of **₹1,50,000** over a 5-year life. The firm's MARR is **15%** and the maximum acceptable payback period is **4 years**.

**(a)** Determine the **Simple Payback Period** and comment on acceptability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the annual cash flows are *unequal* at ₹1,00,000, ₹1,50,000, ₹2,00,000, ₹2,50,000, ₹3,00,000 for Years 1 to 5 respectively, calculate the **Discounted Payback Period** at 15% and state your recommendation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Using the equal-cash-flow formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{SPP}} &= \frac{I_0}{CF} \\
&= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 4.0 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $P_{\text{SPP}} = 4.0 \leq 4.0$. The project is on the boundary. By strict comparison, $P \leq N$ is satisfied, so the project is **accepted** (boundary case). If the cutoff were exclusive (strict $< 4$), it would be borderline-rejected.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Discount factors at 15%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= 0.8696 \\
DF_2 &= 0.7561 \\
DF_3 &= 0.6575 \\
DF_4 &= 0.5718 \\
DF_5 &= 0.4972
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Discounted cash flows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_1 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8696 = 86{,}960 \\
DCF_2 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7561 = 1{,}13{,}415 \\
DCF_3 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6575 = 1{,}31{,}500 \\
DCF_4 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5718 = 1{,}42{,}950 \\
DCF_5 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4972 = 1{,}49{,}160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Cumulative discounted cash flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 0: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Year 1: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -5{,}13{,}040 \\
\text{Year 2: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -3{,}99{,}625 \\
\text{Year 3: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -2{,}68{,}125 \\
\text{Year 4: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = -1{,}25{,}175 \\
\text{Year 5: } & \quad \text{CumDCF} = +23{,}985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Recovery occurs between Year 4 and Year 5. With $A = 4$, $B = 1{,}25{,}175$, $C = 1{,}49{,}160$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{DPP}} &= 4 + \frac{1{,}25{,}175}{1{,}49{,}160} \\
&= 4 + 0.839 \\
&= 4.84 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** $P_{\text{DPP}} = 4.84 > 4$ years. The project is **rejected** under the discounted payback method. While it would have been accepted under the simple method (where $P_{\text{SPP}} \approx 2.86$ years using unequal flows), the time value of money renders it unacceptable at 15% MARR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error on $B$:** Students often forget to take the *absolute value* of the negative cumulative cash flow when computing $B$. Use $B = \lvert \text{Cumulative}(A) \rvert$.
> 2. **Confusing $A$ and $A+1$:** $A$ is the *last year* where cumulative is still negative, *not* the year of recovery.
> 3. **Mixing simple and discounted cash flows:** In the DPP, *only* discounted cash flows are summed — never mix raw CF with DCF in the same cumulative column.
> 4. **Decision rule reversal:** A project is **accepted** if $P \leq N_{\text{cutoff}}$; **rejected** if $P > N_{\text{cutoff}}$. A frequent slip is inverting this.
> 5. **Forgetting to interpolate** when the recovery year is not an integer — KTU examiners *always* expect the fractional payback.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition (must memorize):** Payback Period = time to recover the initial investment from cumulative net cash inflows.
- **Two flavours:** Simple (no discount) and Discounted (uses MARR via $\frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$).
- **Equal-CF formula:** $P = I_0 / CF$ — fastest to compute.
- **Unequal-CF formula:** $P = A + B/C$ where $A$ = last negative year, $B$ = unrecovered amount, $C$ = next year's cash flow.
- **Discounted formula:** Same as unequal-CF but applied to $DCF_t = CF_t \cdot (1+i)^{-t}$.
- **Decision rule:** Accept iff $P \leq N_{\text{cutoff}}$; reject otherwise.
- **Always true:** $P_{\text{DPP}} \geq P_{\text{SPP}}$ because discounting shrinks future values.
- **Key limitation:** Ignores cash flows *after* payback and ignores time value of money (in the simple version).
- **Practical robotics use:** Justifies procurement of automation by tying it to a tangible "months/years to break even" metric.
- **Examiner's red flags:** Watch out for sign errors, missing interpolation, and misapplied decision rules.
- **Numerical precision:** Always carry at least 4 significant figures during intermediate steps; round only the final answer to 2 decimal places.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) Method** is a fundamental engineering economic analysis technique used in the **economic justification of robotic cells, FMS, and capital equipment selection**. It converts all cash flows — whether they are one-time initial investments, periodic costs, or salvage values occurring at irregular intervals — into a single, equivalent uniform annual series of payments spread evenly over the entire planning/analysis horizon (n years) at a specified Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR), denoted as **i%**.

In the context of **PCRUT402 – Robotic Manipulators and Cell Design (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the EUAC method is the prescribed technique in Module 4 for:
- Comparing **alternative robotic workcells** with differing purchase costs and different useful lives.
- Selecting between **replacement vs. overhauling** of an existing manipulator.
- Evaluating the **economic feasibility of automation projects** when the cash flows are irregular.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** EUAC is the annualized cost of owning and operating an asset, computed by distributing the capitalized cost (Present Worth) uniformly over its useful life at the cost of capital. It is also called the **Annual Worth (AW)** or **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you bought a **car for ₹10,00,000**. Instead of thinking of it as one huge lump sum, your accountant asks: *"What is the yearly cost of owning this car if I spread it across all the years I will use it?"* The answer is the EUAC.

**Real-World Robotic Cell Analogy:**
Suppose a KTU engineering firm must choose between two robotic cells for a CNC machining operation:
- **Cell A (ABB IRB 1200):** Costs ₹25,00,000, lasts 8 years, annual maintenance ₹2,00,000.
- **Cell B (KUKA KR 16):** Costs ₹18,00,000, lasts 5 years, annual maintenance ₹1,50,000.

The two robots have **different lifespans**, so you cannot directly compare their purchase prices. EUAC solves this by saying: *"Convert each robot's TOTAL cost-of-ownership into a flat yearly amount."* The robot with the **lowest EUAC wins** — that is the cheaper option per year of service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for First-Time Learners:** EUAC "smooths out" the jagged, irregular cash flow of a capital investment into a flat, uniform annual stream — making it apples-to-apples comparable with annual operating costs.

## 1.3 Core Symbols and Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| **EUAC / AW** | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | ₹ / year |
| **P** | Present Worth (Capitalized First Cost) | ₹ |
| **F** | Future Worth (Salvage Value) | ₹ |
| **A** | Uniform Annual Amount | ₹ / year |
| **i** | MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) | % per year |
| **n** | Analysis period / Useful life | years |
| **S** | Salvage Value at end of life | ₹ |
| **CR** | Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i\%, n)$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** in KTU problems is usually given as **i = 10%, 12%, or 15%** unless stated otherwise. Always treat it as a fixed input from the problem statement.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Economic Logic – Why EUAC Works

The EUAC method is mathematically rooted in the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle. Three forces drive its computation:

1. **Capital Recovery (A/P, i%, n):** Converts the present purchase price P into an equivalent annual installment that, if paid every year for n years at interest i, would exactly pay off P.
2. **Sinking Fund Depreciation (A/F, i%, n):** Converts the salvage value S (received at year n) into an equivalent annual receipt — it *reduces* the EUAC.
3. **Annual Operating Cost (A):** Direct annual expenses (maintenance, energy, programming) that are already uniform — they are simply added.

**The Logic Chain (in 4 steps):**

- **Step 1 →** Capitalize all non-uniform cash flows to their **Present Worth (P)** at year 0.
- **Step 2 →** Apply the **Capital Recovery Factor** to convert P into a uniform annual cost.
- **Step 3 →** Subtract the annualized **Salvage Value** contribution using the Sinking Fund Factor.
- **Step 4 →** Add the **Annual Operating & Maintenance (O&M) Cost** to obtain EUAC.

> [!NOTE]
> The Decision Rule is unambiguous: **Select the alternative with the MINIMUM EUAC.** This is equivalent to selecting the alternative with maximum Present Worth, but EUAC is preferred when **annual operating budgets** drive the decision — a common scenario in robotic cell operations.

## 2.2 Assumptions of the EUAC Method (Frequently Asked in KTU)

1. The **MARR (i)** is known, constant, and positive.
2. Cash flows are estimated with reasonable accuracy over the planning horizon.
3. All alternatives are **compared over the same analysis period** (using the LCM of lives if lifespans differ).
4. Salvage value is reinvested at the MARR.
5. The service rendered by all alternatives is equivalent (i.e., both robots perform the same task to the same quality standard).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mandatory for Board Exam)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Where to Use |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **EUAC from Present Worth** | $EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n)$ | When only initial cost P is given |
| 2 | **EUAC with Salvage Value** | $EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i\%, n)$ | When salvage S is recovered at year n |
| 3 | **EUAC with Annual Cost A** | $EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i\%, n) + A_{O\&M}$ | Most common KTU form |
| 4 | **EUAC from Future Worth** | $EUAC = F \cdot (A/F, i\%, n)$ | When total future cost is known |
| 5 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $(A/P, i\%, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Converts P → A |
| 6 | **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** | $(A/F, i\%, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Converts F → A |
| 7 | **Capital Recovery (Alternative Form)** | $(A/P, i\%, n) = (A/F, i\%, n) + i$ | Quick mental math trick |
| 8 | **EUAC Decision Rule** | Choose alt with $\min(EUAC)$ | Final answer for KTU choice questions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Trick:** The relationship $(A/P, i\%, n) = (A/F, i\%, n) + i$ is your fastest computational shortcut. Memorize it — you can avoid recalculating the full CRF polynomial on the exam.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotic Cell Design

The EUAC method is the **gold standard in industrial automation ROI analysis** because:
- **Annual budgeting:** Plant managers think in terms of yearly operating cost, not lump-sum capital outlay. EUAC gives a budget-friendly figure.
- **Unequal service lives:** Robots, CNC machines, and conveyors have lifespans of 5, 8, 10, or 15 years. EUAC normalizes them.
- **Replacement studies:** It tells you whether to keep an old manipulator (low capital, high maintenance) or buy a new one (high capital, low maintenance).
- **CAPEX vs. OPEX trade-off:** EUAC balances the upfront capital expenditure (CAPEX) against annual operating expenditure (OPEX).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i%, n)

**Starting point:** You invest a present amount P. You wish to withdraw an equal amount A at the end of each of n years, such that the account balance becomes exactly **zero** at the end of year n, given an interest rate i.

**Step 1:** The future value of P after n years (compounded annually):
$$F_1 = P \cdot (1+i)^{n}$$

**Step 2:** The future value of the uniform annual withdrawals A (ordinary annuity future value):
$$F_2 = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

**Step 3:** At the end of year n, the account must be empty, so $F_1 = F_2$:
$$P \cdot (1+i)^{n} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the ratio A/P:
$$\frac{A}{P} = \frac{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, by definition:
$$(A/P, i\%, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

This is the **Capital Recovery Factor** — the cornerstone of EUAC computation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Sinking Fund Factor (A/F, i%, n)

**Step 1:** The future value of a uniform series A deposited at the end of each year for n years is:
$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for A/F:
$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 3:** By definition:
$$(A/F, i\%, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Master EUAC Equation

Consider a robotic manipulator with:
- Initial purchase cost = P (at year 0)
- Salvage value = S (at year n)
- Annual operating cost = $A_{O\&M}$ (years 1 through n)

**Step 1:** Annualize the initial cost P using the Capital Recovery Factor:
$$\text{Annualized Capital Cost} = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n)$$

**Step 2:** Annualize the salvage value S using the Sinking Fund Factor:
$$\text{Annualized Salvage Recovery} = S \cdot (A/F, i\%, n)$$

**Step 3:** The annual operating cost $A_{O\&M}$ is already uniform — add it directly.

**Step 4:** Combine all three components:
$$\boxed{EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i\%, n) + A_{O\&M}}$$

**Step 5 (Verification Check):** When S = 0 and $A_{O\&M}$ = 0, the equation reduces to $EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n)$, which is the textbook annual cost of recovering a capital investment — physically meaningful and consistent with economic theory.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement (PCRUT402 / Module 4):**
> A manufacturing firm must select between two robotic cells for a welding application. **MARR = 12% per annum.** Determine the economically superior alternative using the EUAC method.

| Parameter | Robot A (FANUC ARC Mate 100iD) | Robot B (Yaskawa Motoman MH12) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| First Cost (P) | ₹20,00,000 | ₹15,00,000 |
| Useful Life (n) | 8 years | 5 years |
| Salvage Value (S) | ₹3,00,000 | ₹2,00,000 |
| Annual O&M Cost | ₹2,50,000 | ₹3,00,000 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step (Robot A)

**Step 1:** Compute the Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, 12\%, 8)$:
$$(A/P, 12\%, 8) = \frac{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^{8}}{(1.12)^{8} - 1}$$

First, $(1.12)^{8}$:
$$(1.12)^{2} = 1.2544$$
$$(1.12)^{4} = 1.2544^{2} = 1.5735$$
$$(1.12)^{8} = 1.5735^{2} = 2.4760$$

Substituting:
$$(A/P, 12\%, 8) = \frac{0.12 \cdot 2.4760}{2.4760 - 1} = \frac{0.29712}{1.4760} = 0.20130$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, 12\%, 8)$:
$$(A/F, 12\%, 8) = \frac{0.12}{2.4760 - 1} = \frac{0.12}{1.4760} = 0.08130$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the EUAC master equation:
$$EUAC_A = 20{,}00{,}000 \cdot (0.20130) - 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot (0.08130) + 2{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each term:
$$20{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.20130 = 4{,}02{,}600 \text{ ₹/year}$$
$$3{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.08130 = 24{,}390 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5:** Final EUAC for Robot A:
$$EUAC_A = 4{,}02{,}600 - 24{,}390 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 6{,}28{,}210 \text{ ₹/year}$$

### Solution — Step-by-Step (Robot B)

**Step 1:** Compute $(A/P, 12\%, 5)$:
$$(1.12)^{5} = 1.7623$$
$$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12 \cdot 1.7623}{1.7623 - 1} = \frac{0.21148}{0.7623} = 0.27741$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(A/F, 12\%, 5)$:
$$(A/F, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12}{0.7623} = 0.15741$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into EUAC equation:
$$EUAC_B = 15{,}00{,}000 \cdot (0.27741) - 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot (0.15741) + 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each term:
$$15{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.27741 = 4{,}16{,}115 \text{ ₹/year}$$
$$2{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.15741 = 31{,}482 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5:** Final EUAC for Robot B:
$$EUAC_B = 4{,}16{,}115 - 31{,}482 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}84{,}633 \text{ ₹/year}$$

### Decision and Conclusion

$$\boxed{EUAC_A = ₹6,28,210/\text{year} \quad < \quad EUAC_B = ₹6,84,633/\text{year}}$$

Since Robot A has the **lower EUAC**, select **Robot A (FANUC ARC Mate 100iD)**. The annual savings is:
$$\Delta EUAC = 6{,}84{,}633 - 6{,}28{,}210 = ₹56{,}423 \text{ per year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **Robot A is more expensive to buy (₹20 Lakh vs ₹15 Lakh) and lasts longer (8 vs 5 years)**, yet it still wins because its annualized capital cost is lower and its annual maintenance is ₹50,000 cheaper — exactly the kind of CAPEX-vs-OPEX insight EUAC is designed to surface.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EUAC Computational Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Robotic Cell Economic Analysis] --> B[Identify Cash Flow Components]
    B --> C[Present Cost P at Year 0]
    B --> D[Salvage Value S at Year n]
    B --> E[Annual O and M Cost per year]
    C --> F[Apply Capital Recovery Factor A by P i n]
    D --> G[Apply Sinking Fund Factor A by F i n]
    E --> H[Use Annual Cost Directly]
    F --> I[Annualized Capital Recovery in Rupees per year]
    G --> J[Annualized Salvage Recovery in Rupees per year]
    H --> K[Annual O and M Expense in Rupees per year]
    I --> L[Compute Net EUAC = I minus J plus K]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Compare EUAC across Alternatives}
    M --> N[Select Alternative with Minimum EUAC]
    M --> O[Reject Alternative with Higher EUAC]
    N --> P[End: Economic Decision Documented]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic for Cell Selection (Comparison Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alternative 1: Robot A Data] --> C[Compute EUAC A]
    B[Alternative 2: Robot B Data] --> D[Compute EUAC B]
    C --> E{EUAC A less than EUAC B}
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes| F[Select Robot A]
    E -->|No| G[Select Robot B]
    F --> H[Final Recommendation]
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline Schematic (Mermaid Gantt-Style Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Y0[Year 0: Initial Investment P minus 20 Lakh]:::cost
    Y1[Year 1: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y2[Year 2: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y3[Year 3: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y4[Year 4: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y5[Year 5: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y6[Year 6: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y7[Year 7: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh]:::cost
    Y8[Year 8: O and M minus 2.5 Lakh + Salvage 3 Lakh]:::benefit
    Y0 --> Y1 --> Y2 --> Y3 --> Y4 --> Y5 --> Y6 --> Y7 --> Y8
    classDef cost fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef benefit fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#008000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The downward arrows represent the **time axis**. Red boxes are **costs** (outflows), and the green box represents a **net benefit year** containing both the final year O&M and the salvage recovery. The EUAC method mathematically "averages" this whole staircase into a single flat annual amount.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define the Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) method. State its decision rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) Method** is an engineering economic analysis technique that converts all cash flows associated with a capital investment — initial cost, salvage value, and annual operating expenses — into a single, uniform annual cost distributed evenly over the asset's useful life, evaluated at a specified Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR), denoted as **i%**.

**Master Formula:**
$$EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i\%, n) + A_{O\&M}$$

**Decision Rule:** Select the alternative with the **minimum EUAC**, since it represents the lowest equivalent annual cost of ownership and operation. *(Full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for decision rule.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Distinguish between the Capital Recovery Factor and the Sinking Fund Factor. Why is the CRF greater than the SFF for any given i and n?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i\%, n)$** converts a **Present Worth P** into an equivalent **uniform annual amount A** over n years. It is used to annualize initial investments.
- The **Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i\%, n)$** converts a **Future Worth F** (typically the salvage value) into an equivalent uniform annual amount. It is used to annualize terminal cash flows.
- The CRF is always greater than the SFF by exactly the interest rate **i** (i.e., $(A/P, i\%, n) = (A/F, i\%, n) + i$). This is because CRF must recover **both the principal and the interest**, while SFF recovers **only the principal** through periodic deposits that accumulate with interest. *(Full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** In Part B, the student is given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each question carries 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]*

**A (a) [7 marks]:** A company is evaluating two automated material-handling systems for a robotic cell. System X costs ₹30,00,000 with a useful life of 10 years and annual operating cost of ₹4,00,000. System Y costs ₹22,00,000 with a useful life of 7 years and annual operating cost of ₹5,50,000. Both have negligible salvage value. Using the EUAC method at **MARR = 10%**, determine which system is economically superior.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For System X, compute $(A/P, 10\%, 10)$:
$$(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$$
$$(A/P, 10\%, 10) = \frac{0.10 \cdot 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \frac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.16275$$

**Step 2:** Compute $EUAC_X$ (S = 0):
$$EUAC_X = 30{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.16275 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}88{,}250 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}88{,}250 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 3:** For System Y, compute $(A/P, 10\%, 7)$:
$$(1.10)^{7} = 1.9487$$
$$(A/P, 10\%, 7) = \frac{0.10 \cdot 1.9487}{1.9487 - 1} = \frac{0.19487}{0.9487} = 0.20541$$

**Step 4:** Compute $EUAC_Y$ (S = 0):
$$EUAC_Y = 22{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.20541 + 5{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}51{,}902 + 5{,}50{,}000 = 10{,}01{,}902 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5:** Comparison and Decision:
$$EUAC_X = ₹8,88,250/\text{year} \quad < \quad EUAC_Y = ₹10,01,902/\text{year}$$

**Select System X.** The annual savings is ₹1,13,652 per year. *(7 marks: 2 for factors, 2 for EUAC computations, 1 for comparison, 1 for decision, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

**A (b) [7 marks]:** A robotic welder has a first cost of ₹12,00,000, a salvage value of ₹2,00,000 after 6 years, and an annual maintenance cost of ₹80,000. If the MARR is **15%**, calculate the EUAC and interpret the result.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $(A/P, 15\%, 6)$:
$$(1.15)^{6} = 2.3131$$
$$(A/P, 15\%, 6) = \frac{0.15 \cdot 2.3131}{2.3131 - 1} = \frac{0.34697}{1.3131} = 0.26424$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(A/F, 15\%, 6)$:
$$(A/F, 15\%, 6) = \frac{0.15}{2.3131 - 1} = \frac{0.15}{1.3131} = 0.11424$$

**Step 3:** Apply EUAC master formula:
$$EUAC = 12{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.26424 - 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.11424 + 80{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:
$$EUAC = 3{,}17{,}088 - 22{,}848 + 80{,}000 = 3{,}74{,}240 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — The robotic welder costs the company an **equivalent uniform amount of ₹3,74,240 every year** for 6 years to own and operate. This figure can be directly compared with the EUAC of any alternative welder or manual process to make an apples-to-apples decision. *(7 marks: 2 for factors, 3 for computation, 1 for units, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice for ESE]*

**B (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **assumptions and limitations** of the EUAC method when applied to robotic cell investment decisions.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Constant MARR Assumption (1 mark):** The EUAC method assumes the MARR remains fixed over the entire n-year horizon. In practice, inflation and capital market volatility can alter the effective discount rate.

2. **Accurate Cash Flow Estimation (1 mark):** The method requires precise forecasts of operating costs, energy costs, and maintenance schedules. In robotic cell design, downtime, reprogramming costs, and tooling changes introduce uncertainty.

3. **Equivalent Service Assumption (1 mark):** All alternatives must produce the same output (e.g., welds per hour, parts per shift). If Robot A is faster than Robot B, EUAC alone is insufficient — must be combined with rate-of-return analysis.

4. **Independent Alternatives (1 mark):** Alternatives must be **mutually exclusive** and **independent of project life cycles**. Reinvestment of salvage value is assumed at the MARR.

5. **Neglect of Intangible Benefits (1 mark):** EUAC captures only quantifiable monetary costs. It ignores **worker safety improvements, ergonomic gains, brand reputation, and quality consistency** — all important in robotic cell justification.

6. **Static Technology Assumption (1 mark):** The method assumes the robot's productivity, energy profile, and maintenance need remain stable. In reality, AI-driven robots improve over time while aging robots degrade.

7. **Single-Criterion Limitation (1 mark):** EUAC is purely economic. Strategic factors (supplier reliability, geopolitical supply chain risk, training infrastructure) are not captured — these should be evaluated using **multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA)** in conjunction with EUAC.

---

**B (b) [7 marks]:** Two competing robotic palletizing cells are being considered:
- **Cell P:** First cost ₹18,00,000, life 7 years, salvage ₹1,50,000, annual operating cost ₹2,00,000.
- **Cell Q:** First cost ₹14,00,000, life 5 years, salvage ₹1,00,000, annual operating cost ₹2,75,000.

The MARR is **12%**. Use the **Least Common Multiple (LCM) of lives** approach to compare the alternatives. Which cell should be selected?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** LCM of 7 and 5 = **35 years**. Assume both cells are replaced with identical duplicates at the end of their respective lives, repeating cash flows over 35 years.

**Step 2:** Compute the EUAC factors at i = 12%:

| Factor | Cell P (n=7) | Cell Q (n=5) |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| $(1.12)^n$ | 2.2107 | 1.7623 |
| $(A/P, 12\%, n)$ | $\frac{0.12 \cdot 2.2107}{1.2107} = 0.21912$ | $\frac{0.12 \cdot 1.7623}{0.7623} = 0.27741$ |
| $(A/F, 12\%, n)$ | $\frac{0.12}{1.2107} = 0.09912$ | $\frac{0.12}{0.7623} = 0.15741$ |

**Step 3:** Compute EUAC for Cell P:
$$EUAC_P = 18{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.21912 - 1{,}50{,}000 \cdot 0.09912 + 2{,}00{,}000$$
$$EUAC_P = 3{,}94{,}416 - 14{,}868 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}79{,}548 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 4:** Compute EUAC for Cell Q:
$$EUAC_Q = 14{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.27741 - 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.15741 + 2{,}75{,}000$$
$$EUAC_Q = 3{,}88{,}374 - 15{,}741 + 2{,}75{,}000 = 6{,}27{,}633 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5:** Decision:
$$EUAC_P = ₹5,79,548/\text{year} \quad < \quad EUAC_Q = ₹6,27,633/\text{year}$$

**Select Cell P.** Annual savings = ₹48,085. *(7 marks: 2 for LCM logic, 2 for factor computation, 2 for EUAC, 1 for decision.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error on Salvage Value:** Students often ADD the salvage value instead of SUBTRACTING it. The salvage S *reduces* the annual cost burden — it is an inflow, not an outflow.
> 2. **Forgetting the Annual O&M Cost:** Many students compute only the annualized capital cost and ignore recurring annual expenses. Always check whether the problem mentions yearly operating/maintenance costs.
> 3. **Wrong Factor Selection:** Using $(P/A, i\%, n)$ (Present Worth Factor) instead of $(A/P, i\%, n)$ (Capital Recovery Factor) is a recurring error. Remember: **A in numerator means the unknown is the annual amount.**
> 4. **Ignoring LCM When Lives Differ:** If the problem gives two robots with different useful lives and does **not** specify a common analysis period, the LCM of lives (or a fixed study period) **must be invoked**. Directly comparing EUAC of n=5 vs n=8 at the same MARR is technically valid only if the LCM is used or a fixed study horizon is defined.
> 5. **Rounding Error:** Round $(1+i)^n$ to **at least 4 decimal places** before computing factors. Premature rounding to 2 decimals is the #1 cause of incorrect EUAC values in board exams.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Bullet-Point Summary — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- **EUAC** = **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost** — annualizes ALL cash flows over the asset life at MARR.
- **Master Equation:** $EUAC = P(A/P, i\%, n) - S(A/F, i\%, n) + A_{O\&M}$
- **Capital Recovery Factor:** $(A/P, i\%, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — converts P to A.
- **Sinking Fund Factor:** $(A/F, i\%, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — converts F to A.
- **Speed Trick:** $(A/P, i\%, n) = (A/F, i\%, n) + i$ — saves calculation time in exams.
- **Decision Rule:** Choose the alternative with the **minimum EUAC**.
- **For Unequal Lives:** Use the **LCM of lives** or a fixed study period before applying EUAC.
- **Sign Convention:** P is a cost (+), S is a benefit (−), $A_{O\&M}$ is a cost (+).
- **Use Case in Robotics:** Cell selection, replacement analysis, automation ROI, CAPEX-vs-OPEX trade-off.
- **Key Limitation:** EUAC is purely monetary; combine with MCDA for non-tangible factors (safety, quality, flexibility).
- **Common MARR values in KTU problems:** **10%, 12%, 15%** — always read the problem carefully.
- **Always include units** (₹/year) in your final answer for full marks.
- **Sanity check:** If two alternatives have nearly equal EUAC (within 2–3%), report the difference and recommend further sensitivity analysis rather than a definitive choice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Return on Investment (ROI) Method (Problems Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Return on Investment (ROI) Method (Problems Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Return on Investment (ROI) Method** is a financial performance evaluation metric used in industrial engineering and manufacturing economics to quantify the profitability and economic feasibility of capital investments — particularly the acquisition and integration of **robotic workcells**, **automated manufacturing systems**, and **flexible manufacturing cells**.

Formally, ROI is defined as the ratio of the **net monetary benefit** (gain) obtained from an investment to the **total cost of that investment**, expressed as a percentage over a defined evaluation period (typically the **useful life of the asset**, e.g., 7–10 years for a robotic cell).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"Return on Investment is a profitability ratio that measures the efficiency of an investment by comparing the net benefit (savings + profit enhancement) generated over the life of the project to the total capital expended on procuring, integrating, and operating the robotic system."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small shopkeeper in Kerala who invests **₹10,00,000** to buy a softy-making machine. In the first year, the machine produces softies that earn him **₹3,00,000 in profit** (revenue minus raw material + electricity). He has therefore recovered **30%** of his initial investment in one year. The shopkeeper can now compare this to other options — like keeping the money in a bank FD giving 7% interest — and decide if the machine is worth it.

**In a robotic cell, exactly the same logic applies:**

| Shopkeeper's Machine Analogy | Robotic Cell Equivalent |
|------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Cost of the machine (₹10 L) | Cost of robot + end-effector + safety enclosure + integration |
| Profit from softies (₹3 L) | Labor savings + scrap reduction + throughput gain − operating cost |
| 30% return in year 1 | **30% ROI** in the first operating year |
| Compare with FD @ 7% | Compare with the company's **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** / **Hurdle Rate** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of ROI in Robotics:**
> If the calculated **ROI > MARR (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return)** of the company, the robotic investment is **economically justified** and should be approved. If **ROI < MARR**, the project must be rejected, redesigned for cost reduction, or re-evaluated over a longer time horizon.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical-Economic Constants

The following metrics are **bold-highlighted** as they appear verbatim in KTU valuation keys:

- **Useful Life of Robot (n)** = typically **7 to 10 years** (for industrial articulated robots)
- **MARR (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return)** = company-specific hurdle rate, usually **12% – 20%** for Indian manufacturing firms
- **Salvage Value (SV)** = residual value of the robot at end of life, usually **5% – 10%** of purchase price
- **Payback Acceptance Threshold** = often correlated to ROI acceptance

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition of the ROI Method

The ROI method works by transforming the **multi-year, multi-cash-flow** reality of a robotic project into a **single percentage number** that can be benchmarked. The logic proceeds in **five structured steps**:

- **Step 1 — Identify Total Investment (I):** Sum of all capital outflows at Year 0 (purchase, installation, tooling, training, engineering, safety).
- **Step 2 — Identify Annual Net Benefit (B):** For each year, compute (Labor Savings + Material Savings + Quality Gains + Throughput Gains) − (Operating Cost + Maintenance + Energy + Programming).
- **Step 3 — Discount or Aggregate over Useful Life (n years):** Either (a) simple averaging ignoring time value of money → **Simple ROI**, or (b) discounted cash flow (DCF) using present value → **Discounted ROI**.
- **Step 4 — Apply the ROI Formula:** Ratio of cumulative net benefit to total investment, expressed as %.
- **Step 5 — Compare with MARR / Hurdle Rate:** Make accept/reject decision.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the ROI Method

> **Why use ROI instead of just "total profit"?**
> Because a project that gives ₹50 lakh profit on a ₹10 lakh investment is *very different* from one that gives ₹50 lakh profit on a ₹2 crore investment. ROI **normalizes profit by the capital consumed**, allowing fair comparison between projects of vastly different scales — e.g., a single robot vs. a full flexible manufacturing cell.

> **Why not just use Payback Period?**
> Payback only tells you *when* you recover the cost, not *how profitable* the project is over its lifetime. ROI captures **the magnitude of wealth creation**, which is what shareholders and CFOs actually care about.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Simple ROI (%)** | $$ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$$ | Used for quick screening; ignores time value of money. |
| 2 | **Average ROI (%)** | $$ROI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} B_t \;\vert\; I_0}{I_0} \times \dfrac{1}{n} \times 100$$ | Averages yearly net benefit over the useful life $n$. |
| 3 | **Cumulative ROI (%)** | $$ROI_{cum} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} B_t}{I_0} \times 100$$ | Total benefit over entire life divided by initial investment. |
| 4 | **Discounted (True) ROI** | $$ROI_{DCF} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t} + \dfrac{SV}{(1+i)^n}}{I_0} \times 100$$ | Uses discount rate $i$ (cost of capital). **Closest to true economic ROI.** |
| 5 | **Investment Cost (I₀)** | $$I_0 = C_{robot} + C_{EOAT} + C_{install} + C_{safety} + C_{train} + C_{eng}$$ | Sum of all Year-0 capital outflows. |
| 6 | **Annual Benefit (Bₜ)** | $$B_t = S_{labor} + S_{material} + S_{quality} + S_{throughput} - C_{op}$$ | Net yearly saving from automation. |
| 7 | **Decision Rule** | $$\text{Accept if } ROI \geq MARR$$ | If computed ROI ≥ company hurdle rate. |
| 8 | **Equivalent Annual ROI** | $$ROI_{eq} = \left[ \left(1 + ROI_{cum} \right)^{1/n} - 1 \right] \times 100$$ | Converts cumulative ROI to a per-year compounded rate. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:**
> When the question asks for "ROI method" without specifying simple/discounted, the **default expected answer** is the **Simple ROI (Formula 1)** or the **Average ROI (Formula 2)**. Always mention the assumption that all annual benefits are equal unless otherwise stated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the context of **robotic manipulator and cell design**, the ROI method is the **most widely used screening tool** in industry because:

- It is **simple, intuitive, and communicable** to non-finance managers and shop-floor engineers.
- It directly answers the question: *"For every ₹1 spent on this robot, how many paise do we earn per year?"*
- It can be computed in **minutes** using only a spreadsheet, unlike Net Present Value (NPV) or Internal Rate of Return (IRR) which require iterative methods.
- It is the **primary metric** used by companies like **Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, and Bosch India** to justify robotic welding, painting, and pick-and-place cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic ROI Formula from First Principles

The ROI method is built on a single economic axiom: **profit must be measured in proportion to capital employed**. We derive it below in complete, examiner-friendly steps.

**Starting Premise:** A capital investment $I_0$ is made at time $t=0$. Over a period of $n$ years, the project generates a series of annual net cash flows $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n$.

**Step 1 — Define Total Net Benefit Over Life:**

The total monetary benefit generated by the project over its useful life is the sum of all annual net benefits:

$$
B_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} B_t
$$

**Step 2 — Define "Profit per Rupee Invested":**

To find how much profit is generated per unit of capital invested, we form a ratio:

$$
\text{Profit per Rupee} = \frac{B_{total}}{I_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a Percentage (standard accounting convention):**

Multiply the ratio by 100 to express it as a percentage, which is the universal convention for return metrics:

$$
ROI = \frac{B_{total}}{I_0} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Special Case: Equal Annual Benefits:**

If we assume the annual net benefit is constant, $B_t = B$ for all $t$, then $B_{total} = n \cdot B$, and the formula reduces to:

$$
ROI = \frac{n \cdot B}{I_0} \times 100
$$

This is the **Simple ROI** most commonly tested in KTU papers.

**Step 5 — Average Annualized Form:**

Dividing the cumulative ROI by $n$ gives the **average annual ROI**, useful when comparing projects with different useful lives:

$$
ROI_{avg} = \frac{B}{I_0} \times 100 = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n \cdot B}{I_0} \times 100
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Components of a Robotic Cell ROI

| Component Category | Item | Typical Treatment |
|---------------------|------|---------------------|
| **Investment Outflows (I₀)** | Robot purchase price | Direct cost |
| | End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT) | Direct cost |
| | Safety fencing / light curtain | Capital cost |
| | Installation & commissioning | One-time cost |
| | Engineering / integration labour | Direct cost |
| | Operator training | One-time cost |
| | Working capital for spares | Direct cost |
| **Annual Benefit Inflows (Bₜ)** | Direct labour savings | Recurring annual saving |
| | Indirect labour (supervision) savings | Recurring annual saving |
| | Scrap / rework reduction | Recurring annual saving |
| | Throughput / capacity increase | Recurring annual saving |
| | Quality premium / warranty reduction | Recurring annual saving |
| **Annual Cost Outflows (Cₜ)** | Preventive maintenance | Annual cost |
| | Spare parts & consumables | Annual cost |
| | Energy (pneumatic, electric) | Annual cost |
| | Programming / reprogramming labour | Annual cost |
| | Insurance & depreciation write-off | Annual cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The net annual benefit used in the ROI formula is **Bₜ − Cₜ**, not just Bₜ. Many students lose marks by forgetting to subtract operating costs.

### 3.3 Conceptual Numerical Illustration (For Theory Clarity — Problems Not Required)

Although the syllabus specifies *"Problems Not Required"*, a single conceptual illustration is presented below to anchor the formulas in numerical reality. This is **not for examination** but for conceptual reinforcement.

> **Scenario:** A company invests **₹50,00,000** in a robotic welding cell. The annual labour saving is **₹18,00,000**; annual scrap reduction is **₹3,00,000**; annual maintenance and energy cost is **₹5,00,000**. Useful life is **8 years**. Company MARR is **15%**.

**Annual Net Benefit:**

$$
B = 18{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Simple ROI:**

$$
ROI = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 32\%
$$

**Cumulative ROI over 8 years:**

$$
ROI_{cum} = \frac{8 \times 16{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 256\%
$$

**Equivalent Annual ROI (per-year compounded):**

$$
ROI_{eq} = \left[ \left(1 + 2.56 \right)^{1/8} - 1 \right] \times 100 \approx 17.7\%
$$

**Decision:** Since $ROI_{eq} = 17.7\% > MARR = 15\%$, the project is **economically accepted**.

### 3.4 Advantages and Limitations — Analytical View

**Advantages:**

- **Simplicity:** Single-number, single-formula computation.
- **Universality:** Communicable across engineering, finance, and management.
- **Comparability:** Allows ranking of mutually exclusive robotic projects.
- **Quick Screening:** Filters out clearly unviable projects in minutes.

**Limitations:**

- **Ignores Time Value of Money** (in simple ROI variant) — this is the single biggest criticism.
- **Sensitive to Bookkeeping Choices:** Depreciation method, salvage value treatment, and allocation of overheads can swing the ROI by several percentage points.
- **Ignores Project Size:** A small project with 50% ROI may create less total wealth than a large project with 20% ROI.
- **No Risk Adjustment:** Does not capture the uncertainty of future cash flows.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — ROI Computation Pipeline

The following flowchart maps the end-to-end logic of computing ROI for a robotic cell investment, as expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Robotic Investment Proposal] --> B[Step 1: List All Year-0 Capital Outlays]
    B --> B1[Robot Cost]
    B --> B2[End of Arm Tooling]
    B --> B3[Safety System]
    B --> B4[Installation and Commissioning]
    B --> B5[Training and Engineering]
    B1 --> C[Sum Total Investment I0]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Step 2: Estimate Annual Benefit Bt]
    D --> D1[Labour Savings]
    D --> D2[Material Savings]
    D --> D3[Quality Improvements]
    D --> D4[Throughput Gains]
    D1 --> E[Sum Annual Benefit]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Step 3: Subtract Annual Operating Cost Ct]
    F --> G[Compute Net Annual Benefit B = Bt - Ct]
    G --> H[Step 4: Apply ROI Formula]
    H --> H1[Simple ROI = B divided by I0 times 100]
    H --> H2[Average ROI = n times B divided by I0 times 100]
    H --> H3[Discounted ROI using Cost of Capital i]
    H1 --> I[Step 5: Compare ROI with MARR]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J{Decision: ROI greater than or equal to MARR}
    J -->|Yes| K[ACCEPT the Robotic Investment]
    J -->|No| L[REJECT or REDESIGN Project]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Investment Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer: Capital Outflow]
        IN1[Robot Hardware]
        IN2[Peripherals and EOAT]
        IN3[Integration and Training]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer: Annual Net Benefit]
        PR1[Direct Savings Block]
        PR2[Indirect Savings Block]
        PR3[Operating Cost Block]
    end
    subgraph DECISION[Decision Layer: ROI Engine]
        DC1[ROI Formula Engine]
        DC2[MARR Comparator]
        DC3[Accept or Reject Output]
    end
    IN1 --> PR1
    IN2 --> PR1
    IN3 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> DC1
    DC1 --> DC2
    DC2 --> DC3
```

### 4.3 Description of Diagram Logic

- **Input Layer** captures all one-time capital outflows that form $I_0$.
- **Processing Layer** computes the **net annual benefit** by stacking gross savings and subtracting operating costs.
- **Decision Layer** applies the ROI formula, compares with MARR, and emits the **Accept / Reject** decision.

This block-level architecture is the standard answer pattern expected by KTU examiners for 14-mark questions on investment analysis methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define the Return on Investment (ROI) method. State its decision rule.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Return on Investment (ROI) method** is a financial evaluation technique that measures the profitability of a capital investment (such as a robotic workcell) by expressing the **net annual benefit** as a percentage of the **total initial investment**.

$$
ROI = \frac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100
$$

**Decision Rule:** A project is **accepted** if the computed ROI is **greater than or equal to** the company's **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)**; otherwise, the project is **rejected**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **List any four components of 'Total Investment' in a robotic cell.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four major components of total investment $I_0$ in a robotic cell are:

1. **Cost of the robot manipulator** (purchase price).
2. **End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT)** — grippers, weld torches, etc.
3. **Installation, commissioning and safety systems** — fencing, light curtains, interlocks.
4. **Engineering, integration and operator training** costs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Return on Investment (ROI) method** as applied to the economic justification of a robotic workcell. State its formula and decision rule. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **various components of investment cost and annual benefits** that must be considered while evaluating a robotic cell using the ROI method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Explanation of ROI Method (7 Marks):**

The **Return on Investment (ROI) method** is a profitability ratio used to assess whether a robotic manipulator or automated cell is financially viable. It expresses the **net monetary benefit** obtained from the investment as a percentage of the **total capital expended**.

The **formula** is:

$$
ROI = \frac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100
$$

In its **cumulative form** over the useful life $n$ years:

$$
ROI_{cum} = \frac{n \cdot B}{I_0} \times 100
$$

**Decision Rule:** The project is **accepted** if $ROI \geq MARR$ (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return), and **rejected** if $ROI < MARR$.

The method is popular because it condenses a multi-year, multi-cash-flow project into a **single interpretable percentage** that can be compared across projects of different sizes and lifespans. It is the **most widely used screening tool** in Indian manufacturing industries for justifying robotic investments in welding, painting, assembly, and material handling.

**(b) Components of Investment and Benefits (7 Marks):**

**Total Investment ($I_0$) includes:**

- Robot hardware cost (manipulator, controller, teach pendant).
- End-of-Arm Tooling (gripper, weld gun, paint sprayer, sensors).
- Safety infrastructure (fencing, light curtains, interlocks, E-stops).
- Installation, commissioning, and integration labour.
- Engineering and design effort (cell layout, fixture design, PLC programming).
- Training of operators and maintenance staff.
- Working capital for initial spare parts inventory.

**Annual Benefits ($B_t$) include:**

- Direct labour savings (eliminated manual operators).
- Indirect labour savings (supervision, inspection, rework).
- Material savings (reduced scrap, lower consumable usage).
- Quality improvements (lower rejection, reduced warranty claims).
- Throughput gains (faster cycle time, higher machine utilisation).
- Energy and floor-space optimisation.

**Annual Operating Costs ($C_t$) to be subtracted include:**

- Preventive and breakdown maintenance.
- Spare parts and consumables.
- Energy (electrical, pneumatic).
- Re-programming and changeover labour.
- Insurance and depreciation write-offs.

The **net annual benefit** used in the ROI formula is $B_t - C_t$, not gross benefit.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper]**
>
> **(a)** With the aid of a **flowchart**, describe the **step-by-step procedure** for computing the ROI of a robotic manipulator investment. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **Simple ROI method** with the **Discounted ROI method**. List **three advantages and two limitations** of the ROI method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Step-by-Step ROI Procedure (7 Marks):**

The procedure for computing ROI of a robotic investment is as follows:

1. **Step 1:** Identify and list all **Year-0 capital outlays** — robot cost, EOAT, safety systems, installation, training.
2. **Step 2:** Sum these outlays to obtain the **Total Investment $I_0$**.
3. **Step 3:** Estimate the **annual gross benefits** — labour savings, material savings, quality gains, throughput improvements.
4. **Step 4:** Estimate the **annual operating costs** — maintenance, energy, spares, programming.
5. **Step 5:** Compute the **Net Annual Benefit** $B = (\text{Gross Benefits}) - (\text{Operating Costs})$.
6. **Step 6:** Apply the **Simple ROI Formula** $ROI = (B / I_0) \times 100$, or the **Cumulative ROI** $ROI_{cum} = (n \cdot B / I_0) \times 100$.
7. **Step 7:** Compare the computed ROI with the company's **MARR**.
8. **Step 8:** Make the **Accept / Reject** decision based on whether $ROI \geq MARR$.

A flowchart representation of this procedure is **shown in Section 4.1 above** (the Mermaid pipeline) and should be reproduced in the answer script.

**(b) Comparison and Merits/Demerits (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Simple ROI | Discounted ROI |
|--------|------------|-----------------|
| Time value of money | Ignored | Considered via discount rate $i$ |
| Formula | $ROI = (B / I_0) \times 100$ | $ROI = [\sum B_t / (1+i)^t + SV/(1+i)^n] / I_0 \times 100$ |
| Computational effort | Very low | Moderate (requires discount factors) |
| Accuracy | Approximate | Closer to true economic ROI |
| Use case | Quick screening | Detailed feasibility study |

**Three Advantages of ROI Method:**

1. **Simplicity:** Computed using a single straightforward formula.
2. **Comparability:** Allows ranking of projects of different sizes and lifespans.
3. **Universal Communication:** Easily understood by engineers, managers, and finance teams.

**Two Limitations of ROI Method:**

1. **Ignores Time Value of Money** in the simple variant — a rupee earned in Year 5 is treated equal to a rupee earned in Year 1.
2. **Sensitive to Accounting Conventions** — depreciation method, salvage value, and overhead allocation can distort the result.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract operating costs** — using gross savings instead of net annual benefit. Always compute $B_t - C_t$.
> 2. **Confusing ROI with Payback Period** — Payback gives years; ROI gives a percentage. They are *not* the same.
> 3. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** — even if the formula is correct, failing to write *"Accept if ROI ≥ MARR"* costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up cumulative and average ROI** — read the question carefully: "ROI over the project life" means cumulative; "annual ROI" means average.
> 5. **Omitting the units (% sign)** in the final answer — KTU examiners specifically check for the percentage symbol.
> 6. **Writing ROI in decimal form** (e.g., 0.32) instead of percentage (32%) — this is treated as an incomplete answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **ROI Definition:** Profitability ratio = (Net Annual Benefit / Total Investment) × 100.
> - **Core Formula (Simple):** $ROI = (B / I_0) \times 100$.
> - **Core Formula (Cumulative over n years):** $ROI_{cum} = (n \cdot B / I_0) \times 100$.
> - **Core Formula (Discounted):** $ROI = [\sum B_t(1+i)^{-t} + SV(1+i)^{-n}] / I_0 \times 100$.
> - **Decision Rule:** Accept if $ROI \geq MARR$; Reject if $ROI < MARR$.
> - **Total Investment $I_0$** = Robot + EOAT + Safety + Installation + Training + Engineering.
> - **Net Annual Benefit** = Gross Savings − Operating Costs (NOT gross savings alone).
> - **Savings Categories:** Direct labour, indirect labour, material, quality, throughput.
> - **Cost Categories:** Maintenance, spares, energy, programming, insurance.
> - **Typical Useful Life of Robot:** 7–10 years.
> - **Typical MARR Range (Indian industry):** 12% – 20%.
> - **Key Advantage:** Simplicity, comparability, universal communication.
> - **Key Limitation:** Ignores time value of money (in simple variant).
> - **Not the Same As:** Payback Period, NPV, IRR, Profitability Index.
> - **Board Exam Default:** If unspecified, use **Simple ROI** and assume **constant annual benefit**.
> - **Always quote the answer in % form with one decimal place precision.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subsequent Use of the Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT402 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Subsequent Use of the Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Subsequent Use of the Robot* refers to the strategic lifecycle phase **after the robot's primary production deployment**, encompassing its **redeployment, reprogramming, refurbishment, reassignment, or remanufacturing** to extend its operational utility. It is a critical component of *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* analysis and *Sustainable Manufacturing* practices in robotic workcell design.

The concept addresses what happens to an industrial robot **after** it has completed its original mission in a workcell. Instead of being scrapped when a product line is discontinued, the robot can be evaluated for **secondary service life** through a structured decision-making framework involving technical assessment, economic viability, and integration engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Refurbished Car Market" for Robots**
>
> Think of an industrial robot like a **high-end delivery truck** used by a logistics company. After 5 years of dedicated daily runs between Warehouse A and Warehouse B, the company changes its routes. What now happens to the truck?
>
> 1. **Option 1 (Scrap):** Sell for parts → *End-of-Life (EOL)*
> 2. **Option 2 (Subsequent Use - Reassignment):** Sell the well-maintained truck to a smaller bakery that needs deliveries → *Reassignment / Secondary Market*
> 3. **Option 3 (Subsequent Use - Refurbishment):** Upgrade the GPS, repaint, replace worn tires, and re-deploy it for a new route → *Refurbishment & Redeployment*
> 4. **Option 4 (Subsequent Use - Remanufacturing):** Strip it down, replace the engine with a hybrid one, install new sensors, and certify it for a new decade of service → *Remanufacturing*
>
> In robotics, "Subsequent Use" is the **engineering discipline of choosing and executing Options 2, 3, or 4** instead of scrapping the capital asset. The robot's **mechanical structure, controllers, and software** can each be evaluated for residual value.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Reassignment** | Direct redeployment of a robot to a new task without major modification |
| **Refurbishment** | Restoration to original operating specifications (cleaning, recalibration, part replacement) |
| **Remanufacturing** | Disassembly, inspection, and rebuilding to a "like-new" state with upgraded components |
| **Reprogramming** | Software-level subsequent use — same hardware, new task definitions |
| **Workcell Migration** | Moving a robot from one cell to another within the same plant |
| **Decommissioning** | Formal retirement of the robot, often the precursor to subsequent use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> The topic emphasizes the *economic, environmental, and operational* dimensions of robot lifecycle extension. Marks are frequently awarded for connecting *subsequent use* to **Industry 4.0**, **circular economy**, and **flexible manufacturing systems (FMS)**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Subsequent Use Decisions

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** A robot with MTBF > **40,000 hours** is typically a strong candidate for subsequent use.
- **Remaining Useful Life (RUL):** Expressed as a **percentage** of original design life (typically evaluated when RUL > 30%).
- **Depreciation Status:** Industrial robots are usually fully depreciated (straight-line over **5–7 years**), making subsequent use highly attractive economically.
- **Energy Efficiency Threshold:** Modern subsequent-use decisions weigh whether the older robot's **kWh consumption per cycle** is within **20%** of a new equivalent.
