## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Strategic Pathways for Subsequent Use

The lifecycle of an industrial robot does not end at the completion of its first production task. The KTU 2024 framework recognizes **five structured pathways** for subsequent use, each governed by distinct engineering and economic criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> The decision pathway is **NOT arbitrary** — it follows a decision tree based on the robot's *Condition Index (CI)* and the *Return on Subsequent Use Investment (RoSUI)*.

#### Pathway 1: Reassignment (Direct Re-tasking)
- The robot's hardware and software are **functionally intact**.
- The end-effector and program are changed to suit a new product family.
- **Typical candidates:** Painting robots moving from automotive bodies to agricultural equipment.
- **Required Downtime:** 1–3 days.
- **Capital Avoidance:** **40–70%** of a new robot's cost.

#### Pathway 2: Refurbishment
- The robot is restored to its **original OEM specifications**.
- Involves replacement of wear parts (grippers, seals, cables), recalibration of encoders, and software re-flashing.
- **Typical candidates:** Robots at 50,000–60,000 operating hours with documented maintenance history.
- **Cost:** **30–50%** of new robot price.

#### Pathway 3: Remanufacturing
- The robot is **disassembled to component level**, inspected, and rebuilt with some **upgraded** parts.
- Often involves a **new controller** paired with retained mechanical structure.
- **Result:** "Like-new" performance with a **warranty comparable to OEM**.
- **Cost:** **50–70%** of new robot price.

#### Pathway 4: Repurposing (Component Harvesting)
- The robot is **not viable as a unit**, but its components (servo motors, RV reducers, drives) are harvested.
- These high-value parts enter a **secondary spares market** for other operational robots.
- **Economic Value:** 15–25% of original price.

#### Pathway 5: Recycling (Material Recovery)
- Final fallback: recovery of **steel, copper, aluminum, and rare-earth magnets**.
- **Material Recovery Rate:** Up to **90%** by mass for steel-arm robots.
- **Environmental Driver:** Compliance with WEEE (Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment) directives.

### 2.2 The Decision Matrix Logic

The choice between pathways is driven by a structured evaluation:

1. **Step A — Condition Assessment:** Measure the robot's current health using MTBF, joint backlash (in **arc-minutes**), and axis-positioning repeatability (in **mm**).
2. **Step B — Economic Modeling:** Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) of each pathway.
3. **Step C — Strategic Fit:** Check if the new task's *payload, reach, and repeatability* requirements are within the robot's residual capability envelope.
4. **Step D — Risk Profiling:** Assess integration risk with the **new cell layout** and **existing PLC/SCADA** infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters (Engineering Reality):**
> A typical automotive FANUC R-2000iC robot costs **₹45–55 lakhs** new. A refurbished equivalent can be acquired for **₹15–20 lakhs**, with delivery in **2 weeks** versus 12–16 weeks for a new unit. This makes subsequent use a **strategic lever** in *capital-intensive industries*.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for subsequent use calculations. Memorize the formulas, units, and typical value ranges.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Present Value of Subsequent Use** | $NPV_{SU} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $B_t$ = Benefits in year $t$; $C_t$ = Costs in year $t$; $r$ = Discount rate; $N$ = Lifecycle years | ₹ (currency) | NPV > 0 ⇒ Proceed |
| 2 | **Cost Ratio (Refurbishment)** | $CR_{refurb} = \dfrac{C_{refurb}}{C_{new}}$ | $C_{refurb}$ = Refurbished cost; $C_{new}$ = New robot cost | Dimensionless | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| 3 | **Cost Ratio (Remanufacturing)** | $CR_{reman} = \dfrac{C_{reman}}{C_{new}}$ | $C_{reman}$ = Remanufactured cost | Dimensionless | 0.50 – 0.70 |
| 4 | **Condition Index** | $CI = \dfrac{MTBF_{current}}{MTBF_{original}}$ | $MTBF_{current}$ = Current MTBF; $MTBF_{original}$ = OEM-rated MTBF | Dimensionless | CI > 0.6 ⇒ Candidate |
| 5 | **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** | $RUL\% = \dfrac{T_{design} - T_{operated}}{T_{design}} \times 100$ | $T_{design}$ = Design life; $T_{operated}$ = Hours already operated | % | > 30% viable |
| 6 | **Energy Efficiency Ratio** | $EER = \dfrac{E_{new}}{E_{old}}$ | $E_{new}$ = kWh/cycle of new robot; $E_{old}$ = kWh/cycle of existing | Dimensionless | EER < 1.2 ⇒ keep old |
| 7 | **Payback Period** | $PP = \dfrac{C_{subsequent}}{A_{annual\_saving}}$ | $C_{subsequent}$ = Investment in subsequent use; $A_{annual\_saving}$ = Annual cost savings | Years | < 3 years ideal |
| 8 | **Material Recovery Value** | $MRV = \sum_{i} m_i \cdot p_i$ | $m_i$ = Mass of material $i$ recovered; $p_i$ = Market price of $i$ | ₹ | 10–25% of original cost |
| 9 | **Robot Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = Failure rate; $t$ = Operating time | Dimensionless | 0 < R(t) < 1 |
| 10 | **Availability for Reassignment** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair | Dimensionless | A > 0.95 preferred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule (Exam Hall):**
> *Never use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside a markdown table cell* — it breaks the parser. For absolute values and determinants in tables, use `\vert` or `\mid`. Example: write $\vert CI \vert$ instead of `|CI|` inside the cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Subsequent use analysis is **not academic** — it is core to:
- **Automotive Tier-1 suppliers** managing 100+ robot fleets across model transitions.
- **MSME (Small & Medium Enterprise) adoption** of robotics at **40% lower capex** through certified refurbished units.
- **Sustainability reporting** under **GRI 306** (Waste) and **ISO 14001** frameworks.
- **Defense & Aerospace** where long-life-cycle programs (20–30 years) require **spare-part continuity** via component harvesting.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Present Value of Subsequent Use Decision

The economic justification for any subsequent use pathway is formalized through the **NPV equation** over the *extended lifecycle horizon*.

**Given:**
- Initial investment for subsequent use at $t = 0$: $C_0$ (currency units).
- Annual operational benefit (avoided cost + revenue uplift) in year $t$: $B_t$.
- Annual maintenance cost in year $t$: $M_t$.
- Discount rate: $r$ (dimensionless, e.g., 0.10 for 10%).
- Extended lifecycle: $N$ years.

**Step 1 — Define the Net Cash Flow in Year $t$:**
The net cash flow for any year $t$ during the subsequent use phase is the operational benefit minus the maintenance cost and any recurring integration cost.

$$
NCF_t = B_t - M_t
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the Initial Subsequent-Use Investment at $t = 0$:**
At $t = 0$, there is an outflow equal to the cost of the chosen pathway (reassignment, refurbishment, or remanufacturing).

$$
NCF_0 = -C_0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Discounting Principle:**
A rupee (or dollar) received in year $t$ is worth less today due to the *time value of money*. The discount factor is $(1 + r)^{-t}$.

$$
PV_t = \frac{NCF_t}{(1 + r)^{t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum All Discounted Cash Flows:**
Summing the present values across the entire extended lifecycle gives the NPV.

$$
NPV_{SU} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{NCF_t}{(1 + r)^{t}}
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Sum for the First Three Years (KTU Exam Format):**
This is the most commonly asked expansion in KTU valuation scripts.

$$
NPV_{SU} = -C_0 + \frac{B_1 - M_1}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{B_2 - M_2}{(1+r)^{2}} + \frac{B_3 - M_3}{(1+r)^{3}} + \ldots + \frac{B_N - M_N}{(1+r)^{N}}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Proceed with Subsequent Use} & \text{if } NPV_{SU} > 0 \\ \text{Procure New Robot} & \text{if } NPV_{SU} \leq 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $NPV_{SU} > 0$, the *discounted future savings* exceed the upfront investment, and the subsequent use pathway is economically justified.
- If $NPV_{SU} \leq 0$, the firm should either procure a new robot (if performance gaps are critical) or scrap the unit.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem:**
> A manufacturing company is evaluating **refurbishment vs. new purchase** for a KUKA KR 16 robot. The refurbishment cost is **₹18,00,000**, the new robot cost is **₹42,00,000**. Annual operational savings from the existing infrastructure compatibility are estimated at **₹6,00,000/year**. Annual maintenance for the refurbished unit is **₹1,20,000/year** vs. **₹80,000/year** for a new one. The discount rate is **10%**, and the planning horizon is **5 years**. Decide using NPV.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables:**

$$
C_0 = 18{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{₹ (Refurbishment cost)}
$$

$$
B_t = 6{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{₹/year (Operational savings)}
$$

$$
M_t = 1{,}20{,}000 \quad \text{₹/year (Maintenance)}
$$

$$
r = 0.10, \quad N = 5 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Net Cash Flow per Year:**

$$
NCF_t = B_t - M_t = 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Discount Factor for Each Year:**

$$
DF_1 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{1}} = 0.9091
$$

$$
DF_2 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{2}} = 0.8264
$$

$$
DF_3 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{3}} = 0.7513
$$

$$
DF_4 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{4}} = 0.6830
$$

$$
DF_5 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{5}} = 0.6209
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Present Value per Year:**

$$
PV_1 = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 4{,}36{,}368 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
PV_2 = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 3{,}96{,}672 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
PV_3 = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 3{,}60{,}624 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
PV_4 = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 3{,}27{,}840 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
PV_5 = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 2{,}98{,}032 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Present Values and Subtract Initial Investment:**

$$
NPV_{SU} = -18{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}36{,}368 + 3{,}96{,}672 + 3{,}60{,}624 + 3{,}27{,}840 + 2{,}98{,}032
$$

$$
NPV_{SU} = -18{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}19{,}536 = +19{,}536 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$
NPV_{SU} = +19{,}536 > 0 \implies \text{Proceed with Refurbishment}
$$

**Step 7 — Sanity Check (Cost Ratio):**

$$
CR_{refurb} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{42{,}00{,}000} = 0.4286
$$

This ratio of **0.43** falls in the typical range of 0.30–0.50, validating the **Pathway 2 (Refurbishment)** choice as both economically and technically sound.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is frequently reproduced in 7-mark sub-questions. Memorize the layout.

| Condition Index (CI) | Annual Operating Hours | New Task Payload Demand | Recommended Pathway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CI > 0.8** | < 3,000 | ≤ Original payload | **Reassignment (Pathway 1)** |
| **0.6 < CI ≤ 0.8** | 3,000 – 5,000 | ≤ Original payload | **Refurbishment (Pathway 2)** |
| **0.4 < CI ≤ 0.6** | 5,000 – 7,000 | ≤ 0.8 × Original | **Remanufacturing (Pathway 3)** |
| **0.2 < CI ≤ 0.4** | > 7,000 | Any | **Repurposing / Spares (Pathway 4)** |
| **CI ≤ 0.2** | Any | Any | **Recycling (Pathway 5)** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Subsequent-Use Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram maps the **engineering decision logic** for selecting a subsequent-use pathway. It is the standard KTU module-level schematic representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Completes Primary Task] --> B[End of Initial Production Run]
    B --> C{Is Product Line Continuing?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Continue Original Use]
    C -- No --> E[Initiate Subsequent Use Evaluation]
    E --> F[Condition Assessment: Measure CI and MTBF]
    F --> G{CI Greater Than 0.6?}
    G -- No --> H{CI Greater Than 0.2?}
    H -- No --> I[Pathway 5: Recycling and Material Recovery]
    H -- Yes --> J[Pathway 4: Component Repurposing for Spares]
    G -- Yes --> K[Economic Modeling: NPV Computation]
    K --> L{NPV Positive?}
    L -- No --> M[Procure New Robot Instead]
    L -- Yes --> N{New Task Within Reach and Payload Limits?}
    N -- No --> O[Pathway 3: Remanufacturing with Upgrades]
    N -- Yes --> P{Minor Wear and Calibration Drift Only?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Pathway 1: Reassignment]
    P -- No --> R[Pathway 2: Refurbishment to OEM Specs]
    Q --> S[Redeploy in New Workcell]
    R --> S
    O --> S
    J --> T[Return Components to Spare Parts Inventory]
    I --> U[Send to Material Recovery Stream]
    S --> V[Update Asset Register and Maintenance Logs]
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Subsequent Use Cycle Complete]
```

### 4.2 Lifecycle Loop Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Design and Manufacture] --> A2[Primary Deployment in Workcell]
    A2 --> A3[Mid-Life Upgrade and Service]
    A3 --> A4[End of Initial Product Lifecycle]
    A4 --> B1{Subsequent Use Decision Gate}
    B1 -- Reassign --> A2
    B1 -- Refurbish --> A2
    B1 -- Remanufacture --> A2
    B1 -- Component Harvest --> C1[Spare Parts Pool]
    B1 -- Recycle --> C2[Material Recovery]
    C1 --> A3
    C2 --> A1
```

### 4.3 Cost-Value Comparison Chart (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CAPEX[Capital Expenditure Spectrum]
        direction LR
        X1[New Robot: 100 percent] --> X2[Remanufactured: 50 to 70 percent]
        X2 --> X3[Refurbished: 30 to 50 percent]

        X4 --> X5[Spare Component: 15 to 25 percent]
        X5 --> X6[Scrap Material: 5 to 10 percent]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "Subsequent Use of the Robot" and list any four pathways through which it can be achieved.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Subsequent Use of the Robot* is the structured lifecycle strategy applied **after the robot's primary production task is completed**, where the unit is evaluated for redeployment, refurbishment, remanufacturing, component repurposing, or recycling instead of being scrapped. *(1 mark)*

The four pathways are:
1. **Reassignment** — direct re-tasking with minimal changes. *(0.5 marks)*
2. **Refurbishment** — restoration to OEM specifications. *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Remanufacturing** — disassembly, inspection, and rebuilding with some upgrades. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Repurposing / Recycling** — harvesting spares or recovering materials. *(0.5 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Condition Index (CI) of a robot, and what is the minimum CI value generally accepted for reassignment?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Condition Index (CI)** is the ratio of the robot's **current MTBF** to its **original OEM-rated MTBF**, expressed as:

$$
CI = \frac{MTBF_{current}}{MTBF_{original}}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1 that quantifies the **residual health** of the robot. A CI value of **0.6 or higher** is generally accepted as the minimum threshold for considering reassignment or refurbishment. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) for a 14-mark question follow a **two-part structure**: (a) carries 7 marks and (b) carries 7 marks. Each part typically demands *diagrams, formulas, and numerical working* for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **five pathways** for subsequent use of a robot, with a comparative cost table. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A robotic arm originally cost **₹50,00,000** with a design life of **80,000 hours**. It has been operated for **48,000 hours** and its current MTBF is **30,000 hours** versus the original rated MTBF of **50,000 hours**.
  - (i) Calculate the **Condition Index (CI)** and the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL) percentage**.
  - (ii) Based on these values, recommend a **subsequent use pathway** with justification. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Seven Marks Model Answer:**

The five pathways for subsequent use of a robot are:

1. **Reassignment:** The robot is moved to a new task without major hardware changes. The end-effector, program, and fixtures are updated. Typical cost: **5–15% of new price**. Suitable when the robot is functionally healthy and the new task falls within reach, payload, and accuracy envelopes.

2. **Refurbishment:** The robot is restored to original OEM performance. Worn parts (cables, seals, grippers) are replaced, encoders are recalibrated, and software is re-flashed. Typical cost: **30–50% of new price**. Suitable for robots at moderate operating hours with documented maintenance.

3. **Remanufacturing:** The robot is disassembled to component level, inspected, and rebuilt with a mix of original and upgraded parts. Often the controller is replaced with a modern one. Typical cost: **50–70% of new price**. Result is "like-new" performance with OEM-equivalent warranty.

4. **Repurposing (Component Harvesting):** The robot is no longer viable as a unit, but high-value components like servo motors, RV reducers, and drives are harvested for the spares market. Typical value: **15–25% of original cost**.

5. **Recycling (Material Recovery):** Final fallback where the structure is shredded to recover steel, copper, aluminum, and rare-earth magnets. Recovery rate: up to **90% by mass** for steel-arm robots.

| Pathway | Cost as % of New | Typical CI Range | Typical RUL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reassignment | 5–15% | > 0.8 | > 50% |
| Refurbishment | 30–50% | 0.6 – 0.8 | 30–50% |
| Remanufacturing | 50–70% | 0.4 – 0.6 | 15–30% |
| Repurposing | 15–25% | 0.2 – 0.4 | 5–15% |
| Recycling | 5–10% | < 0.2 | < 5% |

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for listing, 3 marks for explanation, 1 mark for diagram/flow reference, 2 marks for the comparative table.)*

**Part (b) — Seven Marks Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $C_{new} = 50{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $T_{design} = 80{,}000$ hours
- $T_{operated} = 48{,}000$ hours
- $MTBF_{current} = 30{,}000$ hours
- $MTBF_{original} = 50{,}000$ hours

**Step 1 — Calculate the Condition Index:**

$$
CI = \frac{MTBF_{current}}{MTBF_{original}} = \frac{30{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Remaining Useful Life percentage:**

$$
RUL\% = \frac{T_{design} - T_{operated}}{T_{design}} \times 100
$$

$$
RUL\% = \frac{80{,}000 - 48{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{32{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Step 3 — Reference the Decision Matrix:**

From the KTU analytical decision matrix:
- CI = 0.60 falls in the **0.4 < CI ≤ 0.6** boundary, leaning into the **0.6 < CI ≤ 0.8** band.
- RUL = 40% falls in the **30–50%** band.

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**

Based on CI = 0.60 and RUL = 40%, the recommended pathway is **Pathway 2: Refurbishment** to restore the robot to OEM specifications, provided the new task's payload and reach requirements are within the robot's residual capability envelope. If the new task demands significantly upgraded precision or a modern controller, **Pathway 3 (Remanufacturing)** should be considered.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for CI calculation, 2 marks for RUL calculation, 1 mark for decision matrix reference, 2 marks for justified recommendation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Discuss the **economic, environmental, and operational drivers** that make subsequent use of robots a strategic priority in modern manufacturing. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A firm is evaluating whether to **refurbish** an existing robot or **purchase a new one**. The refurbishment requires an upfront investment of **₹20,00,000**, while a new robot costs **₹48,00,000**. The refurbished unit will yield annual savings of **₹5,50,000** and incur annual maintenance of **₹1,00,000** for **5 years**. The discount rate is **9%**. Compute the **NPV of the subsequent use** decision and recommend a course of action. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Seven Marks Model Answer:**

**Economic Drivers:**
- New industrial robots cost **₹40–60 lakhs** with delivery lead times of **12–16 weeks**.
- Refurbished or reassigned units cost **30–50% less** with delivery in **1–3 weeks**.
- The **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** is significantly lower because the existing support infrastructure (controllers, training, fixtures) is retained.
- Working capital is freed up for other strategic investments.

**Environmental Drivers:**
- Manufacturing a new industrial robot generates approximately **500–800 kg of CO₂ equivalent** in steel, electronics, and transport.
- Subsequent use pathways reduce this carbon footprint by **40–60%** per reused unit.
- Compliance with **WEEE Directive** and **ISO 14001** environmental management standards.
- Aligns with the **circular economy** principles and **GRI 306** reporting standards.

**Operational Drivers:**
- Robot models like the **FANUC R-2000iC** and **KUKA KR 16** have proven designs with **MTBF > 60,000 hours**, making them candidates for long service lives.
- The original workforce is already trained on the platform, eliminating retraining costs.
- Spare parts continuity is assured through OEM support and the secondary spares market.
- Flexibility in **FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems)** is enhanced because the same robot can be re-tasked across product variants.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for economic, 2 marks for environmental, 2 marks for operational, 1 mark for real-world example.)*

**Part (b) — Seven Marks Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 20{,}00{,}000$ ₹ (Refurbishment)
- $B_t = 5{,}50{,}000$ ₹/year
- $M_t = 1{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year
- $r = 0.09$, $N = 5$ years

**Step 1 — Net Annual Cash Flow:**

$$
NCF_t = B_t - M_t = 5{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount Factors:**

$$
DF_1 = \frac{1}{1.09} = 0.9174
$$

$$
DF_2 = \frac{1}{(1.09)^{2}} = 0.8417
$$

$$
DF_3 = \frac{1}{(1.09)^{3}} = 0.7722
$$

$$
DF_4 = \frac{1}{(1.09)^{4}} = 0.7084
$$

$$
DF_5 = \frac{1}{(1.09)^{5}} = 0.6499
$$

**Step 3 — Present Values:**

$$
PV_1 = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9174 = 4{,}12{,}830
$$

$$
PV_2 = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8417 = 3{,}78{,}765
$$

$$
PV_3 = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7722 = 3{,}47{,}490
$$

$$
PV_4 = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7084 = 3{,}18{,}780
$$

$$
PV_5 = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6499 = 2{,}92{,}455
$$

**Step 4 — NPV Computation:**

$$
NPV_{SU} = -20{,}00{,}000 + (4{,}12{,}830 + 3{,}78{,}765 + 3{,}47{,}490 + 3{,}18{,}780 + 2{,}92{,}455)
$$

$$
NPV_{SU} = -20{,}00{,}000 + 17{,}50{,}320 = -2{,}49{,}680 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
NPV_{SU} = -2{,}49{,}680 < 0
$$

The NPV of the subsequent use (refurbishment) pathway is **negative**. Based purely on the economic NPV criterion, the firm should **NOT proceed with refurbishment** under the given assumptions. The annual net savings of **₹4,50,000** are insufficient to recover the upfront cost of **₹20,00,000** at a 9% discount rate over 5 years. The firm should consider:

- Extending the planning horizon beyond 5 years (typical refurbished robot life is 8–10 years).
- Negotiating a lower refurbishment cost (target: ~₹17,00,000 to make NPV ≈ 0).
- Or, procuring a new robot if the new task's precision and reliability demands justify it.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for discount factors, 2 marks for PV computation, 1 mark for NPV summation, 1 mark for justified recommendation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write `|x|` for absolute value inside markdown tables. This **breaks the markdown parser** and **costs presentation marks**. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **RUL** (Remaining Useful Life) with **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures). They are *different* metrics. RUL is in hours of design life remaining; MTBF is a reliability statistic.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students compute NPV but forget to **subtract the initial investment at $t = 0$**. The KTU model answer always shows $-C_0$ as the first term.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to state the **decision rule** explicitly: *"If NPV > 0, proceed; else, reject."* Examiners award 1 mark specifically for this statement.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing *"recycling"* when the question asks for *"subsequent use of the robot as a working unit."* Recycling is the *terminal* pathway, not a redeployment strategy.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- **Core Definition:** Subsequent use = any post-primary-deployment strategy that extends the robot's service life.
- **Five Pathways:** Reassignment → Refurbishment → Remanufacturing → Repurposing → Recycling (memorize the order).
- **Cost Spectrum:** 5–15% (Reassign) < 30–50% (Refurbish) < 50–70% (Remanufacture) < 15–25% (Spares) < 5–10% (Scrap).
- **Key Formulas (must memorize verbatim):**
  - $CI = MTBF_{current} / MTBF_{original}$
  - $RUL\% = (T_{design} - T_{operated}) / T_{design} \times 100$
  - $NPV_{SU} = \sum NCF_t / (1+r)^t$
  - $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$
- **Decision Threshold:** CI > 0.6 and RUL > 30% are the typical "viable for subsequent use" cutoffs.
- **Environmental Angle:** Subsequent use is a **circular economy** lever — 40–60% CO₂ reduction per reused unit.
- **Syllabus Hooks:** Always connect your answer to **Industry 4.0**, **FMS**, **TCO**, and **sustainability** in your introduction and conclusion.
- **Numerical Hygiene:** Show the discount factor table; show units in every line; write the decision rule explicitly.
- **Real-World Anchor:** Mention **FANUC, KUKA, ABB, Yaskawa** as OEM examples — examiners reward industry awareness.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse *subsequent use* (post-mission strategy) with *initial deployment* (workcell design).
- **KTU Buzzwords to Use:** "Total Cost of Ownership," "Mean Time Between Failures," "Condition Index," "Remanufacturing," "Circular Economy."
